title: Melon Salad With Nectarines, Tomatoes and Basil  Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: I saw this inspiring photo, read the blurb (love these people's approach), scanned the ingredients, and got right to work on my lunch.  I had apricots and Queen Anne cherries, neither very interesting eaten out of hand; small golden tomatoes; red onion; some leftover lemon vinaigrette; a hunk of asiago; and salted pistachios.  Together they made a delicious and healthy feast of my lunch, and I felt like I'd done something.  Thanks for this recipe and for loads of other great ones, NYT.

@Ingrid, I agree completely! What a lovely recipe did was inspire others to rethink summer fruits with a savory touch, plus a dash of heat! I made the recipe as a presented, as well. But I'll be using up some red plums and blueberries with Manchego cheese this week, along with some red baby pepperoncini forcsone heat.

My favorite part of the recipe: “you take what is great from what is right around you and do very little to it.”  With neither a caper leaf or a tablespoon of Chardonnay vinegar in sight, it was fabulous on its own with a tablespoon of white Modena vinegar.
"Salted capers, cut in half."  Made me laugh.  Never gonna happen in my house.

@Tracy
My grandchildren are starting to cook. Cutting the capers in half would be an excellent opportunity!

I’m not the kind of person who takes the time to nurture a salad, no matter how good it is. I hope that changes. 

I’m keeping it in my recipe box in the hopes that I find the quiet moments where I can make great things. Alas, today is not that day. 

But the story was wonderful. We all move on, leaving the good and the bad behind.

I didn't have the right fruit, I didn't do the ice water, I didn't build it up in layers but rather threw everything in together, I didn't use salt or pepper or bread.  It was delicious.  If you mix some kind of sweetish fruit with good tomatoes and onions, add decent cheese and mix it all up with a vinaigrette you like, it'll be great thing enough.  I hope you do it tomorrow.
Would love some clarification regarding “caper leaves.” Are these supposed to be torn apart capers or literally the unpickled leaves of the caper bush?

@Deb Onley
I believe they are referring to nasturtium leaves which ate peppery flavoured. Cspers cone from the flowers of the plant. My grandma used to add nadtirtiums flowers to salad too.

Sorry, but capers (the buds), caper berries and caper leaves are nothing whatsoever to do with nasturtiums. They come from the caper bush (Capparis spinosa), which grows all over the Mediterranean (and probably widely elsewhere, these days). You can buy them pickled or brined. I think the caper berries are larger, which is, I would guess, why the chef halves them in this recipe. They add a lovely tang to any dish (and usefully, last a long time in the fridge).
This looks fantastic - can't wait to make it. Thank you for the details in your Eats stories. I'll look for your reviews!
I think you could also use Tajin instead of the crushed red pepper, it goes so well with fruit. Haven’t tried it yet, but looks like a perfect summer salad.
@Susan You sure could. It works well.
The greatest piece of this for me was to season each item separately and then  layer — best advice!  I haven’t made this yet but I will soon for friends who enjoy a good symphony of taste.
This is why I liked the food in Sicily the best of all the Mediterranean countries we visited. The Sicilians cook the way I do. Find the best food and treat it gently.

Oh, how delicious and simple this is!!! I swapped out tajín for the chili flakes (as someone else wisely suggested) and also added cherries, since I had them on hand. This may be my new favorite summer lunch!

Wonderful salad. I cut the olive oil by a third, tossed in a handful of arugula, chopped up a small fresh cucumber from my garden, and added half a minced fresno pepper (low heat). Tangy, sweet -- a party in my mouth.

We had ripe melon and red onion on hand. I plucked ripe plums from our plum tree, and mint weeds from out back. Soaked the onion in lemon juice from the start. Sliced fresh tomatoes, shaved grana padano. Skipped the capers and vinegar (lemon juice felt like sufficient acid). One of the best salads we've ever had!

We loved this salad 
Happen to have some small fresh mozzarella balls
which were good added to the salad mix

Years ago a friend took me to a Bangkok restaurant that served a salad like this using barely-ripe but fragrant mango and with a Thai-inflected dressing. Similar to a northern style somtam but different. I remember that salad to this day because of the small dried fish that made one flavor layer. Sour salt fruit chilies and fish. With rice, of course.
I bought 20 mangoes off a truck, so I subbed mango for melon, and had on hand: a few cherries, firm little round plums, lime juice, Spanish meseta cheese…  all else as written. Superb.
Absolutely outstanding salad. Husband already requested it again for an upcoming special occasion. Worth the time to prep it...and luckily our capers were tiny so they didn't need to be cut in half.

This was great! I used mint and black pepper and sherry vinegar. I’d omit the onions next time. My combo was canary melon, white nectarines and yellow tomato with pecorino Romano.  I can’t wait to make it again

The introduction says "be sure to season each ingredient separately and build the salad in layers".  The instructions say to put the melon, peaches and tomatoes in the bowl together and then season.  Which yields the best result?

There is no contradiction here. Step 4 says, "generously season the fruit with sea salt" -- the author is treating the 3 fruits (melon, tomato, and peaches) as a single ingredient, which is reasonable. The onions and other savory ingredients are seasoned separately. Just follow the actual steps spelled out in the "Preparation" list and don't worry about a minor variation in language between the intro and the recipe
What is a caper leaf?

Delish! Subbed fresh mint for the basil. Thanks for the suggestion to mellow the onion in ice water. Never tried that before, and it works. The onion becomes a crunchy element with a gentle onion flavor. No need to hassle about the capers. I just threw in a few of the regular little ones I had in the refrigerator and they really topped the dish off nicely. Can’t wait to make this again when the white peaches sitting in the paper bag on my counter become ripe!
Caper…leaves? Today I purchased both fresh curry and perilla leaves. I have NEVER heard of caper leaves. What?

For once I found a recipe that looks as attractive when made at home as shown in the charming NYT Cooking image!
A few thoughts:
Vinegar not needed due to acidity of the lemon.
Mint is also a nice addition with the basil.
Why cut capers in half? No need!
Yellow watermelon, now availble at Trader Joe's, is a nice addition, replacing the nectarine.
Passed on the onion.
Made it exactly as written and without the layering of oil/lemon strategy. Both are great.

One of the best things we’ve made here.  Perfect combination of salty and sweet, and a wonderful expression of summer.  We followed the recipe but omitted the onion.  It was perfect, and I can easily imagine using different fruits as others have done.

I’ve never been compelled to comment on a recipe before this one—this was exceptional in so many ways. Like others, I scanned the recipe and measured with vibes. So little effort for so much payoff. Summer bursting forth with every mouthful. Farmer’s market nectarines and sungolds made it all the better!

Wonderful recipe!  I switched out the Melon for large grilled gulf shrimp, the nectarines were replaced by some great homemade pappardelle, 86'd the capers and added 8 crushed garlic cloves to the olive oil and red peppers and warmed over a low heat.  Tossed the shrimp and cherry tomatoes with the prepared al dente pappardelle, topped it with shaved Pecorino and julienned basil and black pepper.  Now my go to.
Used a peach balsamic vinegar and cucumber chunks as I did not have nectarines and also added some fresh mint leaves with the basil. Looks very flavor and texture!
This was great. I also added a little watermelon.

Utterly gorgeous salad. Must use all the right ingredients though. Layering of salt is important. Then the result will NOT be boring but a quality served at top restaurants. Do use the best ripe fruits and do use the sweet onions!
Wonderful recipe and summer meal! Made as written, except found no extra salt needed.

I’m completely confused by the “caper” references. I’ve never seen a caper leaf, bottled or otherwise,and never seen a caper that needed to be cut in half. I have seen larger bottled “caper berries” in stores: is that what is meant? And please explain the leaves and where to find them.

Making tonight and can taste in advance it will be first rate. 
I almost always make composed salads: prep in advance, mix each component in order of placement in the same bowl with appropriate amount of dressing, assembling on each plate. That way each ingredient shines. I find the outcome far superior to mixing everything at once. Presentation is elegant, beautiful and guests are wowed. Well worth the extra few minutes.

I used cantaloupe, yellow pear tomatoes,  orange cherry tomatoes, and basil leaves from my garden, plus Palisades Peaches, canary melon, Santa Claus melon, capers, and Aleppo pepper. I didn't have chardonnay vinegar, so I drizzled balsamic with the olive oil. Asiago and feta on top. Excellent way to used the best of my garden and what Colorado had to offer!

I made this pretty much as written. The only changes I made were to keep the onions separate for friends who can’t tolerate them (so sad), tajín instead of red pepper, and use a combination of sherry and balsamic vinegars. As others have said this is a highly adaptable recipe. It was delicious.
This was a lovely, refreshing, cooling, simple, flavorful combination on a surprisingly hot September afternoon--no-cook and no-fuss--loved it.

Delicious.  I made this mostly as written with the exception of using South Carolina peaches ( my mother always said they were the best) in place of the nectarines.  We have a small raised bed with little yellow pear tomatoes and basil so this provided a way to use our home grown produce.  My husband does not usually care for tomatoes but said that this was very good.

I made this salad on a bed of baby spinach, I loved having the greens and there was plenty of dressing without any adjustments to quantity. I didn’t like the basil and will leave that out next time. Also added some roasted chicken pieces to make it an all in one dinner. Loved it!
I added thinly sliced ribbons of prosciutto for a little protein lift. Tasty and so wonderful for the season!
It was delicious. I used Golden Petite Nectarines and Tajin instead of red pepper flakes.
Doesn't need bread but this is amazing!!!

Ok, I made it and it is another great 5 star recipe!  For those of us with tummy issues related to raw onion—the soaking of even a sweet onion in this recipe solved that issue. I took this to a friend healing from a fall and all four of us enjoyed it immensely.  With a no-boil pasta bake and light chocolate cake it was easy, pretty and impressive before we tasted—then it was fantastic!
I think I’m in love with this recipe.  Made it again for guests and they couldn’t stop raving!  You won’t be sorry.
Good summer salad. Used a little stilton blue, which added a nice tangy flavor. Layered as recommended, although not sure that made a difference. Paired with simple grilled chicken tenders.
Instead of chayote, I prefer green papaya, which is more traditional I thought. It's always lovely to read Ligaya Mishan's writing.
In the Philippines, there are lots of versions and sayote or papaya versions abound.

Loving this version with the crispy skin and a richer soup! My mom made this all the time and her version was different from my other Filipino friends’ moms (who also had their version), but it’s always the kind of dish most Filipino kids just recognize and love. Don’t be deterred by the comment from David M Garcia about “more authentic”; this one’s plenty authentic and Filipinos like me will be the first to tell you that someone out there will tell David that his is probably less authentic etc!

For a more authentic Tinola, some refinements are in order. Certain steps and proportions here are little excessive.  The browning of the chicken skin if done at all . should be less intense and not look like fried chicken gold.  A lot of the inherent chicken meat moisture would have already seeped out with color. The quantity of 2 cups of Malunggay leaves is excessive. This not supposed to look like a pesto. The proper appearance of Tinola should be like in the book Kulinarya.
This one’s plenty authentic! I always side eye Filipinos who do this in public forums—you and I both know that every nanay and lola and tito has their own version of tinola.

@Belinda M, exactly. Drives me nuts when I read things like this. Every kitchen, every province has their own version. "Refinements (major eye roll)".  I'm Cebuana and I remember this nationally renowned foodie from Manila telling me she was shocked that we used sayote in Tinola instead of the "more authentic" green papaya. My Tita then told me that during the war, that's what they had more access to in the mountains so they got used to sayote. Out of necessity comes innovation and it is definitely not the end of the world to use it. Here in the UK, I use spinach. It's still Tinola!

@David M. Garcia The Philippines is too diverse to officially label one recipe authentic. In my family, each tito / tita has their own unique version of tinola. Some use papaya, while others use sayote. Some use regular luya, while others use luyang dilaw. I like using both. Personally, I prefer browning the chicken separately, but I also sometimes cook it with the broth when I don’t feel like the extra effort. Regardless of the ingredients used, all these versions of tinola are equally authentic.
Is it possible to use reconstituted dried moringa leaves? If so, how much to use?
@Suzanne F I just tried it and would say it 100% worked. Although I don’t have fresh to compare with. I probably did 1 cup of dried and the rehydrated quite nicely.

The soup version of Tinola was often recommended by my favorite RN coworker in recent years, to her Filipina moms of the newborns we saw in our pediatric clinic. Salamat Vanessa, your wisdom and support were much appreciated by your patients, Filipina and otherwise.
Cooking a whole chicken (vs select parts) somehow is more tasty. Try it.

Authentic Filipino cooking is whatever we learned from our families, hehe. 

This is a wonderful recipe that reminded me of what my grandfather would make for me when I got sick as a kiddo. I never thought of the crispy skin topping before, will definitely be doing that from now on! It was a great addition.

I use leaves from bell pepper plants instead of malunggay, and a bit less fish sauce, but this is my go-to winter dish. I have friends that say it cured their colds when I made it for them when they were sick!
I thoroughly enjoyed making this and learned what a chayote is in the process.  Everyone loved it and wants it again.  Easy to prepare and a nice, healthy, hearty meal.

This was so delicious and comforting. Followed the recipe exactly, but substituted zucchini for chayote and bok choy for moringa because that's what was available. Skipped the crispy skin topping because I was lazy, but know it would be a great crunchy addition. Will absolutely be making again.

Another ingredient that will elevate/transform the beloved tinola: coconut water instead of chicken stock and the addition of thin coconut meat for that hint of sweetness. In our household's version, we use the entire palm-sized ginger for that kick, especially when the soup is left sitting for a few hours and reheated for dinner. Yum!

I swapped out moringa leaves and swapped in collard greens. Baby bok choy would have been ideal though!

I also shredded the chicken, which I felt would be easier in terms of spooning out this soup for a crowd.

title: Seco de Pollo (Ecuadorian-Style Chicken Stew) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: Goya sells Lulo/naranjilla purée in flat packs, you may find them alongside the more common guava, etc. in the refrigerator or freezer section. I have found frozen whole Lulo as well, from other brands, but only at bigger Mexican/South American markets.
@Leek I won’t buy from Goya anymore, but there are other brands that sell similar products.
If good fresh tomatoes aren't available, do you recommend substituting canned?  Or is it better to use fresh even if they're commercial/supermarket grade?

Personally I'd used canned over bland supermarket toms. 1 lb. fresh = about one 14.5 oz can. Might be less liquid and more concentrated flavor though. Maybe use 1 can plus another half pound of fresh for a nice compromise : )
@Anne Fresh tomatoes is best for this recipe (An Ecuadorian here) but you can use canned but I would put a lot less of it because you don’t want the flavor of the tomatoes to overpower the flavor.

I made this last night and it was so GOOD! I used canned tomatoes and I added a jalapeño to the sofrito because I had one. I also used the oranges. The cooking time was longer than in the recipe to get it thickened but it was yummy and I will make this a lot in the future.

Really delish. As a practical matter I used a tin of crushed tomatoes. Also, this can all be done once the chicken is browned, in a cast iron Dutch Oven in the oven with the lid tilted. The braise works fine and the sauce reduces.
Awesome!!! Finally a recipe from Ecuador. Really happy for my heritage.
Does anyone have a good recipe for the gorgeous rice that’s in the photo? Seems like it would be a shame not to make the whole dish!

@Julia it looks like a traditional yellow rice. Honestly, the easiest thing to do is buy a package of it, often found near the rice pilaf kits. There’s usually an ethnic brand that fits the bill. It’s called Yellow Rice.

@Julia this is traditional arroz Amarillo (yellow rice) that accompanies many Ecuadorian recipes. It’s typically made by first making a sofrito of onions in a red/orange spice called achiote (also known as annato, typically comes as a paste or oil), then adding your rice and cooking as usual. The rice takes a beautiful, deep yellow color from the achiote that is more intense than the one from, say, turmeric. It’s a must side for dishes like seco de pollo, and as you observed, it would be a shame not to try them together! You might be able to find achiote in the Latin American sections of some stores, and I’ve also seen it in some Filipino products (where it’s marketed as “achuete” and blended with cornstarch). I haven’t paid much attention to the “yellow rice” pouches that @vivienne suggests above, but they could be a practical solution too! Best of luck!

@Julia the rice in Ecuador is made usually with achiote (annatto) oil. Usually in the US you only find the Mexican annatto which is the closest to the one I use brought from Ecuador. To make the oil you put a bit of the annatto seeds and some seed or corn oil in medium/low heat until it becomes a deep red. You use that oil to toast the rice a bit before putting in the water. Hope that helps.
Lager is not like the corn based chicha I had in Columbia.  Too bitter. Sweet high alcohol (maybe like a mixed party drink) with a light taste. Maybe a light vermouth could sub.

@Roadster the lager is fine as long as you use the naranjilla. In Ecuador when I’m from we use beer a lot in this dish because the chicha is not as easy to find. I also have made with it with cider here in the US which works as well.
@Roadster this is an Ecuadorian dish that typically uses beer.
I make my own recaíto/sofrito in large batches. How much by volume is the pureed onion, pepper, and garlic?
Use fire roasted canned tomatoes.  Any flavor.  Skip blending them.  Straight from two whole cans.
This is one of those recipes where you’re not sure how it will turn out, but it absolutely deserves its 5-star rating. I’m definitely adding it to my quarterly rotation.

This was delicious.  I served it over rice. My husband is not an adventurous eater by any stretch of the imagination but said he would eat this every night.

You could play with the flavor profile easily by adding spice or touches of sweetness with prunes. Overall an excellent recipe with easily accessible ingredients.

I halved the recipe for 2 of us and used only thighs. I also removed the skin - why add extra fat when it really doesn't add to the flavor and crispy skin isn't the goal? That's my opinion, anyway.

That being said, it was delicious! A lot of work up front, but once it was put together, it cooked by itself. Can't wait to make it again.
@Stacey PW chicken fat is delicious and good for you. Why do you think chicken soup is a remedy?

So happy NYT has an Ecuadorian recipe! I consulted this recipe, and then a mix of videos in Spanish--search "seco de pollo receta." The "refrito" (onion/bell pepper/tomato mix), not usually called "sofrito" in Ecuador, tends to be cooked in achiote oil before being pureed. Take the seeds out of the tomatoes before you chop! If you cut the vegetables finely enough, you wouldn't need to blend. Once I added the chicken back, I braised at 325 in the oven in a covered Le Creuset.
If I have store bought Sofrito, how much should I add? Thanks!
could the beer be substituted with something else?
If alcohol content is a concern, perhaps alcohol-free beer would suffice? (Any residual alcohol content would likely cook out.)

This was one of the best recipes we have tried from NYT. Wow. And simple. Instead of making sofrito buy the jarred stuff. Add a small onion chopped and jalapeño chopped. 14 oz crushed tomatoes, beer, and good lime juice. Served with rice. Easy and delicious.

Case, find below:

Ingredients:
•	2 cups rice
•	2 TSP olive oil/butter
•	1 shallot, finely diced
•	2 garlic cloves, minced
•	1 TSP salt
•	¾ TSP turmeric
•	1/8 TSP (a pinch) ground cinnamon
•	1/8 TSP (a pinch) ground coriander

Instructions: 	
•	Heat oil/butter, add shallot, garlic and sauté until just beginning to brown, ~3-4 minutes
•	Add salt, turmeric, cinnamon, coriander and stir until combined
•	Add rice and water / broth and boil, covered, for ~20 minutes

inadvertent substitution: mistook a bottle of spiced hard cider for some leftover winter ale. didn't seem to impact overall flavorthose, so win!

used boneless skinless chicken breast, lime juice instead of vinegar, and minimized salt. excellent as a slow cooker dish for a rainy cold Sunday!

Very labor intensive and time consuming, but the result is worth it.
Use a vey large pot or cut the recipe down. Meatier tomatos might make it easier to thicken.
What is sofrito?
@Jane: for a detailed explanation of sofrito, see Wikipedia. In this recipe, it consists of the ingredients and processing in Step 1.

this might sound dull, but what is that yellow rice in the picture and does anyone know a good recipe/technique for making it? i’m sure it’s just something really basic, but what’s making it yellow? saffron seems unlikely. turmeric?
@case hi case. It’s a spice known as achiote or annatto. Might be able to find it in some Latin American stores!

A lot of work for something that came out just OK. I made my own sazon, so I don't know if I did something wrong. This won't be going into the rotation. I much prefer Peruvian Seco de Carne (which can be made with chicken). A completely different dish, less work and more tasty, IMHO.
What kind of tomatoes would be best for this recipe? Roma? I'll just grabbing them from my local supermarket.

I followed the recipe exactly.  But the chicken white meat parts came out very dry, stringy, and tough.  (I’m chopping it up into a hash  and using it as a rice and beans topping, and for the cat and dog).  The sofrito, the yellow rice, and plantains were perfect, though.  What did I do wrong?  The cooking time looks really long for poaching chicken.

@Lee Middleton you’re absolutely right.  Even the dark meat comes out just right after about 20 minutes.  White meat would be done in roughly half that time, but it’s good to make sure by either using a meat thermometer or cutting into it to check for doneness.  In fact, for the amount of poaching liquid this recipe calls for, I’ve been poaching about 6 lbs of legs and drumsticks in two batches.  You can still reduce the sauce (without the chicken in there of course)  and have plenty for all the chicken.
I found frozen lupo at my tienda, used an Australian lager, canned crushed tomatoes and made my own Sazon con  Achiote. It was delish.
Think I can use chicken backs?

No, the plastic wrap stays on until the steaming is finished. You can also use a foil lid. The plastic/foil lid prevents condensation from getting into the custard, marking the surface with irregularities.

The texture and flavor turned out great. I missed the detail about plastic wrap so skipped that. No regrets - plus less microplastic in my food. Next time I’ll double the recipe and add more liquid for a true 4 serving yield. As written, I could have filled one 4” ramekin with the egg mixture but chose to split across two.

That’s way too long steaming time. 10min tops, at low to medium heat, them turn off stove and let the egg sit inside, using remaining heat, also need to cover the ramekin to get smooth glass like top, or egg will have lots of air bubble inside it.

Thanks for the tip.  And for others, i don't  know why 1/2 pound of butter is needed for the celery root.  Perhaps 1/4 cup.  And the recipe also call for too  much water.  1/4 cup is all you  need on a low simmer for 20 minutes as the celery root releases a lot of water.

@Bob Sutis
The recipe calls for 1/2 cup of butter, not half a pound.

Really 5 min prep 
Oh wait I just found my dashi stock in back of the fridge

I missed the part about plastic wrap and it wasn’t needed plus I really don’t need more plastic in my life, all turned out fine.  I had no celery root and subbed in Daikon radish to great success.  This was so delicious and fine and interesting I’m having some smoother for breakfast! Wow!

@FrankW. I change my mind since I’ve made this now 3 times, once with lid on tight no cover, lid ajar no cover, and finally lid ajar with with foil cover and the last one was by far the better one.  In fact the texture and consistency of the egg mix went from spotty with many rough small craters (air bubbles) with a spongy texture.  While with the cover on it took the full 18 minutes to steam with the lid ajar but the egg mixture both in look and feel on first bite was silky smooth even sublime.  The subtle flavors combine well and I used puréed Daikon Radish instead of celery root and yes there was room for the sweet peppery flavors.
Is the plastic wrap removed before steaming?
The chawanmushi that I remember having in various places in Japan in the early 90s always had bits of seafood in it.     It wasn't just a plain custard.

We substituted salt with 4 anchovies. 
Boy was that a power move
You won’t be sorry

Soak the porcini when put the pasta water on the stove to boil. Cuts down on total cooking time. Also consider doubling this or sauteeing additional mushrooms because it’s ridiculous delicious! Our whole family, kids included, were scraping the pan! This is a “Top 10” 2025 recipe for me!
You must have read Step 1 for the idea of soaking the porcini and starting the water to boil!
@yogiskimom Same in our family! We now always double the recipe because it’s become one of my kids’ top 5s.
Add a hit of lemon at the end and it's perfect!
Or just grate a bit of zest.

This is a wonderful recipe!!!  5 stars all the way!!!  

Only suggestion - please don't write recipes for weird amounts of noodles.  I live in rural NH in a town without a stop light, let alone a store that sells noodles in 9 ounces.  Seeing as this is my new favorite recipe, I'll overlook this one annoying aspect :)

@Nicole
What is the problem with leftover noodles. They keep forever
@Nicole I found Bionaturae Tagliatelle comes
@Nicole I found that Bionaturae Tagliatelle comes in an 8.8oz package. It is available at our local food co-op for $4.49. I am in a small town, as well — Nevada City, California.
I think this could really have benefitted from some caramelized onions.
Good idea. But in this case, in should deglaze with a little bit of dry white wine to balance the acidity.

@Nina S
I agree and a splash of white wine with Carmelized onions. May also try catmelized shallots.

We thinly sliced a half onion and sautéed until soft before adding garlic, parsley, and red pepper flakes. Used even amounts of porcini water and pasta water. Finished with a little lemon zest. We loved it. 5 stars for sure. So easy. Served with carmelized Brussels sprouts

My grandmother made a somewhat similar sauce (with porcini she foraged herself in annual trips back to Italy, dried there and then brought back to NYC), but her version included abundant very slowly melted onions in butter as the first step. Just fantastic.

Fabulous flavor!

I substituted coconut milk (canned) for the heavy cream since we don’t love cow dairy too much in our house. It’s only a few tablespoons spoons and doesn’t affect to final flavor. This one will be a regular in our rotation!

@Keely C Right on leaving the cows out. I have found another good “white creamy thing”, which is Kite Hill unsweetened Greek yogurt. We can’t do coconut fat. Creamy effect without greasy, yogurt tang doesn’t seem to detract.
@Keely C I wonder if cashew cream would work in this case, too? Haven’t tried it, but it’s always nice to have options!

I miss the cured pork, not to mention the three pounds of fresh mushrooms. Is there any reason not to ask for the restaurant's recipe the next time she visits? Unless, of course, she is a vegetarian and does not want any of us making it. The worst they could do is say no.
Tina, I understood this recepy as a vegetarian dish. "This Quick Vegetarian Ragù Is a Shortcut to Glory". But you're free to add anything you like. The kitchen is a space of freedom.
From the article: “Or so she has had to deduce from taste, because the recipe is secret and belongs to Acone alone.” So the restaurant is not sharing it.
@Tristan The best cook I ever knew was Ursula Knaeusel of Atlanta, originally from East Germany. Her motto: “You are the boss in the kitchen”. I miss her.

Thank you for this one! The porcini mushrooms gave the sauce so much taste, and the parmigiano and cream balanced out the taste perfectly. I used maybe half of the olive oil required, I also didn't have a lot of parsley and it was still delicious. Next time, I would use less crushed red and black pepper though, it was a bit too spicy. Apart from that, it was perfect.
Actually very good. I wasn’t expecting so much flavor, and I even missed the direction to save the porcini water. Probably used double the parm though 😂
Crushed red pepper is not Tuscan.
As the article states very clearly, "Note that this is not penne all’Aconese, just a homage to it."  In other words, no one is claiming that this recipe is Tuscan, just inspired by Tuscan cooking.
We spent a few weeks in Oltrarno and the porcini pasta everywhere was usually laced with truffle oil and lots of cream, and black pepper.  No tomatoes.  This is a different recipe.

@Brooklynite

I was born & bred in Brooklyn zipcode 11209, loved Italian( Northern )cuisine… I have been to Italy & their cooking is much more simplistic… In contrast to Italian-American, heavy on the red tomato sauce vs cream/ butter, flour roux?  Classic French Béchamel …

I’m quite concerned about the time the cooked pasta is left to wait once drained - at least 3 minutes (!!). By that time, any egg tagliatelle I know of would become a solid tangle that would be impossible to loosen and properly mix with the sauce. I’d start finishing the sauce before the pasta is due and maybe, to get a “starchier” water from it, use less water to begin with.

@marcella from Italy
Delish!!! Liked mother’s cooking for hours in her labor of love… Ingredients first most… technique 2nd … tied for 3rd Equipment… lastly, if 100 hours of practice in the kitchen, perfection…I doubt Michelin reviews are worthy because critics were not “super “ tasters in my humble opinion…
@marcella from Italywhen the pasta is drained, drizzle a bit of olive oil on it to keep it from sticking together.

I don't often follow the recipe exactly - but I wanted to on this one - since the ingredients were so $$$ - I paid $15 for the dried mushrooms at our local grocery - i couldn't wait for my mail order supply to arrive - I REALLY wanted to make this for Sunday Supper (a la @SamSifton - see you on Sunday kinda meal.

Licked the bowl, the pan, the plate, licked all the things!

@Domestic Goddess  dried porcini mushrooms are available by the pound online at a reasonable price compared to the little packets at 5h3 grocery store.
I store mine in the freezer, and they are good Indefinitely!

@Barbara Marmor I too am happy with bulk mushrooms online. It’s the mushroom water that’s tasty; to me the mushrooms are rubbery and comparatively tasteless after soaking so I toss them and sub dry-fried fresh brown mushrooms.

Delicious! Easy! Company-worthy! Made as directed with the addition of two large, sliced portobello caps, which I sauté'd for about 5 minutes in the garlic parsley pan, after Step 2, (no extra oil needed). Then I continued as written, with Step 3.
This is a fantastic recipe. It is so easy, quick and tasty. I keep those dried porcini in my pantry so this can be my emergency meal. Also, Ixta’s book Mezcla is SUPER!!!

@Michael

Agreed 100%
@Michael I brought porcini back from northern Italy and was told to store them in the freezer. Passing it along for what it’s worth.

Watch out for grit on the dried porcini, it'll ruin your meal. You can give them a gentle brush after the soak, maybe even a gentle rinse but obviously don't lose any of that flavor. Also I always drain the soaking water through a kitchen towel before putting it into the sauce - or carefully decant it to leave behind the dregs. And I'd use all the soaking water and reduce it down because that's where a lot of the flavor is.

This was a big hit for a small dinner party. Dried porcini mushrooms are a bit pricey, but for an occasional meal, definitely worth it. I subbed in 3/4 cup white wine for half of the pasta water and it added an extra layer of flavor, to an already amazing recipe. I used two .7 ounce mushroom packets and was worried there wouldn’t be enough mushroom, but it was more than enough. I could have still added pasta for two more and been satisfied with the portion and flavors.
@David Not everyone is a Costco member but they have it in bulk that I vacuum pack in ounces to have for future needs.

I always make a recipe exactly as written before commenting. The only thing that I did differently was to use 50 grams of dried mushrooms instead of 40 grams as called for. This recipe was very disappointing. There wasn’t enough sauce for the pasta. The next day I sautéed shallots in butter and olive oil. Then I added 8 oz of sliced baby bella mushrooms and dried thyme and salt. After the moisture was rendered from the mushrooms I added this to the pasta with mushrooms from the night before. I added a splash of cognac (not necessary) and heated this and added parmesian cheese and a bit  of cream, maybe a quarter of a cup. I stirred the mixture while heating and obtained a lively velvety sauce. This was much better than the original recipe.

@Robert J
This is why I love this app. I changed mine up too- cooked the mushrooms for super long in just the juices before adding the pasta water and more cream than the recipe calls for. I used any kind of non-hallucinogenic mushroom I could find and boyyyyyyy was this tasty. About to finish my last mouthful and I’m sad it’s about to be over.
This is pretty good.  I don't use heavy cream and substituted yogurt which worked out very well.  This is a flavorful, hearty dish.

@loiss. I’m guessing perhaps you skip cream for the same reason I do?  I must limit the amount of saturated fat in my diet.  This recipe looks good, but without the nutritional values I’m afraid to try it,  Your tip would help, (although yogurt isn’t as mellow as cream) then perhaps cut back on Parmesan as well.  Really miss the nutritional values when that info is missing!!
Sauté shallots and minced porcini mushrooms in olive oil then proceed with recipe.
Wondering about options to make this vegan.  Seems like the heavy cream and Parmesan are pretty integral.
@Zac I’m wondering about a little silken tofu whizzed up till smooth in a blender. And then nutritional yeast in place of the parm
@Zac how about nutritional yeast and cashew cream? Works for me.
There are some great vegan parmesans out there, and I'll probably use a vegan cream.
Even without the parsley, fresh mushrooms instead of dried porcini, and pappardelle instead of tagliatelle, this turned out great.
I’m curious how this could be made with fresh mushrooms, such as how much you’d need and if the cooking time or texture would be affected?
Do you have fresh porcini mushrooms? Certainly fresh mushrooms would not need to be hydrated. Depending on your fresh mushrooms, time and texture would have to be gauged by you.
@LQ on the back of my dried porcini mushroom pkt, it says 1g dried mushrooms = 8g fresh.
Pretty spicy even with 1/4 tsp of hot pepper flakes! 1/3 sour cream and 2/3 low fat milk worked fine, as did minced arugula in place of parsley.

This is why I read NYT cooking comments!  We don’t like parsley so we just leave it out whenever the recipe calls for it. It never occurred to me to sub arugula, which is in the fridge pretty regularly. Thank you!!
@Momof3 parsley is the most bland common herb. Almost anything is better. I’m gonna try thyme instead. I also love arugula though… Hmmmm
@Momof3 after it cooks you can’t taste the parsley.  Give it a try!
I find that using a pour over coffee maker with a  paper filter is helpful for straining the bottom grit from soaked and rehydrated mushrooms so we get to keep all the tasty mushroom broth.
I rinse dried mushrooms before soaking.
My sauce slid off the pasta like a toboggan on an icy slope. On day two this dish was fantastic as the noodles were permeated with flavors.

Some cooks here seem to complain about the use of red pepper flakes however, peperoncino is found in Tuscan cuisine and an example would be Aglione della Valdichiana, the garlicky tomato sauce served with pici pasta. Another example is ribollita (a hearty bread and vegetable soup), which some cooks might spice up with a touch of peperoncino.  So yes feel free to add red pepper flakes here.

Phenomenal. I literally just finished making and eating this, and felt compelled to run straight to the site to post this. It's that good. 

An amazingly silky sauce with deep mushroom flavor and just the right touch of heat from the pepper. I need to find a way to adapt this sauce to other recipes. I'm thinking this will make a dynamite lasagna...
I have a question. Can you substitute water or wine for the cream?
I thought the cream was necessary, it carries the flavour very well. Maybe use half-and-half?
Not if you want it to be creamy. Water and/or wine won't thicken, only thin.
I had no cream in the house so substituted extra porcini soaking water. Thought about adding a big spoon of whole milk greek yoghurt to the finished bowls but in the end just left well enough alone.
Very good! And so I can be a true NYT commenter- I subbed chanterelles for porcini and oat milk for cream. Would have been better with something more similar to cream tbh

I think this was one of the best pasta recipes I've had in a long time!  The flavors were just outstanding.  I could have eaten the whole pot!!!  Substituting arugula for parsley saved me-thought I had more in the frig than I actually did-that's why I love reading all the comments.  I will definitely be making this again and again.

@Linda
chose arugula because I love the flavor and our supermarket parsley was pre-packaged and lackluster- disappears into sauce but suspect it contributed to fantastic depth of flavor!

@Linda
Chose to use arugula because flavor appeals and the perilla

Delightful! But I altered it. I added some hot Italian Sausage (and cut the crushed red pepper). My dried mushrooms were Shiitake, but their earthy flavor came through thanks to adding the soaking liquid to the sauce. After adding the reserved pasta water, the sauce needed a bit more time to reduce than specified in the recipe. Still quick overall. Served with steamed fresh Spinach, which made a nice complement. I will search out Ixta Belfrage's book and the others she has done with Ottolenghi.
I made this as directed except I didn’t have any heavy cream so I used cashew cream.  Came out great but I agree that a splash of wine would be nice

Absolutely delicious and simple. Not having porcini, I substituted equal amounts of oyster and shitaki mushrooms and followed the recipe as-is, doubling it to serve for dinner with friends. Can’t wait to make it again

Normally I stay away without the nutritional values, but this is easy to calculate by just looking at the pasta package and dividing the olive oil by the number of people. I’ll either leave out the cream or experiment and see if half and half will break in this

I never comment. But I furtively made this recipe, which looked incredible, and wanted my picky children to eat it. So I offered them a choice: the usual Marcella Hazen tomato sauce with penne, or this. I made both, put them side by side, and put one piece of each into a bowl for my kids to decide for themselves which to eat for dinner. I just called it ‘Mom Pasta.’ BOTH kids asked for Mom Pasta, and my eight year old son ate three bowls! I still haven’t told them they ate a zillion mushrooms…for the first time. (The idea of mushrooms of course disgusts them), I love tricking my children into eating great food….

I live in a rural area and had planned to get dried porcinis during my latest trip “to the city” - but there were none to be found! I was set on making the recipe, so got 8 oz of cremini instead and, after removing stems, was left with about 6 oz mushrooms. Chopped extremely finely and cooked for more like 8-10 min vs the 3 as written for rehydrated porcini. Omitted the addition of soaking liquid (only added the 1.5 c pasta water). Turned out beautifully! I’d still like to try recipe as written but wanted to share an alternative take in case helpful for others with limited grocery offerings.
You need to pay careful attention to the portions of ingredients, time of each step, and the heat, especially if you are cutting the recipe down. It was good but I think it could be really great!

I agree with the commenter who said that the red pepper is not Tuscan. It seems like EVERY recipe I encounter these days has red pepper flakes as if every dish needs heat. I can't have any heat spices since I was diagnosed with severe ulcerative colitis a few years ago, and I've noticed that I don't miss heat in food, especially in cuisines that have more delicate flavors that heat can overwhelm. And the amount of black pepper in this dish is quite enough heat!

It's perfectly fine to leave out red pepper when it's called for. I do it all the time and the recipes still are delicious for those of us who are spice adverse. There's no kitchen police watching our every moves.

@Nell you may not be familiar with Ixta Belfrage, who developed this recipe. This recipe isn’t meant to be (nor claims to be) authentically Tuscan. Her passion is combining diverse cuisines, inspired by her diverse background (Brazilian mother, who grew up in Cuba and Mexico, and a Mexican grandfather), and she grew up in Tuscany, Brazil and Mexico.  Fusion cuisine is often unfairly derided, but she really come from a place of love and respect for these influences.
This is identical to a sauce I've made since the mid-70's except I add fresh thyme, lots.
This is an absolutely brilliant recipe! Has anyone tried scaling it up? I’d love to cook it for 4-5 people! Double the porcini, in that case?
This was absolutely delicious. I added a splash of red wine with the liquid and used about 2 oz. of mushrooms. Also, added more parsley.
Made as written--very good.  However, I will cut the red pepper in half.  I like heat, but the red pepper overwhelmed the other flavors.

@Lisa Curcio I agree that the red & black pepper were overwhelming!  I went with 1/2 tsp red & 50 twists of black as written.  Next time I will cut red in 1/2 & measure the black vs ambiguous twisting. Going to add onions next time too.  Def could be a favorite with a couple changes for take two.
Would Shiitake mushrooms be a reasonable sub for the porcini? I have a LOT of those and no porcini, but don't know the effect in a recipe like this, if much.

You can do so and it will be a lovely dish; it is just that the flavor will be different. The porcini or other dried European wild mushrooms just have this forest floor flavor that shiitakes will never replicate. It will not be wrong; just different.

@BDFL
Would dried morels work?
@DH Morels should work. Porcinis have a really deep forest scent and flavor, but morels come from the same forests haha. Enjoy!

Excellent recipe. I took yogiskimom's suggestion about adding some chopped fresh mushrooms especially because my store of dried porcini had less than 40 grams in it. I also added quite a bit more than five Tbsp of the porcini soaking water in addition to the starchy pasta water.
Lighten up on the black pepper. Between the red pepper flakes and the black pepper, it masks a lot of the mushroom flavor, and for dried porcinis that's not an insignificant effort.

This is what you make when you want to impress someone: a date, your MIL, dinner guests, yourself. My plan is to teach my kids, nieces and nephews to make this so they will have it for themselves but also for me when I'm old and visit them when they're grown!

Subbed different pasta as I didn't have tagliatelle. Can't wait to make this again.
Quick question: I only have a dried mushroom medley (from Costco) and am wondering if this would be as good. Any advice?
I have exactly the same question! I was thrilled at how cheap the Costco ones are, too.

@Béatrice Yes, feel free to use your dried mushroom medley. It won’t be the same flavor as sauce made with only porcini since each type of mushroom has a distinct flavor and many are less robust than porcini, but it will no doubt be good.

@Béatrice the dried mushroom medley is a melange that includes dried button (domestic) mushrooms. (I have used it in the past in other preparations.) It is not as flavorful as porcini mushrooms alone would be. Necessity is the mother of invention and your dish, while more subtle, will likely still work.
this, is, without a doubt, one of the BEST recipes I have ever prepared off this site, and I havw made MANY!!
In Italy Tagliatelle is sold in a 250 gram package of four nests. The reason this recipe calls for about 9oz is that 250gram is 8.8 oz. It's not just a random choice of pasta weight.

Delicious BUT….
that’s about $30 in dried porcini mushrooms 
AND..
that’s too much pepper!
@Brad is too much pepper!!

@Brad - we got 3oz (enough for 2 batches) on Amazon for $15.

Totally agree about the pepper, tho. We cut it down to a teaspoon, and it was still a little heavy handed. I don’t know where on earth there is a pepper grinder that takes 50 twists to get to a teaspoon. Ours only took about 8.

Nothing short of spectacular. Even my teen who "doesn't like mushrooms" rated it as restaurant quality. Used a little less oil and a bit more of the porcini water but otherwsie followed the recipe to a T. Five stars all around from our side.
PS. I cook ~5 NYT recipes every week and this is the first time I've actually written a review. It's that good!

Made as written but mixed all the porcini liquid with the pasta water. I would never throw that deliciousness away.  Absolutely beyond good!!! Licked the bowl. Will make again. Great for Lent Fridays.

That this dish is insanely, five-star delicious (especially when tossed with mushroom fettuccine from a local specialty shop) does not mitigate some issues with its preparation. To wit:
•as other cooks have noted, you need to start the sauce before your pasta begins cooking. And you should use the full 1+ cup of porcini soaking liquid (minus the grit at bottom) and just a splash of pasta water at the end— not the other way around.
•no Italian cook I know tosses grated Parmesan into a dish while it’s still cooking. You add it at the end, otherwise half of it congeals and settles at the bottom of the pan (and later in the back side of my kitchen sponge).
•chopping up the porcini so finely seems a bit unnecessary as does 50 (50!) twists of the pepper mill. Yikes!! I came close, and it was delicious, but still …
Excellent with home made tagiatelle!
Superb! My new favorite pasta recipe. And, pretty easy, just chopping! Thank you Ms. Belfrage and Ms. Mishan.
5 stars - just as it is!

The only thing wrong with this recipe is the volume -- you can't possibly make enough!  It is perfect as written; granted i didn't have 3T of cream sitting around, but a bit of butter did the trick.  Also a fresh, fine black pepper really added to overall flavor.  Make this now!
I didn't have cream either. I used a glug of whole milk and a tblspn of butter. was very fine

Both my husband and I were rather disappointed with this dish. I was skeptical about using only tomato paste rather than whole or crushed tomatoes & the need for heavy cream. But I went with the recipe based on the rave reviews. I did add some extra sautéed mushrooms per some comments. In the end, I don’t think it was worth the time or expense. I did not skimp on ingredients: I used good quality porcini, imported Italian tomato paste & tagliatelle, as well as parmigiano reggiano cheese. In the end, it was just okay. Sorry, but I would not make this again.
This is a super quick and delicious recipe. I used a small fresh chilli pepper in place of the red pepper, it worked perfectly if you enjoy relatively spicy flavouring.
Taking others’ suggestions, I added some “leftover” caramelized onions, lemon and lemon zest.  The dish was rich and SO delicious without being too creamy.  Thank you NYT reader/cooks!

Doubled the recipe, used Costco mixed dried mushrooms (instead of just porcini), added a whole caramelized onion to the parsley mixture, used 4 anchovies and a few shakes of salt. Apparently one of the best things I’ve ever made!

I kinda knew that I was not going to be a fan because of the smell of the dried mushrooms.
Overall the flavor was good  and I’d try it again but with a much milder dried mushroom or fresh mushrooms
I substituted absolutely nothing and made it exactly per the recipe. I thought it was going to be too watery; I was wrong. This recipe is a total winner just as it is.

@alexanderalmgren
7 months ago
We substituted salt with 4 anchovies.
Boy was that a power move
You won’t be sorry
Brilliant! I followed your lead and I’m not sorry in the least. I’ll dial the pepper down next time and add a squeeze of lemon at the end.
Delicious !

Make this. It's easy. Do not complain about the cost of the mushrooms. You will not get a better main course at a restaurant for twice that. I cut back a bit on the pepper flakes and black pepper because I know how much heat our house likes--you do you. IT WAS AMAZING.

Tripled recipe for 6. Ample servings.
Followed @alexanderalmgren's power anchovy move. Brilliant.
Had a large handful of dried morels which I rehydrated and added to the ragu whole. (Looking forward to using the morel and extra porcini stocks this weekend.) Spectacular recipe.
Accidentally added my husband’s homemade harissa paste instead of tomato paste. Was actually pretty awesome.
Used previous tip and added anchovy filets…excellent. Also a spritz of lemon juice at the end was ideal.
We found it to be great flavors but too bold on black pepper.  I recommend half the pepper.
Would adding fresh mushrooms be a bad idea? Thought to make it more mealy?

Made this last night. Really delicious! 
I had some shaved steak that I needed to use so I seasoned and seared it and added it with the pasta. All of it was sooo good.

This was fine, but perhaps not quite as extraordinary as I'd expected. Took me forever to find dried porcini mushrooms, before I realised that these are called 'cepes' in France and quite easy to find.

My first reaction was, why use so little of the mushroom soaking liquid? That is liquid umami! I had 2 cups of it and used a bit more than 1 1/2 cups (filtered of course).  I also liked the substitution of anchovies for the salt. I use both of those with my regular bolognese for a umami boost.
Great meal, look forward having it again.

Made this last night. Doubled the mushrooms. 
Lease do yourself a favor amd don’t double the mushrooms!  I like mushrooms as much as anyone but they completely took over in an overpowering way.   

That said, my wife liked it but nowhere near her usual reaction to nyt rec
This is delicious; the recipe as written serves two hungry people. We add one large porcini mushroom, sliced relatively thinly, for texture and a bit more veg.
This recipe was delicious! But, I strongly recommend adding the Parmesan AFTER adding the pasta to the pan. Otherwise, you get stringy clumps of cheese that don’t mix with the pasta.

I made this exactly as written and it's very, very good.  I made it a 2nd time and sautéed shallots and added shrimp -- also outstanding. The third time I will make it using arugula instead of parsley.  Highly recommended.

This is an incredibly good recipe.  It was over the top good.  Highly recommended.   Fun story: I sent it to my kids ( grown young women) and my younger daughter and her fiancé were actually in the middle of making it already.  What a wild coincidence.
Swap in 40g dried mushroom mix from Costco (porcini is listed along with several others) and it comes out great. Made it 4-5 times out, bangs every time with homemade fettucini or dried whatever.

This brilliant dish is much improved w/ caramelized shallots, dry marsala to deglaze pan. I use Costco dried mushroom, which are notoriously gritty & a little tough. I strain them through a coffee filter, rinse them lightly, then dice in a small food processor into a ground beef size. After cooking mushrooms w/ onions, add 2 tbsp butter & full cup of mushroom liquid. Simmer on low for ~30 minutes. Add pecorino Romano, parmesan & parsley. Then 1 cup pasta water. So much richer & more flavorful.
Outstanding! Thank you.
Made as is, it’s really delicious. I’ve made it twice in 2 days, once for vegetarian friends. Bowls were empty.
Skip the heavy cream. It does nothing for the sauce and it's never used in italy

@Nicole - We just might live in the same rural NH town without any traffic lights. Even in a full size package, I don’t think I could find tagliatelle nests or the dried porcini! But you make this sound like it’s well worth taking a trip to a market in a larger town.

I ended up just buying a 16 ounce package of pappardelle noodles and doubling everything else.  The math still doesn't work out exactly, but close enough.  I'm usually able to find the dried porcini at Market Basket, surprisingly enough.

I added a half of a chopped onion and 1 chopped carrot to Step 2.  I used cilantro since I did not have parsley on hand.  I also used crème fraiche instead of cream.  It was delicious.  I will definitely make this again.  Very flavorful and pretty easy!

Tried this because we love mushrooms. I read all the comments so: I saute half a thinly sliced onion in some of the olive oil, then added the rest of the oil, garlic, red pepper and anchovy paste ( instead of the salt) When done sauteing the bits, deglazed the pan with a scant 1/4 cup white wine.  Chopped and added mushrooms as directed. Also added 2 tsp miso. Used 3/4 cup porchini water and 3/4 cup pasta water.While  adding cheese and cream also added 1 Tbs butter to finish the sauce. Yummy!
This was fabulous and very easy to prepare! For the first time ever, my version looked just like the photo.
Highly recommend saving the porcini water and using it with heavy cream in omelets or frittata.
I seriously cannot recommend this highly enough. It is the perfect fulfilling comfort meal with easy ingredients and prep. Using Oyster and Shiitake mushrooms works great if you can’t find porcinis!

Actually, flat leaf parsley it what's used in all the recipes I come across. I'm not a chef or professional cook, but I cook a lot and am a recipe junkie! I think curly parsley is more of a garnish and I've never seen it specified in any recipe.

This was absolutely incredible. I don’t like parsley but I added it anyway and I wasn’t mad about it. The flavor of the dish is incredible! I think there was a little too much cream for me personally, but I would absolutely make this time and time again!
Fantastic. Threw in a little more parm for fun. Flavors blend beautifully, nice umami punch. Can't wait to make it again!

This was amazing.  I made a gluten/dairy free version using dairy free parmesan and heavy cream made by emulsifying (with a milk frother!) oat milk, corn starch and a drop of olive oil.  The fake cream was so good that I also used it in the gluten/diary full version I made.  I am definitely making this again and will use anchovies.

Also, I saved all the remaining porcini water to use for heating up leftover pasta and for flavoring in other dishes.
worked great. lots of flavor. goes into our rotation.
Definitely use anchovy paste instead of salt. Use ten times the amount of dry mushrooms.
Anyone have a good source for the dried porcini mushrooms?

@Kirsti I bought a large quantity of oyster mushrooms that were very reasonably priced from our local Asian grocery store. I would have preferred porcini but they didn’t have any. I hydrated them myself in my hydrator. It’s one of the easiest things I’ve hydrated. I used them in this recipe and it came out great.
Loved it!!!
Super easy and delicious.

Made this AMAZING dish as written. We used dried porcini mushrooms that we brought home from Italy. This will be a “go to” for a cold winter’s night or a rainy evening when one needs warmth in their belly.
Can you make without cream?
Yes; I did, and did not miss it the cream at all.

I would omit the red pepper. Since I grew  up in the Italian Apennines this sauce is a bit like my own Proust madeleine. Red pepper might cover the porcinis flavour unless it's so scarcely used that there's no point in using it at all.
Would I be able to use dried shiitakes if I don't have porcinis available?
Could someone clarify where to find the dried mushrooms at Costco? That seems to be my only hope here.
@Essex Jay Pretty sure they were near the herbs & spices & olive oil type things
What about using dried shiitakes if not porcini available?

This is a terrific recipe.  Pantry staples.  I did add a little dry vermouth after sautéing the garlic.  Love the anchovy suggestion.  used evaporated milk (because my family insists) and that worked fine (cream would have been great).  Grabbed the first long pasta on the shelf - thick spaghetti.   Start to finish, under 30 minutes.  This is one of the best.

Wow, this is crazy good and low effort! I was short on heavy cream so I added cream cheese to make up the difference, and thought the extra tang was nice. Maybe I’ll try crème fraîche next time. Definitely a keeper!
Excellent and easily veganized (with plant-based Parm and half-and-half). I would skip the red pepper flakes next time.

Splendid recipe. Fairly demanding, so attention must be paid, but it’s rewarded with flavor. We rarely indulge in heavy cream but the modest amount required here seems prudent. Next time we’ll double parsley and cut the black pepper by 1/3 and reduce the red pepper by 50%.
I suggest do not use boiling water to soaking the funghi porcini, but 40-60 C. In Italy we use some calamint , just a few, with porcini, during the cooking of the porcini sauce
@George where can one find calamint in the US? Would regular mint work?
So disappointing. What did I do wrong? Scratching my head.
Excellent
Used homemade Fettuccine noodles.  Turned out great
Made this exactly as written and it was delicious! My non-mushroom eating child went back for 3rds!
Made this with fresh tagliatelle. 200g flour/2 eggs. (2  servings, depending). Needed about a cup of pasta water but otherwise made the recipe as written. Mind blowing.

Delicious and rich as written with incredible mushroom flavor.    Doubled the sauce as recommended and now have a left over serving for lunch tomorrow.  It was a bit rich for my taste with the cream so likely will try a splash of milk instead of cream and cut back on the parmesan next time.  Would also be delicious over polenta.

This was delicious!  I didn’t have access to porcini mushrooms, so I swapped in some baby portobellos…a perfect substitute.  Due to the substitution, the first step was a little different as I cooked the portobellos down a little…just till they released their moisture…which I kept and added back to the mixture when I returned the shrooms to the pan.
Wonderful recipe.  I used papardelle noodles (8 oz) because that's what I had.  Excellent!
Delicious! I always read the notes, so I also saved the soaking liquid for the sauce and added additional fresh mushrooms. Perfect for our meatless Monday. Served with sautéed swiss chard. YUM!

This is fantastic. It is like something my Piedmontese grandmother would make. I had some gorgeous mushrooms from the Farmer's Market, which In sautéed and added , and I served it on  polenta.  It was fantastic.
Disappointing. Drizzled with truffle oil to rescue it.
Careful with the pepper (50 twists was too much, even though we’d doubled the recipe. Better to measure). Add nutritional yeast to add more umami flavor, and perhaps vegetable bouillon.

Loved this recipe.  Made it exactly as described - think I will add a touch more tomato paste and spice next time to give it a bit more intensity - but really had the texture and look of a meat ragu, only with more flavour.

We thought this was FABULOUS!  And pretty darn easy to prepare. I used the food processor to chop the mushrooms. I used a mixture of dried mushrooms - porcini along with chanterelles, shiitake, and black trumpets. And I had some fresh cremini in the fridge so I incorporated them. A little truffle oil drizzled over the top before serving would be a lovely touch. I made a double batch and froze half, so some busy day soon, I look forward to having it again!

@Nicole I am a fan of doubling the recipe for the sauce. This way, you eat half now and then freeze the rest and eat it at another time. Those extra noodles go in a ziploc bag into the cabinet. They will stay perfectly fine. And then when you're feeling like having this again, it Dawns on you... the sauce lives in the freezer and don't you know I've gotta half a package of noodles in the cabinet...Shablam!!!  A whole another meal!

I had leftover shitakes and a jar of porcini powder from G Detou that I didn't know what to do with.  So I mixed the powder with the pasta water since I didn't have soaking liquid. My G-d, it was the bomb. I cannot believe how great it was.
Sadly lacking in flavor
Instead of draining the pasta when I want to use the pasta water I just scoop the pasta directly into the other pan and then ladle in more pasta water as needed.

Followed the directions except I used more of the soaking liquid after straining it through a coffee filter.
Unbelievably delicious!!!
Outstanding! Use dried Calabrian chilis if you can find them! Looking forward to repeating this with a high protein pasta (not waiting for this in tagliatelle shape).
I’m amazed by how flavorful it is. It has this umami, earthy and meaty flavor. It satisfied my craving for a mushroom dish.

When I first saw this recipe I knew I needed to make it. Porcini mushrooms are an umami delight that I wish we could get here in the U.S., but the dried version gives us a reasonable facsimile. 

I added a pound of very good 80/20 ground beef to this, which I browned, with some salt and just a bit of Italian herb blend, at the beginning, drained off the fat (saving it) and put the meat aside in a bowl. I added it to the porcini mixture after it was cooked. The pasta water and porcini water did a good job of deglazing the pan to bring all of the fond from the ground beef and porcini mixture into the sauce. There was more porcini water than was needed for the sauce, so I tossed this into the pasta water to add a bit more flavor to the pasta. 

This dish was WONDERFUL!  My wife exclaimed that it was one of the best pasta dishes she’d ever had in our home.
Fabulous.  Doubled the garlic (as we always do), added fresh portabellas, used the porcini water w salt for the pasta.  Raves even from the all-meat eaters.

Super yummy. I was a bit skeptical as I was working my way thru the recipe, but turned out great. I probably used more mushrooms than called for which was a plus. Delicious recipe and easy to follow.  We’ve made it twice and it’s hard to stop eating this poricini ragu, and I don’t even like mushrooms that much!

I'm not sure where the comment I wrote a week or two ago went, but we loved this recipe so much that I'm trying again. Spend the money. Buy the mushrooms. DELICIOUS. Thanks NYT Cooking for allowing the rest of the world outside the US to use metric. Much appreciated.

Want to make this but how does 1.5 Tbsp of tomato paste make a dish as saucy-looking as the picture??  How could it possibly coat that amount of pasta?
In step 5 you add 1½ cups of the reserved pasta water plus the (5 tbsp) reserved porcini soaking liquid.

If you use estratto di pomodoro, it will. That's the very thick paste that you get in Sicily, made from dried San Marzano tomatoes. You can find it on the internet. NOTE: it is so thick that you have to thin it with the liquid  that is going into the dish, before you add the last ingredients.

@Marty

How much boiling water should be poured over the dried porcini mushrooms?

Enough to cover them completely, and it is ok if you have a bit more water rather than less water. What I do when soaking mushrooms, I keep all the soaking water until the dish is done, because you never know if you might need more than the recipe calls for. You can always pour it out at the very end when no longer needed, but it ends up being a tasty liquid that can be used as cooking liquid in another recipe. You may want to strain it through a cheesecloth to catch any grit from the mushrooms.

I changed this up a bit and did half the porcini but added 1lb ground beef. Doubled everything else. It turned out great. A few tips:

-Soak the porcini in a smaller amount of water so you can add it all to the sauce. No sense in discarding flavor. Add all the porcini water to the sauce and allow it to reduce for at least 30min covered. 

-Add 1 medium carrot and 1 large celery stock, minced, to stir fry with the mushrooms. This will give the sauce a more conventional Italian ragout flavor. 

-If the sauce is getting too think while reducing, add some chicken stock.

I used to soak then cook dried porcini mushrooms decades ago. They were delicious, but always had a lot of grit. I didn’t see any mention of this in the recipe or comments that I have read thus far. Does anyone have a trick for getting rid of the gritty/sandy parts, or are the ones sold now different?

There a few tricks.  Grit settles to the bottom during soaking, so don't strain the mushrooms / liquid all at once, or else the grit will pour back over. Instead, carefully lift the mushrooms out using a slotted spoon, fingers etc. [ You can also spoon out liquid from the top, if needed.]  Other options: 1)  pre-rinse mushrooms in cold water. 2) Gently blot the mushrooms with a towel after soaking.  [ To your point, I find that dried mushrooms sold currently have little or no grit vs yrs ago.]

It always takes me aback, when I read something like, "red pepper flakes, are not Tuscan." The author spent her childhood in Tuscany--she's an authority.  Black pepper is more definitively Tuscan for the region around Florence, but even there, cooks use peperoncini to support flavors. There is a peperoncino conserva from that region, that's delicious. At the seaside of Tuscany, you'll find them in loads of dishes featuring seafood.
