- took out the parsley because I didn't have any
- used 1 cup each mushroom liquid cup pasta water 
- used three GENEROUS tablespoons of cream
- added ground beef at the reheat phase for protein

If making it for two, make the full ragu recipe but use half the amount of pasta. If making for four, we double the ragu ingredients but use the prescribed amount of pasta. Hit it with a hit of fresh lemon at the end.

I made a few modifications. I combined this recipe with the one pot pork ragu ragu. I cooked all day on low in the crock pot. I added a little fish sauce and WS. I wish I had a little red wine to add. Delicious
For the effort and the minimal number of ingredients, this is a true out of the park winner!!!

Dan: Standard recipe makes enough sauce for 1lb of pasta.

Definitely would like to try adding 1-2 anchovies next time, but excellent as-is.
One of the best recipes on this app. My kids not only eat it but go for seconds. Reducing the red pepper flakes to 1/8 tsp is just right for us.
Is it possible to make this and freeze it then defrost and serve later? Any tips or changes needed?
Porcinis are very expensive.  Is there a viable substitute?

I don't think there's a viable sub for dried mushrooms in general, but you could put tempeh in a food processor (if you're looking to keep this vegetarian) and pulse until it's the consistency of ground meet and then use whatever marinade you like (it really soaks up any flavor).  I would probably use vegetable base diluted with water and let it marinate for about an hour and then reserve the rest to use in the sauce.  good luck!

I made this and it was delicious, even though I changed it quite a lot. I had 3 big field mushrooms I wanted to use up (are these a thing in the US? i think you could use any fresh mushroom and it would work but I guess an equivalent would be portabella) I chopped them very small and otherwise followed the recipe except I added a glug of red wine at the first reduction stage. I had less mushrooms than the recipe says but I forgot and used the same amount of water, so it took ages to reduce but when it did it was really worth it. I also did not add any parmasan to the sauce, I just sprinkled some on top (way less that stated- I'm watching my cholesterol) , it was still amazing and felt quite decadent for a weeknight! will make again for sure

Leftovers make a great frittata!  And although I didn't think to use anchovy paste (or anchovy) I did use tomato paste and vegetable base as well as garlic and onion.  I also left off the heavy cream and mounted the sauce with a tablespoon or so of butter - YUM!
Even my 6 year old cleared the bowl, so so good.  Came together easily.  I used a full 2 tablespoons of tomato paste.
Made this tonight and loved it. Pretty much as written but I used dried mushrooms that I brought back from Latvia last fall and added some dry vermouth to deglaze the pan after frying the mushrooms.
If I had this at a restaurant I’d be very happy, since we made it ourselves we were over the moon. It’s delicious. We doubled the mushrooms and added ground beef. Phenomenal.

Made this almost exactly as written for Valentine's dinner with an additional squeeze of tomato paste, an extra tablespoon or so of cream, and penne instead of tagliatelle because that's what I had on hand. Surprises were that the sauteed parsley adds a lot of flavor, the red pepper did not make the dish noticably spicy, and the seasonings were so spot on that we never touched the salt or pepper. Amazing dish that is definitely going into my regular AND dinner party rotation.
Fantastic recipe.  I used half the Porcini and a variety pack of fresh shrooms.  Added carnalized onions and Nduja instead of chili flakes.  The Nguja leveled up an already good recipe.
So good!

I have been a NYT Cooking Comment lurker for years.  This was so good I must comment.  I havent worked with dried Porcini mushrooms before-  my husband walked through the kitchen and asked, are you making something with prosciutto? They are deliciously meaty.
I used fresh pasta from our local pasta shop.  We had just over a pound-  doubled the sauce as recommended - added 2 thinly sliced shallots to soften before adding the garlic and added the anchovies to the mushrooms which someone here recommended.  I dreamt of that pasta last night!  paired with a Kale Salad, creamed spinach, and a 19 year old Oregon Pinot Noir and had a very memorable evening.

Made the recipe exactly as written with the exception of using papardelle pasta instead of the tagliarini.   I think next time I will double the sauce - it is so good it is nice to have extra and it is absolutely divine.  I will make this again and again.
Needs a least 2 tablespoons of tomato paste and a bit more pasta (12-14 ounces) to feed 4.
I'm sure the Porcini are best but I made a lovely dinner with this recipe using the usual fresh button mushrooms -- no soaking necessary, and it was super flavorful with just pasta water.
Pretty good and very easy for a weeknight supper.  I didn't love the texture of the mushrooms; it may be a technique issue on my end.  Next time, I'd use fresh mushrooms.
The cheese didn’t actually “melt” it just split. The sauce never emulsified once I added the cream. It just split. Tasted mostly like pepper. I would add half as much pepper.

I cook the recipes on this list every year, this time I'll comment.  Here goes!

NYT 2025 Cooking Challenge (1 of 50) -- Porcini Ragu

Good!  A touch spicy for my husband, if you have a similar husband, reduce the red pepper flakes.  If not, ROCK ON with the full amount!  The flavor was very dark, could stand the brightening touch of some white wine and/or lemon.

Added a small shallot and doubled the tomato paste.  In addition to parsley and a dusting of parmesan, garnished with flaky sea salt.  Other than those slight adjustments, followed the recipe to the T. 260 grams of fettuccine nests was enough for 3. Delicious!
Is this something you could reheat?
Yes! No issues for me.
Is this still good to reheat?
i’m making this tonight with freshly foraged morels and it smells like HEAVEN!!

I made with fresh local porcini since I’m in the PNW. Mostly stems which changed the consistency a bit I assume, but still delicious. Will try dried when I make this again. Used Romano instead of Parmesan, and coconut cream instead of heavy cream.

What a treat! I’d never cooked with dried porcini before. This is a great recipe, but no way does it feed more than 2 people. I went with my gut and doubled everything, to make a nice big pot of food. Went a bit heavier with the tomato paste and mushroom water, why not. Used Trader Joe pappardelle. Will make again! We loved it.

I made this a “porcini pappardelle” but had to sub half of the mushrooms with dried shiitake because the market near me had limited supplies. The flavor was rich and complex, but also a bit spicy with the full measure of crushed red pepper. Still, easily a five star recipe. I paired this with a Vino Nobile di Montepulciano.

Amazing and so easy!  Substituted arugula for the parsley - it was the only green had on hand - and wow.  Will make this again and again.  Can’t wait to try it with the anchovies as suggested by another cook.  Thanks

Delicious! Like someone else suggested, I used a couple anchovy filets in oil instead of salt. And I don't drink, but I would agree that some acid would help lift the dish a bit. I squeezed a little lemon juice over the top after plating, but might add in a TBSP of red wine vinegar to the whole thing next time. Definitely will be making again soon.

title: Avocado, Edamame and Yuzu Dip With Furikake Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: Don’t the defrosted edamame need to be cooked before they are blended with the avocados???

@Julee I’m surprised they haven’t corrected the recipe yet: I advise against blending defrosted-but-raw edamame with your avocados because that is destined to produce an unsalvageable, undigestible mess! I boiled my edamame a few minutes as per the package directions. I added a splash of extra avocado oil while blending. It was an enjoyable dip, but I wish the author specified how much sesame oil to add. Start with less because it’s strongly flavoured and you can’t get it back out! If i make this again, I’d hope to use less because it overpowered the yuzu.
@Julee no need to cook if “blanched” -usually indicated on frozen packing. If raw yes need to be cooked!

This sounded really good. But it has too many hard to find, otherwise not used ingredients for me to ever make it. Also, the article mentioned that the chef used to eat avocados with sugar. That suggested the large avocados rather than Haas avocados. Yet this recipe doesn’t say which kind to use. I’m thinking it meant haas bc it said 4 avocados. If I ever did come across Yuzus or the rice spice in one of my ethnic groceries here in South Florida I would try to make this with the large sweet avocados. My caribbean and south american friends have explained to me that those are used for sweets while the haas are used for savories.

@LaPoodella

Check out your local ethnic grocery store.  Yuzu comes in a bottled form like lemon juice.  Furikake would be with Japanese dry ingredients.  I found some in a large general grocery store in north central WI.
Trader Joe's has a spicy yuzu sauce that you could use in place of both the yuzu juice and the sambal. At least, that's what I'm planning to try!
@LaPoodella Trader Joe’s also had a great (imo) furikake seasoning!
Sweet serendipity in that I had a decent avocado, so I made 1/4 of the recipe with Meyer lemon. Delicious on sourdough. Definitely a keeper!
I love that you substituted ingredients. I didn't have any Sambal or sesame oil around so I used some chili crisp I had on hand. Two birds with one stone, oil and spice - it was sensational!

@LaPoodella you are right in that they need to start to specify kind AND amount of Avocado! I’ve an avo tree that gives about 4 cups of buttery avo from ONE. 
Also instead of furikaki maybe snip up some seaweed and black sesame seed for garnish. I will make this as soon as I replenish my edamame.
@Dorothy in Hawaii wow - four cups!
Do the edamame need to be cooked?
@Sandy yes

The frozen shelled edamame I bought, was already cooked. It said it on the bag. Just defrost. 
I enjoyed this. Used Mandarin orange juice and lemon since that’s what I had on hand and a gochugang  paste. Next time, I’ll definitely look for the bottled Yuzu juice since it has a distinct and delicious taste that I think would improve this. A very nice light dip with rice crackers.

This should almost be a “by intuition” recipe because everyone is going to want to adjust the seasonings. I added extra soy sauce but skipped the sambal due to the heat. Once you have the balance close, the flavor of the edamame shines through with the avocado flavor. I also added extra ginger and some lemon zest to punch up the citrus flavor. Fortunately I have yuzu furikake!
Could you use Meyer lemon in place of the yuzu? It is sweeter than regular lemons.
The dip was very satisfying but - needs more acid , more kick . Salt . Very healthy and a nice change from humous

I found this to be under -seasoned.  I added quite a bit more soy sauce and ginger to taste. Couldn’t find yuzu juice and sub’d fresh  lemon and Cara Cara orange juices, which worked well. After some tinkering with the various seasonings, it came out great. 
(Definitely should cook the edamame).

This is tasty and easy but two words of warning. It makes a TON so unless you are making for a crowd, I would cut recipe in half. And even with more citrus/acid it will turn a very unappetizing shade of army green.

Extraordinary, umami-rich, highly textured, zing-y spread! mild corn crackers (Fritos send a guac vibe, not Asian) Thawed, uncooked edamame are great. Proportion of edamame-to-avo is up to you; I used 1:1. I did the lemon + clementine juice.  Instead of sambal, I had Chinese chili garlic sauce on hand. Yes, be cautious with sesame oil. Toasted sesame seeds to garnish. ==>must be eaten soon after prep; it browns in the fridge!!! mini chopper is perfect size for 1/2 recipe
I thought this needed some allium. I added garlic and it felt like it had all the notes it needed then.
This dip is fire!! It is a must-make for lovers of Asian flavors.
This dip sounded so good but was very lackluster. Needs a lot more flavor.
You don't have to cook the frozen edamame?  Just defrost?
Frozen edamame are parboiled so you just need to defrost them.
In California, Trader Joe's stocks fresh edamame, not frozen, and furukake.
Is furikake vegetarian? I thought it had dried fish in it.
@BST see the notes. Can get a fish free version. Ms Boghal brand is recommended.

@BST
There’s 2-3 easy to find standard furikake mixes that are consistently vegetarian/vegan—the one with just sesame and salt, the one with nori sesame and salt, and one with shiso salt. I suggest the nori one and always double check the ingredients.

Japanese ingredients are getting mixed into lots of new multi-ethnic recipes these days, helter-skelter, but this is truly the most disgusting.

No I haven't tried it, and I certainly don't plan to.
@Louise. Your comment isn’t helpful.
I love the ingredients and enjoyed the taste. It was the pasty result of the shelled edamame what I did not enjoy. Will try without shell next time
Incredibly underseasoned. I added probably 3-4 times the recommended amount of everything, plus rice vinegar.
Can this be made the day before or does it turn brown from the avocados?
Recipe is fairly bland - I added LOTS of yuzu juice, LOTS of salt, and some rice vinegar!

This is Lisa Kyung Gross--founder of the League of Kitchens and author of this cookbook.  Some people have said that this is too much salt.  The salt is RINSED OFF before serving.  You salt the cabbage with 3 Tablespoons of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt (the large grains of salt are important--table salt or fine sea salt will make this much too salty), crunch it in with your hands, and let it sit for 10 minutes. Then you wash off the salt before dressing it.
If you are using table salt or fine sea salt, I would use 1 tablespoon.  Then rinse it off before dressing the leaves.

@Lisa Kyung Gross Thanks, Lisa.  I’m sorry to report that even though I did as you instructed, thoroughly rinsing the kosher salt from the softened cabbage, this recipe still turned out extremely oversalted.

@Ethan did you weigh out the cabbage to get the asked for amount? And did you use diamond crystal kosher salt? Unless you actually weigh out the cabbage, it’s hard to know if you are using the right amount. And Morton’s kosher salt is considerably saltier than diamond crystal.

One thing that’s missing is the addition of shio kombu — salty seaweed pieces that are typically in this when served in izakayas in Japan. You can get a pack on Amazon or at a local Japanese grocery store. Absolutely essential.
@Rajiv how do you prepare the kombu? Or should it be sliced and thrown in crunchy?

@Betsy shio kombu usually comes prepackaged and already sliced and flavored (a little salty and sweet, tons of umami) so you just sprinkle it on the cabbage. Kurakon and Fujikko are common Japanese brands you can find at a Japanese grocery store or online. When using shio kombu, I personally just chop the cabbage into big chunks and don’t pre salt it or add salt to the dressing bc the shio kombu packs a lot of flavor!

I'm going to add julienned carrots and green onions after marinating and draining, along with some shredded rotisserie chicken. Sounds like a great stand alone salad with any Asian flavors grilled meat, as well! breast in spring roll wrapper

@Maria 
Your ideas are excellent!

@Maria great idea! If you like that, then I’d recommend you try a Vietnamese coleslaw with shredded poached chicken. There’s a great recipe on RecipeTin. Be warned - it is a gateway drug and soon all you’ll crave is this bright, zingy crunch.
@SallyThanks for the reference to RecipeTin. I’m enjoying her recipes & love it that she has a food bank!

In answering some questions about whether regular cabbage can be used, yes, it's doable, and yes, there are Japanese recipes similar to this one that call for regular ones. However, the Napa cabbage has a very different kind of texture and crunch compared to cabbage. Therefore, although you can make a perfectly tasty salad with regular cabbage using this recipe, that salad will be a different (but also authentic Japanese) dish from the original.
could you use regular cabbage?

Yes, I frequently use this technique with regular cabbage, which is inexpensive and available year-round. Slice it more like coleslaw and massage the salt into it until the texture starts to soften and break down, so it’s not all hard and crunchy. It’s a little work, but it’s worth it!

Yes, you can. There are Japanese recipes similar to this one that call for regular ones. However, the Napa cabbage has a very different kind of texture and crunch compared to cabbage. Therefore, although you can make a perfectly tasty salad with regular cabbage using this recipe, that salad will be a different (but also authentic Japanese) dish from the original.

@Rebecca
Adding some rice vinegar breaks done the cabbage as well.
Used 2 tablespoons salt to massage the leaves and was plenty salty. Very easy. Holiday crowd pleaser.
@M. Meeks 2 tablespoons is plenty. Thanks for sharing
I have found that Whole Foods Market calls these cabbages Chinese Napa Cabbage.  They are flat compared to regular Napa Cabbage and at my store located beside them.

Also, just a note that "flathead Taiwanese cabbage" is not Napa cabbage.  It looks like someone has taken a conventional cabbage and flattened it.  It's softer and more mild than conventional green cabbage, but is closer to that flavor than Napa.  You can make this with Napa, but it has a different flavor and texture. If you use conventional cabbage, you need to salt it for closer to 15 minutes so that the salt can penetrate the tougher leaf (then make sure to rinse off all the salt).
@Lisa Kyung Gross Thanks for describing the cabbage. I will know what I am looking for at the Asian market

@Lisa Kyung Gross
Thank you for clarifying the cabbage. I only find this at my local Asian grocery which has a lovely produce section. It is exactly as you describe. It is more tender and sweet than regular round head cabbage. I love it! Also, I am able to grow a “conehead” cabbage in my garden sometimes. It has a similar sweetness and loose leaf habit.
How about pairing with Katsu chicken?
It goes with any Japanese main! I can't think of any that won't go with it.

I make a version of this year round and top with everything under the sun: fried egg, pork cutlet, crispy tofu, leftover christmas ham. My advice (plea) is to skip the sesame seeds and add a couple of healthy shakes of dashi powder.
@Ana why is it a plea? Are sesame seeds not good in some way? How is the powder you recommend preferable? Sincere question from a novice.

@Carol V I don’t know why they recommend dashi powder, since that’s basically bouillon. 

What *would* be good is furikake, which is a blend that usually contains sesame seeds, nori, sometimes fish, and seasoning. So good over rice, and I think it would be great on this too. Only potential downside is visual, since you would have dark specks of nori. But if you don’t care about that and can access/make furikake, you should give it a go.
@Carol V sesame seeds are fine, they provide crunch.  I’ve been using Furikake instead which has sesame seeds, nori and other nice flavors.
This sounds so good but I have no idea what to make with it. Any suggestions?
@Kate S I’m serving it with roasted duck that is seasoned with orange and five spice, also from NYT cooking.
Anything Japanese will go with it. Teriyaki chicken/salmon/any protein might be an easy one.
@Kate S this is often served as an otoshi or amuse bouche the minute you sit down at an izakaya or kushiage eatery.

Looks like some people are trying to avoid garlic. 
When I cook for people who find garlic too sharp, I microwave the sliced garlic in a small microwave bowl with oil for a very short time. 
I can use just the oil or the oil with the garlic, depending on the person’s needs. And we still get to have that marvelous flavor.
Some folks who have trouble with garlic do fine with the taste of garlic in oil. They just need to avoid actual pieces of garlic.
Delicious but too salty! No need for salt in the dressing img
Addictive is right. I air-fried salmon with ramen and this was the perfect crunchy cold side dish. I could eat this right out of the fridge. Thanks for a great recipe!

One of my favorite things is regular storage cabbage, coarsely chopped (you want chunky juicy pieces), dressed with olive oil and white wine vinegar, plus salt and fresh ground fairly coarse pepper. Not really coleslaw, not oriental, but truly addictive.
could you steam it a little?
Napa cabbage will likely end up getting very soggy and soft when heated, so I don't think steaming can be recommended. You will be giving up the crunch factor almost entirely.

@Japanese expat

Where does the recipe say to steam?

@Waves56
It doesn't say to steam it, but someone asked about whether it could be.

My husband has been making cabbage just like this for quite some time.  I am floored that his “recipe” is now “famous.”  We eat this at least once a week but I don’t think I am going to be able to remember the name.  We just called it sautéed cabbage.
But it’s not sautéed?
Julia, perhaps Nora and her husband were the ones who were 'sautéed'.....
@Nora but this recipe is not sautéed so probably not the same as your husbands
This was yummy! I used regular cabbage and topped with gomasio (toasted sesame seeds, salt and seaweed)! My 10 yr old and I devoured it!
Used green cabbage and served with my new years day meal of blackeyed peas, rice and cornbread. It was a hit and will definitely be made again.
This is delicious even with a regular old savoy cabbage! I got cheeky with it and added a splash of chili oil and rice vinegar to the dressing -- divine!

I use this technique with purple cabbage (but different flavors), and I do like to get it quite dry after the salting and rinsing; I spin it well in the salad spinner—need to do it a fairly small amount at a time since it's heavier than lettuce.
Oh of course, I’ll just pop over to the grocery and grab some flat leaf cabbage!

I ran across this flat cabbage at my local Asian market about a year ago. It was labeled “Korean cabbage”. I fell in love with the tender mild sweet leaves and have been buying it ever since. Excited to try this recipe. I’ve not seen it at my local Whole Foods or any regular grocery store, so it’s always on my list when I shop at the Asian market. I go there when I can to buy fresh mushrooms and  greens that are hard to find otherwise.
It’s definitely not a standard grocery store item, but I got some today at the farmers market, from a farm that specializes in Asian produce.

@SusanSF
I just made it with a spare 1/4 head of the cabbage I already had in the fridge. It disappeared astonishingly fast!

Kosher salt (like Morton's) and Diamond Crystal salt are not the same and are not interchangeable and shouldn't be swapped without adjustments. Three tablespoons of Morton's is denser (heavier, saltier) than 3 tablespoons of Diamond Crystal. In some recipes, it matters. For a more in-depth explanation of these salts and their history: https://www.seriouseats.com/ask-the-food-lab-do-i-need-to-use-kosher-salt https://www.nytimes.com/2023/08/01/dining/diamond-crystal-kosher-salt.html

Thank you Laurel
I get somewhat perturbed that many of the subscribers the Cooking Section still don't understand the difference between table salt and the leading two Kosher salts.  How many times does a recipe call for Diamond brand Kosher salt and people seem to ignore it and then just complain that the end result is too salty
@LAUREL that is an excellent article! I’m glad I read it before trying this recipe.

This is Lisa Kyung Gross--the founder of League of Kitchens and author of this cookbook.  One more note about the saltiness of this dish: like many Japanese side dishes, this is meant to be eaten alongside unsalted white rice.  It therefore acts more like a pickle that's eaten as a contrasting accent to the bland rice, rather than a coleslaw style salad.  In fact, this salting technique is more similar to making a quick kimchi or quick sauerkraut.
Lisa, thank you for this very helpful and information info!

One of my favorite izakaya dishes. It’s even better if you add a generous pinch of salted kelp(shiokonbu) and garlic is optional. Look for tender Japanese Haru cabbage in early spring at Japanese grocers or the pointy cabbage at farmers markets.
If tearing the cabbage is recommended why do the instructions only describe a very precise cutting with a knife ( 1 1/4” squares)  ??

Two points:
The intro says that one of the instructors prefers tearing the leaves, which I would take as a suggestion to try this, rather than being a recommendation.  It seems like more work, so you might try the tearing technique after finding you like this dish made following the recipe instructions.

The sizes are only approximations.  In Japan, they do not use inches.  1 1/4" is very close to 3 centimeters, which seems like a pretty typical ballpark number to use.

In the UK, a tender cabbage similar to Napa (Chinese) cabbage or Taiwanese cabbage commonly available in the USA is Hispi cabbage, also known as pointed cabbage or sweetheart cabbage. It has a conical shape, tender leaves, and a mild, sweet flavor, making it comparable to the texture and taste of Napa cabbage.

If Hispi cabbage isn’t available, spring greens (a loose-leaf, early-season cabbage) or a young savoy cabbage can also serve as a substitute for Napa cabbage in many recipes. These options are tender, versatile, and work well in stir-fries, salads, or as a steamed side dish.
This was so salty as to be completely inedible. Sadly, we threw it out.

It's very important to rinse off the salt before dressing it.  This technique allows the salt to break down the toughness of the cabbage and to penetrate inside the leaf, rather than just sitting on the surface. But if you don't rinse it off, it will indeed be too salty.  It's also very important to use Diamond Crystal Kosher salt.  3 tablespoons of of table salt or fine sea salt will make this too salty. If that's all you have, I would use 1 T  (This is from the author of the cookbook : )

@Lisa Kyung Gross I followed the instructions and used diamond crystal salt an washed it off thoroughly. Still much too salty. Next time I will use 1.5-2tbsp of salt instead of the 3 tbsp called for and omit the salt in the dressing.

Some commenters are saying it's too salty as written; I haven't tried it yet but yeah wow that's a lot of salt. A reasonable rule when using salt to draw liquid out of vegetables is to use 1-2% salt by weight, so for a pound of cabbage a tablespoon of kosher salt (maybe at most 1 1/2 Tbsp) should be plenty.
@Heather It's not a lot of salt. You're supposed to rinse it well afterwards. Not even as much as a pickle should be in a serving.

@Heather I think you’re making the same mistake as many other commenters in mixing your measurement units.  You mention the cabbage by weight (pounds/grams) but then the salt by volume (tablespoons/ milliliters).  Diamond Crystal weighs less than 1/2 as much as table salt so everyone would be better off specifying weights (preferably in grams) to avoid this confusion.
1 teaspoon Morton table salt = 7 grams 
1 teaspoon fine sea salt = 5 to 6 grams (depending on brand)
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal = 2.8 grams
1 teaspoon Morton Kosher = 4.8 grams
Any ideas for a garlic substitute?
Ginger?
You can omit the garlic. If you want a bit of a kick, try sprinkling some Shichimi/Nanami (both the same thing despite what English media says) as it includes some citrus plus spices.
@Japanese expat yes I love garlic but found it overwhelming in this.
We tried this as written, but it turned out a bit bland. Some of us added a bit of vinegar. But then it's more like regular cabbage salad.

I disregarded the note about using about ~1/2 tbsp garlic and just used the whole amount from the two cloves I grated (probably almost a tbsp)I love garlic! What could go wrong? Anyway I’m safe from vampires for a while and look forward to making this again with a smidge less garlic.

I found this too salty for my liking. I weighed out 1 lb of the Taiwanese cabbage, used Diamond Krystal kosher salt, followed directions and rinsed and dried thoroughly. It was still too salty for my liking.
This looks delicious and I will try it but what is the point of kosher salt? What is the difference between it and any other kind of salt in a recipe like this one?
@RainyDay different salts are actually salty at different levels! Using the same amount of, say, sea salt would make the dish completely different (and probably inedibly salty)

@Alex

As it was, with the amount of kosher salt in the recipe, some at my table found it too salty.
@RainyDay kosher salt is very different than table salt. I tsp of kosher is much less salt than I tsp of table salt bc of the grain size
Would it be sacrilegious to use red cabbage?  Maybe slice as for coleslaw to allow more chance for massaging it to be more tender?
I think using red cabbage would make this a delicious dish. I oftentimes have leftover red cabbage and this would be a good way to use it. Let’s go for it.

@Anna F I don’t know about it being sacrilegious but I don’t think red cabbage would work particularly well here as it has quite a strong flavour which I think calls for an acidic component such as vinegar or lemon juice. I also can’t imagine sesame oil being terribly nice with red cabbage…but you could give it a go and let us know!
3 Tablespoons of salt?! Get outta here. Rinsed thoroughly, drained well. Way too much. I usually don't comment, but go easy on the salt.

I've been making a dressing for cabbage consisting of soy sauce, peanut butter, lime juice, and honey, adding water gradually to thin out. I pout this over sliced cabbage, shredded carrots, fresh peanuts, and chow mein noodles if I have them. Yummy

This is great with rice and fish and/or meats. It seems like some people are finding the garlic and salt on the strong side, but this seems pretty typical for Japanese salads and side dishes, which are meant to be an accent to unsalted rice. I love it (and it really is addictive after the first couple bites).
I cut the kosher salt in half but after the rinse step it was still too salty for my taste, even after rinsing several times.
Made as written and it is delicious.  Served with veg gyoza and tofu

I used standard green cabbage that I already had in the house.  Despite heeding the warnings in the comments to use kosher salt and rinse and drain the cabbage well, it still turned out unpalatably oversalted.  I’m very puzzled and disappointed.

We’re trying this technique right now with some cabbage looking for a recipe. My husband is in charge of this one! I thought to maybe serve this with the Korean ground pork meatballs somewhere here in NYT Cooking.
WAY TOO SALTY!! Inedibly so.

In my experience I have found that Napa Cabbage is far tastier than regular cabbage, easier to cook and to consume. Be it raw in a salad, in a simple stir fry and in a soup. Sometimes the actual taste can be a little sweet which makes the thicker white ribs perfect for soups. I slice the ribs and add first into soup to allow the flavour to be well absorbed, and lastly add sliced leaves.  Napa Cabbage works well in any salad, and can blanched in boiling water and then dressed with oyster sauce.
The salting and squeezing reminds me of the preparation of Hungarian Cole slaw.
If this is best eaten the day it's made, it's no good for a person living alone. I love cabbage, but think I'll skip this one.

I followed the recipe as written. I weighed the cabbage, I used kosher salt, I rinsed the salt off the cabbage before dressing it and it was WAY too salty. Inedible, in fact. A sad waste of a nice Napa cabbage.

I used 3 Tablespoons coarse Signature Kosher Salt, and rinsed it quite well. I also added recommended amount of garlic. The dish was inedible. It tasted MUCH too salty, and too garlic-y. Horrible!! 

I will try again with much less salt and garlic.

This is almost good; but, for me, too much garlic and too much salt. It was so salty that we couldn’t eat more than a bite or two and so much garlic! I like garlic but I don’t like to taste it in my mouth all the next day.
This is addictive!! Used regular cabbage instead of Napa because I wasn’t paying attention.  It was delicious - can’t wait to try with Napa.

This is like kimchi, just not aged. I think it would just get better on subsequent days. I make my kimchi with Napa cabbage (and carrot, green onion, chopped apple, ginger). It's a lot more tender than regular cabbage, but if you want the crunch, definitely use regular cabbage.

Followed directions (including rinsing thoroughly). It came out extremely salty and overpowering with raw garlic (I used a microplaner to grate the garlic, so maybe this part is my bad). Not addictive at all.

Despite rinsing very thoroughly, this was still way too salty for me and lacked a depth of flavor. If I did it again I would use much less kosher salt, a little less garlic, add some ginger when grating, and fish sauce to the dressing

I’ve never posted a negative comment in this space, but this recipe is deeply flawed. Too many if/then scenarios regarding salts and cabbages. It’s simply too salty. I read ALL of the comments, dialed back the salt, and thoroughly rinsed the cabbage twice. There’s no need to add salt to the dressing.

This was a delicious side bowl with a breakfast of egg sandwiches, and our enjoyment of it was not diminished in any way by using "jarlic" or regular green cabbage. I've become immune to the interminable complaining of the NYTC Anti-Salt Brigade, and ignoring all the pearl-clutching in the comments was (as always) the right call. This was a tasty treat and I'm happier today for having made it.
Too salty here, and I rinsed the cabbage three times. Might try it again with a third the salt, if that. It could also use a little acid for balance.

A lot of work for a lot of nothing. Flat. Boring. Lacking in any depth or layering of flavour. I’d rather eat raw cabbage or sauté it in some butter until melt in your mouth tender than this disappointing slippery and boring dish.

Wow, I musta done something wrong.  I love cabbage, love garlic, love sesame.  I even love salt. Used Napa cabbage. Followed instructions.  Didn't find it too salty; just tastee kinda blah.  I notice some people are adding some things that would supply some tang.  That might've helped but sounds like it's not exactly authentic.  Oh well.  De gustibus non disputandem est.

I'm sure it was user error, but this was too salty for me. I have Morton kosher salt. I used 2 TBSPs since it's saltier than Diamond Crystal. Even so, it was a salt lick salad. I thought I rinsed well, but if I made this again, I would basically bathe the cured cabbage to get the salt off. I'm bummed! I'll have to find this dish at a Japanese restaurant so I can experience how it is supposed to taste.
@Elizabeth I used 1.5 Morton’s and it turned out well.
Please, please... if the success of this dish is based on a particular brand of salt, why can't you give the salt amount by weight, so it doesn't matter what brand we use?????

@Mk because small amounts often won’t register (or be accurate) on typical kitchen scales. Something similar to a jeweller’s scale would be required. For this reason, even when a recipe is given in weights, ingredients required in smaller amounts will still be noted by volume.

I sometimes make a slightly wilted cabbage salad by simply microwaving chopped cabbage for 20 to 30 seconds. Then add dressing. This eliminates the need for wilting the cabbage by salting it, and the hazard of oversalting.

I dismissed the "too salty" comments as being from overly sensitive folks, but alas, this is actually hella salty, even when following the instructions to a T. If I try try it again it'll be with less salt generally, and probably only letting it sit in the salt for 5 min max.
I have a different name for this dish: very salty cabbage with sesame oil and raw garlic.  And yes, I rinsed it twice. Most of it ended up in the garbage can. A are miss for NYT Cooking.

I mistakenly used the wrong salt so it was terribly salty. Also it’s quite hard to have a colander or bowl big enough to handle this recipe. That said the toddler gobbled this one up. Might try the advice to use a tablespoon of Morton’s next time. More NYT recipes should really just use Morton’s.

Coming back to say that I took my way too salty leftovers and added them to the NYT red lentil soup recipe (minus some salt and subbing some water for broth) and that was delicious. (Also added some spinach wild rice and celery).

Thought I rinsed thoroughly but still found borderline too salty. Next time will omit salt from dressing and rinse cabbage in successive immersions in salad spinner. Otherwise, we loved the simplicity and crunch! Definitely more than the sum of its parts. I thought the pepper especially shone, with its subtle fruit and even smoke noted.
@Andie It calls for Diamond Crystal kosher salt, which measures differently from table salt, if that’s what you used.
Agree that it’s a little too salty. Made recipe exactly as written with Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
I made this, it was way too much salt. I would use 1 tbs instead.
I can’t stop making this. It’s delicious! I highly recommend adding a sprinkle or two of MSG.
I added red pepper flakes and it added a nice bite to the dish

This was delicious. If you’re looking for a little more punch in the taste, try adding s bit of rice vinegar and a dash of light soy sauce. Since the soy sauce is generally salty you can anticipate this by holding back on that final 1/8 tsp of salt

I made it with green cabbage because I had a head on hand when the recipe popped up. 
Delicious!  But next time I’ll leave out the extra salt in the dressing and add it only if it feels needed later. I also tend to be generous with the garlic but for this one I’d start with exactly what’s recommended- you can always add more if you feel it’s not garlicky enough.

I made this with Napa Cabbage and will look for the flat cabbage but I also think Savoy cabbage would work too.   Rinsing and spinning the cabbage removes most of the excess salt but the lingering flavors of the salt, and dressing in and on the cabbage are divine! 
Today I used this as a topping for some Baked Steel Cut Oatmeal that I made.  
Delicious recipe.

Oh my word. Addictive is right!  You had me at sesame oil.  Two of us polished off a double batch in an afternoon.  Starting with 1/2 head of Napa cabbage, the softened cabbage was way too salty even after three rinses, and the dressing made without salt. So I washed, chopped and softened the second 1/2 with much less salt. Made more dressing and tossed it all together.  This is definitely a new staple in our household. Thank you for the recipe!

I used 1.5 T Morton’s kosher salt, 1 garlic clove, and 2 T avocado oil. I liked it a lot and I will make it again! 

Now that I know what to expect, with one successful attempt under my belt, I can add on a bit more next time. 

I added only one garlic clove because I read other comments about the garlic being too much. 

I also do not have Red Diamond kosher salt. A very helpful comment included the link to an article about the differences in salts by Kenji Alt-Lopez which led me to my estimate of halving the salt. 

I used avocado oil because I couldn’t find my toasted sesame oil until after I finished tossing the salad. I added a bit.
Excellent recipe!  Used regular and savoy cabbages interchangeably without issue.  I know why it’s referred to as “addictive cabbage!”

I made this with normal, boring green cabbage (because that's all I had.) I liked how the salt softened and flavored it, though I did leave it on much longer. My dining companion and I agreed the garlic was too much, though we both liked the sesame flavor.
Why is something that is so simple can be this good??
I used napa cabbage and I was surprised at how much we liked this!  I served it with sticky rice and a spicy soy marinated chicken thighs.  It's going on the make again list!

Easy, delicious, and I will be making this again. A salad spinner is a necessary tool to make this easily. Also, this recipe is for people that love garlic. I use just a half clove and we're still tasting it the next day.
Yum! Even my cold salad-hating husband liked this. Really good and so easy!

I’ve become addicted to this recipe and make it frequently enough that it’s become a regular pantry item next to my in-house KimChi and Harissa. Frequently a side dish in every meal period and more recently on a tartine and in a sandwich and omelet.  In NYC’s lower east side the flat cabbage is not expensive and readily available.  The cabbage is durable and a second rinse and drain using a salad spinner works if you think it’s too salty and try a third time if that doesn’t work.

At first, I thought there's nothing special about it, but I couldn't stop eating it. Napa or Taiwanese cabbage would be ideal, but I used red cabbage and it worked fine. Please don't skip soaking it in salt water - it crisps up the cabbage, brings out the sweetness and prevents water leaking. 3tbsp of big salt crystals seem about right for me. After letting sit in the fridge for half a day, I saw barely any water pooling and no drop in quality. This is such a simple dish so prep work is key.
It was decent...not addictive. It got my veggie hating father to eat cabbage that wasn't in coleslaw or corned beef. That's a win.

Made this (nearly) as written with hispi cabbage. I used the last of my fresh garlic for that night’s main dish so I used dehydrated garlic in the dressing. I’m sure it would have been better with fresh but I still really enjoyed it!

Too salty! Yes, I rinsed the cabbage off, I even let it soak for a minute in fresh water. But my partner and I both agreed, too salty. It was still edible but not pleasant. For the record I used Napa cabbage. Idk if maybe it works better with traditional green cabbage
Practiced a little of restraint to not add anything else and what a payoff. Anazing, just pulled back a bit on the salt but what a gem this simple recipe is.

Made to use up half a head of Napa cabbage. Hubby gave it 5 stars and said he didn’t get the same zip that I did from the pepper. Maybe it mellowed. Great way to prep cabbage! Easy and pantry friendly
Followed the recipe exactly and the cabbage was so salty it was inedible.
Used Taiwanese cabbage from H Mart, 2T Morton's kosher salt, not too salty at all.

While not quite addicting it was tasty. I used 1/4 head of green cabbage, 1T generic kosher salt, halved the dressing ingredients. Sprinkled with red pepper flakes and furikake to boost the flavor. Delicious, and not overly salty.
This is way more delicious than I hoped. OMG will be making this all the time. Thank you!
After rinsing I’m going to stir fry this as a hot side dish….why not?
@Honey Lynn Sure, you could, but it wouldn't be the same dish. This is meant to be more fresh and crispy and salad-like. Not a cooked cabbage dish.
Should the woodier pieces from the bottom of the cabbage (that it instructs you to cut into thin strips) make up the majority of the bulk here? Seems like these woody parts will be too chewy?
Is raw garlic not too strong? Not for taste, but for sociability?

Oh, yes! Taiwanese flathead cabbage is of the highest quality.  At our farmers market in Hollywood, CA , there’s one vendor who sells this.  It’s a bit pricey, but worth it, and his is also organic! Some people are a bit surprised to hear that this variety is brilliant (raw) in salads: tender and slightly sweet. I don’t do the pre-salting, and it’s just fine!  Adding a bit of grated ginger and Mirin to the dressing is fabulous.  No Mirin?  A dash of Japanese Rice Vinegar will suffice.

For the lazy among us - can you just salt this with less salt and leave it sitting for longer and skip the rinsing?  That's my go-to technique with vinaigrette coleslaw and it has worked for me in the past.

Perhaps I made a mistake but I thought this cabbage recipe was borderline inedible. Just overwhelming garlic and sesame oil taste. It cried out for some acidity.
I have no idea why they say to add salt to the dressing. I love salt, and even after very deeply rinsing the cabbage this is WAY too salty. Way.

I tried this because it sounded odd and the odd ones are often surprisingly good recipes.. I made a mistake and used regular rather than Kosher salt, but I was able to soak some of the saltiness out. But the dressing? It absolutely did not work for me. The sesame oil was far too predominant and there was no sour contrast. It’s four hours since I ate it and I can’t get the taste out of my mouth!

I added harissa paste, fish sauce, and fresh squeezed lemon to the dressing. I was distracted and let the salt marinate longer than advised, so I soaked the cabbage before spinning dry in the salad spinner. I’ll make sure to rinse when advised next time as it was saltier than I would have liked. I’m a garlic and sesame oil fan, so I added more of both to taste. So, very salty.

@H. Elizabeth Smith  ooh, fun! Also a nice texture variation, and speaks to the brightness sought by another commenter.

The garlic is essential in my opinion, to cut the richness of the oil a bit. I'd probably use 1/3 or 1/2 less oil next time though and maybe a little less garlic.
Also, any leftovers can be mixed with the other half of the raw cabbage and roasted in the oven for 18-20 mins, maybe a couple of more drops of sesame oil before - came out very nice.

Amazing!!  And easy.  Added some rice vinegar. Can use any kind of cabbage chopped up any old way. MAKE SURE YOU USE KOSHER SALT - or very coarse.  Otherwise it will be inedibly salty.  Trust me, I know…. Squirt on some sriracha and bingo.

I agree with a previous comment- too salty!  I followed instructions precisely, except based on the previous salty comment I only added two tablespoons of salt... Just made this! Two recommendations to truly bring out the flavors: gochujang and lemon juice. A little heat and a little acid really go a long way.

Made this as a side to bo saam--it's not only a fantastic (and yup, addictive) palate-cleansing side, but we all found ourselves eventually replacing the bo saam's lettuce leaves with it. It's an almost shockingly perfect foil to every other element in the bo saam.

I made it with just standard white cabbage.

Thanks 🙏🏻
Big hit in my house. One of our favorite side dishes.
Next time, will use a little less salt for Napa cabbage.  It soaks up the salt better than traditional cabbage. Still delicious.  Will be a new staple.
I made this last night exactly according to the recipe - delicious!! We served it along with rice.
What a delicious simple dish. This will be a favorite! Thank you for this great recipe.

Well, I made this dish with high hopes.  After a lot of washing/kneading cabbage, salt content was still too high.  Dressing alone wasn’t enough to tamp it down.  And finally, dressing was a little … “flat”.

@KT W. Did you use kosher salt? I you did which brand did you use, Morton’s or Diamond? Morton’s is almost twice as salty Tustin than Diamond. I’ve always use Diamond to make kimchi and things like this recipe, which comes out amazing if using the correct ingredients and carefully washing each piece of cabbage. Yes, it’s time consuming, but, if done right, it won’t taste salty. I’ve made this recipe at least a dozen times.
Odd the notes state the author suggests to tear the cabbage yet the recipe instructs to "cut" it. (?)
Love it!  I used napa cabbage.

Why is it that recipe makers insist that we weigh things like flour, that are difficult to measure by volume, but REFUSE to do the same thing for salt.  I don't always have "Diamond Crystal" salt in the house.  Sometimes it's Morton.  Sometimes it's BJ's brand, sometimes all I have is sea salt.  Can you all PLEASE, give a weight measurement for the salt so we don't have to go out and buy a box of Diamond Crystal for no reason?

According to a search, 1 Tbsp of Diamond Crystal kosher salt is about 10 grams, Morton kosher salt is about 16 grams, and table salt is about 20 grams (values provided vary from 18 to 23 grams). The reason these values can vary a bit is because of differences in crystal size and packing from brand to brand. But the pattern is: table salt gives twice as much saltiness as the Diamond Crystal standard for a given volume measurement.
Not as tasty as I’d hoped
Horrible. That calls for a massive amount of salt that I could not even wash off. Also too much oil. I added a regular entire cabbage to try to soak that stuff up.

Be sure you use Kosher salt, not regular table salt. It's listed in the ingredients, but it says only "salt" in the instructions. If you use table salt, that will be about three times as much as you need or want.

@Susan *and* if you use Morton’s kosher salt, halve it. That worked well for me.

Adding a bit of grated ginger and Mirin to the dressing is fabulous.  No Mirin?  A dash of Japanese Rice Vinegar will suffice.
I have been making a variation on this adding a small measure Chinese mushroom powder and a small amount of sichimi togarashi. Sometimes I will add halved cherry tomatoes for variety. Great dish.

Sometimes called 'impatient pickles,' these can be made with a little hot red pepper, some finely julienned ginger root, as well as the Napa cabbage, salt & kelp. A pickle press comes in handy & one can leave them for hours or more in the fridge before squeezing & serving. I never rinse them; they never seem too salty. A little shoyu @ serving is all.
In UK use Cornish sea salt crystals. I do this recipe with kale and then airfry for a few minutes, delicious crunchy snack.
Is toasted sesame oil something that comes in a bottle or do you need to "toast" it yourself--and if so, how?

Comes in a bottle, at any supermarket really.  One thing to really take note of if you decide to use it in any preparation, is that a little goes a long way.  A few drops added to ramen for example, is all you will need!
It comes in a bottle.  "Untoasted" sesame oil is more like a neutral oil in flavor.  Toasted sesame oil has a strong toasted sesame taste.
I thought it said to rip the cabbage, not cut into squares?
Used Diamond kosher salt and rinsed well. Still tasted too salty for me.

I used kosher salt, and I thought I rinsed the cabbage very well. It was tasty but a bit too salty. I regularly salt just about everything I eat, so I'm not one to complain about it. Next time I'll cut back a little. We ate the cabbage with roasted chicken and a baked potato.
It would be helpful to see a photo of a Taiwanese flathead cabbage - I've never heard of this before and would not recognize it.
@Ellen you could Google it?

We found this boring. We did use kosher salt. Added some special wasabi spice powder we picked up in a recent trip to Japan. That improved it slightly. 

The most addictive use of cabbage is part of the batter for okonomiyaki. 

We wouldn’t make this again
Kind of like an Asian version of sauerkraut. But the sesame oil makes it special.
It is way, way too much salt. Much more than is needed to taste fine.

I made this last night with a regular green cabbage as that was what I had.  Otherwise, I followed the instructions exactly.  It was too salty and the garlic was too strong for my family’s taste, but we can see the potential.  I plan to try again with the proper cabbage, less garlic and perhaps no salt in the dressing.  Looking forward to experimenting until I get it just right!

Made it with addition of rainbow radish, green onions and cucumber. Tasty and a great accompaniment to grilled salmon. 
Question; the leftover triangular pieces of cabbage leaves, seems like a waste to not use them. I am thinking of sautéing them with some shitake mushrooms and green onion to add as another side dish. Any other suggestions?
Australian here. Is Napa cabbage the same as wombok?
@Jenny from Oz hi Jenny I’m from Oz and I would make this with wombok or anything sold as “Chinese cabbage” in our stores.
Easy and delicious.
This was good.  I'm hungry and hoped for better.  Yes, it is a bit salty after a thorough rinse.  I would do again with a barbecue.
Any thoughts on replacement for sesame oil?

This reminds me of a recipe (based on ordinary cabbage) I used to cook after remodeling my house during the day and working the night shift at my job. (I used to have limitless energy. Now that I'm old, not so much.) I'd peel 3 or 4 potatoes, cover with water and a tbsp of salt in a deep pot, with some cloves of fresh garlic. While that came to a boil, I'd cut a head of cabbage into pieces and dump them over the potatoes. Cover and simmer till all is tender. Leftovers for lunch.
There is no acid - could you add some rice vinegar?
Recipes aren’t commandments—you can do whatever you want. I added vinegar to mine, but it didn’t help.
@J Webster why do you need acid? Try it first.
pretty good. Wouldn’t call it addictive good. But very easy to make. Yes I rinsed the salt off
Loved it!!!

I am not addicted to this cabbage. I may never bother to make it again. The one note (salty) needs some balance -either spice or sweet but as is ( made with napa cabbage) - it is anything but addictive. Rather, it’s forgettable.
Glad to see I’m not the only person who was disappointed!

Used  Napa Cabbage and it was way to0 salty even with just 2/3 the salt and thorough rinse.  Didn't even add to the dressing.  Going to try 1/2 the amount next time around.  Maybe the Napa absorbs the salt more quickly?

I actually have to add a second comment to this because I am so deeply disappointed in this recipe. I prepared it as written and it was a pain in the butt to make, made my kitchen messy, and tasted horrible. I actually threw it out it was so bad. This is the first time any recipe from the Times has failed me. I’m really sad. It’s just awful.
Cabbage is hugely unappreciated in the U.S., yet one of most nutritious, yet it is cheap, highly nutritious, low in calories, and infinitely versatile. This recipe is an example.

I usually love her recipes and made this to serve with miso cod. It was alright but just alright. The garlic gave it a bite and I liked the crunch but overall I found the flavors kind of bland and one-note. I would make it again but probably to add to a larger tossed salad with Asian flavors
I can’t help adding more vegetables for color and nutrition….a shredded carrot and handful of diced kale made this delicious and beautiful!
@Kwp I repurposed and expanded my leftovers as a side for tamales by adding red bell pepper, onion, cucumber, and a sprinkle of feta. Held up even the following day!
I used kosher salt and it was still way too salty. And I did rinse off the cabbage under running water as stipulated. I will try it with half the amount of salt.

Should call this Odiferous Cabbage, ha! I wanted to love this and I thought I would, but I just don't love toasted sesame oil enough to want it to be the star flavor. Will make again with a different oil. 
Those flat cabbages are amazing, I get them at my local Asian market. They are crispy, juicy, and mild, so worth looking around for.
Can this process be used with kale instead of cabbage?
Not a fan of sesame oil, I turned this into more of an Italian salad by substituting a lemon vinaigrette and by adding grated carrots and crispy/caramelized leeks.  It's just as addictive.

“This is best eaten the day it’s made, as the garlic intensifies in the dressing over time.”

And?? :)

Heated up on a plate topped with leftover baked salmon and a spritz of lemon. Not too garlicky (for me), quite delicious.
@Halina I repeated that to make sure it got finished!

True confession time: I had planned to pick up a Napa cabbage today so I could serve this for dinner tonight. I forgot. Ended up using a bag of the preshredded coleslaw stuff that had been sitting in my fridge. And it still came out great!  I can only imagine using the better ingredients will be sublime.
Finally made this, adding and mixing in leftover sous vide chicken breast sliced thin. Absolutely perfect meal with a glass of white wine!

I made this today under less than ideal circumstances.  No Diamond Crystal Salt, no sesame seeds, etc.  I used Kirkland pink salt, and after rinsing and spinning it was not too salty.  I have my Amazon order in for the right salt for next time.  I wouldn't refer to what I made as addictive, but after a few shots of tamari and white vinegar, it was pretty good.  I know the D Crystal Salt is a big deal, but I sure hope it doesn't get to where all interesting recipes require it.

I made the recipe as directed.  I weighed and timed everything.  I rinsed the beejuzus out of the cabbage and it was too salty for my tastes.  I will make it again with Diamond Crystal salt. I think the amount of salt is proportional to the size of the cabbage.  I used a smaller Napa cabbage.  I can see it's potential though.
I was unclear in my original post.  I used DC salt in my original attempt.

Hmmm. I’ll probably get skewered for this but it needs something…lemon or lime, rice vinegar, something. It was just meh, so I added carrots and cucumbers and rotisserie chicken for some protein to make it a meal and finally sprinkled some rice vinegar on top for some more depth. Yes I admit, that is a different recipe.  But I DID start with the actual one and can say it was just ok.
Will red cabbage work? I keep it on hand for all my salads— love the flavor along with the crunch.

Regarding the too salty comment & Ms Gross' response-  Perhaps I did not rinse adequately because I found it a bit too salty.  I had some extra cabbage.  Soaked it (sans salt), rinsed it and worked it into the already prepared cabbage.  That I found took a some of the salty bite out of the batch as a whole.  The only variation from the recipe was using Napa cabbage over the preferred flathead cabbage.  Overall though I enjoyed it and will certainly make it again.
Can it be made with coleslaw mix?
Easy tasty side dish.  I added yuzu for our tastes that lean toward spicy and a little acidic.

Made it with diamond crystal kosher salt, rinsed three times w fresh water and I’m here to chime into the chorus of too salty.  I was a little loosy goosy with the timing, and know it was at least 15 min maybe more, and wondering if that’s why.

Make sure to keep an eye on the timer! I got distracted and let mine sit with the salt for an extra 5 minutes, and even well-rinsed, it ended up being inedible. I'll definitely be giving it another shot, though!

I only had sea salt and lost track of time so probably left it for 20min. Way too salty that way! Next time I'll cut the salt in half and stick to the 10 min (being honest w/ myself, not gonna purchase different salt for this recipe). Could see how w/ less salt this would be great...the dressing was garlicky and tasty!
I wish I would have read the comments prior to making the salad!  Mine was way too salty as well - I also lost track of time.
No amount of rinsing kept this from being way too salty (I used kosher coarse). A rare miss down the drain, it was that bad.
Just a question about the salt... does it all get rinsed away? Or is some absorbed and remains in the cabbage? Asking for heart health/hypertension reasons. Thanks!
Mine came out way too salty tasting.  I think because I let the cabbage sit with the salt for 20 minutes.  Next time I will stick to the 10 minutes suggested!
I used Himalayan pink salt and it was a complete mistake. Even though I rinsed, it was way too salty. Almost inedible. Recipe should adjust with exact salt type. Will try again.

After doing exactly what the recipe said and not enjoying it, I tasted it and added 1/4 tsp sugar and 1 tsp of rice wine vinegar to liven it up and liked my results much much better.  Frankly, I recommend following the instructions for salting and rinsing, then applying a (vinegar-based) dressing you already like.

I'm baffled as to why the recipe was so boring on its own.  I've checked my work and I didn't make a mistake. And it's not like garlic or fish sauce are subtle flavors.
Unfortunately, I didn't have toasted sesame oil, so my non-toasted seemed a bit bland. I added a splash of rice vinegar which helped and for leftovers & umph I added some fresh udon noodles.
Anyone know why it calls for untoasted sesame seeds? Aren’t the Asian kind already toasted?
What’s a good way to add spice to this recipe?

@Robin, I think gochugaru pepper flakes and/or -sichuan peppercorns would be delicious in this. I had planned on adding one of them, but found the tang from the garlic was enough spice for me - this time. I would start with a small amount, maybe 1/4 -1/2 teaspoon and add more to taste.
I love cabbage, so I couldn't wait to try this. Simply put - for my part, I did not find it "addictive." It's okay as a side dish, but that's about all.

I was able to get flat head (Taiwanese) cabbage for this recipe and the cabbage itself was a revelation. Even while prepping the cabbage I found myself nibbling on it and enjoying its sweetness.  I am fond of regular cabbage too but sometimes it has a sulfurous quality that isn't totally pleasing.

We made this as a side dish for guests and between four people, we ate almost all of it. Great flavor combo. I missed the adjustments in the comments for Morton’s vs. Diamond Crystal - next time I would reduce the salt, as it was a bit much for me even after thorough rinsing. But the garlic/sesame oil combo in the dressing was fantastic. A guest suggested including some thin-sliced fennel next time, and I thought that was a great idea.

This was pretty good (not "addictive," though!)
Any thoughts on toasted sesame oil vs. untoasted sesame seeds?
I didn't have toasted sesame oil (just regular). I could have toasted the seeds to compensate ... should I have? Is the *toasted* oil vital?
Also, I haven't seen Diamond Crystal salt for sale here in Mexico. Any other tips on finding large grain (but not like Maldon flake) salt -- terms or other brands, or types (kosher, etc.) to avoid here?
Try Morton's Kosher Salt. It's "saltier" than Diamond so use less.
I love cabbage, and was excited to follow the recipe to a T, alas it was just ok.  Japanese bean sprout salad has a similar taste profile but is easier, cheaper and far better IMHO.
Super easy and super tasty with inexpensive ingredients I already had on hand - improves with a day in the frig.
Way too salty. Rinse more. Less oil.

I followed the recipe exactly as directed, using Napa cabbage. Used kosher salt, rinsed thoroughly. It still turned out overly salty and I had to add cucumber and carrot to make it edible. Wondering if the problem lies in the Napa cabbage? Maybe it absorbs too much salt? Will try with round green cabbage next time… or Taiwanese if I can find it.
Made with salted, rinsed cabbage and a drizzle of sesame oil. Finished with cracked black pepper
too much salt, even rinsed, and too much raw garlic for me. caveat emptor.

Although there’s no hard and fast rule, any recipe titled Yamitsuki suggests fast and easy with one or maybe two main ingredients. So, you can get fancy by adding chicken , carrots etc, but that wouldn’t be called Yamitsuki. Also, it shouldn’t take so many steps. Akin to American “dump” recipes, you simply add all ingredients at once, which means in this case, you use less salt and don’t rinse it off. It may require more massaging but keeps the flavor.
Great with savoy cabbage, and I add 2TB lite seasoned rice vinegar as well.
Way too salty.  Used diamond crystal, rinsed off thoroughly etc but still inedible.  Also no acid.  Added lime juice to try and save it but still no one in my family would eat it.
I do not understand the hype. Had to add salt, msg, and extra garlic and it was still boring. 🤷
Does it not need an acid? Vinegar or something to offset the oil?
try cream of tartar...
Missing dashi.
Also tasty with Shanghai bok choi, an inexpensive green vegetable. Made some yesterday.
This was way too salty, and I fully rinsed the leaves.  The recipe should make it clear that using regular fine salt will not work.  We had to throw ours out.
Im not a big fan of raw garlic so I blanched the whole garlic cloves in three changes of water, then grated with sesame oil; the blanching reduced the raw garlic taste significantly.
It's a nice, easy recipe and a good way to eat more fresh vegetables, but this was WAY too garlicky for me (and I like garlic!).
I used regular cabbage and found the end result to be blah so I added some apple cider vinegar. Much improved overall taste.

.. if anyone is interested- look up Bon Appetit's recipe "Spicy cabbage salad with turkey and peanuts". it is scrumptious!!   ! and the base of the salad is almost identical to this recipe.. raw cabbage, massaged for a few moments with salt- more for 'softening' effect than as a spice (you can add very little- just simply knead the cabbage with salt on your hands for a few moments)
I have been to two large Asian supermarkets and the Taiwanese cabbages are huge! They are the size of three Napa cabbages. Are they always this large?
I love shredded cabbage, boiled with just salt and water.  Drain and add butter to the final dish.  It is delicious.

I used 2 tablespoons of kosher salt, rinsed very well, and it was still a bit too salty for my palate.  I saved it by adding a few tablespoons of balsamic vinegar to offset the saltiness.  Of course it totally changed the flavor, but the result was really tasty.
Can you prepare the cabbage a day ahead. And keep dressing separate then mix and use next day?

I followed this recipe exactly. I used Napa cabbage. I tore it by hand. I rinsed the salt off after 10 minutes. I mixed the dressing and tossed. I ended up with a soggy, salty, overly-garlicky mushy mess. Yuck. I can’t imagine what I did wrong but what I made was inedible.
I’ve tried this twice and neither time was a triumph. I cut the salt in half, made sure to use kosher salt and it was still far too salty.
@Nora, this is not sauteed or cooked. A raw, scrunchy salad dish.
I made this exactly as recipe stated. It’s just plain boring, but it is a good base to add other veg and proteins.
very tasty. Really rinse the salt off the cabbage, otherwise it will be super salty.
Suggest adding toasted black sesame seeds if you have them.  Makes the dish look more visually exciting and adds a subtle sesame flavor.
Has anyone tried this hot…sautéing the cabbage?
I like salt and I like garlic, but this was way too strong in both flavors. Maybe I didn't use enough cabbage.

We used half a Napa cabbage and eyeballed the salt, otherwise made as directed. Very good, would be especially good after a long day of sweating. Added rice vinegar after eating one bowl, and found it very nice (but then, we are rice vinegar fans). This comment section seems to be full of people who made a recipe that calls for cabbage to sit with salt on it for at least 10 minutes, and are shocked that that tastes salty. There are many other recipes that use less salt on this site.

title: Ipo Pain Perdu (Coconut-Bread French Toast) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: How do you steam the bread?? It’s in a loaf pan then what???? Sounds so good!!
@Angela steam it in the loaf pan

So. I have made this three times now. 
Holy smokes is this yummy. Yes it’s a BIT denser than other French toast breads. But to its favor! 
I recommend using dry active and per usual doing an activation with 1/4 warm water. So reduce your coconut milk by 1/4c. I also did a proof/rise after mixing and forming into a ball for about 1hr. Then after placing in loaf pan doing a second proof/rise for 30mins to an hour. Bake 45 at 350. Bake with a loose aluminum foil cover too. Helps it not brown too much on top. 
This bread is fine alone but really benefits as a French toast. The crumb is wonderfully thicker but not at all “grainy”. The idea that it has a different texture loans from the coconut pieces. If that’s not your cup of tea then maybe use a finer coconut flake? 
Also after doing some research there is much more coconut than suggested. So I doubled it.  Yum!
@SpookyPlonks I followed this advice to a T, including both proofs, the foil, and the doubled coconut, and it was a smashing success. Not too dense at all!
@Mary Mac happy to hear this worked for you too. I’m going to revisit this recipe again. Got a new mixer and had been craving this again!
Has anyone tried this out yet? I’m curious about the texture of the bread
It was quite dense. There is a whole tablespoon of yeast in it, so it might benefit from proofing. Not sure if there is a glitch in the actual recipe
The recipe says you can bake the loaf rather than steam it, but doesn't give a temperature to bake it at. Help!
@Alison—Step 1 says 350 degrees…
The beginning of the recipe says to preheat the oven to 350
It says 350 degrees
How do you steam the dough in the rectangular loaf pan? Put it awkwardly on top of a round pot of boiling water, which obviously won't fit right?

I made this for my Christmas morning breakfast, and it was a pleasant twist on French toast. The bread was quite dense, and if I made it again I might proof the dough so that it can rise. I served it with blueberries and maple syrup
This sounded so good - but was a big fail. I followed recipe exactly - but bread did not rise at all and was dry and super dense. Disappointing!
Should one grease the pan for the ipo?
Yes---or coat with nonstick cooking spray
Not related to the recipe but if you find yourself in Mo’orea I highly recommend Heimata’s food tour. (tahitifoodtours on Instagram). One of the highlights of our trip to French Polynesia.
What is coconut creme? Where does one purchase it or is it made at home?

@JeanieDiva coconut cream is a mixture of coconut flesh and water, thicker than coconut milk. You buy it in a grocery store, depending on the store you might find it in the international foods – Thai food section. In theory, you could also make it from fresh coconut, but I don’t know how you’d have to look it up.
Some steaming instructions would be very helpful. Is it just sitting on a rack in its loaf pan? Does it need to be covered? How much is it expected to rise to know how big the pot should be?
Very yummy, but it's ALOT of effort for French toast.
Where do you get coconut cream? What exactly is it?

title: Mashed ‘Ulu With Passion Fruit Chutney Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: We have about 40 Ulu on our tree outside our window, looking forward to trying this mash of Ulu and cultures…mahalo

Just made the “ulu” recipe today, here in Maui. Had all the ingredients for the chutney, quite tasty with quite some heat, as well as ulu right of the tree.
Always looking for new way to accommodate ulu which grows abundantly in our jungle back yard. Very flavorful.

On Kauai we have multiple places to get the Ulu for free. I have been using it instead of tofu. We boil the fresh Ulu whole in the skin...it is so much easier than trying to peel the fresh, uncooked fruit.

Once peeled and cored, I mash the Ulu, press it into a half pan and freeze it, then cut the 1/2" thick frozen product into squares, tightly wrap, then freeze. It is so healthy. 

I made this recipe following the instructions to the letter. It was delicious.

We just tried this with an ulu we bought from the side of the road in Moorea. We had most of the ingredients and we improvised. It’s pretty good! I skimped on the sugar and that seemed fine. We didn’t have jalapeño so we used a bell pepper and cayenne pepper. No black mustard seeds, so substituted curry powder.
Like a fancy deconstructed samosa! The chutney is dreamy and we’ll definitely make this again.

I would NEVER have half a cup of “leftover” mashed potatoes! (That little would disappear from somebody’s plate in this household.)

But would (Gasp!) INSTANT mashed potatoes suffice - either made ahead or by adding to the flour (with a proportionate increase in liquids)?
@Peter Trader Joe’s sells frozen mashed potato discs. Probably better than the dried stuff!
@Mary  I don’t turn up my nose at any form of potato and instant would definitely work, but I second the TJ’s frozen mashed potatoes- they’re pretty good!
How could any recipe even mention margerine.

Most likely because the recipe is from a war time English pamphlet distributed by the Ministry of Food (1984 anyone?) because basic staples like butter were rationed, and if you had no butter, then margarine would do.
@OZ. 1949  - NewYork . My mother would mix a red dye capsule into margarine to make it look like butter
@Kathleen V Naples Why not? Tons of people choose to not eat dairy or are dairy intolerant.

My mother would make what she called potato pancakes with left over mashed potatoes. She’d serve them as a side, kind of, to mop up the gravy left on the plate - as only the British can!

My sister-in-law grew up with the same thing, but her mom called them potato scones (skahnz).

My mother also made potato pancakes with leftover mashed potatoes, and I still make them today. While married to my late English husband, I realized "bubble and squeak" was made with leftover mash as well as cabbage. Potatoes are so easy to cook, I don't know why anyone would serve packaged dried potatoes.
@bgall. Scottish potato scones, or tattie scones as they are called, are a totally different thing…they are flat.
@Ruth Probably because of the inevitable cycle of food shortages and food surplus - dry the potatoes during a surplus and use them when in short supply. Which we are about to experience...

“ Out of a limited batch of ingredients, you 12 scones, ”

You get 12 scones.
Just want to flag a tiny error in an otherwise stellar recipe I’m definitely going to try.
And you could just put butter and jam on them, or ham and cheese, or anything else you put on a scone.
It looks good 👍

Yummy and very easy.  Slices of our last, small garden tomatoes called out to me, along with a bit of brie.  Lovely.  Next time will make larger in diameter (personal preference).  Baked in two batches--one took 16 minutes, the other 13.  Just check as you go.

I love the idea of using less sugar in the recipe.  To get the sweet taste sprinkle a little sugar on the tops of the scones.  Or use jam when you eat them.  I don't miss the over sweet offerings made by the bakeries.
My personal preference is a scone without sugar topping. That changes the whole texture for me. Such a small thing, I know.

i thank and think it important that some reader pointed out the actual pronunciation of any english word has not all that much to do with how it may seem to be spelt, but rather obscure tradition holds sway. there are more than just two 'o's in our english, or one could insist on words like debriss, and so many more that i'm not giving away a free show here, tho' i am tempted

My mother used to make scones with mashed potatoes! They’re excellent! Everyone seems to be twisting themselves into knots over this…. Obviously, if you use mashed potatoes with some sort of herb seasoning, you’re going to get that flavor profile. Or you could add raisins or nuts…
Follow the recipe exactly as written, and with high expectations. Yielded 12 flat inedible hockey pucks. If I ever try again, maybe with fresher ingredients.
same!
Definately not a Tattie Scone !
What a great way to use up the leftover spuds for this small family!
How would sweet potatoes change this? Less sogar?

The scones in the photo seem rather thick.  I'd try slicing the scones in half horizontally giving 12 sandwiches, in lieu of the 6 of the recipe. Otherwise, the ratio of filling to pastry seems way out of whack, similar to how the Welsh/Brits used to make their sandwiches.  

Two pieces of thinly sliced bread, no more than 3"x3" after the crusts were removed. Once assembled, the sandwiches were cut into 4 tiny triangles. The ham sandwich contained one paper-thin slice of ham and nothing else.

I had hardly any butter, but lots of (smoked blue) cheese, these came out amazing. my mashed potatoes were very liquidy, so only added 1 cup of flour. Will definitely make again next time there are leftover mashed potatoes.

Recipe was not clear as to what it referred to as "leftover" mashed potatoes. "Leftover" mashed potatoes already have butter, dairy and seasoning. If that "leftover" will be used for this recipe should the other ingredients be adjusted, or has the recipe already taken this into account?
Click on the mashed potato recipe link to see the answer to your question.
can I use potato flour instead of mashed potatoes?
So the mashed potatoes should have how much butter in them?
Hey BC, if you note the mashed potatoes is a link to its own recipe, which by the way, uses 4 tablespoons of butter, but for the entire recipe - however the scones only uses the leftovers.
Doesn't really matter. Says wartime British mashed potatoes wouldn't have any butter at all, but if you have leftover mashed/whipped with butter it's fine to use that.
You lost me at “margerine”.

For those who are concerned about using standard supermarket margerines, consider vegan butter or even virgin coconut oil as alternatives. But these days, even a good vegetable shortening (like Spectrum) should work in lieu. Just skip anything with "hydrogenated" on the label!
@Liv Nilssen or just use butter…. It’s mentioned as well

@Liv Nilssen

 Remember: this is a wartime-rations recipe. Butter is mentioned but margarine was widely used and often more available than butter. Assuming this recipe would also work well with a top grade vegan baking butter, I recall my grandmother’s post-Great-Depression kitchen where margarine was also readily available “just in case”.
I tried this today and mine didn't rise at all. I'm not sure if it's because I used gluten free flour. They are also pretty plain- might be OK with a sweet filling. Not sure I'll make it again.

There is no reason to buy or use spray-on cooking oil. Pour some vegetable oil onto the pan and spread all over with a paper towel. Easy. Or use butter or margarine. You can skip the parchment paper too. Fewer things cluttering up the kitchen.

The parchment paper makes it possible to remove the cake from the pan by lifting it out in one piece rather than dumping and risking the likelihood of pieces sticking to the pan and ruining the cake.

Bakers spray is more effective than butter or margarine which tends to be absorbed by the cake or burn on the pan.

There are reasons for specific procedures.  Just because something has been done a certain way for hundreds of years doesn't mean that is the most effective way of doing things today.

My favorite hack when a recipe calls for lining a pan with parchment paper is to thoroughly wet the parchment paper, wring it out well, give it a vigorous shake and then line the pan with the now malleable damp paper. This is always my first step. By the time the batter is ready, the parchment is dry and molded to the shape of the pan. No cooking spray required.
@Sue What a brilliant idea! Thank you!
Made this today. Followed the recipe as written. It is fire. May never be the same.
