Please use whole pieces of ginger in syrup and the actual syrup.
Eataly has it and it's a delight to use in baking, especially in the recipe that ask for ginger. The fresh one isn't enough.
What kind of syrup?  I looked on Eataly and couldn’t find a ginger syrup.
What you are looking for is "ginger in syrup". Lots on the internet, or make your own.   Use a piece of the ginger, chopped up, in the glaze/decoration.  Yum
Sounds sweeter than crystallized ginger

It seems folks are asking about crystallized ginger to sprinkle on top of the icing. These are usually sold as slices or chunks cured in sugar and sold as a dry good (rather than in a liquid syrup). Some are more like candied ginger where as others are more like dried crystallized ginger (similar to the method used for dried apricots and other fruit).

If you want wonderfully spicy candied ginger, then save your small diced ginger after you use it here. It is so easy to crystallize, and half the crystalizing process was done by doing the early cook of the cubes for this recipe. There are simple recipes around, but I added the ginger cubes to pan, added equal weight baker's sugar, and two TBSP water then cooked for 20 mins on med high/stirring and cooled on a sugar coated parchment paper pan. Easy and great spicy cube of ginger amazing...!

This is great, if you follow the recipe, which I did.  I weighed the ginger, and did include the peels (after peeling and dicing) into the tea- I was surprised at how much honey 1/2 cup is- I needed all that I had in the house! And I did not put the glaze on it, as I wanted more of a tea cake, which this is....This is super super yummy- yes to fresh ginger!  Also, I used the ginger to make a tea that I simmered for the next couple of hours- also yummy.

Very good and very moist! I made this as directed,  except I added a little garam masala--it pumps up the flavor a bit. Also, I only had extra large eggs on hand, so that's what I used. It turned out really well and makes a great tea cake.

This cake turned out wonderful for me. Tender delicate crumb and when the glaze dried it added a unique sweet texture. And it was simple and quick to make. I used Tupelo honey. I buy a ceremonial jar every year.
Baked 45 minutes on convection, little too dark
Agreed. Honey burns easily, even at 350. Have to watch it. An hour baking time is too long.
In my “true euro convection” oven: 45 minutes at 350° and then 20ish minutes at 325°. Perfectly browned.

Took 50 minutes to bake so be careful to check so it won’t over bake.  I was worried that it would be too gingery so used less chopped ginger …delicious cake anyway..reminds me of a light honey cake.  Next time I will go the full ginger route.  I don’t think it would be too much.  I added water, squeezed lemons and honey to the strained pieces of ginger and now I have delicious iced ginger/honey/lemon tea in my fridge.
Agree with others who note that this lacks ginger oomph.  It's a nice honey cake.  Make sure you love the honey you are using, because it dominates the flavor.
Absolutely delicious. My glaze didn’t look like that. But it was still killer. Used gluten free 1:1 baking flour. Everybody loved!
If it takes 25 minutes to make and cool the salabat, I don’t think you should preheat the oven first.

Overall a nice pleasant loaf cake.  Found myself confirming weight for honey, seemed like a lot, but it is not. Followed directions to the gram, and the minute, expected it to be more assertive.  Cake is not overly sweet, but is missing a little zing.  Any suggestions to spice it up?  A pepper that would complement the ginger profile?  

Was amused by the glazing directions, instead of putting foil lined pan under cooling rack, a piece of paper towel, wax paper or parchment is less cumbersome...
Black pepper would work, maybe. It is used in the Chewy Gingerbread Cookies recipe from this site. I make those every year at Christmas to rave reviews.
Buy ginger tea, it cuts out half of the work.

I followed the recipe but added a few pieces of diced candied ginger and also no wax paper needed just smeared butter in the cake dish. It came out very tasty. Although an hour seemed a bit long after a little short of 50 minutes it was done.

This cake just so-so.  I bake a lot, and my loaf cakes are moist and very flavorful.  This is not. I baked it quite a bit less than the directions said, and it's still very dry. The flavor is OK, not really good. 
I have a delicious lemon cake recipe that I could add some freshly grated ginger to, and it would be much better than this one.
Where’s your recipe then?
This was delicious and I will absolutely make it again. Don't skip the crystallized ginger. I took it out at 50 minutes and it was perfect.
Made this with 1/2 cup of ginger, which is a lot less than 80 grams - next time I’ll add more ginger.  Used 2 8” round pans at about 30 minutes, which made 2 nice tea cakes.

This cake is fine. It baked really well and the texture is perfect. Glaze is nice too. The cake itself though seems nothing special? I see in the notes that maybe I should have weighed the fresh ginger vs just using a half cup. It was a nice cake, just wasn't super flavorful.

so, so good!!  made it w/ 1 cup whole wheat flour + 3/4 cup almond flour.

chef's kiss!!

;-)

also:  made it sans glaze.  imho, simply not needed...

;-)

I just now figured out that if you click the “start cooking” button in the app, the view switches to a handy view with separate tabs for ingredients and instructions, with larger, more readable font allowing you to easily switch back and forth instead of scrolling up and down. Life changing! Can’t believe I’ve been doing it the hard way for so long. 😂

I’m a big fan of ginger and I’m on a life long quest looking for the best Gingersnap recipe out there that has plenty of snap and ginger flavor. This cake satisfies my needs.  The ginger flavor doesn’t come at you like a sledge hammer but by the time I reached the end of my first piece my mouth was on fire.  This moist ginger honey tea cake is fantastically and deliciously satisfying.  For the others who saw failure try calibrating your over oven, get some fresh ginger and try it again.

I baked this delicious cake yesterday. Already half gone. Some reviews mention that theirs didn't come out with a strong ginger taste. Mine is even spicy. My trick? Before I even turned on the oven I sliced the unpeeled ginger ( no time for peeling & chopping) and put it in the pan with the water AND the 1/2 cup of honey. The ginger steeped before and after in this honey water salabat. Then I followed the recipe (BTW Thnx for the wet parchment paper trick, perfection) after I strained the ginger I froze the honey water steeped chunks for tea. It came out super delish. Will repeat.

Made per instructions, but without icing. The only change I would make is adding another warming spice—maybe cardamom, for some mystery or cinnamon, for the comfort. The ginger was a little one toned. The lemon didn’t shine through. I used a mild local PA honey, that added light floral notes, but not a heavy honey punch. It was just right. Very enjoyable, and not super sweet. Will make again.

Made this cake as instructed but instead of chopping the ginger, I finely grated it using a microplane grater which I think brought out more of the ginger flavour.  The lemon didn’t contribute much.  Like others have suggested, another warming spice is needed.  Perhaps cardamom?  And perhaps lime instead of lemon? I might make it again with these alternatives and I’ll let you know if I do. But otherwise, I don’t think I need to make this cake again. It’s “ok” but not amazing and there’s just so many other recipes out there to try.
Great cake, made as per directions first time, I used finely chopped crystalized ginger for the batter the second time... Both were wonderful.
Fabulous, moist, flavourful cake. I did add 1tsp of nutmeg...and made my own crystallized ginger with the pieces left over from the salabat (following the comment below!).

Wonderful tea cake. We highly recommend it, especially with a cup of tea. It is also terrific a day or two later. Make sure that your ginger bits are on the small side. They might be overwhelming for some if they are too large.
Made to recipe aside from using olive oil (because that’s what I have of course) and it is delicious- the ginger flavor is in a category of its own thanks to the salabat- delicious!

This is an amazing cake that I've made several times for lots of friends. The first time I made it I had printed several recipes and glanced at another recipe which called for a tablespoon of molasses, which I added. I do believe it added a rich, deep flavor. Also, as a fan of the Great British Baking  Show, I always use stem ginger (balls in sugar syrup) rather than crystallized ginger to chop and decorate the top.
Delicious!! Labor intensive for the result. It was amazing, but better served as a bread, toasted and buttered, than as a cake.

Could I nuke the ginger then put it in the food processor?
Work smarter, not harder and all that .... Advise.

i wouldn't. that's not a whole lot of ginger to go into a food processor, unless it's a mini. besides, once you slice the ginger, and i didn't peel it, you can let it simmer and steep while you collect the rest of the ingredients and you will only have a small pan to wash, a processor bowl with all its nooks and crannies.
The recipe calls for the ginger liquid of the tea - not pureed ginger.
Read the recipe.  It calls for ginger tea.  I fail to see why boiling ginger in a microwave rather than on top of the stove where it can be controlled is working smarter.
Is it really necessary to peel the fresh ginger?
Yes, you’re trying to extract maximum flavor so increasing surface area makes a difference

Nope!  Peeling ginger really isn't necessary, especially if you're going to steep it and then strain the ginger pieces out of the liquid.  I don't even peel ginger when it's going in a stir fry.  The papery peel just disappears as it cooks.
Do you wash the ginger if you don't peel the skin off?
Since it is a root, and has been at the store, a brief water rinse wouldn't hurt!
Yes. My shop says all raw food should be washed before consuming (unless it’s salad leaves, some herbs are pre-washed)
Any thoughts on how long this cake will keep?

In a warm house (not extremely hot and humid), 2-3 days in a metal biscuit tin or plastic container LINED with a sheet of kitchen roll, to absorb moisture in the air (otherwise the top goes sticky soggy), and then in the fridge for up to 7 days, 10 days maybe, to prevent it going mouldy or stale at room temperature. You can also freeze slices/whole cake for 2-3 months (double wrap in plastic cling film or metalfoil to avoid freezer burn - ruins food quality/freshness taste)
Question about ginger in syrup. Do you use this instead of making the ginger tea? And in what quantity? Do you decrease the amount of sugar?
I would think a mandoline or thin slicer on a food processor would be a time saver and give the increased surface area.

I have a huge jar of a Ginger/Honey jellied base that I just add water to for tea – got it at H-Mart and it's delicious!  I'd love to just use this and save steps... only Q is how much... advice appreciated.
I think that would be a great one for one substitution for the honey in the glaze!
Has anyone modified this and made this a milder ginger cake ? I like ginger but would like to modify amounts
I don't prefer lemon with ginger. Has anyone tried this by substituting orange juice (and orange zest in the glaze) instead of the lemon?

I am debating whether to make this. No one has commented on the strength of the ginger in the cake.  Does it have a really powerful ginger taste?  I am looking for a recipe that is extremely gingery, full-bodied and deeply gingery, almost hot and spicy.
Mine did not turn out hot and spicy. It has ginger flavor but no ginger fire.
Is there a gluten-free version?

Charles - you ask about a gluten free version.  You ought to be able to use a Gluten Free flour in 1:1 formula (Bob's Red Mill and King Arthur both have one and I'm sure others do too) and use it in place of the regular AP flour with good outcome.  Scroll thru to see JCS' comment:  "Used gluten free 1:1 baking flour. Everybody loved!"
I don't have sour cream on hand.  Has anybody used plain Greek yogurt as a substitute?

Karen - You ask about using Greek yogurt instead of sour cream.  I often to that when I don't have sour cream handy (which is often), using 5% (whole milk) Greek yogurt.  I also use a whole milk Kefir to substitute for sour cream as well as the brand I use (Nancy's) is quite thick.
Karen, Greek Yogurt is a fine substitute and so is crème fraîche I always use them interchangebly.
Presumed the ginger would soften after steeping, but it really didn't.

Don't know what I did wrong, but wasn't nearly as gingery as I wanted—perhaps it was the old ground ginger that I had? But I also oversteeped the ginger root accidentally, and I would've thought that would have made it more gingery, and not less. As a cake though, it was quite good. Definitely going for fresher ground ginger, and upping the amount of diced ginger I use in the tea. 

Other commentors suggestion to candy the ginger after you steep it is good!
Made it again, 1/4 Tsp black pepper and 2 T of ginger syrup subbing for 2T of ginger infusion, adding some chopped ginger in syrup and cutting baking time down really amped up the flavor.
If you love strong ginger, Marion Cunningham has a triple ginger muffin recipe in "The Breakfast Book".
@MJ  I love that book! Such appealing recipes and a wonderfully cozy tone…thanks for reminding me of it.

We made this as muffins instead of a loaf cake and it worked beautifully. Baked at 350 for about 20 minutes, topped them with chopped crystallized ginger and sunflower seeds before backing. Love them!

I have cooked this twice as I feel as if it had great potential. I cooked it first in a tin pan and it was really burnt on the bottom and the sides. The second time, I cooked it in a Pyrex for 50 minutes and it was still burnt on the bottom and the sides. I think it has the potential to be a great cake, but not sure how to avoid the burnt aspect.

This cake looks very pretty and I like the glaze and candied ginger on top.  I like ginger and use it in cooking a lot. However, the cake has an unusual flavor which I didn't like.  I think it's the ginger tea ("Salabat") that tastes odd to me.  My wife didn't like it either.  I will use the glaze and crystallized ginger on something else, maybe a traditional gingerbread?

I baked this cake twice.  I diced the ginger and steeped it but I don't think the cake had much ginger flavor. And you would think that adding that much powdered ginger would punch it up.
Has anyone shredded the fresh ginger and steeped for longer?
I made this Gluten Free  by using GF Flour blend and adding 1 3/4 tsp xanthin gum as my flour blend doesn't include it. It is my new favorite cake!

We loved this in place of usual honey cake for Rosh HaShana.  Made as directed except forgot to set a timer on the tea, so may have steeped an extra 10 minutes, and used the extra tea in place of water for the icing and used one extra tsp to make the icing a little thinner.  It tasted of bothginger and honey. It looked just like the picture.
Delicious, warming tea cake.  But 1 hour is too long to bake, by 10-15 minutes.  Edges were hard and overbrowned.

I had leftover sabalat and left over lemon juice this time. I mixed them together with a teaspoon of honey intending to drink as tea later in the day. I used this mixture teaspoon at a time when making the glaze instead of water. It really boosted the flavor. I will do that again

This was great! I only had two inches ginger but ground up in Cuisinart, made the tea, and it was fine. Tea was very spicy, strong, flavored whole cake! Used extra lemon, a whole lemon, in the glaze. Created more of a lemon-sugar soak, recommend this, it was delicious! My brother ate half the cake before was even done explaining what it was...This is a great recipe! A sleeper but great!! Enjoy!

title: Lazi Ji (Chongqing Chicken With Chiles) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody:
Fuchsia Dunlop is the "Julia Child" of authentic Sichuan cooking!

Anthony Bourdain, on the infamous heat level of Sichuan’s food: "It’s the spicy, sensualist heartland of all the things I love about China… food that can burn you down to a charred, smoking little stump."
Bourdain, on Lazi Ji, a Sichuan spicy chicken specialty: "The dish, in practical terms, is a game of finding the bits of chicken in the mountain of butt-burning goodness. C’mon, it’s fun for the whole family."

Yes, Anthony Bourdain was right. This is a plateful of butt-burning goodness. Plus, my scalp is still tingling.
Do not skimp on the Sichuan peppercorns.

Made this as directed.  Very tasty.  Reminds me of Chongqing Chicken I had in China.

Not sure it's worth the effort to bone skin-on thighs and then cut 3/4" chunks.  Much of the skin was lost when cubing the chicken.  As for prep time it doesn't include boning the chicken and the 5 min allotted can't include cutting and seeding 2 C of chiles.  I spent over an hour on the chicken and chiles.  I'm not sure how this feeds four.  Two of us polished it off with rice.

Fantastic recipe. 
I also follow an old recipe from a fabulous cookbook. It’s the 1974 The Good Food of Szechwan by Robert A. Delfs. He calls it Ma-La Zi Ji. 
Always love Ligaya’s articles and recipes. Thank you.

This was great, and will hold me on my Szechuan food fix when I don’t want to make a trip to my local restaurant. In the future, I will use skinless thighs as the skin didn’t really add anything for me, and will use much less oil to double cook the chicken. I added some chopped cilantro at the end as well as ground Szechuan peppercorn. This also would have been good with adding some chopped roasted peanuts, but was delicious as is.
Question: why remove the chicken and then return it to the wok?
It's a way to remove some of the fat and water from the chicken, turning it into much tastier morsels with a wonderfully delicate toothsomeness. Try it, you'll like it, as the saying goes.
We made this or dry-fried chicken from Fucshia Dunlop recipes pretty much every Saturday night during the Covid lockdown.  Luscious!
My favorite at our local Chengdu Taste (Providence). Once I start thinking about it I obsess until I go and get it. I’m thrilled to see that I can now make my own!
I don’t buy many cookbooks these days, but I have all of Fuchsia’s.  And whaddaya mean you’re not supposed to eat the chiles?  That’s half the fun!
Add some chopped roasted peanuts

Over an hour to cut the chicken and seed the chiles. (if you don't seed the chiles, the heat level goes way up). Dish was OK, not great. Had to eat half of it before getting any effects from the peppers. Watch the timing on the chicken- if the pieces are smaller, don't fry as long. Tasted like what I've had in Szechuan restaurants- and I did not really like it there either.

A question from a newbie cook. Step 4 says to remove the scallion and ginger from the marinade… and then put the chicken in the pan. Do I pour the chicken into the pan along with the marinade or should I be transferring it with something like a slotted spoon?

It's by design wasteful of chiles so you might use only a cup or so, and leave some seeds in if you would like it spicier.  I tried chicken tenders, which are probably too dry for this dish. Dunlop is well known for her Szechuan compilations, but we've gotten more mileage out of her Hunan cookbook.
Curious: Why keep the skin on the thighs? It seems easier to buy boneless skinless meat!
This recipe was inedible. Shame on you for printing it.we live in Texas & are very familiar with spicy  this  was beyond spicy. Horrible
Needs a lot more bean sauce. Chicken should probably be cooked at higher heat for shorter time to keep it from drying out.

stats.Cook Time: 10 minutes, plus 3 hours’ cooling and chilling

comments.commentBody: No gelatin in Mehallabiya! Its an authentic arabian pudding. In Kuwait, they flavor it with orange blossom water. In Egypt, with vanilla or rose water. In Lebanon, rose water and sometimes mastic. Every Arab country have their own version of Mehallabiya. Kuwaitis call it “Emhalbiya”, Egyptians “Mehallabiya”, in Lebanon and Syria “Mhalbiyyee”. If you have access to buffalo milk, use it here! It takes it to another level. If you have some clotted cream; mix a TS when u turn off the heat.

Those who ask about other uses for Mastic, let me tell you about some delicious ways of incorporating it into ur dishes, the arabs way! 1) before cooking ur rice, melt 3 to 5 mastic pearls with some oil and butter or ghee, then add ur rice and liquid, the result will be heavenly! 2) add a few pearls to ur chicken/meat stock pot and thank me later! 3) melt a pearl or two with some fats before searing ur meats. (Dont be generous with mastic, it’ll impart a sour taste if used more than needed)

This is, in fact, a beloved Palestinian dessert and that’s not a political statement. It’s a recipe. Happy to see a recipe from Chef Kattan. We make this a bit different in Lebanon with rose water but there’s natural variations in the recipe depending where in the Middle East you are. Great to see everyone’s contributions below and I’m gonna try them out!

4) Melt some mastic with butter and pour the mixture on ur risotto during the last moments of cooking, mamma mia! We, arabs, have a thing for mastic. Mom has a drawer full of top quality Saffron, cardamom and Mastic in her bedroom. Our family call her The Kitchen Queen for her over the top dishes.

Mehalabi was a favorite street-cart treat in Limassol as an eleven-year old visiting my father’s birthplace of Cyprus for the first time.   Families in the villages each had their own way of preparing it—with milk or water, or with or without nuts. Sometimes cut into cubes, or sometimes as a firmer pudding spooned out of a small individual cup portion. The new and unique taste of rose water splashed over a cool and refreshing mixture was lovely!
So delicious!  I grew up eating this made by my Palestinian grandmother.  It came out exactly as I remember it.  She also used Mastic to flavor ice cream.  Delicious!

This is a  luscious dish but if you can't get mastic there's another way to make it (or a near-identical twin) without mastic. It's called malabi.  Search.  Recipe is  easy to find and make. Chinese cuisine has something very similar as well.  Almond pudding or almond float.

I was skeptical that a tree resin grown only on the southern coast of a single island in the Aegean could be an affordable mass-market ingredient (still dubious that it's terribly sustainable, really), so I ran the numbers:

The most popular source for mastic tears on Amazon right now is 20g for $15, or $0.75/g.  Powdered ingredients seem to clock in at around 4g/tsp, so one recipe's worth of mastic is going to cost about $3. Make of that what you will.
I had a version of this once, years ago, made by a Lebanese grandmother. I have never forgotten it and have been seeking it ever since. THANK YOU!!!
My Syrian grandmother (by marriage) used to make a version of this with Junket, sprinkling chopped pistachios on top.

Whole Foods in NC used to carry a beverage called "Mastiqua," which was flavored with Mastic. I loved it. I'm happy to see there's an easy powdered form to work with. I didn't have good luck with the previous version I was able to find. I'm looking forward to making this recipe!

A disaster. The pudding never jelled, but the gummy mastic instantly clung to the inside of my kettle as soon as I turned up the heat, and stirring only splashed it onto my cooktop, where it congealed and has been very difficult to remove. Even after cooling, the pudding never thickened. I just had to throw it all out. Or POUR it all out, actually, down the drain of my kitchen sink. 

It took me more time to clean out my pots and clean up my kitchen than it did too make the recipe.

NEVER AGAIN!
On my phone so can’t answer under comments, but thank you for the ideas, Essa and Judy! I’ll definitely try some of those. The risotto sounds amazing
Anyone know how to use a saffron/ gum mastic jam I bought I picked up in Turkey last year? It’s a gorgeous yellow color, very stiff, and sticky. Thx
Can I use Gelatin?
Has anyone tried making it with almond milk?
This published recipe says either use whole milk or almond milk.
Just ordered mastic from Curio Spices. Any suggestions on other things I can make with it?
I use mastic in maqluba - a Palestinian chicken & rice dish (that’s an oversimplification..). It is heavenly.

I grew up eating mahalepi as we call it in Cyprus. It's flavored with rosewater and served, when it solidifies, in a bath of ice cold water sometimes flavored with rose syrup. One of my summer favorite treats when I visit Cyprus. Thank you for the recipe.
This is traditionally (I.e. ALWAYS) made with rose water, and I have no idea why anyone would leave it out!  It’s not even the same dessert without it!
I could barely perceive the taste of the mastic. Is that how it should be? Wondering if I have inferior or old powder.

How very interesting that NYT comes out with a mastic-based recipe. I've been using mastic for gastro issues for years and it does wonders. Never tried cooking with it - I wonder if it offers the same properties.
I have a jar of mastiha, which has a thick honey consistency.  Could I use that as a substitute for the crystallized mastic?

I've had a desert made with mastic and it was not for me: can't stand the taste. However I have been wanting to make this milk desert since eating it years ago at a Azmi's place in the Village.  What else, besides rose water can I use for flavor and how much?
Another version of this is called Sea Moss Pudding.  It is an old Maine recipe.  Our mothers always had a jar of dried white sea moss in the kitchen.

The Salvadoran quesadilla I remember from my childhood in the country was always made with rice flour, fresh farmer's cheese, mantequilla (crème fraîche) that had been aged in a clay pot (batidor) for several days to develop a delicious nuttiness, plus the eggs, the sugar and sesame seeds.
The rice flour gives the quesadilla a unique and delicious crumbly graininess.
I made this with rice flour for my GF daughter and everybody absolutely loved it!
@Anonymous was it a like for like swap?
Perfect! As a Salvadoran expatriate who has never made this, it brought me back through all the crevices in my temporal lobe!

Made this yesterday and followed the recipe exactly (used parm cheese where noted). It's absolutely delicious and came out perfect. Mine only took 40 minutes to bake, so check it early. Highly recommend - I will be making this again!

This is awesome! Made it exactly as written with the Parm and crème fraiche. I enjoyed it as is with a cup of tea, but am thinking that it would make a great breakfast sandwich with an egg, a bit of Mayo and perhaps a thin spread of pesto (yeah, I know - not very Salvadoran).  The only thing I may do next time is omit the salt - I don’t think it’s necessary given the 100 gm of Parmesan.
Definitely a keeper!
From the description, I was Looking forward to a gluten free cake with three interesting flours.  Wish you had given us the original ingredients list and not just the Salvadoran cheese and cream.
Made exactly as written with parmesan and creme fraiche. It was incredibly delicious, light and tangy and a little sweet. This is a keeper.
Do you think masa harina could be used 1:1?
The photo certainly looks as if cornflour was used. I look forward to the answer to this question.

I made this last night, and it's lovely.  Used parmesan and sour cream, as that's what I had on hand, but followed the recipe exactly otherwise. I think it gets its color from the eggs, as I used AP flour and the cake was still a very pretty yellow. This is going to be my granddaughters' favorite after school snack.

For me this is too sweet. I made it today using queso duro, creme fraiche, and rice flour and it's just ... too sweet. I want it mildly sweet and smack-you-in-the-face funky / salty. I'll go search for something more like that.

When my son was in the Peace Corps in El Salvador my husband and I visited him. The grandmother of one of his friends grabbed my husband by the hand to show him in the kitchen how to make what I now know is this dish. It was delicious.  Cannot wait to make it. Sadly we could not take it home on the plane.
I hope it lives up to your memories of it!
I'm assuming the entire stick of melted butter goes in before the egg whites?
Yes - it's part of Step 2: "Gradually add the melted butter to the queso mixture and mix until fully incorporated."
To use rice flour, what would be the substitution?  1:1?
I kept waiting for my cake tester to come out clean and ignored my better judgment of watching the edges get too brown. Don't make my mistake! So mine was overbaked, but still delicious!

I made this with the spices as in the recipe, including the hoisin sauce, but per another recipe, pulsed the jackfruit in the food processor just a bit to get a great texture.  Served with soft tacos, small, but again another change - shredded the carrots and mixed with lime juice, a bit of honey and a bit of red pepper flakes, per another web recipe. Used cuke as in the recipe. Topped with some mexican queso fresco, and my honey had no idea this was not meat!

Made mostly as directed and paired with Mark Bittman's Almost-From-Scratch Corn Tortillas. Teenager approved! I only used 1 can of jackfruit and a medium orange but kept the other portions the same. It was very good and I will make it again. The jackfruit broke up really well. It was my first time using it and it was easy. It fed 3 of us with a bit left over.

I’m looking forward to making this.  My husband is Indonesian and jack fruit is used in so many different ways there.  I’ve also tried it as a substitute in salad.  such a versatile plant-based alternative!
Adding beans or tofu and maybe avocado would make it better. Needs something to cut the acidity.

Jackfruit is an acquired taste I think. I've tried it in tacos and didn't care for it. The law of seven might apply here: try it seven times before rejecting it completely. When my kids were young this is how I introduced them to new flavors. After 7 tries I shelved it
I mix jackfruit with tofu in any dish to get the protein and the nice textures.

Asian markets (at least in Berlin) have plenty of fresh (huge whole ones, or in pieces) of jackfruit, and they also sell canned young jackfruit. Some of the ordinary grocery stores also have the cans. We learned recently that the fresh ones are challenging to "fillet" for eating and super sticky! Looking forward to trying this out with the canned variety.
Tastes pretty good but doesn’t look anything like the photo. Not sure how they got the nice dark brown color on the jackfruit, mine came out a medium beige.

I'm so happy to see this recipe. I will make it once I get all ingredients. Would love to see more recipes that include Jackfruit. I was introduced to Jackfruit at the Indian restaurant we go to in Ocala.(Flame) It is a superb dish and I order this dish every time we go there.

Just keep in mind that jackfruit is not a source of protein. Cans of 14-15 ounces of jackfruit contain between 0 and 5 grams total, so this recipe would provide between 0 and 2.5 grams of protein per serving, a negligible amount.

For some people, this is a huge benefit.  My grandson cannot eat more than 4 grams of protein per day because of a genetic condition (PKU), and jackfruit is a fantastic resource for him.  It can be adapted into "tuna" and "egg" salad and replaces meat in many soups and other recipes.  In these protein obsessed times, everything seems to have protein crammed into it, so for us no protein is a bonus.  There is a silver lining to every cloud, I guess - in his world, low protein jackfruit tacos rule
They don't seem to have it at the bodega
Yummmm looks good! Can't wait for the comments from the esteemed chefs here and follow their helpful suggestions!

I was so excited to read this recipe because I have an exceedingly abundant jackfruit tree and have yet to figure out how to make the most of the fruit. I have never heard of canned jackfruit. I’m hoping one day someone will teach me how to process the jackfruit in a palatable way.
I’d add in tofu crumbles w the canned  young jackfruit. Just be sure you are not getting sweetened jackfruit. You can find it at most supermarkets these days. Or Chinese grocers.
I didn’t love how fruity this recipe was but that could be because I used the white interior of a ripe jackfruit. I’ll try this again with canned jackfruit and that may be a better fit

I made these as specified with the slightest variations. Swapped out red onion for green , just as a personal preference. And I cut the oil in half - using just 2 tbsp. It was totally sufI used naan vs tortilla and thought these came out delicious

These jackfruit tacos were great and very easy to make. I used organic canned jackfruit (sold at Whole Foods). I used half of the instructed oil and the dish was excellent. I don’t think the extra oil is necessary. The combo of citrus and all of the seasonings made the jackfruit tangy and flavorful. I served mine in naan vs tortilla. I subbed out red onion for green onion just as a personal preference . Will make again and again for a quick , flavorful vegetarian meal.
In Jamaica and in NYC, we make this with salted mackerel and coconut cream not milk. Not sure who created this recipe, but please make it both ways and decide which one is better.

One of the main points of this dish seems to be the fish. With that said, my interest was piqued by the seasonings and I made a vegan version with smoked tofu and some seaweed salt instead of the smoked makerel. The rundown sauce is delicious, now to look for other ital rundown recipes.
Does this call for true yams or yellow sweet potatoes?
Actual yams, not sweet potatoes. Jamaicans differentiate between two.
This calls for true yellow yam.

Run-down is one of my favorite dishes from home. I haven’t made it this way but am willing to try. It’s also good with fresh mackerel. If you’re using salted mackerel be sure to boil off some of the salt.
Very good! I ended up making it with Japanese (white) yams, less than the amount of mackerel called for (4 instead of 6 filets), and no bay leaves, and it still came out very tasty.

I couldn't find smoked, so I used fresh, farm-raised sturgeon seasoned with a little salt. I think any fresh, oily fish would be great. I also added a sliced jalapeño with the other vegetables which cut through the richness of the sauce nicely. Love this recipe, thank you!
Loved the dish, but wasn’t a big fan of the canned smoked mackerel.   Are there other acceptable fish substitutes, or even chicken thighs?  The sauce was fantastic.
Green bananas, as in very unripe bananas or plantains?

Pimento is allspice?

Or is pimenta Jamaican alspice?

from Wikipedia:  Allspice, also known as Jamaica pepper, myrtle pepper, pimenta, or pimento, is the dried unripe berry of Pimenta dioica, a midcanopy tree native to the Greater Antilles, southern Mexico, and Central America, now cultivated in many warm parts of the world.

Here's what I found:
Is Jamaican pimento the same as allspice? Basically, yes.
Allspice, also known as Jamaica pepper, myrtle pepper, pimenta, or pimento, is the dried unripe berry of Pimenta dioica, a midcanopy tree native to the Greater Antilles, southern Mexico, and Central America, now cultivated in many warm parts of the world.

We loved this!  The flavorful aroma made this a winner.   Substituted sweet potato for the yellow yam and used lite coconut milk to reduce the fat and calories a bit.  I was skeptical of the canned mackerel in the recipe, but it worked well against the delicious sauce.  Sardines would probably be fine here, too.  Will definitely make this again (and again!)
Fantastic - used smoked tinned mackerel, plantains, and followed the recipe.  The pumpkin cooked faster than 15 min.  Highly recommend.
what fish would be best to substitute?  we can't get mackerel here.
Used 4 cans of Trader Joe’s smoked trout and a scotch bonnet pepper. Added salt to taste. Delicious!

title: Ricotta Toast With Roasted Grapes Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: I avoid fish sauce as I and others have severe reactions to it and it leaves a taste no one in my family likes.  I just leave it out and recipes work just fine without it. just the name “fish sauce” is unappealing.
I used a commercial fig balsamic glaze, and the result was pretty spectacular.
@Dena agree, the balsamic glaze was exceptionally and one less step / dirty dish. The recipe version also leaves some room for error as fish sauce and vinegar can easily be messed up portion wise.
Don’t forget mint on your grocery list. It’s suggested  in the recipe tip but not noted as an ingredient.

I too am "allergic" to Balsamic vinegar; indeed all vinegar except ACV. 

It's called Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS). Also known as Histamine Intolerance. Has to do with the fermentation vs the grapes themselves. Fresh grapes are fine; but not fermented, processed or aged *anything*. It's a boring diet, requires lotsa subs.
 
Here I'd use ACV, fresh grapes; Sourdough vs usual yeasted bread, tho not all can tolerate it. Lemons are N/A, too. Sigh

It's v hard. Have patience with us, please!

@RudisHuman my friend was recently diagnosed with MCAS so thank you for the advice on potential substitutions. I’ll run them by her and see if she can tolerate them. I know that everyday is a an adventure when it comes to food.

I was cooking for one, so limited myself to 4 small pieces of toast. But when they were gone, my spoon kept going. Incredible flavor combo. It doesn’t stay pretty like the photo but…who cares? PS the mint takes it to an A
This is tasty, but way too much balsamic. Cut it by 75%. More than a couple of ounces will turn this into a balsamic soup.
Instead of ricotta use small curd cottage cheese. Very delicious.
Homemade ricotta is easy and would take this up a notch.

I also cut down the amount of the glaze, there's too much. I have just put the grapes straight onto toast, on top of the spread and it's a great summer dinner. I've made it once with the ricotta and once with large curd cottage cheese and I think I prefer the cottage cheese. It makes it a bit lighter.

The quality of the ingredients here really matters! I made this twice - once with organic seasonal grapes from a local coop and artisan ricotta - it was phenomenal and received rave reviews from my dinner party. The second time I made it with Trader Joe’s grapes and ricotta - the grapes were not juicy enough to create a saucy consistency and the ricotta was too runny, resulting in more of a puddle than a dip.
For all the people that can’t have balsamic vinegar- try grape molasses as a substitute. You can find it in middle eastern grocery stores or online.

Followed the recipe. Found the whipped ricotta too bland so added diced preserved lemon peel and fresh thyme.  Fortunately for me at least , tried the glaze prior to adding it to the dish. Tossed it - too funky a flavour and I like funky flavours as a rule. Substituted a drizzle of honey and plain balsamic glaze.  Was a perfect starter and will make again minus the suggested glaze.
Is it okay if the glaze and grapes are room temperature? Thinking about making in advance and assembling at the last minute

This is very tasty, but the recipe for the Balsamic glaze makes way too much. Will cut in half next time. I used a combo of fig/balsamic and straight balsamic and would recommend that. I switched out Burrata for the Ricotta because who doesn't love Burrata? Used small toasted baguette rounds to serve as an Easter Appetizer.  A hit.
Substitute Worcestershire sauce for fish sauce.

My husband threatened divorce if I made this again - the smell of fish oil and vinegar just got to him. But the finished sauce was neither fishy nor vinegary - just an interesting umami flavor. Still, my husband preferred just the honey as a sauce with the roasted grapes and ricotta.
Poor him.  Actually, I guess poor you. In my house, people not cooking are not invited to criticize, much less threaten.
Loved the flavors but the ratios are off - too much ricotta and balsamic and not enough grapes. An easy, sophisticated, eye-catching and tasty dish.

I made this as indicated.   Loved it and will make again as an elegant first course.   I suggest serving the glaze on the side (or drizzle just enough to give color) - you really don’t need all of it and it turns the ricotta into soup.   

I didn’t include the mint - but will next time!
Wayyyy too much glaze - wasted a ton of balsamic on this, unfortunately.  Would cut by 75%. Flavors were certainly good, but I would want more grapes and less vinegar.  Otherwise, yum.
thoughts on substitute for fish sauce?

Made this with Concord grapes and it was absolutely incredible. Tried again with regular black grapes and it was good but not as amazing. Next time I see Concord grapes I’ll make this for everyone I love to savour with me!
This is really tasty and easy.  Making the glaze yourself is the way to go, too.  If you’re allergic to balsamic, honey is great.  Go to a legit cheese shop and spring for ricotta.  Totally worth it.
I have read this through a couple of times and I don't see an indication of grape variety, other than seedless. Should I assume seedless white, red, or purple would all work fine here?

Yes, it's the roasting of them that will mainly determine their flavor.  Maybe be aware though that some varieties of red grapes have tougher, earthier skins and decide whether or not that is okay for your dish.  For this recipe I would be happy with that.
I made this tonight and it was super easy and yummy.  I have an uncle who is allergic to fish so I am going to experiment with soy sauce and the mushroom powder.
Is this an appetizer, an hors d’oeuvres or a dessert.? It reminds me a bit of the Greek yogurt dessert with the black cherry, yogurt and walnuts.
Wow, this is fantastic.  I skipped making the glaze, just dribbled a good quality balsamic instead.
pretty good.  makes more like 5 servings Grapes took longer than I had expected, but everyone liked the result!

3/4 c balsamic seems like much too much. I think it all depends on the balsamic vinegar you use, especially the age. It's a shame to waste something so precious. I skip the fish sauce too, not sure what it adds.
It was quite bland - not a great success here unfortunately.
Good but not inspired. This felt more like an amalgamation of ingredients than a composed dish.

I am thinking about making this dish and having it out for people to snack on before the main dinner. Would this dish be fine room temperature or is it only worth having if it's eaten while the grapes and toast are still warm?
How about adding a tip or something suggesting how to find or make the mushroom seasoning?  Something beyond just naming the ingredients would be super.
I'm allergic to balsamic vinegar. Any satisfactory substitute for this recipe?
Maybe a reduced raspberry vinegar?

@Sue F, (real) balsamic vinegar is made from grapes. Nothing else. This recipe calls for grapes. If you are truly allergic to balsamic vinegar, this dish is not a good idea for you. 

There is an Italian pine syrup called Mugolio that is similar to balsamic syrup in effect, if not in taste. You could follow this general recipe, but skip the reduction part, and use something like cherry tomatoes instead of grapes. Tomatoes would add the umami element. Drizzle the toast with Mugolio.
The only thing in balsamic vinegar is grapes. So, if you are allergic to balsamic vinegar, it is probably better that you avoid this dish entirely.
Yes, folks:  seedless grapes all taste remarkably like grapes, regardless of color.

There are variations in grapes; it's called "variety."  Maybe you were referring to the fact that a lot of fruits and vegetables in our grocery stores have been paired down to those that ship and store well, but I doubt that is what you were doing.
Worcestershire also has fish in it, so if you’re trying to avoid fish, that won’t work. Any other suggestions?
Maybe vegan Worcestershire sauce. I'm a vegetarian so I use a brand called Suzie's, it tastes just like the real thing and works great for French onion soup, chex mix, etc.
Vegan fish sauce

Thanks to all re my balsamic vinegar problem. I can eat any kind of fresh grapes and all other usual kinds of vinegar. So, I’ll try suggested honey and/or reduced raspberry vinegar or maybe honey   cider vinegar.
Served with gluten free rain forest crackers—bread seemed like it would be too much.
You need only half that much balsamic glaze and should reduce it more so it doesn't run off; grapes best if roasted a little less; consider smoked salt for ricotta; add shredded mint leaves on top.
I don't see mint listed anywhere...???
Assuming these 8oz measurements are weight not volume?

OMG this is soooo good! Forgot to add the balsamic glaze at the end and it was perfectly delicious without it. I think it would overwhelm to be honest. And YES to torn mint leaves at the end right before serving! My friends and I felt like we could eat a bowl of this as a meal!!!
Like this alot. Use more grapes. I whipped cottage cheese instead of ricotta - it was really good
The whipped ricotta and the grapes were inspired. I served this with spiced apples as well, and it was a great addition.

So you should consider: make this, but put the roasted grapes in a bowl, not on top of the ricotta. Also make Sarah diGregorio's Slow-Cooker Tomato Compote. If you really want to go all NYT but still go easy on yourself, also make the Jim Lahy / Mark Bittman Speedy No-Knead Bread (I know, I know; there are multiple versions and various reasons why each one is better than the other...). Or buy a baguette. At any rate, your friends will be extremely grateful that you have a subscription to NYT.
The balsamic glaze overpowers the dish. Next time I would swirl some good olive oil and flaky salt at the end. My grapes needed much more time in the oven.

Wonderful flavors. My grapes were very large and took 30 minutes (tripled the quantity of grapes but not anything else). Based on reviews saying the ricotta was bland, I used half chili flavored olive oil. Was glad I didn’t do more. But the little bit of heat played nicely against the sweet grapes. I would serve it next time on something more crunchy than toast — either crackers or crostini.
least favorite appetizer of the evening... not sure why

I made these grapes and another batch that were tossed with 1 tablespoon of butter and 2 tablespoons of honey; the honey grapes were much tastier. I'll serve them as a side dish; I agree with the other comments that the ricotta is bland, plus it's a bit sloppy as an appetizer since the grapes are still a little juicy.

I used red and green grapes for color.  I didn't have thyme, so I just roasted them with oil and little red wine.  I topped them off with tarragon.  I made a balsamic glaze -- like others, I don't really like fish sauce.

Yum. Unusual and delicious. Had a pound of grapes so used them all. Also had a seriously creamy Italian ricotta so did not whip. It was fine as is/was. The glaze is blow-your-socks-off. A little goes a long way. Served on a slice of toasted sourdough. Will def make again.
Very good.  Cut the glaze by 2/3rds as per others' comments and it was more than sufficient.
We always add a tablespoon of Grand Marnier, Kahlua, bourbon, or whatever happens to be in the liquor cabinet, except when we make it for the grandchildren.
The Swiss Cookbook had a memorable chocolate fondue made from cream and Toblerone bars.
Without the cookbook and without the recipe, this doesn't tell me much. How is cream and a Toblerone bar different from the cream and chocolate specified in this recipe? Chocaholics want to know!

I wonder if the place Ligaya Mishan had the chocolate fondue was La Fondue in mid-town. It  delighted me as a kid in the 80's and saved my sanity a few times in the 90's when my gen X life was so awful that only a bowl of cheese and/or chocolate shared with too many friends squeezed around a little table would do.
Only a handful of comments here but they did not disappoint! any recommendations or thoughts on type of chocolate?

I used dark chocolate (85% minimum cocoa).  A splash of rum for depth, a healthy few dashes of cinnamon to bring warmth, and some honey to balance the slight but wonderful bitterness from the dark chocolate. Don't forget the salt to sharpen the flavors!

I used GHIRARDELLI 60% Cacao Bittersweet Chocolate Premium Baking Chips and was happy with the results. I have previously used Jacque's House 70% Dark Chocolate Baking Discs, which was good also. I like to add a splash (or two) of Chambord. Shortbread cookies are my favorite delivery vehicle.

@Peter
Where sis you find 85% chocolate?

We made this for a group dinner and the results were wonderful! We paired with strawberries, pineapple, clementine slices, marshmallows, & pound cake pieces. It was a great use of our fondue pot we would love to use more! Will do this for future dinners as our kids especially LOVED it!!
can I heat up the cream in a microwave?
You need hot cream. Heat it however is convenient for you.
Double the recipe. Easy and delicious
This is just basically ganache.
Used a bag of semi sweet, used 1/4 cup of Baileys and 1/2 cup of cream- fantastic!

This works as an elevated camping dessert!  Just put the ingredients into a small metal bowl or pot and set it over the grill of a campfire that is dying out.  Stir vigorously and dip fruit on skewers.  Also complementary to making s'mores, as you can smear the chocolate on the graham crackers.
It’s a bit bland and cold. The chocolate melts but it’s too thick and needs more time to heat up. Disappointing.
Add 1/8 cup honey.

title: Hamachi Sashimi With Ginger Ponzu Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: We had this dish at Tiffany's in Maui few weeks before Christmas...it was fabulous and everything about the restaurant was memorable and nostalgic.  I will be making this but just wanted to say thanks for keeping Tiffany's alive and well while reinventing it's menu.

Salty, citrusy, and tart but mellowed by the addition of ginger and onion.   I was afraid it would be too assertive but it wasn't, and indeed I enjoyed splashing it on my rice even without the fish.  Didn't make the lemon oil, instead drizzled the oil on the fish and zested a bit of lemon peel over it to finish.  Delish.
Is the onion raw or cooked wonton a bit?
Is the onion raw or sweated down a bit?
My reading is its raw. This could be why sweet onion is specified.
Question.  2-3 Shiso leaves are not enough to make the sauce that rich red color, is it?
Sure wish the kombu and bonito flakes were in grams.
Any reason this ponzu sauce shouldn't be made the day before?

title: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

stats.Cook Time: 1½ hours plus 3½ hours’ cooling and chilling

comments.commentBody: This is beyond tasty. The only difficulty was having patience to let it soak before devouring

What a brilliant idea.  I've always liked the concept of tres leches cakes, but in practice they're invariably so supersweet that I find them cloying.  Cutting all that sweetness with the astringency of tea seems like a tremendous idea.

But before investing in the Thai powder, I think I'll experiment by heating the milk and steeping black tea in it.  This might result in an unwelcome color, but I'll risk it.

I made this for my husband’s birthday (he’s a huge fan of all thing tres leches). This cake blew his socks off! I used ChaTraMue brand tea; it has a little sugar, but I cut the added sugar by 1/3 for baked good recipes, so it didn’t much matter. This cake is a fair amount of process, but worth it. Since it was my first go at making it, aside from cutting sugar, I limited deviation. To the dry mix, added a pinch of cinnamon, cardamom, mace, 1/2 tsp vanilla bean, and 1/2tsp salt to punch it up.
Sacrilege I know, but can anyone recommend a “supermarket” style cake, or box cake mix that could substitute?
@Christine I have done tres letches with a Pepperidge Farm pound cake and it’s amazing! No haters please
@Carolyn No haters whatsoever but thank you for the idea! Sara Lee makes a lovely pound cake!

I made this last night for a dinner party, following the Momofoku Bo Ssam. It was a hit! The toasted coconut and lime zest are so necessary and added some freshness from the lime and a nice slight crunch from the coconut. I made it 2 days in advance and flipped the slides over on the second day. It was hard not to keep sipping the tea steeped in the tres leches. Highly recommend!
@kiki I’m totally stealing this party menu idea—yum!

Made with liquid Thai tea from Whole Foods and reduced the condensed milk to compensate for the extra liquid. Worked like a charm! 

Soaked for 2 days, came out delicious
The Wangderm Thai tea contains artificial color. Is there another similar product without this?
https://rishi-tea.com/products/sweet-thai-iced-tea-powder?variant=44467233947898 This looks nice, though I have not tried it.

Most, if not all, commercial Thai tea mixes have added food coloring to render the bright orange hue. I would suggest using black tea to steep, just in larger quantity than used for making regular tea. I would suggest buying from Indian grocery stores, which sell black tea loose tea powder/leaves.
@Julia I was excited to try, but this powder has no spices in it. It’s tea, sugar, gardenia powder (??), beet powder. That’s it. No star anise, cinnamon, etc.
I used 12 teabags instead of the 42g instant powder. Half in the milk mixture and half in 12oz of boiling water which I added to the mixture after steeping. The flavor is the right strength.

So, funny story about this.  I misread this as Chai Tres Leches cake, but then thought, what a great idea!  So we omitted the Thai part and steeped 3 Thai teabags in evaporated milk overnight.  Absolutely amazing!!!  So yummy
@Willi this is maybe the best typo ever. Also now I want chai tres leches cake.

@Willi

Bwahaha! I thought I read the exact same thing and was going to use chai tea. And now I will! Great minds think alike😂

I love this recipe! I would definitely recommend adding 0.6 cups of Thai tea powder but it turned out amazing with only 0.5 cups. I would definitely recommend using this recipe 5/5 stars.

Ps. Make it late at night so it soaks while you sleep if you are impatient.
Maybe you could create a recipe that calls or either a full can of evaportated/condensed milk. I had too much evaporated milk left over.

I tried making this recipe's cake from scratch but found it challenging, time-consuming, and not that good. Instead, I started using yellow/white box cake. The box recipe usually calls for water, but substitute it with whole milk. I also whisk 1/2-1 tsp of cardamom powder into the box cake dry mix. I follow NYT's recipe for the Thai tea milk mix, and I soak the box cake with it. I've made this cake 2-3 times this way, and it's always gone in minutes. People are always shocked its box cake.

I love Thai tea but dislike tres leches cake. I don’t like the texture, which is pretty much what that cake is all about. For those who dig it, this sounds like such a great riff and it suggests more variation. What about adding ground freeze dried strawberry powder? Or coffee or chocolate?) If I end up making it, I’ll report back.
To DD, the whole point of a Tres Leches cake (or any "poke cake" for that matter) is to change the texture of the cake. Why not make it the way it's designed to be made? It's wonderful.

Subbed out the Thai tea powder for 1/3 C matcha powder. It turned out beautifully - light yet decadent, with the tea flavor cutting through the sweetness. For the tres leches, I loosely followed the proportions based on the can sizes- then filled out with whole milk. Great holiday dessert for a crowd!

The sponge for this cake came out absolutely beautifully!  Like this is the most beautiful cake I have ever seen!

For the tres leches, I ended up using 3.5 cups of milk with the 1.5 cups of evaporated milk because I could only find cans in 12 oz portions, but I didn’t notice any obstacles to the process.  It may seem like a lot of liquid at first, but this cake can take it.

I made this exactly as the recipe states (with an 3-day soaking time) and it was mind-bogglingly good. Lighter than I expected, too! The flavors are complex and layer deliciously, and every component could’ve stood on its own. A perfectly airy and sweet chiffon cake; thai tea-flavored milk that I could’ve drunk by the gallon; and the whipped cream was luscious, with notes of tang from the sour cream. And of course, the lime zest and coconut flakes are a must, both for flavor and texture.
10/10, no notes. Loved it. I’m convinced that the people who have criticism for it either don’t like Thai tea or didn’t do the steps properly.
It's good, but needs salt. I'd add around 3/4 tsp per cake, maybe 1/2 tsp in the cake and 1/4 in the soaking liquid?

I don't think you could substitute a cake mix. I haven't tried this recipe yet,  but I've made tres leches several times,  and it's not a typical cake.  It's usually kind of spongy to soak up the milk. A regular butter cake would probably fall apart when soaked.
I wonder if it’s possible to use matcha powder instead of the Thai tea?
Had the same idea! Subbed in with 1/3C matcha powder and it turned out great

To Christine- a sponge cake is used as the base because it holds up to all the soaking. I think most box cakes are a simple "butter" cake and would have an unpleasant texture. If it helps, the cake came together pretty fast.

10s 10s 10s all across the board.

The cake recipe is quite stiff to accommodate all the milk tea and it holds up so well. I added lime zest to the whipped cream for double citrus vibes. Cannot recommend enough!

It is against my religion to soak cakes in anything that changes the texture,😱😁 My daughter wanted me to make this. I love Thai ice tea, one I indulge in very rarely. As a compromise, I made the Thai Tres leches about 2 cups and used a slice or two of regular cake to indulge her . Leftover tea diluted with water and ice made me an indulgent Thai ice tea. 
If anyone else feels the same way about soaking a big batch of lovely cake, here is another option.

I really wanted to like this cake but could not give it more than 3 stars. I wanted the Thai tea taste to come through more strongly - maybe it is quite important to soak for two nights. I did just one night, followed the recipe carefully. Honestly the toasted coconut was the most interesting thing about this cake. Pretty color though!

Amazing flavor with two big notes. 

1. Use tea leaves, not tea powder. It will get the same effect and is easier to strain.
2. The sponge cake is too dense. I followed the recipe to a Tea. I poked all kinds of holes and let it sit 24 hours before serving and only about half of the milk mixture absorbed.
How can you flip it without it spilling all over the place! (How to contain the juices upside down?)
I let the cake sit for only 8 hours and the milk mix had only made it half way down. Next time going for the full two days. Also, I am now obsessed with this whipped cream variant.

Great recipe, my family loved it. Interesting twist on the classic and I actually prefer it!

I added the lime zest to the whipped cream topping which added a nice flavor.

Turned out wonderful, but the sponge could be lighter. Will consider replacing the AP flour with cake flour. Also, it needs at least three days of soaking. After two days, the Thai tea flavor (used Cha Tra Mue Gold Thai tea mix) was quite strong (not in a good way) and a bit sour, but it mellowed on the third day.

Great cake! Things I would do differently: reduce the whole milk to 2 cups, and let it soak overnight instead of the 6 hours I did. There were some spots of cake that were white but that was most likely due to me not getting that spot pierced with the fork.

Made this exactly as described (except used a Thai tea powder with sugar) and it was absolutely spectacular. If you're thinking about it, MAKE IT. Worth the effort and time. And let it sit for at least 24 hours!

This was really good and so fun for the White Lotus finale :) The cake did have to bake a little longer, like 10 minutes, and it was still a little underdone in the middle (next time I would wait until there was NO jiggle even if it gets a little brown) but still delish. I couldn’t find Thai tea leaves so used instant powder and decreased the condensed milk by half a cup because the powder is sweet and it worked great. Just kept adding packets until it tasted like tea (probably drank a good amount of milk tasting it lol) - I think it was about 3/4ths cup powder. I agree the lime zest and coconut is ESSENTIAL - do not skip the lime, this is very sweet. I would even want to maybe find a way to either make the cake or the milk mixture a little lime-y - will try some zest in the cake next time!

Huge hit in our house. I flipped the cake at the end of its first full day, then let it soak for another. Don't skip the coconut or lime, they elevate this tremendously. The flavour is a bit too one-note without them.

Very delicious and not extremely sweet as opposed to most 3leches recipes, the instruction to cut the cake before soaking is truly genius, my only gripe with the recipe is that it doesn't use full cans of evaporated and condensed milk so you have some leftover that you then need to figure out what to do with but other than that excellent recipe! :)

I made two cakes, one with loose leaf thai tea and one with a blend of loose leaf green thai tea & genmaicha. The green tea one recieved many compliments at my work party! Note that loose black tea does not work as well as the suggested thai tea powder because it needs to boil at a longer & higher tempurature, though it does work. The flipping during soaking is important as otherwise the milk tea may miss some spots in the cake. Otherwise I love this recipe, creative and delicious!

The cake is good but it feels like there is too much baking powder in it. The first batch my friend and I tossed because it had that metallic aftertaste. We made it again with a teaspoon and a half of baking powder and it tasted much better!

This ruled! Made it for a dinner party and it was a huge hit. Kind of obsessed with the sponge recipe—SO light and gorgeaous. 

I got Pantai tea mix without realizing it isn’t a powder, so I added ~70g to the tres leches in order get the strength I wanted. For the flip: I cut mine as 5x3, so I ended up taking out one row and setting it on a cutting board. I flipped the remaining slices one at a time, then flipped and replaced the remaining slices and scraped any remaining liquid back over the cake.  Last note: the measurements vs. can amounts IS perverse, but now I have everything I need for Thai iced tea. 10/10!
DELICIOUS. I reduced the sugar in the whipped topping to balance out the sweet cake.
I used coconut milk, condensed coconut milk and coconut cream for the sauce, and it turned out so good! Also used a third less sugar. It ended up divineeee
This sounds heavenly.  Sadly I can’t tolerate caffeine and can’t find decaf Thai tea powder…any ideas for a substitution?

I'm extremely caffeine sensitive too, so have started researching.  I haven't been able to enjoy a Thai tea in ages, so would love to experience that taste again!  
Checking labels/recipes - the Thai powder seems to be: Assam tea + spices (star anise, cardamom, cinnamon and/or clove) + sometimes vanilla and/or pandan + sugar.  I can tolerate Harney & Sons decaf black teas lightly brewed (great quality + taste for decaf!) and they have a Decaf Assam.  I'm going to try it with that and see.
Any idea how many tea bags of Thai tea would equal the 42g of instant powder?
Use 12 teabags steeped, any kind I want. Half in milk, half steeped

I halved the recipe (I'm the only sweet tooth here), and baked for a few minutes less in an 8x8. Otherwise kept the ratios the same. Since I had leftover sweetened condensed milk, I added that to the whipped cream and skipped the sugar and vanilla. Added some lime zest and salt to the whipped cream, too. Delish!
Had this for my birthday and it was magnificent. So most, so delicious, not too sweet. Loved the sour cream whipped cream frosting— but definitely had too much of it!
Does anyone have issues with the entire cake not turning orange? Any help would be appreciated
I think flipping the cake should help with the color dispersion

I made this exactly as written but won't bother to make it again. I found it to be tasteless - not particularly sweet and the leches didn't add anything to it. The only thing interesting were the golden coconut and the lime zest. I bought Thai tea powder from my local Asian grocery so it was orange (and actually stained a plate!) but, again, meh.
Really great recipe! I made half the recipe and used an 8” pan and had to slightly shorten the baking time.

Made this for a party last night and everyone loved it! I adjusted the recipe a bit because I wanted to avoid having partial cans of evaporated milk & condensed milk cluttering up my fridge. I used one full can of each and made up the deficit by adding an extra cup of whole milk. I don't think I was missing any sweetness from the condensed milk, it was perfect! Would totally make this again.
Delicious and tasty, but it does drive me up the wall that none of the measurements (particularly with evaporated/condensed milk) align with the sizes that things are sold in.

I found this all natural Thai tea brand on Amazon (annatto for coloring & vanilla, no sugar or milk added), for those interested.

Brand: Yim Tea Co.
This looks amazing.  Can someone explain the necessity of cream of tartar when the leavening agent is baking powder and not baking soda?

This turned out amazing. A couple of tips for anyone trying this: I just used one 14 oz can of condensed milk instead of trying to measure it out. Also, it’s much MUCH better if you let it soak overnight. I did 4 hours before digging in and it was good, but the next day it was the best

title: Miyar Taushe (Lamb and Squash Stew) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody:
1. The recipe does say later to use only half the squash. I used the whole squash (a large one) and it was great
2.  I put all ingredients in a slow cooker with the squash quartered and laid on top, open side down. After4 hours I removed the squash, took seeds out and rind off. Put lamb with most of the onions in bowl and returned squash to slow cooker and mashed it with the immersion blender. Returned lamb and onions and it was great!!! and oh so easy

@perry
That actually sounds doable. The other version was rather daunting. May try yours. Thank you.
Every Fall I roast, purée, then freeze in half-cup portions, about 20 pounds of butternut squash. So handy to have on hand for soups, pasta, and recipes like this.

I have a zillion (I'm sure that number is right) spices since we do a lot of cooking with them but I am unfamiliar with calabash nutmeg. Not wanting to order some to use here, I looked up how to substitute for it with a spice blend. Good youtube link here if it helps others. I have all the ingredients noted in it. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_ZDqHX95eGY I will use lamb neck, a cut we love to cook with at our house and fun to see it spec'd in this recipe.
thank you for that great video!

So.....a large butternut squash, of which you're only going to use half?  Maybe I'll roast a one pound bag of pre-cubed and peeled squash, or open a large can of pureed pumpkin instead, and steam the spinach over the simmering soup.
You use the whole pumpkin. The recipe just says to halve it to roast it like that.

The recipe does say to "add half of the blended butternut squash to the simmering stew. (Reserve the remaining squash; cover and refrigerate to eat as a purée or freeze for a future stew.)" I may add a bit more than half to make sure this feeds six.

@Shorty Attentionspan --you're correct.  In Step 7, it says to add half of the blended butternut squash to the stew, and to refrigerate or freeze the other half for future use.  I guess we could add half the spices to half the quantity of squash.

Made this as directed with a cut up leg of lamb. I put a little more butternut squash puree in - it took a little more water than 1/4 cup. Instead of putting the spinach on the side , I just threw it in the soup. The taste was very earthy with a little heat. The pepitas on top is a must-gives a good crunch.

1. I added half a farmer’s market scotch bonnet and fished it out before blending. Plenty spicy.
2. I also used the whole squash. I added 1/4 tsp of regular nutmeg. 
3. Needed quite a bit more salt than called for. Layered it in.
4. I also just threw in the spinach at the very end.
5. Served over rice and topped with some diced tomato in addition to seeds to give it some freshness/acidity.

I wasn’t 100% pleased with the texture of the lamb shank. A bit tough. Maybe more time braising?
agreed, the cartilage in the lamb shank wasn't broken down- I may do a long  (48 hour) sous vide next time
Can I use regular nutmeg?  Has anyone done that?
This was okay the first day, amazing the second. I used lamb shanks and assorted mini peppers, because that's what I had. So good!!
I'm not a big lamb fan. Can I substitute chicken.
No @shorty attentionspan. A whole butternut squash, which has been cut in half in order to roast it. But you do you.

Browning the meat would develop more flavor. 

I wouldn’t bother with the expense (and mild inconvenience) of lamb neck next time.

I was impatient and couldn’t wait for calabash nutmeg. Freshly grated nutmeg, coriander, and cardamom (all toasted, 1:1:1 ratio) was a pleasant substitute.

I think you could just throw all of the veg (squash, tomato, onion, peppers) onto a large sheet pan and roast it all at once. Cuts out a step later on, and let’s you relax more on your stew-day.

I have to smile at the "expense" of lamb neck bones in current times. When my father was out of work in the early 1960s in NYC, my mother would go to the butcher & buy lamb neck bones because that was the absolutely cheapest form of meat protein she could get - normally, they were discarded. She would cover them with sauce and bake them in the oven, for a long time.
Am I the only one who found the end result to be insipid?  Nice lamb and squash flavor, but somehow timid.
I failed to get calabash nutmeg. Is it okay to sub regular?
I found ground calabash nutmeg.  How much do I use?  1 teaspoon?

To my taste this recipe lacked umami so I added a small amount of bouillon which filled out the flavor quite a bit.
Why not suggest a small squash rather than a large one and use only half?

Made this as directed.  I used a few piedes of bone in lamb shoulder, which braised out in the alotted time.  The african nutmed I had to go to YouTube to see how to use it, still not sure I got it right.  I think what I got were just the seeds, but i toasted one, crushed it, toasted a bit more and ground it.  Unfortunately for me, I left the whole habenero in the  the pot , blended it and got a soup/stew a lot spicier/hot than my liking, and I’m not at all averse to quite hot dishes
I’m making this with seitan instead of lamb. Any advice? Should i change any of the ingredients?

title: Chickpeas Escabeche With Plantain Strips Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: This came together quickly, I used Goya sofrito and plantain chips from the store. Everyone, including my 11 year old, gobbled it up! Next time I'll double.
Plantain chips can be found at most Latin American grocery stores.

We made this as an amuse-bouche for a dear friend's 60th birthday dinner party. While we didn't change the recipe for it or the homemade sofrito (they were perfect), we did add some fancy and highly recommended flourishes: replaced canned chickpeas with 1/2 lb dried Rancho Gordo Ceci Piccolo (small chickpeas) simmered up with aromatics, and added seeds from one pomegranate for color, sweetness and crunch. It was next-level. We gave it an "amuse"-ing highbrow-lowbrow name:  "Chickpea Caviar."
We did not enjoy this. Felt like it needed a lot more flavor to make the beans interesting. I kept adding more sofrito and vinegar ….   This one gets filed in the tried that, never again file.

So good! Tip: make it ahead, since this dish just gets better after a day or 2 in the fridge.    Meatless/vegan/vegetarian: perfect covered dish to bring to a gathering.   Mash it with a fork a bit, and it's a sandwich filling.  I anticipate being asked for the recipe, often. The accompanying sofrito recipe is a keeper, too. Me gusto!

This is very delicious, more than you would expect.
I used red wine vinegar and GOYA red soffits, served over rice.Do not leave out the green olives!  Wonderful and will become a staple around here!
Is there a way to cook the plantains without frying them?
you can purchase cooked plantain strips at your local grocery store or any hispanic/latin store
Does anything speak against using another type of vinegar, e.g. banana vinegar, apple cider vinegar, or a live one from Acid League or Tart?
So easy to make. If you are not an olive person you can leave them out, personally I don’t think they add much. Paired with shrimp for a full meal. Can’t wait to have the leftovers at lunch.

title: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: One of my favorite salads for years and I always order it when I find it in a resto. However, a few years ago I dropped the breading and frying of the chevre. Instead, I simply heat part of a log of goat cheese in the oven or microwave with olive oil and fines herbs. Then I plunk the warm soft chevre on my dressed greens and eat with crusty bread. For me, it's the same or even better and reminds me of my years living in Paris.
Are there any herbs that are not "fine?"  Where does that term come from?
@Golem I’m guessing they meant fine as in the chop, not the quality.
@Golem fines in fines herbes is a French word translates as “delicate” or “subtle” herbs like chives or parsley as opposed to more “woody” herbs like thyme or rosemary. Does not mean chop it fine.

A recipe that comes with such an interesting and well written background story behind it makes it all the more enticing. I'm looking forward to making this soon. The dental floss is a practical touch as a stand in for the wire often used at cheese counters, and I laughed at "unflavored" as I entertained (and subsequently dismissed) the idea of using mint flavored floss!
Sherry vinegar is much better than red wine vinegar in this recipe
Add sliced warm beets too!
The essay accompanying this recipe was beautiful, and I can't wait to try this salad sometime soon!
Love this story, totally adds to the simple romance of the dish. I might add a poached egg or two as well when I make it.

Great story about why we travel and how the history and culture of a Place can be found on the plate and in the glass. One of the reasons I love New York City is the availability of foods and drink that can allow me to recreate a meal that I enjoyed elsewhere in the world and of course good writing also does this. Thank you for both the article and the recipe.

There are no ingredients in this recipe that are not available beyond the provincial boundaries of the Hudson and East Rivers.  We have goat cheese, greens, eggs and all sorts of ingredients.  

We even have Dijon mustard.

“In the maze of the old quarter, we sat at a table outside, on stone stairs descending from another century.” 

I am rarely in the kitchen as it is my husband’s domain, however, a version of this recipe has long been my contribution to our dinner parties.
Michele, that's the perfect line that got me too.
This reminds me to try and thank all the kind people who helped me when I was a young traveler
Excellent. I just finished it! Followed the recipe exactly and my only regret is that I didn’ make more.
Thanks for sharing! I like mine with lemon juice, a bit of honey, minced garlic and the lemon zest. EVO of course!

Nice salad
Great story
Beautiful essay/intro. Should be expanded into a novel.
Chopped apricots and hazel nuts make the salad first class without being fussy.

Make salad dressing in mostly empty dijon mustard jar, or with the dregs of the mustard in the jar. Eyeball the oil & vinegar, salt & pepper, whatever extra herbs (fine or not), give a good shake. Oh, and a pinch of sugar (gasp!!!) Extra keeps in fridge for weeks-just keep adding to it.

I love this story. It reminds me of when I was young and traveling on my own, always with low funds. I have made this recipe for many years, and guests are always amazed at how delicious it is, yet so simple. I've never used the egg mixture, but it makes sense...and after patting them with the bread crumbs, I put the little cheese rounds in a toaster oven...my go-to kitchen appliance. And thanks for the idea of sherry vinegar instead of red wine vinegar, which I don't use much any more.
I first had this salad years ago at Alice Waters' Berkeley restaurant Cafe Fanny.  We've been making it at home ever since, a version very close to this recipe, even down to the dental floss.

"In the maze of the old quarter, we sat at a table outside, on stone stairs descending from another century."  Yes, she got me there, too. Just happens I have fresh fromage from the chèvre at hand, market greens, shallots, and a glass of red reminding me of those sunlit evenings in France, Italy, and Spain ... and the people who shared laughter, wisdom, and so many stories. I raise my glass to them, in quiet, wistful gratitude. Merci, Grazie, Gracias!

That hint about the fresh thyme leaves in the bread crumb mixture really kicked it up to the
next level!
Delicious, easy, simple, classic elegance. She is the moment. This dressing is fantastic in its flavor profile and, again, simplicity, and will be regularly in the rotation now.
I just made this tonight.     Had a balsamic dressing already made.  Did the goat cheese as instructed.    Added fresh pear (ala the 80's).   thinly sliced shallots.     Mixed lettuces.
Great salad.  I had one today in Cahors.

Made this.  Quite good and doesn't take much to cut the recipe down to a single serving.  Best buy on goat cheese is at Costco if one is near - less than half the price of other retailers.  Good stuff.  I like the addition of beets but I'd probably used canned beets.

We grab a bunch of beets at the farmers market each week (or the supermarket in the winter) and roast them: cut off the greens and roots, place on a sheet of foil, sprinkle them with olive oil and chopped garlic, wrap the foil tightly, and bake them in a dish at 400 for 60 to 90 minutes, depending on size. Peel and slice them after they’ve cooled. These are way better than canned or boiled, and very easy to prepare. We keep a container of them in the fridge but they don’t last long!
This is a delightful article - I'll be looking for more from Ms. Mishan!  And this is one of my fav salads. She has inspired me to make it tonight.
