Nothing takes me back to the joys of eating in France like le salade de chevre chaud. I usually just make little chevre toasts in the microwave, but this is a fabulous alternative.  I usually favor white wine vinegar, and add egg and/or walnuts for my own trip down memory lane.  Thanks so much!
Such a lovely essay to go with a favorite salad. Can't wait to try this home version.

I've had (and loved) various salades de chèvre chaud around France and even Quebec. But when I try to recreate, I feel as if the chèvre here in the U.S. just isn't the same. I've tried goat brie, which is closer but still not a match. Any suggestions?

This is one of my favorite salads- but the first I have heard of frying breaded goat cheese.  I will try it out.  The more standard preparation is to smear the goat cheese on small pieces of toasted soft bread (not baguette).  There are other variations.  One I enjoyed in Paris had the goat cheese on toast pieces with pesto and honey.  Also, there is no rule that the salad has to be only greens.  Add what you like.
Has anyone tried this other NYT version of the salad by Alice Waters? https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1012960-baked-goat-cheese-with-garden-lettuces?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share
I order it every time I eat at Chez Panisse Cafe (preference for the laid back vibe upstairs) it's wonderful. Best salad ever, yes I've made this but not ever as yummy as at the restaurant.

Variation for lazy cooks (and the one I grew up with in France): toast good bread, add one or two slices of chèvre onto the bread, a sprinkle of herbes de provence and toss under the broiler for a few minutes, serve on top of the greens.

Answering that question about "fine herbs". The term is actually French: "fines herbes". Here's an explanation: https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-fines-herbes-995671#:~:text=Fines herbes is defined as,be found in fines herbes.

I've never made a salad like this before with warm goat cheese rounds, so I followed the recipe as is and it was simple and delicious - as promised. The dressing is superb and one I expect I'll be making often. The goat cheese rounds turned out browned and yummy (I used some chopped thyme in the breading as suggested). I cooked them for 2 minutes on each side. I might cook them a little longer next time so they get a little more gooey. A winner.
Beautiful story. It took me back to other trips I've taken where a chance meeting turned into a wonderful evening. This is traveling at it's finest. And this salad is on my must have list!
Adding candied pecans and beets are a great edition.  This has become my husband's favorite salad.
Even better, I’m guessing, with slices of fresh figs.
Perfect.
Easy to make and so, so delicious.

This was yummy. I used panko bread crumbs as I prefer their texture. As a runner, this wasn't quite enough food for a full meal for me, so I added a chicken apple sausage and a hunk of warm crusty bread to round it out. I also threw some fresh figs into the mix for a sweet contrast with the tangy goat cheese.
I used our air fryer to cook the cheese coins and added some rotisserie chicken breast to make it a terrific weeknight dinner.

I forgot to get beets so used from jar, and added chopped Lucy rose apples, bit of honey and ume plum vinegar to dressing, in addition to fresh tarragon leaves in salad and minced in dressing. Fresh thyme in breadcrumbs of course!  Yum :)
Add honey to dressing!
Really simple and good.  Adding herbs to the salad really brings it all up a notch.

I am always looking for a salad recipe that does not call for tomatoes.  I grew up in Iowa eating wonderful tomatoes, but here in Arizona they are tasteless.  I had some really good seasoned Italian breadcrumbs so I left out the salt and pepper.  Very good.

Nothing takes me back to the joys of eating in France like le salade de chevre chaud. I usually just make little chevre toasts in the toaster oven, but this is a fabulous alternative. I usually favor white wine vinegar, and add egg and/or walnuts for my own trip down memory lane. Thanks so much!
T s salad or warmed log with herbs and bread or a ll  car  twith

As Bill wrote, do read the article; the link is in the intro.  For anyone who has stepped into Le Suquet in Cannes, it will resonate.

Then make the salad, and flash back.
This recipe was embedded in a breathtaking New York Times Magazine essay by Ligaya Mishan (8/30/23). Prep this recipe, pair it with a Chardonnay, and create your own moment of Proust!
Brings back my last visit to Paris, several years ago, when I practically lived on this wonderful salad during the day; saving our money & main appetite for more comprehensive dinners.

I understand exactly how the author felt when she tried this salad. I had the same experience 25 years ago in a restaurant in Place des Vosges in Paris. The simplicity of the ingredients were the revelation. Thank you for sharing!
The salad is an appitizer. The coverimg story is a delicious meal.
I ordered this exact salad in a little restaurant on Rue Mouffetard in Paris. So simple yet delicious!  I wouldn’t change a thing. Thank you for the recipe!
I'm heading to the market now — eager to try the recipe and still smiling about the article it came with: A Meal in France Showed Me the Brilliance of Simplicity.

This is one of my favorite salads—I taught myself how to make it after first having it in NYC during college! There’s a version @ local haunt here in LA that bulks it up with wild rice—have made that version too, and even cut back on the cheese (a little) & added grilled tofu (I know, eye roll), but these are excellent ways to bulk it up if you’re skipping the bread. 

That being said, I’m going to get back to basics and make this exactly as written as fast as you can say “Oui!”
Great salad. I wanted a crunchy element, so I tossed in a handful of toasted pepitas.

My spouse and I ate this at JoJo almost 40 years ago, when we were young.  So simple yet so elegant and delicious.  Still remember it to this day as the epitome of NYC fine dining at the time.  Still look for it on the JoJo menu!
Simple and very tasty! I'd seen this salad on menus but had never had it before.
I accidentally got goat cheese blended wirh Kalamata olives - would this salad still be ok with this ? I’m thinking so though it might change the flavor profile
This is magnifique.  It will be my go-to salad henceforth and forever when I dine alone.  Not sharing this!

Lala - Great idea! I too lived in France and ate copious quantities of Salade de Chevre Chaud, but I can no longer tolerate eggs or wheat so I've resorted to just crumbling chevre over my salads, but your idea offers another alternative. Love it.

In our house and in some of my friend’s homes, no nearly empty jar of Dijon mustard gets tossed.  Never measure, eyeball good olive oil, and either sherry or balsamic vinegar, a chopped garlic clove or shallot. A good shake is all that is needed. By the way, this salad is the first salad I ever ordered when I ate out the first time when I lived in Bruxelles years ago. I do remember that salad had some herbes de Provence and honey in it.

Great story.  Great recipe. Great dish. 
Of course, the American “want, want, want” is the goal and product of clever ad copy.
It made a hearty meal for 2 with left over goat cheese.  It would make a good starter for 4.  I used caesar salad dressing and that was quite good.  Next time I'll try the recipe.
This is great, and super easy to make. We went with the half the bread crumbs and yolk only, then freezer for 5 min (not fridge). Now its going in our weekday go-to salads.

Perfect!!  How wonderfully inspirational the article and the recipe are!  Swan's Way of the NYtimes Cooking!  I know it transported me!  I served with a cup of homemade chicken broth and  a small fillet of Bronzino and of course some crusty bread!
Made this dish exactly as written and it was so simple, yet refined. A perfect light dinner. I will definitely be making this again and again.

Thank you all for talking about the story with such affection. I just found the recipe in a cooking newsletter, not through the story, but your comments made me want to read it. You were right - there is something so generous in it - not only the Swiss friends, but also her painterly like descriptions of them. Sweet, sweet, sweet. I will enjoy the salad all the more because of the story.

So happy to find this lovely and simple recipe! Absolutely delicious! It transported me to my year as an exchange student in Belgium, when I first enjoyed this salad, and haven’t had it since. This will be a regular for me.
The simplicity but yet, complex flavors of this salad are incredible. We will make this again and again.
I think it works better if you add the salt and pepper (and thyme or other herbs, if using) to the egg before you beat it, instead of adding it to the breadcrumbs.
Delicious recipe. I added slivered almonds, roasted pine nuts, and some pomegranate seeds which gave a great sour punch

I find letting the goat cheese come close to room temperature before you cut/slice it helps it stay in clean rounds. I used a knife to cut cold cheese and ended up with a few crumbled/broken pieces. Still delicious!

Delicious! I added mango slices and might try arugula or all radicchio for the greens next time rather than a mix. This reminds me of a salad I used to eat regularly 20 years ago at a restaurant I worked at. How wonderful to taste it again after so many years!
This was a simple and delicious way to get some greens in. As per a suggestion below, I added pomegranate seeds and found they were a lovely addition!
Per Alexa, one small shallot equals 11 grams.
So simple, so delicious.  Followed recipe with the exception of adding sliced peaches.  Will make again and again.
Just excellent, and dead easy.
My first recipe! I loved this, although my cheese did crumble and I had to ball them up. I added cooked (not picked) beetroot and will definitely be making this again.

This is a pretty boring salad except for the cool fried goat cheese suggestion. I wish they had suggested more ingredients. I went with adding avocado and Kalamata olives and it wasn't bad. The goat cheese thing was delicious, if a bit intense.
lol way too much bread crumbs only used about 1/4-1/2 cup at most..
Add beets and candied walnuts/pecans to make it more filling/even better. Reheat leftover chèvre rounds in the toaster oven at 300 for 20 mins

I was very excited about the dressing but wanted more texture, so I ended up using a walnut dijon in the dressing and added a little chopped walnuts and shaved forelle pear. It was so good that I remain under its spell and fear I will be making variations of this salad forever.
I prefer passing the cooked garbanzos through a food mill. It involves more work than a food processor, but it strains out the seed coating and produces a creamier puree.
@Michael thanks for this comment. I’m getting a food mill.

I make hummus without tahini. Into the food processor: rinsed canned chick peas, olive oil, fresh lemon juice, garlic cloves to taste, salt to taste. I add bits of water to loosen it up or add a roasted red pepper. For heat, sometimes I add a pinch of cayenne. To serve, I will spread on a plate, top with minced tomatoes, cucumbers, red peppers, chopped roasted pistachios, minced parsley and mint, then drizzle with olive oil. I think hummus takes a fair amount of salt to be tasty.
I make hummus without tahini too, but I add some toasted sesame oil so as to keep the sesame flavour.

Didn't have hazelnuts so I used roasted, salted almonds. Eliminated roasting and removing skins, though hazelnuts would add character.  I tripled the lemon juice and sumac; doubled the rice vinegar, and used more salt to punch up the flavor. 
Use GROUND coriander! Since the recipe didn't specify, I used whole seeds. Bad mistake. They didn't break down in the food processor. Nobody wants to chew and swallow the hard, dry pieces. So it's like eating watermelon and spitting out the seeds.
Please note that some people are violently allergic to sumac. Its presence should always be disclosed.
Can you provide some evidence or medical research on this? I was totally unaware and after looking online quickly I couldn't see anything to validate allergies that are specifically linked to Sumac.

Some types of sumac (there are many) have white berries that can produce a painful, itchy skin rash similar to poison ivy. They are not eaten. The kind sold as a ground spice is from one of the red-berried varieties and is widely consumed in middle eastern dishes.

I am extremely allergic to poison oak. Sumac has a very small amount of the active oil in poison oak. Therefore, because I am so very sensitive to that oil, I am also allergic to sumac. FYI, the same oil is in cashews. All these items cause for me an incredibly oozy, itchy, full-body rash. It is only treatable by a doctor. So, yes, use of sumac should always be disclosed, thank you!

Years ago, when my brief fling with a Greek Revival house in Walpole, New Hampshire had come to an end, I went over to a neighbor's garden. The owner had the same name as a tragic heroine in a Brian Moore novel; she, too, was private and reticent. I said I had a stash of old, well-rotted hummus I wanted to give her; bemused, she followed me to my compost pile and gently corrected me on the pronunciation of humus. 
If she's still alive, I hope she enjoys this ancient recipe as much as I did.
Rubbing the skins off hazelnuts is a pain and not worth it for 1/3 cup.  Buy the roasted, skinned version.
Zaatar would work as well as sumac. I prefer it
I believe there's sumac in Za'atar... along with other herbs and spices...
Most hummus is a little grainy, which betrays the lack of time in the food processor.  To get the creaminess that transforms hummus from good to great, blitz for at least five minutes.

From recipe on blog:

“¼ teaspoon of each of caraway, coriander, black pepper, ginger, and cinnamon, ALL CRUSHED”
(emphasis added)
Recipe omits the most important step - cooking chickpeas w a bit of baking soda will help break down the pectin that holds the skin together (and also makes us gassy).
Maybe medieval cooks were unaware of that.

A good trick for making really creamy hummus is to soak and use Chana Dal which you can find in any health store. Chana Dhal is a smaller sort of chickpea, split and, notable, husked. The lack of a husk makes all the difference. However you have to soak them overnight and then cook them.

I find hummus forgiving and flexible. This recipe, the article, and the comments sort of point that out: Use nuts and seasoning ratios that work for you. 

I actually like making chunkier hummus than the totally smooth one from the store. That’s part of what makes it homemade for me. Also, I use the entire two cans of the beans and just scale up the other ingredients to taste so that I don’t have to deal with the leftover beans.
Sumac is also available at Burlap and Barrel. They're also an excellent spice company. Their coriander seeds are really, really beautiful, too. https://www.burlapandbarrel.com/products/cured-sumac
To have perfectly smooth hummus when using a food processor, make sure the chick peas and their broth are hot. If it cools, the skins will not blend properly

I think what was missing in the directions was after toasting the seeds to grind them.  Whether in a coffee mill or mortar and pestle depending on your adherence to such an "ancient" recipe.  Works far better as noted than leaving them whole.
This is fabulous--creamy, tasty, complex.  We love it!
Drizzle the Hummus with some smoked olive oil. Just do it, trust me.

This recipe taught me a lesson I should've known: if something doesn't sound right to you, don't say, "How could the NYT be wrong? I'll make it just as written even though I doubt something!" Instead, follow your instincts. My concern with this was that whole coriander seed would create a gritty, unpleasant texture. That's correct. (If I'd read the notes, I'd have seen someone else say so.) I'll make this again, but will pulverize the toasted caraway/coriander/sesame mix before using.
This recipe can also be found in The Palestinian Table by Reem Kassis. A regular family staple chez nous.
Love this recipe! It’s a bit decadent but love the nuts instead of garlic. Like if pesto married hummus. Recommend double the lemon and more salt.

There is edible sumac ( red berries ) and poison sumac ( white berries ) Red sumac foraged in the wild also makes a great tea similar to red zinger. Have a look at https://youtu.be/4SsAaoMYoCA Learn your Land -Edible vs.Poison Sumac -Learn the Difference
Sumac?
Sumac is the dark red spice sprinkled on middle eastern foods, salads, etc. You can get it on line or your local Penzey's https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-is-sumac-1763131
Sumac is very common in Lebanese cuisine.  It is often used as a garnish with good olive oil on top of hummus, too.
Also Kalustyan's in Manhattan. They do mail order as well.
the blog post link is well worth checking out for a variety of hummus recipes, for those of us who can't get enough variations on the theme...
This is so unctuous and yummy.   I love garlic but withheld it as per the recipe, and really enjoyed all of the flavors that shone through the dish.   Thank you for the recipe!
For my hummus, I like going half-and-half with tahini and peanut butter.

This is a excellent as written. My husband doesn’t like raw garlic, and so many hummus recipes go overboard on the garlic, so was really happy to find a garlic-free hummus recipe. The warm spices shine through and the sumac brings a citrusy tartness. I used canned garbanzo beans and instead of water, used the liquid from the beans. My only caveat is this make a lot. I mean a lot!  Hoping it will freeze as we won’t get through all of it.  Next time, I’ll halve the recipe.

I halved this recipe today.  Amazing flavors and all of the ingredients work together and emphasize each other.  I had everything in the pantry, fridge or freezer (nuts), except for a fresh lemon. I agree that garlic would detract from the emphasis on the toasted spices.  The next time I make this, I might add 1T more lemon juice, chiffon mint leaves, and drizzle more oil on top.  I chopped extra pistachios for garnish, versus reserved chickpeas.

Depth and complexity of flavor elevates this over traditional smooth ‘one note’ hummus. I ground the roasted whole spices before adding to the food processor - and appreciate the whole coriander flavor.
I love hummous, and I have grown tired of hummous variations...jalapeno, olive, sundried tomato, etc. This is the very first "improvment" on the traditional recipe. Thank you so much!

Followed the recipe, using canned chickpeas.  The spice mixture as listed, with its emphasis on caraway seeds, which I ground with a mortar and pestle after toasting, delivers a "rye bread in dip form" flavor.  Should have heeded the more critical reviews.  Next time I will try a less medieval spice mixture (perhaps following Nasrallah's blog more closely) along with the preserved lemon.

I once heard on the Food Network (when that outlet actually had cooking instruction) that tarragon is the herb that dries the best and is closest to fresh.   Of course, with concentrated dried you'd use a tad less.   So I will just buy a jar of tarragon dried, and save some for next time.

I had a full tub of Costco plain hummus.  I added all the ingredients to that and elevated it into savory, sublime deliciousness!  I doubled the lemon juice, simply because the lemon produced that much juice.

This is similar to regular hummus, yet the spice profile is so unique. Really delicious. 

The only unroasted pistachios I could find we shelled, and I have no time for shelling nuts on a weeknight, so I subbed roasted and salted pistachios. It worked fine by just adding a little less salt.

If you have a blender, use it. It makes hummus so much better, even if it is a bit of a pain to scrape out the hummus. Using a Vitamix has transformed my relationship with home-made hummus (in a good way).
I do not have a food processor or blender. Any ideas on how I might make this? (I imagine they didn’t have such things in 13th Century Iraq so… there’s got to be a way :)
This is utterly delicious as is. I did add another Tbs of lemon juice and another pinch of salt at the end. Hazelnuts, caraway seeds, coriander seeds, tarragon—genius flavor combo.

This was nutty, creamy and full of good flavors. I would add actual lemon juice as well as the preserved lemon and maybe a bit more sumac than the recipe calls for.  This is one of the best hummus (hummusi?) I’ve ever had.

I make dukka on a regular basis, using the same seeds used in this recipe. I grind each type by itself since they have slightly different hardnesses. And that means, of course, that I also toast them separately. Try it. It makes a difference.
Excellent flavor, I will definitely crush the toasted seeds next time.
Any suggestions on substituting a different nut for Hazelnuts?
The original blog post uses walnuts.
Can this be made without oil? I am on Esselstyn’s heart diet and cannot have oil.l
I have made a passible hummus using the liquid from the chickpeas (aquafaba). I’d would blend in some olives for flavor.
in traditional hummus, oil is NEVER blended with the chickpeas (only swirled on top). just add additional ice water, if needed.
Is there a canned bean substitution? Life is too short to deal with dried.
Hi, If you reread the recipe you'll see that two 15 oz cans of garbanzo beans are an option. Your'e right, much quicker that way.
I have a friend with hazelnut allergy--what would be a good substitute?
The following link explains the issue with the 'poisonous' sumac. https://www.webmd.com/allergies/poison-ivy-oak-sumac
Never had the patience to "remove (hazelnut) skins and discard".  Am I missing something special by consuming nut skins?

I don't know about the difference it makes here, but in a different recipe I learned to put all the toasted nuts in a dish towel and rub them around.  It just takes a minute.  You don't get all the skins off, but you do get most (and the recipe I learned this from says not to worry about what doesn't come off).

Regarding the question about sumac allergy, most people are allergic to poison sumac, similar to poison oak and poison ivy. However, there is also an allergy to sumac: " Although it isn't a nut, sumac can cause allergies in some people with cashew allergy. Sumac is a spice made from the dried and ground berries of the sumac flower.". Nov 23, 2022 https://www.allergyspot.com.au › ca... Cashew Allergy? Surprising Foods You Might Need to Avoid

I have have Bedouin style baked chicken rubbed with sumac and thought I'd died and gone to heaven!  I didn't bring back enough last year so will have to get more this year!  But I calculated about right for za'atar

Culinary sumac and the plant that makes you itch are two totally different things.  Most people develop contact dermatitis or allergic reactions when in contact with the plant that is much like poison ivy.  The sumac in this recipe is not the same.

The best Hummus for my taste is the simplest, available as street food all over the Middle East. By the time hummus travelled to North America it morphed into something else. If you make it home with some personal changes, great.
However any hummus available in North American food markets is a joke. Little Tahini,lots of Vinegar and/or lemon juice. No taste of Hummus. I add Almond  butter and a few drops of roasted sesame oil to my hummus. Makes it taste like what you get in the Middle East.

Several species of sumac are native to Missouri and one of them grows clusters of small, red berries that taste lemony-sour; I have enjoyed a lemonade-type beverage made from these berries. I don’t know if these are the exact same species as the Middle Eastern sumac, but I use them as if they were (and will do so for this recipe). They are a hassle to clean, though, as the berries are tightly clustered together, hard to separate and rinse clean.

Brilliant! As @Michael notes, creamy hummus is the best. Follow Yotam Ottolenghi’s method for cooking velvety chickpeas and traditional hummus, swap out seasoning for this recipe, and mix in food processor as Ottolenghi directs. Even better if it gets an overnight refrigerator rest, then return to room temp before serving with a swirl of oil and whatever garnishes you like.
Since we're talking about hummus, I personally prefer mine with a little texture to it, and my family has always made it that way. It's up to your preference.
this was too many beans for the amount of liquid. If you want 2 15oz cans, then increase liquid.  If only 1 can, then the same liquid.  I think for 1 can reconsider the amount of nuts and spices.

As noted, there's a difference between *white* or "poison" sumac, which most humans have violent allergic reactions to (and thus, it should never be consumed by anyone) and *red* sumac, which is an ingredient in many Middle Eastern dishes that adds a tangy, tart. almost citrusy note - and which is, for most but not all, quite safe. 

Still, a quick google search indicates that those with cashew or mango allergies should avoid red sumac, too.

Hive, help pls: I have a tiny food processor. Can I do the nuts &tc in there and then use the blender once it's time to add the garbanzos?
Also - how to get the skins off the nuts? I found hazelnuts shelled and roasted, but they still had skins on, and the roasted pistachios I had to shell myself (after which, skins still stuck like glue). Tried rubbing them against the colander, rolling in my hands, etc... minimally effective (pristine nuts elusive in Seattle stores...) Thanks for suggestions -
Maybe use semi-darkly roasted cashews and a pinch of nutmeg instead of the hazelnuts.  The skin on roasted pistachios shouldn't impact flavor much.

Meant to add that it’s important to remove the skins from the hazelnuts because they can be quite bitter.  I rub the warm toasted nuts in a tea towel.  It’s messy but it gets rid of most of the skins.
I made it. I found the caraway flavor too strong and wouldn't make it again as I didn't find the additional ingredients added anything over more traditional hummus.

Rhus coriaria is the name for Middle Eastern sumac. All sumacs are in the cashew family, and are related, but poison sumac is a distant cousin. The type we have here in the US, most typically staghorn sumac, is a relative. Indigenous people in the US have used staghorn sumac (and the related varieties of sumac native to these shores) for medicinal and food preparations since, well, ever. The popular “sumac lemonade” recipe is one such preparation. If harvesting, please do so ethically.

I was just telling friends that as far as culinary trends ago, I was over the whole hummus thing. Like if I never see another bowl on a table of starters, I’d be just fine. And then I saw this recipe and it really intrigued me. I had a feeling I was going to love it – and I do!

I’m not a fan of either mint or tarragon, so I substituted roughly, chopped fresh basil. Also, I put it on top of the hummus when I served it rather than mixed it in. Absolutely delicious.
I didn't have tarragon, so I threw in a few fennel seeds. Delicious!

I'm not sure anyone who's reviewed this has actually made it! My husband and I really liked this recipe. I thought I'd miss the garlic, but I didn't - the rich nutty flavor and caraway seeds made it super flavorful. I soaked the chick peas overnight and then  pressure cooked them for 8 minutes. They were super soft so it was easy to remove the outer skins for a lovely creaminess. I didn't have coriander seeds so just added some coriander powder. Added extra lemon juice too.

I loved mine like this. It seemed like a lot of seeds for the amount of hummus and I’m happy I only used half. I read other comments about the herbs so I skipped those and didn’t miss them. I’d probably use a few less nuts next time too. So, for a recipe to use as an inspiration it’s great, but for me not one to follow to a T.
At the very least double the lemon juice and olive oil. It's fine but the family recipe is easier and tastier.

Made exactly as listed, and appreciated the mellow, earthy notes.

Like many others mentioned, straining through a fine screen got out all the funky grit from the seed skins and unprocessed nut crumbs (the blender didn't get it all, even after a long run).

I feel that the outcome is now a 'base hummus' which I can dress up in different ways.
unlike my family member who shall not be named, actually follow the recipe, don't just toss the ingredients into a blender. Can't wait to make it the right way.

I really enjoyed this unusual hummus. I used pecans and pistachio, and bumped the coriander, all of which resulted in a sweet undertone. The unground spices were gritty at first, but softened over time (absorbed water, perhaps?). Tarragon was a surprise and delicious ingredient. I used way more of it than the recipe called for to showcase it. Will make this again.

So hard for me to follow a recipe exactly but I did my best. Was sceptical about the caraway but it was fab! Used a combo of lemon and preserved lemon. Next time more preserved lemon. I did want a touch of sweetness. Just a touch. So added a trace amount of pomegranate molasses. Just delicious. Inspiring. Great on radishes.

Also, I substituted tamarind paste for the sumac, as I wasn't able to readily find sumac.  I will order some.  Even the tamarind paste was not easy to find.  That was three grocery stores, and then finally a visit to a Mexican grocery store to find that.
Sounds delicious. But I'm afraid hummus is what I go to for a fast cooked meal. I also have been eating whole wheat pita now for so long that I can't imagine eating white flour pita anymore.

If you make this dish, please tell people it has nuts in it, in case they have a severe nut allergy. Hummus is considered a safe dish to most people with nut allergies, and they might just dive in without thinking to ask about nuts. This could be very serious and dangerous.
I used more mint and tarragon. Yumm.

I made this last night. It’s so creamy(texture) and tasty! Next time, I think, I’ll add the toasted spices when I pulse the nuts. I made a Hummus bowl with it & added the Farro Pilaf with Balsamic Cherries & the Sheet Pan Gochujiang Shrimp & Green Beans…had Asparagus & used that instead. All so good!
Great taste, it's my favourite Hummus

Delicious! Earthier and more nuanced than most hummus recipes. I didn't miss the garlic or the lemon juice. (I followed the tip to add preserved lemon and its juice instead.) I loved biting down on bits of coriander and caraway seeds. This is a very special recipe!

I thought this was a welcome change from regular hummus. I made this with a kale salad. I added extra salt and extra roasted hazelnuts on the top. Next time I may add more of the spices to add more complexity of flavors.
You'll never get a smooth hummus if you don't go through the tedious trouble of removing the chickpea skins. Sorry!

Needs more citrus - maybe 2 preserved lemons? 

Makes 2x regular amount - blend in batches 

Def richer than standard
The best hummus! We do not miss the garlic.  It is better.

You can cook the chickpeas in a pot of water with a teaspoon of Bicarbonate Soda for 20 minutes or so. Rinse really well afterwards. This process breaks down the skins so they blend incredibly creamy. Found in a Cookie and Kate recipe.

Lemon juice increased about 2x plus half a preserved lemon chopped. 
     Grind the coriander and caraway seeds!

This didn't win me over. Good hummus is simple and straightforward with just a few ingredients, although Americans tend to add way too much garlic. This tasted muddled--too many different nuts and seeds going on, and as I suspected (and confirmed), there is no way you can taste five mint leaves, much less a sprig of tarragon. I was curious because here in Italy we grow the finest hazelnuts and pistachios in the world, and we have local organic dried chickpeas at the farmer's market.

stats.Total Time: 20 minutes, plus at least 14 hours’ freezing
stats.Cook Time: 15 minutes, plus at least 14 hours’ freezing

comments.commentBody: For people who shy away from unnatural ingredients, try making strawberry soda from real strawberries and sugar and then adding sparkling water. To make the strawberries, hull and halve 1 1/2 pounds strawberries. Toss with about 3/4 cups of sugar in a metal bowl. Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap. Place over a bowl of simmering water and leave for 30 - 45 minutes. Remove from heat and when cool, strain the juice. Or puree the whole thing. Add sparkling water to taste.
I don't know if this will work for the sherbet recipe, but it looks good on its own -- so, thanks!

This was a fun read, nostalgically recalling 
other summertime treats. I remember making, in high school, a dessert called ‘Frozen Passion’ - one large bottle of orange soda and one can of sweetened condensed milk, hand cranked in an old-fashioned ice cream freezer (the only kind available, in my day). Was this concoction overly sweet, artificially colored, nutritionally dubious? Of course. Was it also delicious, easy, fun, memorable? You betcha…..

I remember that crack seed store well, and will be sure to make this ono recipe this summer, dusting it off with the stash of li hing mui powder my ohana from Hawaii sends me.  If you don't understand the flavors from the islands you are truly missing out.  Mahalo plenty for this gem.

Delighted to see this recipe! I was introduced to Neale by a friend of mine and just talked to him the other day. Growing up in Hawaii, I remember friends visiting Maui would bring back Tasaka Guri Guri to their friends on Oahu. The world has gotten much smaller, so things from Maui are less of a novelty for Oahuans, but the memories remain! Thanks Ligaya for finding these interesting treats and thanks Neale for allowing us to access something from our childhood, but with a modern twist.
Is there any reason not to just use a natural fruit juice? Does the carbonation do something important here?

I am enthused.  Is there such a thing as natural strawberry soda and natural gelatin ie without artificial ingredients.

I look forward to making this, if so.
@Lee K, I don't know if it'll meet your stringent requirements, but I tried some strawberry-flavored Izze soda the other day, and I thought it was delicious.
Lee K, Agar agar, which is plant based, can be used in place of gelatin.

This is based on the ice cream from Tasaka Guri Guri in the old Kahalui Mall on Maui. My mother-in-law grew up there and knew the family who owned it. When I was fortunate enough to visit, she would ask, "who wants 'Goody Goody." They used to serve their goody-goody with sweet red beans, but stopped. She scolded them. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tasaka_Guri-Guri#:~:text=Guri%2Dguri%20is%20a%20cross,a%20family%20secret%20for%20decades.

My grandmother in Alabama made this recipe.  The favorite was strawberry.  But she also did cream soda/vanilla and Grapico.  It's interesting how far and wide this recipe spread.   I make it when we go to the lake. I have some leftover Grapico ice cream in the freezer at the lake I think.

My mother used to make something like this for us when we were very young and sick. We called it Crello (Ice cream-Jello) and she did the final step in the blender. We did NOT live in Hawaii, but rather Minnesota. She’d make different flavors and I believe it mixed vanilla ice cream and Jello as the main ingredients. Fluffy, creamy. Crello was the best part of being sick!

Made this with A’Sicilia Sicilian blood orange soda, orange jello and used most of the evaporated milk. My husband sampled it as I was pouring into freezer containers and exclaimed, “Creamsicle!”  Most excellent.

@MaryM Did you skip the sweetened condensed milk? I ask as I would like to make a sherbet using a very sweet, very ripe canary melon that I would puree as my starting point.  I would skip the condensed milk.

@ Marsha Gibson Umeboshi and Li Hing Mui are as different as Chinese from Japanese. They are not the same thing. 
I'm curious about the aversion to "artificial" ingredients. Unless you make everything from scratch, almost everything you get at a supermarket is an industrial product packed with chemicals. It's the only way to feed billions of people.

I want to try this but am vegetarian and my husband is vegan. I’m wondering if agar will work instead of gelatin. Has anyone tried it? Might try it with agar and condensed coconut milk and possibly a different soda (thinking orange soda would taste like a creamsicle—yum
Did you try the agar version?  Did it work?
. . . wondering if Japanese umeboshi plums might be a decent substitution for li hing mui plums . . .
This was a fail for me.  Sherbet ended up way too icy, like shave ice.  Not for us.
Can this be made ahead an frozen?

I would not recommend trying to substitute ume for li hing mui. Li hing has a distinct licorice flavor (salivating as I type this) and is pretty much unlike anything else. Luckily, it's pretty easy to find li hing mui/powder online.

This looks so disgusting and so amazing at the same time. I can’t wait to try it. Excellent article that went along with it.  

Any advice on making it a bit more natural? No processed or canned?

I made a non-dairy version. Used grape Jello, half a can of coconut whipping cream and a couple dollops of frozen grape juice concentrate. Did not use the sweetened condensed milk and mine was PLENTY sweet. For the soda I used Wave sodas that are lightly sweet and not full of chemicals. Turned out REALLY GOOD...not as heavy as my usual all coconut milks vegan ice cream. Will make again with cranberry Jello, cranberry juice concentrate and Wave or Zevia ginger ale for the soda.
Thanks.   I'm thinking of making a margarita flavored concoction. Limeade, lime gelatin, some tequila, a bit of Cointreau and the coconut whipping cream....
Absolutely don’t care if this is made with pop and Jello. I’m thinking small batch lime pop and lime Jello.  Better than ice cream any day!
any suggestions for gelatin substitution (kosher)?
Could this be made with unflavored gelatin? I’m thinking root beer soda, but I don’t want to mix in a fruit flavor gelatin

This is a fun recipe. Good for cooking with kids. I couldn't find strawberry soda so I used raspberry-lime soda and raspberry Jell-O. It came out well. Of course it does taste like artificial raspberry flavoring, not fresh raspberries; but, hey, it's that kind of recipe so that was exactly what I expected. If I make this again, I'll probably switch out the condensed milk for something else. I didn't like the cooked milk flavor.

Not sure what I did wrong but this was a complete fiasco. Final product was riddled with ice crystals, frozen solid as a rock. It took 15  minutes thawing before we could even dig a spoon into it. Flavor was ok, but the texture was awful and I ended up throwing the entire thing away. Not going to waste my time on this again.

Followed the directions to the letter. Not completely satisfied with the results. 
Lots of ice crystals. Not nearly like what I had expected of a sherbet recipe.
I'll probably give it one more try, but honestly, I don't know what else I can do to get a better result.
Anyone have any suggestions?
Don’t forget the classic Hawaiian addition of Li Hing Muy sauce! That will give this a nice acid tart bite that will balance out the sweetness.
I can identify with DG and the Ritz cracker/bottled caramel sauce concoction beating her scrumptious cheesecake. My workplace too has luncheons.
Can you use an ice cream maker for this to save the long freezing time?
Can you make this is an ice cream maker instead of putting it in the freezer?
Could 14 ounces of sweetened condensed coconut milk be used in lieu of the evaporated milk?

I used half a can of Nature's Charm coconut whipping cream instead of ANYTHING that would add more sugar. Mine was plenty sweet from just the Jello mix and the soda. (I used Wave soda...less sweet, no chemicals.) Did not add the extra "milk" at the end either.
Hard to go wrong with condensed milk in dessert especially ice cream.
Awful. Full of chemical weapons ingredients.  Try sherbet recipes with natural ingredients instead.

I don’t know why I expected the result to be an improvement on all of the individual ingredients… but no. It had an artificial strawberry taste that was, for me, unpalatable. Oh well - it was a fun experiment anyway.

I made this with black cherry soda and black cherry because that's what I was able to find. I followed the directly exactly and I also had issues with it crystalizing - the finished product was still tasty but did not have the consistency of sherbet.
Icey unpleasant stuff that tasted lousy. A real disappointment.

I made this using lemon jello, minute maid lemon aid and lemon flavoring and it was excellent! Next time I will add lemon zest. Served with a lemon cookie and a sprig of fresh mint made a lovely dessert. I also tried it with orange crush and orange jello. Also a win but we preferred the lemon.
Can this be made with diet soda?
Deb, doubtful.  Sugar is vital in maintaining consistency in frozen desserts.  Otherwise it will become frozen like an ice cube.
there is plenty of sugar in the sweetened condensed milk. But try it and report back!!

I'm curious about how the Li Hing Mui is added. I can only find the powder. Someone mentioned below that a dusting of the powder on top. But in the article it mentions a sauce. Help? Thanks in advance!
Would this work in an ice cream maker?
I made it in my Cuisinart ice-cream maker and it came out really well.

Yes, I used a Whynter ice cream maker for most of the mixture. Another small portion I just froze as per the recipe.   The sherbet made in the ice cream maker came out lighter and creamier than the sherbet that was just frozen.
One note, this recipe made two batches of sherbet in the ice cream maker
Question for the experts! Does this recipe work if you use an ice cream machine? Do you need to make it in two steps?

Yes, this recipe works in an ice cream maker.  I followed the recipe through Step 5, then used an ice cream maker to finish it. The sherbet came out light and creamy. This recipe made two batches of sherbet in the ice cream maker.
We made this at home in Louisiana with strawberry or red cream soda.  We also added almond extract with the vanilla.  Chill all the ingredients before churning for finer texture.
I used my ice cream maker. VillaWare.
Has anyone tried substituting yogurt or evaporated milk for the sweetened condensed milk in order to cut the sweetness?
No, but the seltzer water helped reduce the sweetness.

No, however, in an effort to cut back the sugar and not use high fructose corn syrup, I substituted Trader Joe’s Sparkling Strawberry Beverage (which has no added sugar) for the strawberry soda.  After step 5, I used an ice cream maker to make the sherbet. The sherbet was light and creamy, with a very strawberry flavor.  I will say that I don’t know what the “original” should taste like, but the sherbet was a hit at the 4th of July party I attended.
Any thoughts about whether this could be made in an ice cream maker?

@Chevalier de Balibari
Many people, including me, do not like artificial ingredients in the food they are taking time to prepare at home. It sort of defeats the whole concept of "homemade". In my opinion, grape & strawberry are two of the worst artificial flavors out there. I would not make this recipe, but to each his own.
Soothing, edible kitsch in a bowl! Welcome back, sherbet. We missed you! (Great story, great recipe.)
has anyone tried strawberry seltzer, instead of sugared soda?  my sense is that it'd cut the sweetness down quite a bit.  which is a good thing, right?

No, however, I did try Trader Joe’s Sparkling Strawberry Beverage (which has no added sugar) as a substitute for the strawberry soda. After step 5, I used an ice cream maker to make the sherbet. The sherbet was light and creamy, with a very strawberry flavor and was plenty sweet.
Very delicious and refreshing. I used peach soda and peach jello. Followed the directions.  Included the evaporated milk. Reminded me of a creamsicle. Yum!

Made it, as written, with Fanta strawberry and the evaporated milk.Texture was great, but, good golly, it was sweet. Next time gonna try it with a berry seltzer and crushed fresh  or frozen fruit.  Going to pick raspberries this week, maybe with lime seltzer and razz jello.
Do you think it is possible to make a non-dairy version with coconut cream and coconut milk or some other variation?
I followed the recipe exactly, but the end product was full of crunchy ice crystals.

Recipe says...
1/2 (12oz) Evaporated Milk
Is this 1/2 of a 12oz can or does it mean 6oz? Not clear on this.

I made this with lime jello and sprite for my lime sorbet loving kid. We made the jello part the night before then the whipped part in the morning; by dinner time it was nicely frozen and everyone liked it. My kid for some reason didn’t like the condensed milk “flavour” in the sherbet though, I thought it was good! Maybe strawberry or orange might have worked better.
You can also use other kinds of berries.
Too sweet

I added the ingredients in the wrong order which made it very difficult to whisk by hand but turned out fantastic tasting with a nice "chew". 

Next time I'll try it with orange soda/orange jello and maybe it will taste like an orange dreamsicle.

When I first made this I used orange jello and orange soda as some reviewers suggested. But hey, the recipe authors knew what they were doing. Strawberry is the best! My only cheat is to use strawberry-rhubarb soda, which is lovely.
Can I make this with guava juice?

First, we love fake strawberry flavor. And this delivered it in spades. It was certainly sweet but the evaporated milk tamed that a bit. Otherwise the directions were easy to follow and made sense. I’m certainly interested in trying versions with less ultra high processed ingredients and even a vegan version.

This was fabulous! I had none of the icy weird texture problems others reported. I used the evaporated milk & it was smooth & delicious. I did use the electric mixer for 10 minutes just to make sure it was well blended. So great on a hot day like today.

My Filipino Dad makes a similar sweet but with margarine back in the day. He adds a few drops of food coloring and then shapes them into roses. Wrapped in clear cellophane, they make beautiful posies. Pretty and delicious.

We used to make this as a child in Bolosan, Pangasinan. Public schools at the time would get cartons of powdered milk from the USA which are then distributed to school children. My mother taught us how to make pastilyas de gatas with the powdered milk we had brought home by mixing it with sugar and water and then rolling a wad between our palms into balls the size of marbles.
Trying to make all things chocolate (haha) - I wonder if substituting cocoa powder for the matcha would give it a nice chocolate flavor.
Made these tonight, subbing cocoa powder for matcha - turned out very nice. Like soft Tootsie Rolls.
If using cocoa powder works, I think I'll try freeze-dried fruits (reduced to a powder in the blender).  Not traditional, but I'll bet they're good!

I used low fat milk powder, it’s all I had, and I’m not a big Candy guy but I liked what I made.  Next time I’ll source out the whole milk powder and some matcha or use cocoa powder as some suggest in the comments.   If the recipe was adapted from someone other than Ligaya I probably wouldn’t have tried making this but after making her cheese cake bars I decided I needed to try this, I’m glad I did.

We used to make this as children in the 60s in Bolosan, Pangasinan. At the time public schools would receive cartons of powdered milk from the USA which were then distributed among the children. My mother taught me how to make pastilyas de gatas, mixing the powdered milk with sugar and adding water to make a doughy mixture. I would then roll a wad between my palms into balls the size of glass marbles. I'd make a pile and eat them while reading the comics section of the latest Liwayway magazine
Simple and delicious. Allow time and patience for the shaping and wrapping. Mine look pretty in irridescent cellophane.
P.S. these are actually way better in taste and texture the next day!

Ok, I only have low fat milk powder, and don't really feel like getting a huge can of whole milk powder just for this recipe, can I use low fat milk powder or is this one of those things where the fat gives it like structure or something?
I tried another batch with rose water and powdered raspberry. Yum! Trick was to add rose water to condensed milk and raspberry powder to powdered milk,
why will goat's mik powder not work? That's what I have
I'm confused.  If you're not simmering the milk for hours, when does the caramelization happen?

There is no caramelization (other than any that previously occurred in the manufacture of the condensed milk).  The accompanying article states: "Unlike dulce de leche, in which milk is cooked down with sugar until it thickens into gold, the milk stays white here — burfi comes from the Persian word for snow — for a purer flavor."
Where do you find the colorful cellophane paper?  I prefer this to the papel de Japon
Colorful cellophane is sold in rolls in the gift-wrap section in many stores.
is whole milk powder hard to find in the US?
out of curiosity, would nonfat milk powder work? (I imagine they'd taste...lean, but mostly curious as to whether it'd physically come together, as I have some at home I'd like to use up.)
That's what I used for mine! Worked great!
Delightful! Didn’t roll in sugar, which I suspect would help with stickiness. But they seemed more than sweet enough without that extra layer.
https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=h2dWTShyAME This is a wonderful video about a special art of wrapping these pastilles de leche in traditionally cut out wrappings. Beautiful!
I did not care for these. I made them as written except added dried fruit powder instead of matcha. I wish I’d tried chocolate.
I was dubious of the matcha powder but it was a delicious addition. Not overly strong, just counteracts the sweetness nicely. We loved these and will try w other flavorings.
Would unflavored whey protein powder be a good substitute for the whole milk powder?

stats.Cook Time: 1 hour plus 1 hour cooling and chilling

comments.commentBody: This is a classic Texas dessert for potlucks, bunco/game nights, etc. It is not fancy, so while puff pastry may seem like a noble idea...just don't. For some reason the Pillsbury crescent dough just works, giving it more of that intended sopapilla texture (and I don't use canned dough otherwise!). A little citrus zest - orange is especially good - added to the cream cheese filling is a great addition, though.

OK, no question is stupid, right?
If the cream cheese filling is covered by a layer of crescent roll dough, how can you tell if it is wobbling in the center to indicate "doneness"? Or does the crescent roll layer on top wobble, too?
Would love to make this but live somewhere where there is no pre-made crescent roll dough to purchase. Any suggestions for a substitute?  Thank you

This is hilarious. I have been making exactly the same thing for the past twenty years. I got the recipe from a lady in church in California and we all called it "Breakfast Cheesecake." I'm embarrassed when anyone asks for the recipe because it's so easy and so full of processed food. But it IS delicious....
Wonder how this would work-out using puff-pastry instead ...

I might be fine, but a word of caution.  We were looking for puff pastry at a couple of grocery stores around the holidays, and none was to be found.  Supply chain!  The store manager told us to use crescent roll dough.  It worked just as good, and easier, than pp.
This cake is also known as churro cake!
Come on. It's called Ann Pillsbury's cream cheese crescent roll bars. It's a very old recipe and there is nothing sopapilla about it.

It may have once been called “Ann Pillsbury's cream cheese crescent roll bars.” 

But a Google search on that name returns Sopapilla Cheesecake Bars on the Pillsbury website.

I've been making almost this exact recipe for years and it's delicious and always a hit. My version has slightly different amounts on the sugar in the filling vs the on the top, and the cinnamon amount is more in this version, but the results are always a popular item for any event. There used to be a version of the crescent roll dough available that didn't have the perforations, and that made things a little easier. Not sure if that's still available or not, but something to look for.

I'd like to make this but am wondering if the cream cheese filling needs an egg (or two) to firm up and allow for easier cutting and serving?  This looks great for a pot luck I'm attending soon. The author doesn't include an egg in the recipe but it seems unusual otherwise.
Yes I was wondering the same as if not it is just cream cheese...

I've made almost this exact recipe for years and have never needed an egg. Once the filling is spread out over the dough and then baked, I think it somehow all sort of melds just right and, once refrigerated, doesn't seem to be mushy. I've never needed to go to the trouble of doing the knife in water thing. I just use a good, sharp knife and wipe with a towel in between cuts.

I don't know how long people in the Southwest have been been eating this, but I have a recipe dating back to about 1998 (and is probably older) that is identical, except that it calls for toasted, broken pecans on top.  It is not called cheesecake but is called Dogtrot Danish.

Someone mentioned getting the crescent rolls without the perforation. I used to buy them for a different recipe, then couldn't find them. I moved to a new location, two hours away and found them in the store this week. I don't know if it was a pandemic/supply chain issue or my local store, but they DO still make them.

I did this for years for my staff. Simple and thrifty for about 50 people at a time.  I bought 12 -14 cans of crescent rolls - laid half out on big cookie sheets, pinched the roll seams together, spread the cream cheese mix whipped with well drained crushed pineapple, right to edges. It doesn't drip or leak. Top w/2d layer of rolls, seams pinched, then cover liberally with fine brown sugar mix -NO lumps.Bake. Cool. Barely trim baked edges & cut diagonally - @1" wide. Looks fancy piled high.
The lemon zest that someone suggested was a hit. We did not care for the sugar's texture on top, so will try honey or powdered sugar instead.

I just made this, it's delicious, simple, cheap ingredients you might already own, easily modified to include other toppings or fillings, and kids would probably love making this one, it's pretty easy and you can decorate it. Just one thing- I dont know on what planet you get 100 pieces from this thing, I used the same stuff, same size pan, I got maybe 30 pieces?
Agreed, 100 pieces no way!
I found 3 tablespoons cinnamon too much.  Did anyone else?

Yes, I am wondering if they meant 3 teaspoons. Others of this recipe ask for a teaspoon to 1 tablespoon of cinnamon. I ended up putting in two tablespoons, which was still way too much. I only ended up using half the cinnamon sugar mixture. It was more than plenty. If I make them again, as they were well received, I may try a fruit topping.

I made something as a little kid that I imagine tastes similar. It involved spreading cream cheese on a piece of wonder bread, rolling it up, dipping it in melted butter and cinnamon sugar, then putting it in the toaster oven. You slice the toasted roll into pieces and voila “cinnamon snails”.

I made a half recipe of this in an 8 x 8 pan and the proportions did not work out well (although the result was still delicious).  It needed more cream cheese filling, less melted butter, and less sugar in both the cream cheese and the topping.  I cut the baking time to 30 minutes and when I make it again (which I will, it was good) I will make the adjustments.

This is hands down one of the easiest, tastiest dessert recipes I have ever made. With everything warmed to room temperature, it took just minutes to roll out the dough, mix and spread the cream cheese mixture, then cover with the remaining dough and cinnamon sugar mixture. I cut the sugar a bit to 100 grams, 250 just seemed too sweet. The extra on top with the cinnamon really caramelizes in the butter though and provides a great crunch.
Look for the Pillsbury crescent dough in sheets to eliminate the step of closing up the holes in the dough.
Made the crescent roll version and recently a puff pastry version - I preferred the puff pastry version, much less palm oil and other additives

Very disappointing. I made this according to the directions. When I took the first bite, I thought, "this is good." About 30 seconds later my teeth started hurting and I couldn't finish one bar. The sugar level of these is gross. If I ever try these again, I will use a fraction of the sugar and add lemon or something to make the flavor more interesting.
Really unexpectedly delicious. I was skeptical, but this came together beautifully.  My so has an egg allergy and cannot have most cheesecake. This was a lovely substitute.
Cut into 100 pieces?

It’s a pretty big rich recipe. When I make those crème de menthe brownies I also cut them into chocolate size pieces because they’re so rich. 
Wonder if you could use puff pastry sheets instead of crescent roll dough?
Do you press the dough to fill the 13x9 pan or leave the two dough pieces as is (since sometimes they come out of the package a little odd shaped)? Thanks!
The recipe says to unroll the dough to cover the bottom of the pan, so I suggest you make it fit the bottom. You can manually stretch it or roll it out a bit.
I don’t have a 13 X 9 glass dish. Is metal ok?

You might have to reduce the bake time since glass usually takes longer to bake.  Start checking for done at around 25-30 minutes.  But, according to the original article, overcooking didn't seem to harm the end result all that much.

This is a classic Texas recipe and seeing this is a great reminder to make it again. It’s definitely a “sum is greater than its parts” recipe. Cutting it into 100 pieces might be ok for a grazing board, but I’d say this makes 24 servings max.

I first tasted this yummy dish at a Christmas open house potluck in Georgia many years ago. I searched for a recipe online based on the ingredients I thought I could identify. No luck. I finally asked the host about it and she told me the name and the ingredients. I had to laugh at how simple it was. Pillsbury dough! Who knew!?

I made these in the 80s or early 90s. Yum! I hate to be the person who comments without making these (but I did make them 35 years ago!), but I cannot stand Pillsbury crescent rolls since they took the lard out (I assume) over 25 years ago. They are gross now.  And the biscuits in the can? gack.  Are there any alternatives besides homemade?

I shudder at the thought of making any recipe to fit a 9 x13 pan in our household of two. Easily halved using 8x8 pan.   I also halve huge cake recipes for 12-cup Bundt pans into a 6- cup Bundt. Only thing is baking time to consider. I wing it sometimes successfully.
This was very easy to make!  Decadent and fun and so simple. If I make it again, I’d cut the sugar topping by at least a third. I love sugar but even for me, it’s too much.
This sounds delicious. Any ideas for gluten free options?
Is the use of a glass dish important, or would a metal pan work just as well?

i'm a retired pastry chef- so even though i havent made this YET (it looks good), i see no reason why a metal pan wouldnt work.  the advantage of the glass pan is, i think, so you can see if the bottom pastry is getting nice and brown(the browner it gets the more flavor pastry has), or conversely, burning...

I made this exactly per the recipe and felt it was "meh."  Good, but not worth the calories.  Maybe I would like it better with puff pastry, but frankly, I would rather have St. Louis Gooey Butter Cake (that confection is worth every calorie!).
I found that it was swimming in melted butter. Maybe reduce to 1/3 cup?  I had to wick off a lot.
Same! It's still in the oven, but I'm skeptical 1/2 c is necessary.
Can this be frozen?
Dump ALL the melted butter on top or just enough to grab cinnamon sugar?  I've used about 1/4 cup and seems to be plenty. .
Had some trouble getting it out of the pan. Next time I put a big piece of parchment paper in the bottom and up the sides. in the fridge over night lifts right up, no mess.

I happened to have canned croissants in the fridge so I made these on a whim. They were a hit. I really thought the cinnamon sugar butter on top was overkill, but far be it from me to second-guess NYT Cooking. It turned out perfect.
An easy make. Everyone raved, even dieters took seconds

I made this in an 8x8 pan as "math geek" suggested and correspondingly cut the recipe in half. Next time, though, I won't cut the cream cheese filling in half. There wasn't enough of the really good stuff.

Baked exactly according to directions. Except I used less cinnamon. I have Penzey’s Vietnamese cinnamon and it’s really strong. It baked up perfectly in 40 minutes. I sprinkled on some gold sanding sugar, and will see how it all turns out tomorrow after spending some time in the fridge:)

OK, results after refrigerating for 24 hours: Yum. I’m glad I didn’t add zest to the filling, because it was perfect. The crunchy cinnamon sugar on top is an absolute must, and Vietnamese cinnamon is delicious. My only disappointment was that I kind of expected the crust to be crunchy, and it wasn’t. I may try this again with puff pastry since I have some in the freezer left over from the holidays.

We made this last night - so delish you can't believe it. Only had one crescent roll tube so I cut recipe in half. (BTW perfect for fam of 4) Only variation added chocolate chips to melting butter and poured over cake. Made it in a square corning ware dish... can't believe how easy it was to make after dinner. Really good...yummy!!! 
(Annie's Organic crescent rolls, or TraderJoe's always has them)

Made this this weekend and they were good, not drop dead spectacular. I would recommend them if you are looking for something fast, easy, and relatively inexpensive to make with readily available ingredients. I would make these little, sweet, cinnamon-y, finger-food bites for an office party or a pot-luck meal as they are easy to transport and easy to eat.
Delicious, but I think next time I'll use 3 packages of cream cheese. Has anyone ever tried that?
@Beth yes! I made 3 times as written and finally added another block of cc this time. It makes a much more robust cheesecake layer. Big improvement imho.

It’s amazing how great this tastes with very little effort.  Easier than cheesecake and possibly easier than a brownie!  Make sure the cheese is room temperature, or it’s hard to beat soft.  I used all the cinnamon which makes a nice sugar crust.  Assembles so quickly.
This was a big hit! I added an egg and two tablespoons of sour cream to the filling and added about 10 minutes to the baking time. It was really good!

Brought these to a party and they were really popular, but I found them a bit too sweet and I was bummed that the dough was no longer crisp after resting in the refrigerator. Super easy to assemble though. Also I would grease the pan, I had to work really hard to get them out.

Mom always spread a beaten egg white spread across the top before baking, along with the  sprinkle of cinnamon sugar and a handful of chopped nuts. This adds crunch to the final product and improves its texture exponentially.
Use 3 packages of cream cheese instead of 2. Same amount of sugar. More creamy filling,  less sweet.
Can you cut this recipe in half and bake it in an 8 inch square pan instead?? God knows I don't need all that temptation!!!! It sounds so simple and so delicious. Can't wait to make it!

8" x 8" = 64 sq in
9" x 13" = 117 sq in
117 sq in/2 = 58.5 sq in
For a PERFECT conversion, you'd need a pan that was 58.5 sq in, or 7 5/8" x 7 5/8"
However, I've always used an 8" x 8" pan when halving recipes for 9" x 13" pans and have never had a problem. One important thing: you may have to reduce the cooking time slightly as there is less mass to heat and cook
Please help me adjust the recipe for a higher elevation.
And what needs to be adjusted for high altitude? Oven temp? Baking time?
Puff pastry and rolls are very different.  Crescent rolls are closer to croissants.  If you can't find premade rolls or want to go from scratch I'd look for a crescent roll recipe.
Next time I'll try maple sugar or maybe almond instead of vanilla extract. It needs a little more interesting flavor.
I don’t have a glass baking dish. Will metal work?
This is one of those recipes that I'm embarrassed to love so much. Seeing it on NYT Cooking makes me feel better about it!
Would it make any sense to mix the cinnamon sugar with the melted butter, rather than pour the butter over, then add the cs?

The recipe as written results in a rather soupy topping at first, but as it bakes it slowly hardens.  There's nothing to lose with experimenting by mixing the melted butter and sugar.  It'll be delicious in any case.
Easy and delicious. My dough didn’t puff much, the bottom not at all. Wonder what I did wrong. Still very good.

Made this cake today. Followed the recipe except as suggested here earlier, I added zest of half an orange and also half a teaspoon of grand marnier.  The bottom and top crusts were not as flaky as I expected. The cheese in between them was delicious!
I just made this. One hundred squares? In what universe, teeny-tiny land? Maybe with a micro laser. I got 25 pieces, with a sharp knife, chilled.

This recipe is accurately named.  It tastes like cream cheese frosting sandwiched between two sopapillas (with the requisite cinnamon sugar.)  I cooked it and the pieces went fast - they lasted about 24 hours.  Very, very sweet.
Has anyone made this vegan and sugar-free? I think you could use Swerve sugar substitute and a vegan (Violife or Miyoko's) cream cheese and butter. Crescent rolls are already vegan.
Really makes about 32 pieces, not 100.
Yum!  I was skeptical it….  What a pleasant, wonderful surprise.  I will be making this after.
I really don't understand the final instruction. Must they sit in the fridge for 3 days, and then they're ready?  Are they only good for 3 days and then need to be tossed?  The writing is unclear.
Meh. Did not like the texture at all and the center filling was nothing to get excited about. I would never make these again. On to the next dessert.

Notes for Vegans: Pillsbury Crescent rolls are vegan. I made this with Philadelphia brand non-dairy cream cheese. They cooked 5 minutes longer, texture was good, flavor fine, “cheese” was grayish after cooking.  Maybe another brand would give prettier results? People gobbled them up despite color.
Try a drop of yellow or orange food coloring?
This was fantastic, and blindly following the directions resulted in little cookie morsels fit for a party!  Will try the lemon zest next time.   Everyone loves this treat.

These are good but not great. I made them exactly as directed, but the filling is smaller in proportion to the crust than as shown in the picture. Easy to make, but I don’t know that I’ll make them again.
This recipe was good but not great. I made it exactly as directed, but there was far less filling than the picture indicates. Easy to make, but I don’t think I’ll bake them again.

I used vegan cream cheese and it worked out very nicely. I did make one mistake though and overcooked it slightly- the center was already firm - not ‘slightly wobbly’ in the center. Still it kept well in the refrigerator for almost a week before it was all gone. A great pleaser for a group.
Does it matter if I don’t have a glass pan?
I found the dough fit better if I cut each sheet in half and placed each half crosswise in the pan. Would not change anything in the recipe. Perfectly sweet and I love the cinnamon.
Is it possible to freeze this?  I'm planning a retirement party and would like to make these ahead of time.
This is the second time I’ve made this, a half batch, using two 5”x5” square Pyrex dishes, a successful halving!

I added the orange zest (2 clementines worth) and also ... wanted more of a cheesecake presence, so I added 4 ounces more cream cheese and 1/4 additional sugar. Tasted great.  Super Bowl fare and leftovers were very welcome at the office.

Also recipe would seem to lend itself to cutting in half for smaller group (although my preference would be full-on CHEESECAKE).
We do not have crescent roll dough in Australia.  Would flaky pastry sheets work?  As I remember the crescent rolls were bread-ier, less like pastry.
@dotcole I believe this would be about the same as that’s all crescent dough is!
I've made this for almost 30 years. I got the recipe from a friend, she used brown sugar on top and not so much cinnamon. Always a big hit at pot luck parties!

title: Vegan Sopa de Maní (Bolivian Peanut Soup) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: I spent some of my elementary and high school years in Cochabamba- where for school lunch we would line up at the lunch kiosko to get a big bowl of soup, freshly fried potato chips and a glass bottle of soda. Sopa de maní, sopa de quinoa, sopa de fideo- all DELICIOUS and made right there in a big pot. That’s really all you need for lunch.
After three years of loving my NYT recipes subscription I will drop first ever note: This soup is FANTASTIC. I’m eating it out of the pot as I write this.
You can find raw peanuts at Trader Joe’s, and I’m sure whole foods as well. But TJs has good prices on nuts.
I have no problem finding raw peanuts at our Asian market. Try there.
This better be really good, or my pantry will be stocked with half a dozen things I will never use again.

A great thing about new recipes can be new ingredients to work into your repertoire in additional ways, with or without a specific recipe. I'm keen to be introduced to quilquiña and to stir it into a batch of egg salad, etc. And then there are the things that can be easily subbed, e.g., replacing the specific bouillon here with whatever you tend to use for concentrated (not-)chicken stock. I hope that folks are turned off of a new cuisine for fear that they'll be stuck with useless ingredients.

I had never used raw peanuts before but happened to have them from a gift and I had no idea what to use them for. Viola!; I will keep some around in the future because we are a vegan household and the peanuts made the most wonderful cream substitute ever! I had to leave out the locato chili powder but everything was already in our fridge. Getting the skins off the peanuts was pretty fast and the soup was delicious. A meal with a simple salad.

I made this without the quilquiña and locoto as my usual sources (Amazon, etc) did not carry them. Providing a link to an online source for such unusual ingredients would help. Before adding the potato and peas, I tasted it for seasoning. It was a bit on the bland side so I added a bit more cumin and a half cup of aji panca, an ingredient I had recently bought for another recipe I found here at the Times. That did the trick and the end result was a nicely flavored soup that was worth the effort.

Delicous soup.  Very forgiving recipe.  Don't have quilquina?  Make this soup.  It will be good.  Do you have lightly roasted peanuts instead of raw or blanched peanuts?  Not a problem.  Make this soup.  Have a bunch of cilantro but only a fistful of parsley?  Don't be deterred.  Make this soup.  Its going to work and it will be yummy.

This sopa is earthy and delicious. Not convinced about using penne here, although the toasting method is interesting. Maybe pearl barley, polenta, brown rice, or some other alternative. Will try next time a little sweet potato with Yukon golds. Substituted urfa biber and New  Mexico chili and dried cilantro. Increased cumin a bit.

I made this as closely to the original recipe as I could but I did use dry roasted peanuts and left out some ingredients I didn’t have and used a jalapeño pepper. 
I shared this soup with two other people and we all loved it. 
I am going to try the toasting of the pasta with other soups when I use added pasta.
This was fantastic. The time required was accurate, and well worth it.
Can I use natural peanut butter in place of the fresh peanuts?
I haven't tried- but I would guess if you match the weight, not volume, you would be okay.
All the peanut butters I have seen use roasted peanuts, so the flavor would be different

Roasting peanuts such as for peanut butter will change the flavor somewhat. A bigger issue is the consistency. Even with just the first 1 and 1/2 cup water, the consistency was thinner than most peanut butter.
Where does one buy raw peanuts?  I don’t see them in grocery stores
Grocery stores with large bulk sections often have them.
Someone above posted that Trader Joe carries them! Who knew?

I have not made this particular recipe before but yes, you can use peanut butter but I would use the all natural, no sugar/salt brands. I have made a similar recipe and I am so excited to try this one. If you love nutritional yeast, go for it. I’m just ok with it so I may leave it out because some of the other ingredients provide that taste umami or otherwise that makes you feel warm and cozy while eating this soup. It has many of the same ingredients I have used in my recipe which I have lost.

Though beautiful and inviting in the market, Quilquiña/Papalo isn't for everyone. It can be quite offensive to some palates. I'd recommend the Cilantro substitution, besides the fact Quilquiña/Papalo won't be easy to find for many - thank heavens. I've worked with thousands and thousands of ingredients and none made me spit it out onto the floor and literally lick my husband's shirt trying to remove the awfulness like this did. Horrid. To say it's stronger than Cilantro is an understatement.

This is a beautiful soup, and very much worth the effort.
I deviated from the vegan paradigm and used chicken stock, and substituted the suggested dried cilantro. Otherwise prepared as written.
The result was a full bodied soup with a pleasing subtle flavor. Served with already prepared potato sticks atop. Next time I may make my own potato sticks.

I didn’t have raw peanuts, but I had raw cashews in the freezer, so I subbed those. I didn’t have a regular potato so I used a sweet potato. I subbed a Tbsp of Calabrian chili paste for the minced ancho chili. Absolutely amazing! On the second night, we combined it with homemade beer bread.
This soup is fantastic. In addition to the serrano I also added a habanero, I used gluten free pasta (rummo makes the best one), and hickory sticks on top. Will definitely make this again.
Any ideas for online sources for locoto powder and quilquina? Thanks!

Another name for locate is rococo, found on line
Anther name for quilquina is papalo.   Also on line.
Tastes just like home!! Delicious, cozy and comforting. Can’t believe it’s this good and vegan, I was stoked to find this recipe. Thank you for featuring Bolivian food!

I’ve also struggled to find raw peanuts, even living as I do in major metro area. To order online seems too risky, as rancid nuts are useless.
(They would be a key ingredient in my “cracker jack granola “ If I could find them)
have you tried a bulk bin place?

Look in your Asian market- a lot of cooking uses raw peanuts that people toast themselves. Also, if you live near an African community, certain countries in Africa, use raw peanuts- they call them ground nuts.
I’ve had this soup since I was a kid and it is not appropriate to make it with peanut butter.
Has anyone tried using the equivalent to 4 cubes of Better Than Bouillon for this recipe?
I used my standard vegetable broth.  It turned out great.  Very pleased with this recipe!

I did but we have the jars so I used the Better than Bouillon Vegetable one. Plus some homemade vegetable broth. I can't compare to the original recipe because I didn't have those, but ours had a very nice flavor.
is the penne cooked first in water, ..or do you toast  without boiling first
No, you toast it without cooking it first.

Does it really need salt, in addition to the bouillon which is so high in sodium?  I’m thinking I’ll either use less bouillon or no additional salt, when I get around to trying this delicious-looking recipe!
Taste it before adding the additional salt. If it works for you then you can skip it.
This is just Mmmmmmmmmmm.   A winter-warmer. Has the cozy comforting creaminess of a pot pie.  Hard to believe its vegan!  Can’t wait to try with the two unusual herbs/spices mentioned.
This was ok. Fairly bland. When puréeing peanuts. Make smooth before addding water

Beautiful looking soup that I wanted to love based on other reviews but didn't. It was very, very bland. Made a half-batch but added whole potato and 1/2 cup macaroni (out of penne), needs something more. Don't know if I'll make it again.
Fantastic. Did not have time for matchstick potatoes. Yet was amazing. Added a dash of lemon juice.
Did anyone use peanut butter for this in place of the raw peanuts?

title: Spaghettini With Bottarga and Colatura Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: I got grated Alma bottarga di muggine at Eataly for $15.90 for a 40g jar, it’s fine and a more practical portion than the whole roe sac which goes for $30 or more, it’s over $100 a pound. The fish sauce is called anchovy sauce at Eataly, they carry Agostino Recca for $10.90 for a 100ml bottle. The whole jarred yellow tomatoes are $5.90 for 12 oz. I spent $37.20 on what would feed three people including the pasta.
This recipe is part of my favorite pasta of all time except I add mountains of fresh parsley and toasted, buttered bread crumbs.  For those who can't find botarga, anchovies are fine in a pinch.

I know you'll all probably find this shocking, but I don't have bottarga di muggine in my fridge and I'm not getting something that I'll use for one recipe and costs $22 plus shipping. So what is a good substitute?

There is no exact substitute for bottarga*, but you might try cured egg yolks. It's a separate project, and a different flavor, but Soy sauce-cured egg yolks could be used to get into the "neighborhood." Here's a recipe: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017850-soy-sauce-pickled-eggs Use Japanese Tamari. For this purpose of, I'd increase the kombu for more flavor of the ocean. Cure for 3 days. Grate. *bottarga derives from "egg pickle" in Greek.
@Pass the Anchovies, you can buy the grated form on Amazon for cheaper and free shipping. Works great in scrambled eggs too.

Had no time to buy bottarga & colatura (plus kinda pricey when cooking this for the first time), so I went with tarama (not taramasalata!) which is salted carp roe. Had that in my fridge. It costs just around $12 for a 10oz jar, and you can still use it to make taramasalata. Used asian fish sauce carefully. Also made the yellow tomato sauce as described but with fresh heirloom tomatoes, and cooked them with some olive oil and s p for 10 mins. Sauce came out delicious & very easy. Loved the dish!
