My wife, who is Italian, and I were excited to try this dish.  We followed the recipe and doubled the ingredients to yield 2 servings.  For the Bottarga we used 5 tablespoons of Supreme Bottarga (2x the recipe for 1 Serving) and found that it overpowered the dish, turning it bitter.  I see several other comments that are similar.  I just checked a calculator online and see that 1/2 oz is 1 tablespoon, not 2 1/2 tablespoons, as specified in this recipe.
What did you serve with this pasta? Was thinking Arugula salad and ?!?!

I used the grated dried bottarga like another reader suggested. Otherwise I followed the recipe. I did not like the flavor at all. I like salty fishy flavors but this was beyond bitter and sour. I had to doctor it up so much with lemon juice and pepper seeds, and it still wasn’t good. I actually added cheese to it and that kind of made it eatable. I don’t know what happened, I seem to be the only one who didn’t like it.
I love the flavor produced by these ingredients, as I live in Milano these ingredients are easy to purchase, I find that if you can't find colatura, use grated fresh lemon rind.

I made this last night and it was fantastic. I made a sauce with yellow cherry tomatoes and I sautéed the garlic in the oil before adding the tomatoes instead of using the fresh garlic. I prepared the sauce in individual serving bowls and then divided the pasta evenly in each bowl and tossed. Everyone loved it!
In the photograph - is that really thin spaghetti?  It appears much thicker but perhaps it's just a visual trick.
This dish is outstanding. It doesn't need any additional ingredients. Its simplicity is what makes the "marine" flavor profile come through so beautifully and makes the dish absolutely perfect.
All, you can buy all the specialized ingredients from Amazon, most available next day.
Does anyone know where to find Bottarga in NYC ? I know eataly sells colatura. Not sure where to find yellow cherry tomato sauce, but it's probably not too difficult.
Amazon
Caputo's in Carroll Gardens, and Di Palo's in Little Italy used to - not sure if they still do.
Gustiamo.com carries wonderful bottarga, yellow tomatoes and colatura di alici. That's how we discovered a recipe quite similar to this one, actually.
Does anyone have a wine pairing to suggest wine to pair with this?
I would do an Alsatian Gewurztraminer; and if you must go red, go with something with light tannins and lower on the acid, like maybe an Italian Dolcetto.
Yes, Gewurz from Alsace would be excellent, as would be an Alsatian Riesling or an even Pinot Gris from the Pacific NW.
Can this be scaled?  Could I just quadruple everything and make it the exact same way for 4 servings?

Diego Rossi recommends preparing one serving at a time: “ The sweetness of the tomato contrasts with the bitterness of the bottarga. It’s best to make the pasta in single servings, as at Trippa, to control that bitterness.”
Can you substitute fish sauce for colatura?

As mentioned in the "Tips"
If you do not have colatura on hand, you may substitute ½ to 1 teaspoon Asian fish sauce. Because Asian fish sauce is stronger in flavor than colatura, be sure to taste and adjust as you go.
Can anchovy paste be used for the anchovy extract? Is it the same thing?

The suggestion to use Asian fish sauce is the best substitute. Use Red Boat brand. It's not expensive, keeps for a long time, and is used in many recipes.

Anchovies or anchovy paste are completely different.

The main problem I had is that the pasta cooled excessively during the tossing, so it was practically a cold dish. Next time, I'll be sure to preheat the bowl I mix it in. As far as flavor, I had to punch it up a bit with the chili flakes and lemon, but I was happy that the fish flavors were delicate.
For two people why not double the recipe instead of doing each serving separately. Since the sauce gets “cooked “ by the warm pasta shouldn’t that work?

Super easy and very satisying.  While the bottarga is difficult to find (for me) and expensive to purchase, a little goes a long way.  Colatura is not expensive and is more readily available.  I would estimate ~ 10 to 12 USD per person for all the ingredients, assuming good eaters.  My first attempt wasn't bad at all, but I can see room for subtle improvement in technique.

A supertaster I know claimed adding pasta water back into a sauce killed the taste of the sauce.  I’m inclined to agree, especially with this recipe, yet it is a very commonly proposed technique for pasta sauces.  What do NYT cooks think?

I didn't have the yellow tomato sauce and followed the directions to place yellow cherry tomatoes in boiling water to take off the skins.  The next time I make this dish, I'll use the sauce and perhaps add a tsp. of sugar to offset the bitterness of the mullet roe.  But this was a tasty dish and certainly amenable to the late night use of pantry staples.

The treasure hunt seemed worth it to me - this was delicious, simple and not like everything else. 
I’ll make it again, now and then. Per previous notes, I topped the pasta with lemon zest, a sprinkle of panko browned in butter and chopped parsley, and paired with a bitter lettuce salad and Gewurtraminer. Yum. Yum! Thanks everybody.
Taking all the seeds out of the cherry tomatoes was tiresome but then my husband used his finger to wipe out his bowl to get every last drop of the sauce and I decided it was worth it. This is yummy.

The only jarred yellow tomato sauce I could find was too acidic and loaded with oregano, thyme and other herbs so it clashed badly with the colatura and bottarga.  So as a second try I sauteed garlic with calabrian chiles on low heat, then added spaghetti, a splash of lemon and colutura.  Then I grated bottarga on top in the bowl and finished with parsley. This was a winner and fine without tomatoes!
2nd time making this, Eataly was out of yellow tomato sauce so I bought large yellow tomatoes to make the sauce. They were much easier to peel and de-seed and still low-acid.

Add canned clams sautéed in butter and white wine for a touch more texture and protein. Finish with parsley, and a dash of red pepper flakes/olive oil.
Home cooked pasta doesn’t get better than this.

The colatura di alici is produced by curing the fish with salt. The suggestion to "heavily salt" the water in which the pasta is cooked is incorrect. Boiling water should be salted to a minimum, if so.

This sounded so intriguing -- such a beautifully written story -- and  happened to have a bar of bottarga (avgotaraho in Greek) plus a jar of the grated roe. Here in Athens Trikalinos bottarga or avgotaraho has been the gold standard of this delicacy for decades. You can find it in the US. José Andrés touts it and it has won international awards. I used oyster sauce, faute de mieux, olive oil, and chopped parsley. I still love bottarga sliced thin with a bit of lemon, but this was yummy.
Cento makes canned yellow tomatoes about a third of the price of jarred ones.

The recipe is a good one and I'm glad to see it doesn't have the usual garlic overkill of many Times recipes. As far as wine to accompany it, try a Sicilian white -- grillo, inzolia, Etna bianco, or a southern Italian falanghina.
I am not sure which is more Boujee the recipe ingredients or the comments.  If you have salted carp roe in your fridge this is the 15 minute pasta for you!

I made this tomight. I got the ingredients from Amazon free shipping,  Highly recommend it! Very subtle taste and is delicious. Goes well with a sauvignon blanc. Thank you NYT for bringing unheard of recipes to us!
The garlic absolutely killed every other flavor. Will not be using next time.

So I made this exactly as written with the exception that I topped with some sautéd breadcrumbs and Italian parsley.  It was…ok.  My husband LOVES pasta in any iteration and he was pretty meh about it.  Very disappointed because on paper, this sounds divine.

This was terrible. I used the colatura di alici and the bottarga di muggine that I always use. I made my own yellow tomato sauce. The result was barely edible, starkly bitter, no nuance. A few hours before I made the recipe, I sent a note to a Roman friend to tell him I'd be trying out this maybe wacky recipe. His response came too late. "I hope I am in time to warn you that bottarga and colatura are not a good combination"...he began. Dixit.
Gavi goes great with this.

I have an overly abundant tomato garden so I used fresh sungold cherry tomatoes - doubling the tomato sauce, 12 leaves of fresh basil, a full garlic clove, and had to substitute fish sauce (half) for the colatura.
I added parmesean at the end because... I have to.  Other than that I followed the recipe carefully.  FANTASTIC!
I love this recipe! I found everything at Eataly in NYC, although the yellow cherry tomatoes are not a sauce, just jarred yellow tomatoes. wondering if I need to cook them like i did this time?

I grew up hitting up the North Shore garlic shrimp trucks in Oahu and this recipe doesn’t disappoint. I’ve been making this recipe from the original blog since 2020, and it’s a hitter - easy weeknight dish or great dinner party dish for garlic lovers. The key is to dry your shrimp out to get the crisp, with or without shell. I dry the shrimp in the fridge between towels on a baking sheet the night before. I’ll also amp up the garlic and pulse it in a food processor with oil.
Shrimp shells are edible, and are a source of glucosamine!  They add a bit of crunch.
Lots of things are edible...but shrimp shells just aren't fun to eat.
@M. Boy!  Am I glad to hear I’m not the only one

@M. Boy!  And I glad to hear I’m not the only person who love shrimp shells.  I also like the tail.  Keep in mind this is for the thin shelled smaller shrimp only.  The big shrimp shells are for stock only!
The shells would seem to be scarcely edible, so why are the shells left on before the crunchy coating is added?

The patted
dry shrimp cannot get the rice flour and the condiments to adhere
to the shrimp.
Just douse the shrimp in beaten egg white before coating, and  follow the rest of the
procedure, and you get
a beautifully
crisp and crunchy coating.

My personal preference is no shells. I cooked this recipe as written but with peeled jumbo shrimp
and it was fabulous!

Delicious (how wrong can you go w/shrimp, butter, (LOTS of) garlic, and lemon?  I used 2 heads of garlic, and only had APF on hand.  I'd caution against browning the garlic. In my experience, incl. this one, garlic that browns is bitter, period (and mine was only slightly browned).  Didn't ruin the dish, but next time I'll cook the garlic slowly and pull it off the heat just as the first bits start to brown.
Cooking with the shell on adds much richness to the flavor as the shrimp cooks in its own juices.

To devein shrimp with shells on, use kitchen scissors. Simply snip up the back and pull out the dark vein. The shells will still stay on. I always eat them, but if you don’t want that extra crunch, they are easy to pull off if you have cut them this way.
How do you devein with shells on??  Maybe I’m doing it wrong?
I was really wary about the shells on but it was delicious. I served it with spicy grits and discovered a whole new way to make shrimp and grits. Try it!

A better use for the shell would be to add them to a shrimp or fish stock or in the court-boullion for making shrimp cocktail.  Just because you can eat them doesn't mean you should.  Your guests will just peel them off and look at you funny.
How is it possible to devein a shrimp with the shell ON?
You cut down the back of the shrimp with a paring knife and pull out the vein.
How does one devein a shrimp still in the shell?

Shrimp molt off their old shell as they grow.  Their new shell is right underneath the one they shed. It is very soft - like a soft shell crab.  It is hard as the dickens to peel cooked shrimp with new, soft shells.  But they are perfectly edible, except for the legs and tail.  Although I have seen people in Hawaii and other areas where shrimp are caught pop the entire thing, sans head, in their mouths.
Used regular flour and peeled shrimp. Delicious.
This was so good, going to make again for sure. Used 4 cloves of garlic and could have used more, otherwise followed exactly.
Difference between regular rice flour and mochiko? What’s the “sweet” rice flour factor?  Thanks!

I was happy to see this recipe just a few days after a Hawaii vacation. We had this at a food truck on the North shore. This recipe was very good. I did peel the shrimp before cooking, so much nicer and less messy to eat!

This was really really good! I didn’t use anywhere near the amount of cayenne advised in recipe, I used about 1/4 of a teaspoon and probably could’ve added more. 
We felt lemon was a must, as well as having very dry shrimp before tossing in flour in mix.
A KEEPER OF 5 STARS!
Is there a decent substitute for the sweet rice flour? I want to try this but dislike buying a box of something that doesn’t get regular use in my kitchen. Thx!
Read the tip: sub AP flour.

You can use mochiko to make butter mochi. 
Google it, it's delicious!
Omg, so good! Roasted broccoli and cauliflower and then tossed in the garlic butter with the shrimp. Used cassava flour instead of rice and hot paprika .
So good and so fast- Great for a weeknight dinner. Very similar to what I've had from the food trucks on the North Shore. I keep tail on shrimp in the freezer and this will be in regular rotation.
I like eating crispy shrimp shells, but, only if they are small to medium. Larger then that, for me, they are to much like trying to bite a piece of plastic.
Isn't a teaspoon of cayenne for 3/4 pound shrimp a lot? When I've cooked with that much pepper, it aerates and makes the entire kitchen smell like pepper for a day or two.

Just adding my 2 cents.  This tastes great, everyone loved it, but don’t skimp on the garlic.  I ran short and i feel like it missed it.  Also, i used deshelled and deveined shrimp and it was wonderful, and i think a better fit for my guests.

Am I the only one who thought there was WAY too much paprika in this dish? I’ve eaten at the North Shore food trucks, and this dish, while tasty in its own right, was nothing like what I got there. The paprika absolutely overpowered EVERYTHING, which — in a dish with as much garlic as this has — is really a feat. I’ll make this again, but I’ll use MAYBE a teaspoon, certainly not a tablespoon, of paprika.

To devein a shrimp with the shell on, fold the shrimp in half around its legs to part the shell pieces along the backside, slip a toothpick crosswise about a quarter inch below the surface, between the shell pieces (perpendicular to the length of the shrimp) and pull straight up. The digestive tract will come out with it. 

That said, this recipe is bomb as hell and if you cook the shrimp right- dry em out and coat them, fry them hot enough- the shells really are just a bit of crunch.
I washed the shrimp in a beaten egg mixture before frying. Ingredients adhered nicely.
I was surprised  to find this rather dull, won’t make it again. A basic shrimp scampi would be preferable and easier.
Use a frying pan, and don't press the shrimp as they suggest.

Made this for my birthday dinner this weekend. We LOVED it. I was concerned about the amount of cayenne, but it wasn't an issue. The "heat" starts a little at the back of the mouth and it was nothing. I cleaned the shrimp and removed their legs and heads, but left the shells intact. My guests got messy. I ate the shrimp, shells and all. Hey... I'm getting old. I NEEDED THE ROUGHAGE. 
Really, terrific! Served with (handmade) pasta with fried lemons and chile flakes. Balanced tastes.
Has anyone actually made this and eaten the shell? How was it? Huladanser's without the shell approach is more intuitive to me.

I can’t see how leaving the shell on works in this recipe…I’m not averse to eating shells but I don’t think they would get crispy enough (teppanyaki style) to eat. Agree also, how can you devein a shrimp with the shell on?

The shrimp wasn’t very crisp/crunchy and the sauce was too hot for our tastes.  The lemon helped cut it, as did the rice (and I threw in some frozen peas for color, veggie vitamins and flavor.). But still not crispy and too spicy.
Made as directed with just a little more butter.  Absolutely delicious!
Should the shrimp be raw to begin with? Most supermarkets offer shrimp raw or cooked. It would be helpful to know, for this recipe and others, which is called for.
Use raw shrimp.  Cooking a shrimp twice would make it dry and rubbery.
How does one devien teh shrimp with the shell on?

Cut the shell in the back and devein the shrimp as usual.  The shell should stay on.  I doubt, however, that eating the shells is particularly tasty, even if possible, and the reason for it is most likely that the original sellers of this street-food found it easier to sell the shrimp with the shell on.
Tasty
Delicious, followed the recipe. A keeper in our house of two, but will double the recipe from now on, as 3/4 lb. Of shrimp was just enough to leave us hangry.
4 tbl butter = 1/4 C

Cooked exactly as directed using the suggestion of drying shrimp on a towel covered baking sheet in the fridge. 

Next time I will remove the shells. I had pink Key West shrimp on hand and the shells were too much. I might try it again when I can get wild caught Georgia shrimp as the shells seem thinner. 

Spice, flavor and crunch from the rice flour were on point!

This is amazing. Next time I make this (and there will be a next time) I am going to use 2 lemons instead of 1.

I used normal flour, it worked great.

Hadn’t been to Hawaii to try the food truck version but we had a dozen frozen (cooked) jumbo shrimp with tails left and tried this recipe with them to use them up. Made the same amount of coating but cut back a little on the cayenne, maybe 3/4 tsp and it still had a wonderful kick. Next time full out! Used a little less butter, garlic and oil because of fewer shrimp. Didn’t have a fresh lemon so just sprinkled with organic lemon juice. I ate the tails, my hubby didn’t. We both loved it!  Keeper!
Delicious!! Made it exactly as written and I loved it. Next time I'll double the broccoli and maybe a little less butter for the garlic but this is a keeper

This is the best recipe on the internet. I will be hard pressed to make shrimp any other way again! We used tail on shrimp because eating shells didn’t sound appealing to us. The only slight modification I made is I poured the garlic butter out of the pan when it was just barely starting to golden. It continued to cook in the hot butter in the bowl and also returns to the pan. Perfectly delicious!! We were all licking our fingers and fighting over seconds!

Be careful of burning the garlic.  They were perfect until the one minute at the end.  Next time I'll turn off the heat BEFORE adding back the garlic and butter.  Still a hit with my garlic and shrimp loving husband.

Wonderfully written recipe. I subbed in all-purpose flour because all of my rice flour was sacrificed in a butter mochi experiment. All-purpose gives it more a 'Popeyes'-texture fry instead of a crispy, light coating like you would get from the rice flour. I also removed the shells from my shrimp during prep because I just can't be bothered to peel as I eat. 
After growing up on the island of Oahu, I will say no recipe can beat what you get fresh off a shrimp truck. But this is a good start!

AMAZING OMG
The shrimp was pretty salty, so I recommend halving!
Fantastic. I had some green onions and processed those with the garlic before adding to the warm butter.

2 Tbsp Panko
1 Tbsp flour
Scant 1 tsp paprika
1 tsp coarse kosher salt

Two big spoonfuls of jar garlic — don’t cook too long in butter.

For shell-less shrimp, cook about 2 mins per side for first batch, then taper down with each successive batch. Last batch is prob 60-90 secs per side.

The skillet temperature when you cook the shrimps can get very hot. The butter may overheat and the garlic may brown and become bitter when poured with the shrimps. Also, the excess flour/spices coating may burn and impart an unpleasant taste. Next time, I will use a separate skillet for the shrimps and the butter/garlic, and will plop the shrimps, once cooked, into the butter/garlic skillet to finalize the dish. 
Finally, the shells prevent the flour/spice coating from sticking to the shrimps.
This is a family fave. Pink shrimp seem to have shells that are soft enough to eat, but become perfectly crunchy. Agree that the wetness is key to getting the mixture to stick. Great recipe!
I have deveined shell-on shrimp with a sharp paring knife. It cuts right through the shell.
Delicious.  I used peeled and deveined shrimp which turned out crispy, spicy and oh so tasty.  I will make this again - and again.
Quick, easy and so, so tasty! Great fast weeknight recipe.

I’ve eaten shrimp with the shells on before, and while I don’t hate it I definitely prefer shells off. So I made it that way and it was delicious. I made as written other than adding broccoli to the pan before the shrimp to add a veggie for a pretense of healthy eating.

Disappointing. I did use rice flour but it didn't stick nearly as well as I expected and the shrimp didn't end up with crusty shells. The jumbo shrimp in my market were 8/lb, probably larger than those in the photo with the recipe. Even though I often eat shrimp shells, these were so thick and tough that I had to take them off the shrimp to eat. Maybe because the spice mixture didn't stick so well or because it was all on the shells which I couldn't eat, the dish was not as tasty as I expected.
Mmmmm, a bit average to say the least. Suggest that you use the shrimp for another recipe such as scampi.

Followed recipe with minor adjustments: subbed regular rice flour, and chili crisp for cayenne. Added a fair amount of crushed ginger.

Came out great. Super jumbo Argentine shrimp (9/16) from Costco. Cleaning was a chore, but that's on me.

Very easy, once shrimp cleaned, delicious! Will repeat.

Make it as is (triple it because 3/4lb of shrimp is a snack). Make sure you fry a whole head of chopped garlic on low in the butter. Makes a perfect crunchy topping. This was just so delicious, a definite add to the “what do I do with this shrimp” rotation. Good with coconut rice and roasted carrots and parsnips I had.

This was very enjoyable.  It tasted even better the next day.  Definitely use the amount of garlic and butter - it makes a big difference in flavor.  I lessened the amount of cayenne and served over coconut rice with roasted asparagus.

I loved it but I cut way back on the cayenne for my kids. I also have super charged cayenne from a friend so I ended up using about 1/2 a tsp for 2 lbs shrimp. Believe it or not it was still a bit spicy. I would have gone spicier if making for adults who like spice.
Subbed in cornstarch for flour. Perfectly crispy!
Why do you keep the shell on?

@Library980

So that you get garlic butter all over your fingers when you peel them to eat them.

TO DEVEIN WITH SHELLS ON:  I haven’t tried this but just saw it done — insert toothpick or pointed bamboo skewer (I think this would work better) between second and third joint just far enough down to get under the vein and pull up.

?  How can anyone eat shrimp shells?

And how can you devein shrimp without  removing the shells?

Why devein? It is time consuming and does not add anything to the recipe. 
I have been eating fresh caught shrimp boils  from the sounds of North Carolina all my life. No difference in deveined or as is.

I remember the original shrimp truck on Kam Highway in Kahuku during the decade that I lived on the North Shore. It was one of my favorite meals. The shrimp were so big and sweet. It was always a real treat. One day I was driving with a girl and feeling hungry and I suggested that we stop at the shrimp food truck to eat. She said that she was not going to eat from a food truck. I was already looking forward to my shrimp meal so I told her that she'll just have to watch me eat from the food truck

title: Norinj Pilau (Rice With Candied Orange Peel, Saffron and Lamb) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: Where do you get sour oranges?
Hispanic/Caribbean markets, and some large chain supermarkets have them also.  My local Trade Fair has them.
@Michael I’ve seen them at Persian and Middle Eastern grocery stores
Can this be made with regular oranges, and if so, would any modifications need to be made??? Thanks.
My mother makes this with any orange that has a thick enough peel...I always have organic navel or valencia oranges handy when she visits! ;)

If you've never tasted this, it really is a magical, delicious dish.   The combination of flavors is unique and probably one of the best and most distinctive from the Afghani food repertoire. Hard to replicate, however, but worth a try!  The side of good yogurt is a must.

The Afghan refugees who lived at our home in New Delhi in the 1980's made do with a brilliant substitute: candied carrot strips, which they would combine with the rice and lamb as described above. It was still very tasty although I can't wait to try the original recipe which inspired the make-do substitute!
You can find sour oranges in Latin, Caribbean, and South Asian markets.

This is delicious! Next time, I will take 2 cups of rice to mix with the orange peel juice. One cup did not cover the entire top, as shown in the picture. I used regular oranges for the orange peel, as I could not find seville oranges. I will also use more orange peel next time.

Delicious! It's not a weekday dish as it takes most of the day unless you make the rice in advance. I used regular orange peels for the syrup and added some lemon juice to make it more tart. I loved the orange-syrup flavored rice that went on the top, but did not get that bright orange color shown in the image, except for the candied peels. Careful of the timing when boiling the rice - 7 min was too long for me and it was a bit mushy. Still very tasty. Don't skip the pistachios.

I halved the recipe, and used a rice cooker to parcook the rice (turn it off when it goes from "cook" to "warm"). I had made candied orange peel from navel oranges a few weeks ago to garnish a rosca de reyes, so I just sliced that thinly and added cardamom, saffron and sugar and water for the orange syrup. Since the lamb pieces were larger and contained bones, I removed the meat from the bones after braising it and added it to the rice. So tasty!!! Would make again. Definitely comfort food...

I neglected to notice that the recipe called for cut up lamb shank, and I had a whole shank. I added (a lot) more water to properly braise it, and did it for 2 and a half hours. I then took the lamb meat off the bone, strained out the onions, and boiled the liquid down to just a half cup or so. That worked well.
i buy dried orange peel slivers from the arab market on the corner. they are dried in a bag and very cheap!
Could you use cumquats rather than seville oranges
Sourcing sour oranges is the problem here.  I’ve seen the 20 lb. box before.  Visited a Hispanic grocery but no luck.   Would love to get my hands on some.

Sounds delicious. It uses the same method with the rice as the Iranians for their chela and is reminiscent of the Indian biriani (itself of Persian origin). I've only had good experiences with the chela. One thing bugs me. Simmering already parboiled basmati in syrup for 25 minutes sounds like a recipe for disaster. Unless you want a kind of paste at the end. And you should most definitely keep a close eye on it for signs of catching.

"Simmering already parboiled basmati in syrup for 25 minutes sounds like a recipe for disaster. "

Not at all. I did this and the orange scented rice came out beautifully-- with individual grains. Note that the cup of parboiled rice is cooked in only 1/4 cup of syrup, so it's more steamed than boiled. And I'll note that mine was very orange (I'll admit I may have used more saffron than called for).
I used Mandarin oranges for this and it worked. Dish was delicious but also if you are not a fan of sweet and savory it may not be for you.

You can get the -seeds- for sour oranges on ETSY. The oranges themselves (Seville oranges) are available in quantity (20lbs!) from a restaurant store online. Some other recipes for this dish indicate you CAN use regular orange peel.

Really delicious and way worth the time. A couple of suggestions: I didn't get the "bone-in shanks cut in 1 1/2" chunks. Supposed to bring out the saw? I just cut the meat off the bone and removed tendons. (A job!) I would reduce rice to 3 cups. Amazing flavor and beautiful!

I tried it with the rind of blood oranges and came close to the special taste of a narinj; though the bitterness of the blood orange really makes it a different dish taste. Still, without narinj, what can be done to get the real taste of this simple and elegant dish?
IIRC in the same aisle that has Goya products -- Goya sells bottled juice from naranja agra.
Meyer lemon. Seville oranges. Often one can find suitable oranges in hispanic markets'

These oranges grow decoratively in southern Arizona cities like Tucson. As I remember it, they mostly fall to the sidewalk, staining it. I've never heard of a use for them or seen them sold. Sounds like a great dish.
Seville oranges also make the best bitter marmalade- Paddington Bear’s favorite kind!
How much water to cook the rice in?
You are draining it, like pasta, so it's however much you need for the pot.
Would this recipe work with goat meat (mutton)?

Hi! What if I can't find sour oranges? Is there a way to mimic the taste of the Candied Sour Orange Peel using regular, supermarket oranges? Maybe by adding lemon? Or lime? Would appreciate any tips. Thank you!

An outstanding recipe, thankyou!! I was a bit dubious at first that there are hardly any added spices in this recipe. I couldn't resist and did crack by adding 2 laurel leaves to the lamb stew, but that was it. The result is just perfect. It is the balance between the lamb and orange peel that make this dish. Lamb is so flavorful on its own so you can count on that. As a diabetic I halved the sugar content and am unsure if I would like it any sweeter to be honest. Thx again

I followed one of the notes to use 3 cups of rice instead of 4 because my largest pot is 6 qt.  It worked beautifully and I think I preferred the larger ratio of orange flavored rice.  This is delicious and while it does take a long time, it isn’t a lot of active time.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 5 minutes, plus at least 2 hours’ cooling

comments.commentBody: Why is the meringue made before blending the other ingredients, and then re-beaten? Is there a reason not to mix the other ingredients first, and then beat the meringue and fold in?

The pan must be plain, not non-stick, and not greased, because the batter needs something to cling to as it rises. And re the order of mixing—baking powder begins to work when it gets wet, so you don’t want that sitting around very long. I would probably get the dry and wet ingredients mixed, then do merengue, then combine the wet and dry, then incorporate the merengue.

@Lee if you use double acting baking powder, some may be activated when it gets wet but there is plenty left over and it will activate with the heat of the oven. At the shop where I worked, we made a ton of cake batter and refrigerate it for the week, using it as needed. We had no problem with our bakes.
The Betty Crocker version was my mom’s specialty. She’ll be 100 next week and I’ll bake this for her. Thanks for the memory.

Meringue will not deflate if egg whites are beat properly. 1) 68°F (20°C) egg whites; 2) on med low until just foamy, but translucent about 1 minute; 3) add cream of tartar, then beat on med low until volume increases and eggs are white and creamy; 4) GRADUALLY add sugar while beating—do not dump it in at once; 5) after sugar is added, increase mixer speed to med high and beat to desired peak level.

It has to do with stabilizing ionic bonds and preventing disulfide bonds.
Potato starch is often sold as potato flour.  In addition to Bob’s Red Mill, sold in stores and online, you can often find it in a Kosher or Jewish products aisle of a grocery store.
How would you make this lemon instead of orange since tip notes acidity can be a problem?
Two options; Meyer lemons (far less acidic) or a mix of lemon and a mild orange/mandarin for the juice.  Zest shouldn't matter, can be all lemon, and that's what adds the most flavour anyway.

This looks wonderful! About 35 years ago, our local fair held a chocolate cake contest sponsored by Hershey. There were many traditional layer cakes, but I made a chocolate chiffon, with a billowy chocolate frosting and took home first prize.
i like the chiffon because, besides being a slice of heaven, it utilizes both the yolks and the whites.
Thanks for this recipe. Can't wait to try.
If you want lemon, try using Meyer lemons.
Joy of Baking made a similar cake to this one. It’s a good tutorial to help make this recipe. https://youtu.be/sK5-uTBw6kc

This cake resembles the lemon scented sponge cake my mother and grandmother made. They used a dozen eggs. My mother made a wine nut version. Five years before she passed at ninety-five, she showed me how to make my favorite passover version of this cake.  She mixed the yolks, sugar and wine, added dry ingredients and nuts, beat the egg whites with cream of tartar until stiff peaks formed and folded the yolk into the mixture and baked. Always a winner and in my opinion tastier than the lemon one.

Great sounding recipe. I have only made a Passover version chiffon cake so look forward to making this one.
To Deb, potato starch has very different properties to potato flour & they are not interchangeable.

Per a commenter's note below: potato FLOUR and potato STARCH are two entirely different products. Never substitute one for the other. Potato FLOUR is quite heavy and not the same lightening agent as potato STARCH, and will in fact have the opposite effect.  Potato FLOUR might be added to a recipe to add moisture, especially if baking gluten free, but only a very very small quantity. Potato STARCH, as the recipe states, is quite light and starchy,  tolighten baked gods and give stability.
How does e come by potato starch? Is there a substitution?
You can buy it in the baking isle at any store around me. Look where the Bob's Red Mill products are and you should find it there.
Many well stocked grocers carry it or try bobsredmill.com under the baking aids menu.
Potato starch is often used in Passover desserts.  Try the kosher section of your supermarket.
Should the tube pan NOT be non-stick?

I would avoid a non-stick pan for this. 

1. The recipe does not call for greasing the pan.

2. From the picture, the cake looks very much like it leaves a good deal of its crusty exterior in the pan.

3. The cake probably needs to cling to the walls of the pan as it bakes.
I think the batter needs to be able to cling to the walls of the pan as it rises in the oven so non-stick is probably not good.
Do not use a teflon/non-stick pan.
Do you have a blender or a food processor? Then save yourself some money and don't buy caster sugar.

Chiffon cake recipes are included in my 1970’s Better Homes and Gardens cookbook.  I’ve made them many times with several variations…citrus to chocolate.  I love the texture and flavor of a chiffon cake.  Please, no icing.
Another commenter said that potato starch is often sold as potato flour. They are not the same, just as cornmeal/corn flour and cornstarch are not the same.

I made the cake exactly as the recipe called for. But I found it rather boring, so when I served it at a potluck dinner party I whipped up a simple, yet fabulously delicious sauce for it. I used a packet of frozen passionfruit purée from Goya (Available in many grocery store freezers in the Hispanic section). I cooked it with half a cup of sugar,  a Half cup of orange juice, And quarter cup of rum. I let it simmestirring,until it got syrup, about 20 minutes. Everybody loved the cake and preferred it with the sauce drizzled over each slice.

To all asking about the pan. It’s not a non stick pan. You’ll be slicing the cake off the sides, inner tube and the base at the end. You can find good tutorials on YouTube if you search Singapore Pandan chiffon cake. 
Those that are citrus allergic, common other flavours in Singapore are Pandan and chocolate. 
Enjoy! This is part of our teatime delights and visitors to Singapore do lug on board a Pandan chiffon cake from the airport!
Any ideas on making a gluten-free version or is that impossible with the need for an airy batter?

One of the reviewers in this article said this: "I wanted to use gluten free flour so I created a self-rising version by making 2 cups of flour because it was easier math wise (220 grams of flour and 32 grams of cornstarch, sifted twice and re-measured to have 1-3/4 cups flour needed for the recipe) and it came out perfectly."

Jon, I whip the egg whites first when I make chiffon cake to avoid having to scrupulously clean and dry the beaters.    It's a lazy solution to be sure to get stiff peaks, and save a cleanup step.  
I've always used regular sugar (not superfine) and all purpose flour (not cake) with excellent results. I'd try it with the finer ingredients, if I have them on hand anyway.
Potato starch and potato flour are *not* the same thing… very different. For potato starch, check out Bob’s Red Mill.

My mother made a simpler version of this cake for years. The recipe was from The Settlement Cookbook which I still have. It was called Sunshine cake. She served it with whipped cream and strawberries. I made it recently for my 86 year old brother and it is still the best!
Just made this with the only removable-bottom tube pan I could find. It was nonstick. Cake rose beautifully and is currently cooling inverted on the pan’s feet.
Buy small bags of potato starch at Chinese or Japanese grocery stores. Bob’s Red Mill package will last you forever if you only need 1 Tbsp for this cake -lol.

I had lived in Japan for nine years before moving back home almost a year ago. Chiffon cake can be found everywhere there. I miss Japan badly, but baking this cake may help mitigate the sorrow and bring back good memories. Thanks for the recipe.

Delicious recipe. I didn't have a pan with a removable bottom so I lined the bottom with parchment and the cake rose properly and came out of the pan easily. I wanted to use gluten free flour so I created a self-rising version by making 2 cups of flour because it was easier mathwise (220 grams of flour and 32 grams of cornstarch, sifted twice and re-measured to have 1-3/4 cups needed for the recipe) and it came out perfectly.  Will definitely make again.

This was my first chiffon cake. Served with orange infused whipped cream and micro-planed scharfenberger chocolate. All but one piece was devoured by my guests and that one piece we decided to take immediately by car to our friend’s who had just begun chemotherapy treatment. 

Life can be tough but there is still cake — and at times like these there is this chiffon cake. Thanks for this wonderful recipe.

this is so wonderful. my mom made this cake my whole life. she called this her "sponge cake". not sure where she ever got the recipe..but loved the cake.  she passed away two years ago at 80. she was adamant about how important to "beat the eggs" properly! she loved the lemon flavor in her recipe.
thx for memories
I started making chiffon cakes a few years back--people do love them; I think they went out of fashion because people's baking skills diminished with the evil of mixes.

Ah, the "evil mixes" -- I completely agree with you. 

(When I was a child, I bemoaned the fact that I was forced to use Betty Crocker or Duncan Hines cake mixes. I thought it was no longer possible to make a cake from scratch!)

If you wanted to make this a lemon chiffon cake, I would replace all the mandarin orange zest with lemon zest and use the juice of regular oranges, not mandarins, which are sweeter than oranges. You could try to split the difference and do half lemon juice and half orange juice. I don't think the juice will flavor the cake as much as the zest will.

I too make a lemon version of this cake (swap lemon juice and zest for the orange)- it’s been my dad’s favorite since I first started practicing it for my  county fair entry 30-odd years ago.  I’ve never heard about adding the potato starch to the meringue mixture though- I’m excited to give it a try the next time I make this.
Jeanette, Angel Food cake doesn’t use egg yolks.
Spectacular cake! Light as air. Loved it with a lemon/lime powdered sugar glaze and raspberry/strawberry coulis. Heavenly!

Not sure why this recipe only averages 4 stars, it deserves 5!  The texture is amazing!  So much better than any traditional cake recipe containing butter that I've tried (I know, these are different, but I much prefer this!)  The 2nd time I made a lemon version and gave it more of a layer-cake treatment, slicing it in half and adding lemon curd, whipped cream and macerated strawberry slices, then decorating the top with piped whipped cream and whole strawberries.  My new cake go-to recipe!
I substituted passion fruit pulp for the orange juice, and the resulting cake was quite delicious.
Potato starch and flour are entirely different and not interchangeable. But yes, go to the Jewish section and you’ll find potato starch. Make sure to sift!
Can I substitute  corn starch for potato starch?
Yes.  It has similar qualities as potato starch.

This is similar to the Passover sponge cake I make every year. The potato starch there is in the cake batter before adding the beaten whites. The important tip for a successful sponge cake is to not use a non-stick pan. I rinse my pan with cold water and shake it out before pouring the batter in.
Check the kosher section of your supermarket. Potato starch should be there, especially around Passover.

This recipe containing such heavy dosage of multiple citrus types, is not such a straight forward ease in baking use. Hence, takes the Chiffon classic cake far to another description. Nor, have I ever found this approach for a desired audience. Hence the classic, hugely successful through time, allows many more uses! Unending list of delectable  from Chocolate to spiced honey dripping, smothered warmed strawberries, or just an almond glaze... any topping creates a mouth watering appeal.
To person who asked about the pan. Chiffon cake recipes often recommend a non-stick pan because the cake will rise better.

Actually, nonstick is the opposite of what you want with sponge, chiffon, or angel.  The eggwhites need something to climb and if the pan is nonstick (or greased) they don't do as well.  The directions for the cake specify this:  "...Have ready a 10-inch ungreased, unlined chiffon cake pan (a 2-piece tube pan).'
My 72 year old Betty Crocker cookbook calls for 3/4 cup of water, not juice in the basic recipe. For lemon I'd try lemon extract, perhaps two teaspoons.

Potato starch and potato flour are not the same thing.  "Potato flour is made from whole peeled potatoes, cooked, dried, and ground into a fine, beige-colored powder. Potato starch is “washed” out of crushed potatoes, then dried to a fine, bright-white powder."  You cannot sub one for the other and expect good results.
What is the purpose of inverting the cake for cooling? (And why can I not upvote some of the helpful comments?) thank you!

When you invert gravity keeps the cake from deflating, which it will otherwise do. Standard for chiffon and angel food cakes, which is why those old tube pans have "posts" which become feet when you invert.
It's inverted so the the cake doesn't collapse.
Can you use an ordinary one piece tube pan or Bundt pan instead?
The pan is not greased, so you need the 2-piece tube pan to be able to get the cake out without destroying it.

Definitely not a Bundt pan because the cake will stick to its sides and curved/intricate top (a NOT non-stick pan is intended so the batter can climb/cling to all the surfaces as it rises to give the cake its lightness, and as well when cooling upside down)
Can I used a molded cake pan with this, or should the sides be completely smooth? I'm looking forward to this!
Sorry to disappoint. But my mother and grandmother made this cake many times during my childhood (1940s and 50s), right down the potato starch, meringue, etc.
Why would this news be disappointing? It's well known that the recipe was popular during that time frame.

Here is my Finnish grandmother's (and my Spanish mother-in law's, too!) advice so that the meringue does not start to fall in its bowl / water before use. You have to put another bowl / kettle, pot etc. of equal height on top upside down. In this way it's not so bad to make the meringue first. And I think I have already read this also in Julia Child's cookbook.

I had to halve the recipe because I didn't have enough eggs. I made it with 5 egg whites and 4 yolks. I followed the recipe exactly - and I have to admit it's a bit fussy but I thought it was worth it. It turned out so good! I usually make sponge cake but this cake held its shape better than a sponge and even more moist. It was like eating a pillow or a cloud. Would definitely make again. Next time I'll make the full recipe.

Did not have cake flour but I had King Arthur 1:1 gluten free so I used that and the result was fantastic!  Otherwise prepared as written.  I also made an icing of OJ and powdered sugar to dribble over the time.  1 cup powdered sugar and slowly add OJ while stirring to until you have a thick but barely pourable paste.
For recipes on flavor variations, please see Rose Levy Beranbaum’s Cake Bible, in the Sponge-type Cakes chapter which includes modifications for using lemon, or different types of cocoa.
Has anyone tried making this GF with Bob’s Red Mill 1-to-1 flour?
I have seen gluten free angel food cakes made with egg whites, sugar and cornstarch as the main ingredients that came out well.
how would you use this recipe to make a chocolate glazed, chocolate marbled chiffon cake?

I have pandan powder from singapore -- any chance of adding a note for making pandan cake? My wife loves it.
I'm sure you'll find something on google!
These are similar to the Angel Food cake I learned to make in my 1971 Home Ec-I got an A. My mom made these frequently at home. I still have her special cake pan.

was wondering if there is a recipe for this cake that can be made without flour?
