I usually beat the egg yolks with sugar till they turn into nice foam when I make this type of cake. It increases the fluffiness of the cake even more I think.
What do you serve it with? Just plain?
We served it with a little bit of vanilla ice cream - but it could also be served alone. The orange flavors and texture are delicious!

Today I made recipe exactly as written and used an old and reliable tube pan that's made many an angel food cake successfully. Cake cooked 60 mins and skewer came out dry. Nevertheless, while cooling on its bottle after 30 minutes the cake fell out. Bottom and sides had a light crust still adhering. The fallen cake was light and properly dry...not undercooked. 
Any thoughts as to why this happened?
BTW, the cake was absolutely delicious. sliced and cooled it on wire rack to preserve fluffiness

Perfect dessert for any occasion. It's simple and elegant. I couldn't get the chiffon cake pan in time for our Christmas dinner so went with a regular springform pan. Definitely was not optimal but the cake was still delicious. So delicious that my son-in-law asked if he could keep the leftover! He was the host so of course we said - yes! This will be my go to dessert!
It was done in 35 minutes.

I made a pumpkin spice version for Thanksgiving:
1/2 c canned pumpkin
1 tsp cinnamon 
1 tsp ginger
.5 tsp ground clove
.5 tsp nutmeg
1 tsp vanilla 
1/4 cup brown sugar swap for some of the castor sugar in the batter. 

Was so good.

I almost didn't get to the end of of this complicated, time-consuming recipe, even thought I was greatly intrigued. Thank goodness for this  at the end:  
"Tip     To save time, you can substitute 6 quarts of store-bought chicken stock for the base stock."

I don't know how many family members the author of this recipe has but the ingredients here seem a bit unrealistic 8-10 chicken Backs? Three chicken necks? And 6 to eight quarts ie: 2 gallons of broth???  Most of my relatives go home after Thanksgiving dinner so whois going to eat all those ramen noodles before they go soft and mushy?

Ramen is all about the stock, and rich stock takes time and effort; if you're going to invest in it, you should make lots. The stock freezes well, and then you can cook up a package of noodles and an egg when it suits you. Definitely don't make the noodles in advance; as you note, they'll go mushy, but they're fast and easy to cook when you need them.

Although I haven't made this recipe, I just made a ton of stock from last night's carcass, so I know I have beautiful broth, nothing to do with following this recipe. And I'll use my turkey leftovers and mushrooms and rice ramen noodles to make this beautiful dish. What a great idea for Thanksgiving leftovers different from turkey croquettes!

I started with store brought chicken stock to make the turkey broth. If you skip to that step the recipe is very manageable and was a real crowd pleaser. Sriracha, gochugang, or some sort of heat is really a must.
To save time skip the chicken broth part and use the turkey broth made from the Thanksgiving turkey.  The two hour boil is awesome.  And the ramen part is simple.  Delicious.  Easy.

I made the stock in an 8 quart instant pot with the carcass of a 12 lb turkey, 2 quarts of Costco purchased stock, a little over a quart of water and big spoonful of Chicken Better than Bouillon (in other words, what I had on hand).  I cooked the carcass on high pressure for an hour and let the instant pot naturally depressurize for an hour more. The stock was delicious, and the leftover stock became a jelly the way it should.  The result with the mushrooms, eggs,  etc. was a hit.
The best part of this recipe was the mushrooms. Next time I will double them and maybe add more veggies for variety.

After making homemade (Chick,Turkey,Beef Pork, Duck) stock for 35+ years, simmering such a small amount of chick parts for 12 hours is waaaaay overkill. After a few hours, the flavor is extracted and you are wasting time and energy. While more turkey and bones are called for in the second stock, still think you'll get all the flavor and goodness out in 4 or so hours. This is from experience of going for the 12-hour simmer enough times, and then being underwhelmed with the results.

This is actually 3 recipes, not 1. I would never make a base stock, a turkey stock and a ramen all in one day. I made stock after Thanksgiving and froze it. Now I've made the ramen, and it is good. I added miso and used what mushrooms I had on hand. Had I been a beginner at cooking, this recipe would have caused me to give up and order takeout.

What a great idea when you have leftover turkey, especially after the traditional Tday meal, it’s nice to venture into other culinary territories. I’m already making Turkey Tikka Masala (one of my fave NYT Cooking recipes) for Sunday. Will have to try this one out next time we roast a big turkey.
I still don't understand how the base stock and the turkey stock come together. Am I missing something? And do we really need two stocks? Isn't one enough?

Don’t cheat and use a store bought broth; the layered cooking with raw bones then cooked ones for hours and hours makes the magic. To the final ramen broth, we added a touch of Hon Dashi for depth. We didn’t use the vinegar or Worcestershire. Instead, we made a taré that we spooned into each bowl before adding the broth to give it the flavor bomb and proper saltiness.
Delicious!  Added some baby spinach and shredded carrots.  Great way to make use of the turkey carcass!
I am quite surprised at the suggestion of re-using the water for the ramen noodles, as the egg shells are usually contaminated by bacteria, not necessarily killed through the boil.

This was fantastic. I did alter the broth and made the broth in the slow cooker using the turkey carcass and a bunch of veggies and herbs that were in my fridge. Then I added miso and soy sauce to taste. The mushrooms, green onions, turkey, and ramen tasted sooo good! This will be on regular rotation, particularly after we have turkey.

Since we had done so much cooking for T- giving- we didn’t want to spend another day prepping. So we took some shortcuts (store bought turkey stock), but added one step which was key to flavor: make the ramen egg using the traditional prep- marinating the 6 minute egg overnight in the soy/ mirin marinade. YUM!
This has been a post-thanksgiving hit for the last two years at our house. Perfect meal as the weekend winds up. Love it!

Great minds think alike!  I made big pot of chicken stock the day before Thanksgiving - way more than I needed for Thanksgiving cooking.   Last night I added all the turkey bones and carcass,   and several pigs trotters,  and it simmered overnight.  What a broth!  Ramen here we come!   Even made some black garlic oil (mayu) today.   Happy leftovers!

This was good but definitely just use good chicken or turkey stock and skip the journey. This is relatively easy otherwise just being patient with the cook time. Definitely helped with the siracha at the end of make it more flavorful. May or may not repeat next Thanksgiving but plenty of leftover broth to have at another time. Fresh ramen noodles also make it next level
Effort/benefit not good enough.  A mass of pots, colendar, dishes, bowls.  I ‘d rather have the ramen packs a la Sifton or whoever. by the time it gets ready, my appetitie is dispelled.
Unless you freeze your leftover turkey, this recipe should only Ben enjoyed in the first 3or 4 days after it was cooked.
I had homemade duck stock in the freezer and a duck breast which went very well with the mushroom! Lovely recipe that was quite filling.

I really love the concept of this ramen.  I have to be careful about things like ramen noodles, but I have recently found a substitute in legume based pasta and noodles.  I plan on making this with my homemade brodo and black bean noodles/spaghetti. Higher in fiber and protein than regular ramen.  
I have never thought about adding an egg, so that would be great, and I like the fact you can play with the ingredients a bit.

This was a delicious and creative way to use our Thanksgiving turkey leftovers. We assembled the final Ramen ingredients in Korean hot bowls, to keep the soup extra warm. We also added chopped barbecued nuts on top, similar to our local Ramen house. It was fantastic. I will definitely use this recipe with roast chicken during the rest of the year.

I tried making this last night and the stock-making was a disaster.  There are omissions and contradictions in the recipe that I wish someone would clear up. 

1. Steps 3 & 5: Boil covered or uncovered? 

2. 6 cups or 6 quarts of chicken stock (see step 3 vs. tip)  If you boil to 6 c., you have a richer, velvety stock, but there's no way it will endure another 2 hours of high heat cooking.  I know.

The stock was gorgeous after step 3 but a charred mess after step 5. I'll try again in November.

I simplified the stock making process and used turkey wing tips and bones. I also halved the recipe which made 3 hearty servings. Otherwise kept to instructions but did add a squirt of lime juice and a few basil leaves as a garnish. My family enjoyed it and we will make it again.

Tweaked a few things. Had homemade chicken stock available, made the turkey stock in an Instant Pkt using the base stock as the liquid and added turkey carcass pieces. The pressure cooker function sped up the process. That's actually how I made the base stock a few months ago. Reduced the final stock by a bit to thicken and concentrate it. Also I had no turkey leftovers so I sous/vided a turkey thigh at 165F for 2.5 hrs and then cooled it and roasted it at 450F for 7 minutes followed by a 3-min broil to crisp up the skin. Let it rest then sliced it up and assembled everything. Spectacular results and would do it again (a bunch leftover for tomorrow's dinner). 

10/10 recipe if followed exactly or make it your own.
If I had rice flour, would the recipe be the same?
Yes, I too would like to make it with rice flour
For those looking for the rice flour based recipe- search here in the NY Times app for “bibingka” - there is a recipe adapted by Tejal Rao.

Step 1: In a large bowl, combine 1 cup of all-purpose flour, 1-1/2 teaspoons of baking powder and 1/4 teaspoon of salt. Make sure the ingredients are evenly distributed.

Step 2: Get baking!

Be aware that this flour has a shorter shelf life than regular flour, as the baking powder tends to lose potency over time. So make a small batch and save it in an airtight container in a dark, dry place.
Very helpful! And I think by doubling these amounts there is just the right amount (2 cups) of the flour needed for this recipe. Basically.

The recipe calls for 1.5 cups of coconut cream.  You mix half of that with the butter, and fold it into the batter.  What do you do with the rest of the coconut cream?  Why doesn't the recipe just call for 3/4 cup of coconut cream?

These taste like corn muffins.  The self-rising flour gives it that distinct "baking powder muffin" flavor.  The coconut sport is the most interesting thing about it.  Otherwise not much of a coconut flavor.  I would not make these again.

Oh, I compared this recipe to basic cupcake recipes and decided that  the original measure of the coconut cream is just describing the volume of a full can.  You only use 3/4 cup in the recipe.
I’ve read the recipe three times. Where does the other half of the coconut cream come in? What am I missing?
it's added with the flour, alternating by thirds, as in recipes with milk/buttermilk
I too am confused about the coconut cream. What happens to the remaining 3/4 cup that isn’t whisked into the melted butter?
What do you do with the other half of the coconut cream? Half is mixed with melted butter and the other half goes where??
I also wonder where the other 3/4 cup of coconut cream gets added?
I wonder, like another poster, why is it that only half of the coconut cream in the ingredient list appears to be used in the recipe?

"In a medium bowl, whisk together the 9 tablespoons of melted butter and half the coconut cream. "

What do you do with the other half of the coconut cream?

Hello! I'm one of the editors at NYT Cooking. The other half of the coconut cream should be folded into the batter at the end of the step 3. Thanks to all who noted that the recipe was unclear; we've reworded it to address the issue. Happy baking!

Lots of questions about what to do with the other half of the coconut cream in the comments. I whisked the first half into the melted butter as instructed... and then whisked in the other half and kept going with the rest of the recipe as written. It worked perfectly. The bibingka came out beautifully - light, tender, delicious. If you can get your hands on the macapuno, it really makes a difference. I also sprinkled a little turbinado sugar on the top for the final bake.
Where is the "featured in" link? Sweet backstory: https://www.nytimes.com/2022/09/28/magazine/bibingka-coconut-cake-recipe.html
Can I use rice flour?
So inauthentic, yet still powerful enough to lucidity evoke my childhood memories enjoying bibingka.
The last step before putting into the pans is to fold in the rest of the cream.

Consider wrapping your knife in a hot towel to warm it instead of running it under hot water. (Wet a towel and microwave it.) In most homes 30 seconds of kitchen faucet flow equals roughly a gallon of water (at avg flow rate of 1.8-2.2 gallons/min). Almost half the US is in drought with much of the west coast in extreme drought. That gallon of water has better uses than warming a knife.
(That said, I'll be trying this recipe this weekend, albeit with a different knife warming method!)

Worrying about 15 seconds of running water is penny wise and pound foolish. One strawberry plant uses about 20-25 gallons of water per season to produce 20-40 berries, equating to roughly 0.5-1 gallon per strawberry. My suggestion is to make the sandwich and then eat the sandwich and then attend a water board meeting about allocation.

For water conservation, I suggest a tall narrow vase/water bottle filled with quite hot water into which one can plunge and keep the knife.

Looking forward to trying the recipe this weekend!

When strawberries are at their best, I sandwich halved or thickly sliced strawberries (depending on size) between buttered bread, with a sprinkle of sugar. It's like eating the freshest of strawberry jam sandwiches. The bread has to be soft, and you don't want it sliced too thick. I've also gently opened a top-sliced hot-dog roll, removed a bit of crumb, buttered the insides, sprinkling them with sugar, and filled the roll with small or halved strawberries. Easy to hold and eat!

I was captivated by the picture in the Times magazine and made these sandwiches immediately (using Julia Moskin's milk bread recipe).  They weren't as sweet as I was expecting but would have known that if I had read the accompanying story!  I doubled the honey and used mango, raspberries and nectarines.  They are beautiful to look at and fun and unique to eat - especially at breakfast rather than dessert!

Sounds odd…but actually pretty good! Highly recommend with mango, only. We made it with all the fruit listed but the bites with mango were best. 

Great snack for a midday coffee or tea.

Boy did this recipe make me stop in my tracks. I like to think I'm an adventurous eater willing to try almost anything with a definite affinity for sweets but can not imagine eating this under any circumstances.  The thought of it makes me cringe and I'm also laughing because I'm so shocked at my reaction.
@Cass I had a version of this at the Japanese Tea Garden in Golden Gate Park and have been searching for a recipe ever since. Don’t deprive yourself! We used Trader Joe’s brioche.

This fruit sandwich is so light and rich at the same time, and not as sweet as I anticipated. I used bread made from Julia Moskin's Japanese Milk Bread recipe, which by itself is delicious. My presentation was not as pretty as the photo, I'll try again using more cream and bigger pieces of fruit. Definitely chill at least an hour after making.

Any suggestion for substitutes for a lactose intolerant?

Thanks!

I'd try beating some dairy-free yogurt (cashew maybe) and lactose free cream cheese with some maple or vanilla syrup for the filling.  But I'd also make it for others as written and then try tasting a small piece of the real thing after taking a few lactase tablets to know what I'm aiming for. I've found that the tablets work if used wisely (not for a big milkshake but for small treats, even a little ice cream now and then). Wishing you luck!

Apropos to nothing, I lived in San Francisco on 2012, and a friend took me to a tiny market store where the owner made strawberry sandwiches with light cream, on homemade bread. We used to buy them and have a picnic on Mount Tam. I’d never heard of this sandwich before  - Now I know the origin- thank you I can’t wait to make these

Fond remembrances. When I was a (very) little girl Mommy used to make me special sandwiches for lunch:  softened cream cheese on Wonder Bread topped with cut pieces of fruit.  Eaten open-faced.

Now I often do the same for breakfast   Stir up a little ricotta until creamy, add a bit of honey, spread it on anything other than Wonder Bread. Finally arrange (not just plop on) my little cut pieces of fruit(s) in a nice design.  

Simple, "pretty" and delicious.
I discovered these in 7-11’s in Japan and was immediately obsessed.  I’m partial to the strawberry only ones.  I’m super excited to try this!
Er, No!   This was bizarre. Palatable with no bread, perhaps, but even then...

Tried this recipe in June with lots of delicious grew fruit, but the honey we used had a bit of an overpowering taste and when mixed with the cheese produced a slightly funky flavor… I would recommend using a milder honey or subbing in maple syrup/sugar for a reduced funkiness.

These were fun to eat, but messy, in part due to the bread being the thinner-sliced Taiwanese version. Go for the Japanese bread's notably thicker slices. 

I disagree w/timing: it's far more than 20 mins to whip cream, fold it into the mascarpone and honey, cube the fruit, trim the crusts, spread the mixture onto the slices, wrap and unwrap them, and carefully slice each. Perhaps after a few times, I'd work faster, but this isn't a recipe I expect to use all that often.

For everyone who looked at this and just said  "no," get out of your comfort zone and give these a try. They are in all the 7/11's in Japan and ever since my husband picked up a mandarin orange version for me thinking it was my beloved egg salad (still scratching my head over that one), I've been obsessed. Strawberries and mandarin orange slices work best,  but most variations are surprisingly good. If you can get the milk bread, this recipe is close to the real thing. Five stars!
On the water conservation vein, I have an electric kettle that heats water nearly instantly. Conversely, a moistened towel in the mircowave will do the same thing. Energy use but no water waste.
To those of you complaining about unfamiliar ingredients:  If NYT Cooking featured only what most of us have on hand, what new techniques would we ever have the opportunity to learn?

Thank you for this of article. It gives me vocabulary I need to find ingredients,there are so many flavors out there, its hard to taste everything, but with this you can imagine how delicious  they are.  Timur peppercorns.
White fermented pepper.  I appreciate this information so much.

Yeah, I don’t have the ingredients either. But if you read the article, you’ll realize that this sauce was improvised because the chef didn’t have on hand the ingredients he’d normally use. So, if you don’t have the ingredients, improvise! The article actually provides some suggestions for doing so at the end.
I'll use some of the tips for alternates, but it's wonderful to learn about new ingredients.  If you have the time and are so inclined, look them up to discover origins, uses etc.
I have the garlic and orange juice on hand.  The other ingredients will need some substituting, but it's worth the effort (and actually fun) to do that to have another good sauce in the repertoire.

The substitutions I ended up using were: szechuan peppercorns and regular black peppercorns (toasted together before grinding), cider vinegar   orange juice   orange zest, and oregano. And it tasted fabulous over salmon. Made a recipe and a half and the two of us gobbled it all up. Definitely a keeper.

I made this mostly as per the recipe. Delicious. I did take the membranes out of the chiles prior to dicing. A mistake as a little more heat would have been good. A bit more acid and salt too. Regarding the Timut pepper it’s a bit gritty between the teeth. Next time I use my big molcajete to grind them to fine dust. A spice mill would be okay too. I do have fish sauce and I plan to add a few dashes. Next time I will quadruple the recipe. My repertoire now has a new item.

I made this last night with fresh snapper and it was fantastic! I made a side relish/dish of castelvetrano olives, sliced San Marzano tomatoes, some pickled purple onion and walnut oil..salt and pepper and some spices to go with it. It was a terrific addition and gave a nice balance to the dish. Will definitely make this again.

Delicious despite the many substitutions, apple cider vinegar, black and cubeb peppercorns l, harissa instead of the calibrian peppers.  I put some of the sauce on while cooking. Looking forward to trying the fermented white peppercorns!

This is the best fish sauce I have ever tasted and well worth the effort, especially considering the fish can be cooked in ten minutes or less. I'm so glad I bought the Timur peppercorns...they are so aromatic and citrusy. Had to order the Moscatel vinegar...not one of the specialty food stores I called in Philadelphia had it.

I made this last night after ordering all the spices and ingredients online. I grilled swordfish. I thought the flavors and aromas were fantastic. I’ve been cooking seafood all summer and was getting weary of the usual methods. This brought out something umami in the swordfish. I will definitely make it again. My family loved it.
Haven’t made this yet, but if for no other reason than introducing me to Burlap & Barrel, it’s a winner! A great resource.
When I was growing up we used to make a very similar mac salad but added frozen peas.   If we wanted it to be really tangy we added pickle relish.  Ono.

Why simmer the russet potatoes intact? Would the flavor be worse if you did what any practical person would do and peeled them, cut them into 1-inch chunks, and then simmered until tender? Or does simmering them whole mean that they are less likely to fall apart?
Simmering them whole decreases the amount of water they absorb and keeps the potato from falling apart.
It would seem to make more sense the way you suggest. Just be careful about over cooking them I suppose.
They are faster and easier to peel if you cook them first. Boiling potatoes with their skin on also reduces the loss of nutrients into the water.

Growing up on Oahu, we kept our potato salad and mac salad separate. Mac salad (my mother's recipe--forgive her, she grew up on the Mainland) included chopped hard-boiled eggs, chopped celery and green onion, tuna, a bit of sweet pickle relish, and the classic Best Foods REAL mayonnaise, but no carrots. I still make it this way 50 years later--and have been known to make a special trip to Costco for the mayonnaise (markets in Japan generally carry only Kewpie). And yes, I use an ice cream scoop.

I don't think it's really necessary to cook the noodles "two minutes past al dente." They'll soak up a lot of mayonnaise and soften up over the course of a couple of hours in the fridge (and the salad tastes better if you let it sit in the fridge a while before serving). You may even need to add more mayo before serving if the salad's looking a little dry.

Cooking the noodles past al dente, allows the starch granules that have "developed" to absorb the mayonnaise.  No, with no meaningful moisture, the noodles/pasta won't absorb the mayonnaise.  Which, incidentally, doesn't have much free moisture (as it's an emulsion).  To see this, try making a potato salad or pasta salad without cooking the potatoes/pasta, and see if they "soak up the mayo."  I agree though, that letting it season for a few hours makes a huge difference!

To each their own, but I grew up eating mainland mac salad prepared as you describe. I didn't like it. 20+ years in Hawai'i has me convinced that this mac salad is a different beast, and it does in fact need to be cooked past al dente to achieve the creamy, almost pudding-like texture that pairs so beautifully with kalbi ribs, chicken katsu with Japanese curry gravy or meat jun. I am so hungry now.

@Nell, simmering intact reduces the break up of starch molecules on the surface of individual cubes, making the final salad less starchy. It’s a preference thing but is probably part of trying to perfectly replicate the texture the recipe author is going for. (If I’m making crispy potatoes, I want all of that starch, so will definitely boil in chunks before roasting.)
Best Foods Mayonnaise in the West is Hellmann’s Mayonnaise in the East. Same product.

Potato tip: At the 13 minute mark, add a bit of salt and perhaps a couple of teaspoons of vinegar to the boiling water. This lowers the pH of the water and slows the breakdown of the natural pectin that holds the potato together. That gives the starch time to gelatinize and stabilize the potato structure. Potatoes fall apart because the pectin "lets go" before the starch has a chance to gelatinize.

I never heard of taro and it sounds a bit scary.  But I'm wondering about grating the eggs.  Why do it that way, it's rather messy.  Why not just finely cut them? Otherwise, this sounds like a tasty dish.
Grating the eggs was a piece of cake and took only seconds.

I too grew up on Oahu eating Mac salad and would suggest the following additions. 
I learned to (1) add seasoned rice wine vinegar to taste to the just cooked macaroni, (2) add some small hole grated onion, and especially (3) add as much as you like canned tuna, preferably not in oil. 
I make it often for family and pot lucks.
This sounds delicious. My mother always did a weird Hawaiian thing with macaroni salad, cubed Spam. To this day, I'm 64 and from the Bronx, I still add it.

As for the Hellmann's versus Duke's debate, this doesn't need to be a contest. They are both excellent, and as they have different flavor profiles, they have their own best uses. Cole slaw, potato salad, mac salad, Duke's adds great acidity and tang. Parmesan-crusted fish or a mayonnaise-chocolate cake, Hellmann's is more harmonious. Buy smaller jars and keep both on hand.

Hellman's is for Yankees.. Below the Mason Dixon it is only Duke's!! In my family we cook the pasta until squishy, add oodles of mayo, some spring onions, maybe some pickles/sweet pepper/celery, but never eggs.  We reserve those for potato salad.
Actually, it’s Cains vs Miracle Whip in New England. Cains is my go-to, it’s so good and no preservatives. Now if they’d only bring back glass jars and lose the plastic.

speak for yourself joan ;) (just teasing!) born and bred in RI, living in the boston area for 20 years, it's hellmanns all the way. miracle whip is not mayo, imho, so it doesn't enter the contest. (never understood that debate - it's truly like comparing apples and oranges, i.e., sure they're both fruits but they're not the same. both sandwich spreads but not the same!) i do keep it in my fridge though alongside the hellmanns because my partner loves it.
May I suggest Duke‘s Mayonnaise? Better than Hellmann‘s.
No! No! Hellmann's (or home made) :-)
When my sister and I shared a house there were 2 jars of Mayo in the fridge - she swears by Dukes, and I will brook no other than Hellman’s. This is a hill I’m willing to die on!
DUKE'S is the only mayonnaise I'll ever buy/use again

#A.Sanchez:

American Taro is readily available at Hispanic markets. Puertorricans call it "malanga jojota" and Dominicans call it "malanga coco". Peel it, cut it, and boil it for about 20 to 40 minutes, depending on the size of the chunks. It makes a good side pureed with butter. On a pinch, substitute yautia (white or lilac).
"Real" taro can be found in Asian markets. They are bringing edoes and "regular" taro at my local Ctown (Hispanic), but I by far prefer yautia and malanga.
Add salt...."so the water tastes like the ocean."   Lovely.

This sounded so different, I was hungry, and I had all the ingredients except the taro, so off to Hawaii I went. It’s so bloody hot here in Houston (over 100) so I skipped steps and just boiled everything (macaroni, cubed potatoes and eggs) together in one pot. Yum, yum! 

I’ll follow the recipe next time-I’m curious to know if my shortcuts made a difference. 

Mahalo! This is a keeper!
Preparing the boiled eggs this way (the egg salad in the Deli way) allows all parts of the egg to be incorporated into the mayo, thus adding more flavour to the salad.

I question cooking the pasta in the water used to boil the eggs?  Friend / biochemist once cautioned me when I gentled rinsed rather than washed a pan I had boiled eggs in that egg shells are not all that clean. Seems better to boil pasta first, then hard boil the eggs. Am I missing something?
My sister-in-law’s Filipino+ version includes cubed roasted chicken, giardiniera, cubed cheese, and chunks of pineapple along with lots of mayo and well done pasta — usually rigatoni. It’s delicious.
My Big Island family’s recipe includes peas, yellow and/or green onions, and crab. So ono!
I tried it with the hellmans but may do dukes next time for more tartness
In the northern CA Hawaiian barbecue shops, they use the short round little macaroni noodles...
Ditalini? (Autocorrect wanted to replace ditalini with Ritalin! LOL)
This sounds delicious.  Yet I am a big fan of Alex Webel's Macaroni Salad with Lemon and Herbs, a big crowd pleaser, also published on this site. It disappears at a picnic!  Try both.

I add many ingredients to pasta salad: eggs, celery, onion, tuna, sweet gerkins~and to reduce the fat content and make it lighter, I half the mayo and add light sour cream. It makes the salad creamier and adds some zip.
When I was growing up we used to make a very similar mac salad but added frozen peas. If we wanted it to be really tangy we added pickle relish. Ono.

My Ukrainian grandmother always grated the eggs.  Along with Hellmans, she added a tablespoon or so of vegetable oil and said that with the grated egg made "their own mayonnaise".  Her potato salad was the best I've ever tasted.  Grating the eggs guarantees some in every bite.

So, since this is now a mayo-off ;^), I’ll put my two-cents in for Heinz. Tried it when Best Foods wasn’t available in my vicinity (the pandemic, you know) and now it’s my go-to. I prefer the flavor and spreadability. Delicious as a spread or in recipes.

Macaroni is not “noodles”…at least I never heard that term used for macaroni during my 17 years in Hawai’i.  I never had macaroni salad with potatoes there, but maybe that’s because in the 1970s and ‘80s good russet potatoes were hard to come by in Hawai’i. Taro and sweet potatoes, yes, but not cooked with pasta in my experience.  The double-starch combo that I do remember from plate lunches on Kuhio Beach was “macaroni salad with two scoops rice.”  Goes good wit teriyaki beef, brah!
I add some milk and less mayo, 2 tbsp apple cider vinegar so that it’s not so mayonnaise-ey. I think the milk really makes the dressing taste lighter and whipped.

No taro available here so I used all russets. It's very good, especially when it warms up a little. Right out of the refrigerator, much of the flavor doesn't come through, but the closer to room temperature it becomes, the more its flavor shines.

Americas Test Kitchen’s Hawaiian Mac Salad advocates cooking the macaroni till they are “fat,” meaning A few minutes past al dente. The reasoning is counter-intuitive - it actually increases the noodles’ ability to absorb more of the fats in the sauce. They’re sauce includes whole milk and regular mayonnaise, and warned against using anything fat-free. A cup of sauce is reserved for a second dressing after the first dressing has time to absorb and cure.

Good stuff. I live in Europe where russet potatoes are not available so I did what another reviewer suggested and peeled and cut the potatoes before cooking. Saved time and effort. Like the idea about adding peas (maybe corn as well?). I served with plain grilled meat. It is very rich.
I just came here to point out that Step 2 has me adding a pinch of salt only so that my water tastes like the ocean.
Can you sub for the taro?
Recipe states you can sub additional russet potatoes for the taro.
Hard boiled eggs and I do not mix well. I cannot stand them. Do you think if I left them out it would make the rest a failure?
Not at all! It is sort of like "kitchen sink" salad, whatever you like!
Maybe a crazy idea, but I'd add crumbled fresh tofu to give a hint of whatever texture and flavor the eggs bring to the dish, and of course, a good boost of protein.
If you don't want the potatoes would you still need three cups of mayo?
You can always start with less and add more if you think it needs it. (And it will after it's been in the fridge for a couple of hours.)

I can't even think about grating eggs...yick, what a mess.
What I do is slice them in an egg-slicer three or four times. The texture is wonderful! Try it.

For Katrin:  Dukes is a regional favorite and, in my experience, often difficult to find outside the south, e.g., I could buy it when I lived in Texas, but it’s unknown here in New Mexico.  Hellmans and Best Foods are readily available nationwide and have a fluffier texture to boot.

If any of your local grocery stores are owned by Kroger (I believe that’s Smith’s in New Mexico) you might be able to find Duke’s now. I’m in the PNW and my local Fred Meyer (another Kroger owned chain) has started stocking Duke’s.
I buy Duke's at Albertson's in Rio Rancho and at Walmart in Bernalillo. I like it because it doesn't have sugar and has a delicious tang. It's the best!

Brought back memories of my mom’s Mac salad; celery, eggs, bit of red onion, salt & pepper. And Hellman’s of course.  Superior to the adjacent coleslaw and potato salad always.  On the list to make this holiday weekend, and l will have the wine as well!

Nell, it's not about practicality, it's about density and texture that is superior to boiling cubed and peeled potatoes. I am going to make this with Yukon golds as I love the texture. Agree completely with others that overcooking the pasta is unnecessary. This recipe is like deviled eggs in that you have to taste your way into the final perfect balance of flavors!

We’re experimenting with pasta here. Due to a temporary dental issue, soft food has become necessary.

So, after overcooking the pasta to go with fresh pesto, it is wonderful and delicious because the pasta absorbs so much more flavor. 

So, I think that the direction to overcook the pasta is correct.

I haven't made this. I've been making hawai'ian mac salad for years from a cookbook of Hawai'ian food.

When I was on the Big Island we had incredibly ono Mac salad at Super Jo’s, a local restaurant. She cooked the macaroni with the potatoes by adding it in at the end, folded the frozen peas into the hot potatoes and macaroni, paprika instead of tobacco.

If you wanted just macaroni, no potatoes or taro, what adjustments would you make?

Elbow macaroni?

Interesting version of a Hawai’ian macaroni salad.  I use kewpie mayo, eggs but no potatoes or taro…and a generous pinch of salt isn’t going to make your water taste like the ocean - and if it does taste like the ocean, that’s way too much salt IMO.

I wasn’t a bad version though.

Mayonnaise??!!  Another thing that divides us? For goodness sake, give it up. Or go to the Opinion page. Add eggs if you want and cut them up in whatever way works best for you.  It’s not that hard, or important.
Taro, potatoes, or eggs in Mac Salad in Hawaii is a not normal-A little bit onion, celery and carrot, but all that really matters is lots of mayo.
In my experience, the macaroni does not soften no matter how long you let it sit.  I think cooking past al dente is key.  If the noodles are soft you don't have to add as much mayo.

“Macaroni salad” = made with elbows. “Pasta salad” = made with any other kind of pasta. I’ve never seen Hawaiian macaroni salad made with diced potatoes. I’m a (basically lifelong) CT resident and my standard macaroni salad is, as it turns out, Hawaiian!  Elbows cooked past al dente, very finely chopped red onion, celery, and carrot, and chopped hard boiled egg.  And a dressing that includes oil, apple cider vinegar, paprika, sugar, celery salt, seasoned pepper - and lots of Hellman’s!

I made half a recipe. The proportions seem very strange. Half a pound of potatoes is two small ones, cut in 1” cubes tossed into 1/2lb of Mac, what’s the point? Maybe taro cut smaller. 1/4t vinegar also seems useless. Altogether unremarkable. I’m adding a small can of crushed pineapple to ensure eaters.

For Hawaiian mac salad I’ve preferred adding 2 cups mayo, and 1/2 cup whole milk instead. After the mac salad has sat in the fridge for a while and needs to be rehydrated just add a splash more milk and stir to bring it back to life.

I have made this twice and it is delicious and is now my favorite macaroni salad recipe.  I shared this salad with two friends who lived in Hawaii and both of the them really liked it and said that “took them back to when they lived in Hawaii”.  Wonderful recipe!
Looks delish. I always steam waxy potatoes. It takes less time than putting them in water and helps keep the shape. Been doing it for years and it always works well.
I made this for a Hawaiian-themed party. I used malanga from my Latin grocery since I couldn’t find real taro. And I made the mistake of adding garlic powder instead of salt. But it was delicious!

I love Hawaiian macaroni salad (and BBQ). Recently, and unfortunately, I have started a low-sodium diet. Usually mayo is not a big deal, but looking at the ingredients here, a single serving would have 835 mg sodium. And I doubt I could limit myself to just one serving. Man, gotta make this for the next family picnic though, (other) people will demolish it.
Have to use Best Foods Real Mayo! ❤️

Small entirely white taro is great to never eat. However large taro, from southern China, with the purple threading all the way through, is marvelous and would add visual as well as other interest. Diced first then cooked, it should be done much sooner than the smaller, boring, cousin.
This Mac salad was bland and boring.
I put my boiled egg in the egg slicer. Then I pick it up, turn it a quarter, and slice it again.  It’s perfect for salads.
Haven’t made this yet, but her comment about a metric ton of mayonnaise made me laugh out loud!
for a Hawaiian version it absolutely is essential. Many cook well past two minutes.@Shirokuma

portion size.
I don't think anyone asked. Adults can choose their own food, supported by their own criteria.

I use Duke’s mayo for Southern recipes because it fits that tradition. But this is a Hawaiian-style recipe calling for Best Foods mayo, which is the same brand as Hellmann’s — Best Foods west of the Rockies, Hellmann’s east of the Rockies. I’d respect the author’s intent here and use Best Foods/Hellmann’s.

I was so excited about the recipe, I dove into making them post haste! Delicious and being a woman who has been making Mandel bread for decades since my Bubbala taught me, the surprise was adding butter! Of course they were even more original with ancho chili’s and cashews (I usually use almonds) Do try a darker chocolate chip, it makes the difference for me… But I think they’re a modern take in a classic.

For biscotti I heat the butter to be a liquid. It makes it much easier to mix. I hope this helps if you don’t have a mixer. I have a mixer but don’t see any reason to dirty more dishes. One bowl and one spoon.

While I have not made these yet, I've made hundreds of batches of biscotti over my career as a pastry chef and my one bit of advice would be when you bake them a second time to leave them standing on their flat side so that both sides of the biscotti can reach the heat of the oven evenly.

I’m sorry to say although I think this is a good recipe in every other way the ancho chili powder is all wrong. Other Mexican chocolate cookie recipes use cayenne. I think the ancho ads a savory smoky almost meaty quality that I find repulsive.

Sounds wonderful. I have perfected my anise almond biscotti and make it in almost the same way. No dry cocoa, of course, and leaving the sliced, baked cookies in the turned-off oven overnight gets the crunch just right. Vanilla dough, almond extract, anise seed. After bakingand cooling, I dip each cookie in dark chocolate then chill. Even after weeks in the freezer they are amazing.

Wonderful recipe and perfect for the holidays.  Like another reader, I used cayenne instead of ancho.  More those with ready access to the ancho, cayenne works well.
Not a subscriber to Cooking, nice to see a nifty free recipe.

Pamela, I have found that biscotti make fantastic gluten-free cookies. That slightly sandy/gritty quality that many gluten-free flours have is not a detractor with a product this dry. Even better, every biscotti recipe I’ve tried has been easy to make with gluten-free flour without further adjustments! Enjoy.
To use regular cocoa replace baking powder with 1/2 the amount of baking soda.
It’s much easier to cut biscotti while the loaf is still warm. I wait about 1/2hr and then use a super-sharp serrated bread knife to do the cutting.
I could not easily find ancho powder so I substituted 1/2 chipotle, and 1/2 sweet paprika.  Spicy, smoky and delicious.
I had chocolate pepper biscotti in Venice and almost cried.  For years have tried to reproduced this delectable treat.  Off to the grocery store to buy the ingredients.
What does one do if one does not have a stand mixer? Small kitchen.
It sounds doable with a hand mixer.  If it gets too stiff for your mixer,  you can finish it up by hand.

I suggest using a small hand mixer.  I've also baked using a whisk to incorporate the butter and sugar together.  Don't let the instructions to use a stand mixer deter you.  The important part is to follow the instructions for the texture that should be achieved.  Go for it!  
I plan on making this as well as other biscotti for a sampler plate- lemon biscotti with cranberries, walnut biscotti, orange biscotti.
I once broke a wooden spoon trying to mix biscotti dough by hand. I do encourage you to use a stand mixer if you have access to one.
I made this vegan by substituting 2 flax eggs and using 1/2 c oil.  Very yummy.  I love the peppery aftertaste!
Make sure your Chile powder is fresh.  My attempt was inedible.
Was it inedible because of the ancho chile powder (different from regular chile powder) or because you used unsweetened cocoa instead of dutch-processed cocoa? Can make a huge difference...

I made these exactly to recipe, except prolonged the second bake to 45-50 minutes, because it took that long for crispness. I used mini semisweet chips for best dispersal in the dough. Might use a bit more ancho pepper next time, but will see how they taste tomorrow. They were luscious dipped in cabernet  sauvignon, although port would have been my first choice. Definitely a keeper!

Seagreen, I live in New Mexico. With the inedible biscotti you experienced, did you use the single spice, ancho chile powder, or a chili powder blend? I’ll be sure to use fresh and am considering chipotle, cayenne and kashmiri plus someone liked a chipotle/smoky paprika. Hmmm, so many choices.

These might be my new favorite biscotti.  I used 1/2 tsp. cayenne and 1/2 tsp of ancho pepper and they just had a small bite to them.  The chocolate flavor was really good, maybe because I used a good brand of cocoa powder (Guittard).  I'm going to make again using a bit more heat.

Love the flavor and the bit of heat. They taste great, but they are a bit more crumbly than other biscotti recipes I've made. That won't stop me from making them again, though. I want to have them perfected before holiday cookie season, because these belong in a box of Christmas cookies.

Just a note to say that if you use cayenne pepper it might be wise not to use 2 Tablespoons. I would imagine it's not a 1:1 swap with the ancho chili pepper. Even my most spicy and heat-loving friends thought it was way too spicy. My husband keeps eating them (slowly) and I can't wait to try the recipe again with the ancho chili pepper.
Made this recipe with 1t cayenne pepper - oh, so delicious!
These look amazing! I wonder, since it's based on mole, how pepitas would taste in place of the cashews?

Needs more complex flavors! I made this recipe as prescribed, and it was just OK, not great. The ancho chile was about right, but the base dough was a little bland for my taste. I researched several mole' recipes for inspiration. I made it again with these changes: Dry ingredients--increased cocoa powder to 1/2 cup, added zest of one large orange, added two tsp. cinnamon, added one tsp. coriander. I increased chocolate chips to one cup, mixing semi-sweet and dark chocolate chips. Try it!

EXCELLENT! I only had dark chocolate on hand (powder and chips) and pecans.
I'm a New Mexico native and have a lot of NM red chili powder around. I have used it in other chocolate recipes, and it didn't disappoint in this one. Two tablespoons of hot chili powder was perfect, just slight heat after the first few bites. 
Don't bother dividing the dough.  Just roll it out in a 24" log or a length that you can cut evenly and then roll to 12" from there.  We had them for dessert with an Oregon Pinot.
I used 1 tablespoon ancho chile powder and 1/8 tsp cayenne powder. Perfect level of heat for me!

In Italy, this type of cookie/biscuit is called cantucci. “Biscotti” is Italian for any “biscuit,” i.e. cookie, regardless of hardness or baking technique, although most of the world has traditionally embraced a (long lasting) dry cookie rather than the soft or gooey variety more recently popular in the US.
For those of us without a stand mixer (but I do have a vintage electric hand mixer) are there any changes we would make to the instructions?
Melt the butter.  It really makes the dough easier to handle.

I saw this recipe this morning and set out to make it right away. It’s currently in the oven for first bake. The dough was very soft and easy, I’m not sure if all the comments about stiff unworkable dough is about this recipe, or other biscotti recipes, because mine is very soft and pliable. It looks like a giant roll of warm, soft Tootsie Roll. I didn’t have ancho, so subbed 1/2 tsp of chipotle, and made up the missing volume with almond flour. Fingers crossed!

Well, they turned out! But maybe I was too shy with the sugar sprinkles because these were not remotely sweet, and I usually cut the sugar in most recipes. I actually coated one side with melted white chocolate and Maldon salt. The 1/2 tsp chipotle was just the right hint of smokey bite for us. While they did crack a bit on top after the first bake, it was fine. I didn't wait hours to slice, just until it was more or less cool enough to touch, and they turned out fine in the 2nd bake.
Good flavor, and liked the chile,powder, but agree that their texture is crumbly and more cookie-like rather than a sturdy traditional biscotti.

These sound like a deft way to deliver all that’s great about biscotti, and the story is a lovely read. But for those of us who are less than enthused by  chocolate and chili, what about subbing in five-spice powder (cf Brunsli chocolate cookies from Ottolenghi Simple)?

The first time around I used the chili powder I had at hand with added cayenne and ended up with taco essence biscotti. 

If you don’t have ancho chili powder, I’d recommend you sub just cayenne.  I used about 3/4 tsp and could have upped it a bit. It won’t have the same flavor As the recipe as written, but we loved them.

I used a mix of regular and Dutched cocoa (Hershey’s), small semi sweet chocolate chips, and the hot red pepper powder I had in the spice cabinet. I followed the recipe exactly (soft butter, not melted) and it was perfect. Easy to cut after the first bake, and the second bake made them nice and crisp. (Certainly not as hard as an almond biscotti). They are delicious.

Used about 1 tsp of cayenne   a dash of smoked paprika in place of ancho Chile powder. Also used regular cocoa powder and replaced baking powder w half the amount of baking soda. Didn’t wait hours after first bake, just sliced the logs when they were cool enough and second-baked at 325 for 15 mins (per the other biscotti recipe bc who wants to wait hours for these!). Turned out delicious with just the slight and right amount of heat.

Used ancho chile powder and regular cocoa powder.  Sliced with a straight knife while still warm, which broke the biscotti less than a serrated knife.  They were a huge hit with everyone.  They did start to crack in the oven towards the end of baking after 20 min. so I may reduce the preliminary bake time just a bit.
I agree with Mette. I found these neither sweet nor spicy but weirdly smoky. To me, the taste was odd, they gave me agita, and I dumped the whole batch in the trash.

I followed the recipe though had a heavy with dark chocolate chips and cashews.  Chili was a new bottle of chain store, large spice company ancho.  Suspect it might be warmer if I used a pkg from Mexican food section.  Cookies had deep chocolate taste with an after bite of warmth.  Made a cookie plate of various styles and shared with two groups.  This recipe was the hands down favorite.  If you don't like spiciness or chilis don't make the recipe.  But for the rest, these are just wonderful.

I made these using 1 tsp of cayenne because I did not have pure ancho chili powder, (just the chili powder blend that incudes other spices/herbs).  It was a good amount of heat, but didn't really add any depth of flavor.  Next time I will buy the pure ancho powder and see if it is worth it.  Otherwise the biscotti was good. I couldn't find roasted cashews that were also unsalted so I got raw and toasted them at home which worked well.

Ancho gave nice, smoky flavor, but not very hot for us.  More ancho would likely be too smoky. I used sliced almonds (forgot to buy cashews!) and big chunk chocolate.  First log (20 min, soft center, as directed) was crumbly. Put 2nd log in for more bake. It was easier to slice (my oven, YMMV)  but every biscotti I've made with butter has been more crumbly than crisp. 
I plan to try ancho/chunk chocolate/cocoa (with added cayenne?), but with my 'go to' biscotti recipe that has no eggs.

Made this and they're so good! I went 1/2 tsp cayenne and 1/2 tsp ancho and I'm very glad I did. They're smoky enough with a 1/2 tsp of the ancho already! But I'm glad I added the cayenne more more of that classic spicy "kick"
In what dream world do these linger in a box for two weeks?!
Has anyone tried a gluten-free flour version?
Thank you for sharing Mark Sopchak and I hope your basil and lettuce rooftop enterprise will be as successful as your bakery

What a great recipe!  Next time I may used regular chocolate chips instead of mini chips.  While I'm happy with the result of the ancho chile powder, I might substitute Spanish Pimentón De La Vera the next time because I've got a big tin in the pantry that a friend brought back from Spain.
I can’t eat cashews but this recipe looks great. Has anyone tried peanuts or pecans?

When substituting cayenne for ancho chili does one adjust the amount? 
Two tablespoons of cayenne seems like it would be overpowering ?
Thank you,

You're right, two Tbs would really clear your sinuses! Ancho chilis are pretty mild in comparison.

Use one Tbs or even two tsp. of cayenne instead of a 1-1 ingredient swap.
If one has a nut allergy can they be left out? Would love to try this recipe. Any suggestions for a substitute?
The ancho is key for the chocolate in this biscotti, it adds a depth to the flavor, enhancing the richness and it doesn’t get in the way of whatever it’s dipped in when you eat it.  Love it!
I made these today and it crumbled when I tried to cut them. I think more butter is required. I love to make biscotti but was disappointed with the texture here.

I've made biscotti many times and while some flavor combinations are big hits (pistachio & cranberry, anything lemon) I have yet to get applause for any attempt that involved chocolate.  I will give these a try for sure though.   Question!  Does the 2 tablespoons of Ancho chili powder represent the ending point for the baker or a happy medium?
I thought perhaps 2T was too much, but I was surprised how subtle the heat was. I don't think these would be too hot even for my Midwestern relatives who balk at using paprika.

I loved the idea of biscotti inspired by the taste of mole. But I don't tolerate chili the way I used to, so my only change was using just 1 teaspoon cayenne (freshly purchased). Just out of the oven, I've eaten 3 in a row (and thinking about a fourth). Good level of heat for my taste, with cold milk as a foil (like yoghurt with Indian food, or sour cream with Mexican). 

Thanks, Oceanlover, for the tip about melting the butter!

I Love making biscotti for family and friends. This recipe was so intriguing that I made an ancho chile run and made them right away!! Great recipe to follow, logs were easy to slice, and the biscotti are delicious.

quite a basic recipe asides from the addition of chili powder. be sure to use high quality chocolate. agree with prior comments - the chili can be repulsive to some, hit and miss depending on the person

i winged this recipe with what i had available in the house--bread flour, almond slivers, and cayenne powder (only 1 tablespoon), a touch of cinnamon and tons more vanilla than called for. you can't go wrong with this recipe. its a winner!
I threw the entire batch of biscotti out. The ancho chile powder was too strong and bitter tasting. Am making another batch sans chile powder. Zero stars.
If it was bitter, it wasn't fresh.

This is the worst fail I've had in 50 years of cooking and baking. The entire batch is in the garbage already. My daughter's comment was right on point -- the biscotti tasted like a bunch of ingredients that didn't belong together.

Looking forward to trying the addition of chile, I love a little heat with chocolate. I make a similar recipe at Christmas, I think from Bon Appetit, that includes chopped pistachios and dried cherries. The red and green are festive and Christmasy.

I made these today. My ancho powder is very fresh and pungent. Next time, I will use less. Perhaps mixing the Chile with the cocoa powder would help the baker get a sense of the balance. Honestly, I can’t imagine using cayenne, which packs so much more heat. 

While my batch is too hot for my morning coffee, it should work fine for cappuccino, or dessert with ice cream and fruit, or mango sorbet. The first batch is always an opportunity to learn and adjust.

And: I used a hand mixer to start, finished with a spatula. Wanted more nuts and chips. But I always do! Raisins could be nice in place of the chips or mixed with, or chopped apricot or mango? A bittersweet glaze to off set the heat? Love the possibilities.
Could this recipe be used to make cookies? I read one of the reviews that said something about a chocolate Mexican cookie.

A big flop for me! I make biscotti routinely. I was surprised that this recipe had the same amount of fat and 'liquid' from the eggs despite adding two ingredients that are extremely dry, the chile powder and the cocoa. The dough would not hold together or incorporate the add ins. I think next time I will add an extra egg (a Noni would!) and try again. I have a tea cup full of chocolate dough smeared pecans and chocolate chips if anyone wants them!
These are fantastic!!!
I followed the recipe and the biscotti cracked on the top during the first bake. Any suggestions?

I made the recipe exactly as written  Biscotti came out perfect.  My only comment is that I barely tasted the acho chili.  To make sure if was fresh, I bought whole achos and dry roasted, and ground.  Very little, if any flavor.  Maybe a little bit of cayenne would perk up the cookie. Otherwise the directions are good.

As an experienced biscotti maker, I have no qualms with the recipe as written.  Except the flavor was awful!  They baked up beautifully but no one could stand them.  I agree that the ancho flavor was all wrong and I love chocolate with chili in  chocolate bar!
Maybe the ancho chili powder was old. When fresh, it's a delightful, mild heat. I want to put it in everything.
Did not love the chili powder. Maybe cinnamon and cayenne with almonds next time?
Followed the recipe exactly as written. Six out of 6 tasters  gave thumbs up. You know it’s a hit when you’re asked to make more. I will honor their requests.
Topping this with 177MilkStreet.com's Mexican Mocha coffee sugar instead of basic granulated sugar in step 5 was an awesome touch...
A possible recipe can be found at this site: https://www.tinyredkitchen.com/recipes/mexican-mocha-coffee-sugar The author also provides suggestions for modifying the recipe to suit your taste.
I used Aleppo pepper. The cookies are in the oven still but the dough was sooo good!
Try paprika instead of ancho chile

I took them out of the oven following the last baking. I ate one. At first I thought, “meh”. Then I ate another. There is something about the heat and dark chocolate mix that is compelling. New. Different. I used cayenne pepper and halved it but they were plenty hot. Dark chocolate matters. Great with a glass of cold milk.

Made these this afternoon, and they’re good.

- substituted Cup-4-Cup gluten free flour … and added a few tablespoons more to get the right consistency
- used Ancho chili powder as directed … but wanted a smokier, spicier finished product — might add some cayenne & smoky paprika next time

I cooked 20 minutes   25 minutes … would go a bit longer on the second cooking to get a crispier biscotti

Recipe made 52 biscotti

I really found these to be terrible.  I cut down on the amount of chili by about 1/3 and they were still virtually inedible.  All we could taste was the overpowering spice.  They had good texture and might have been good with perhaps 1/10 of the chili called for.
Didn't have Ancho Chili powder and subbed with Penzey's Arizona Dreaming blend.  Simply divine!
Needs more ancho chili powder.  I made mine from dried ancho chilis

These were a big hit at my volunteer gig, where chiles in any form, but especially in chocolate, are revered.  My only substitution was using pasilla chile powder instead of the ancho powder.  That, and using my vintage Sunbeam hand mixer at the beginning, then switching to a very large spatula to finish mixing the stiff dough.  Easy to mix and roll.

Per reader recommendations, I used mini chocolate chips, chopped the cashews before toasting, and placed the slices on edge for the second baking.

Excellent recipe. Following the advice of another reader, I used  one tablespoon of cayenne  powder. I thought they were delicious but might use a little less cayenne next time because some found them too hot. The dough was very easy to handle, much less sticky than my  other biscotti recipes, wonder if thats due to adding butter?
I usually make biscotti without adding butter or oil but I am going to add this one to the mix. Thank you for sharing the recipe
Scrumptious.  What flour(s) should substitute for a gluten free biscotti?

My dough had long cracks in it after the first baking.  Thankfully, it seemed to close up (at least some) while it was cooling.   The end result was a delicious crisp cookie that we will enjoy for many weeks to come!

I used fresh, just purchased ancho chile, cup-4-cup GF flour, and unsalted marcona almonds (which are closer to cashews than almonds, IMO). 
The 2 T of ancho are undetectable. The biscotti are more crumbly than crisp--although they are dippable and do not fall apart.

The reviews on these are so exaggerated. They are merely okay. No one will  spit them out, but no one will beg for the recipe, either

Made exactly as shown except I substituted almonds for the cashews (allergy!), and it was wonderful. . .and easy! I was worried that two tablespoons of the chile would be too spicy, but it was perfect. For a number of irrelevant reasons, I let it rest for almost six hours between bakes, but it sliced perfectly. BIG HIT!

I listened to @lynn Newville and added an additional 8g of Dutch processed cocoa powder, the coriander, and the cinnamon. The flavor is great. I used refined coconut oil so I didn’t have any trouble with it being dry. I used a mixture of white and semi sweet chocolate chips and had pistachios so used then instead of cashew. A keeper.
HOWEVER, used 2 T of cayenne and a 1/4 t of Vietnamese Cinnamon.  I think 1.5 T of the cayenne would be more than enough. Very spicy.  I will try it again with a little more cashews as well.
Can you make cookies out of this recipe instead of biscotti? If so, can I use recipe as written or do I need to make some changes and if so what are the changes?
Could the baking powder, salt, vanilla extract be converted to weight? Particularly the salt, I'm just going to assume that's kosher salt and hope for the best.
Made once with half the ancho powder. Nice. Made the second time with all the ancho. Perfect. Substituted Mini Chips.

I ground a dried ancho in a coffee grinder.  One ancho made almost exactly one T.  Next time I will use two anchos for the two full Ts called for in the recipe.  I like chile and chocolate and 1 T isn't enough.  I used Hershey's cocoa with 3/4t baking soda (no baking powder), walnuts and hand mixed.  Really good and no problems with slicing.

I made these exactly as written and they are quite fabulous, but dangerous: I ate all the end pieces before the second bake and a couple more after that! Great depth of flavor and I love that they are not too sweet, even with the chocolate chips added in. Total keeper!
Very good but also very spicy.  I ended up making it 3 times and reducing the spice each time. Final version had about 1.5 teaspoon of the chili powder and that was enough.
Ancho chili POWDER has been something I cannot find.  Can I substitute ancho chili pepper?
Have not made these yet but was surprised by the amount of ancho chili powder of 2 TBSP.  If you substitute cayenne about how much should you use?
These were tasty but were more like brownie brittle than biscotti.
Okay I’m in Australia and we don’t have that kind of chilli powder. I used 1tsp of our supermarket chilli powder instead and it was too spicy for some.

I made these and meant to give them away along with some other cookies, unfortunately I forgot them and was saddened by that, but, these are amazing!! I used black pepper according to a comment below and couldn’t believe how deeply choclate and spicy they were. Really easy to make and very much worth the little extra time. Plus they are small biscotti, so you can indulge your chocolate cravings. Couldn’t really taste the cashews though. I think next time, I might use the small but mighty peanut.

Made a batch of these to share with neighbors over the holidays, with only the substitution of powdered fish pepper (hotter but more delicate than ancho). Perfection! Do heed the suggestion of standing the slices for the 2nd bake - results in just the right texture.

I used cashews because they’re a lot cheaper and easier to find than hazelnuts. Forgot to
add vanilla and only had a tablespoon of ancho chile powder but they came out delicious. Love them after the first bake as well. Great recipe!
Oops!  The recipe does call for cashews. I was mixing it up with a different recipe. My bad.

Did a lot of DJ mixing, and still turned out great! Tried to tone down the heat while keeping the chili flavor. Used gochugaru flakes (~1TBSP) and added a few dashes of cinnamon. Only had 1 egg, so subbed in 1.5TBSP heavy cream for the other. Almonds instead of cashews. Normal grocery cocoa powder.

A forgiving foundation recipe for chocolate biscotti, and a very exciting balance of flavors! Highly recommend even for non-spice lovers (with the obvious warning to use less chili...)

I’m Jamaican. The Cho Cho ( chayote) and raisins are unorthodox but willing to try them in the sauce! However you have to eat this fish dish with Festival or Johnny Cakes to get the true experience of eating escovitch at Gloria’s in Port Royal. BTW this recipe may have come to Jamaica via the Sephardic Jewish community. The Times did an article on just such a fish recipe that has undergone adaptations depending on the region.
Oh Gloria's!  Glorious! Glory Ours! Glory Be!

I'm thrilled to see this delicious Jamaican dish featured in The Times.    As a Jamaican I can tell you it's hard to go wrong with this and I like the variation offered by Francine. Boiled green bananas also make a great side dish. Enjoy!

I have been making Escabeche since we first went to the Bahamas more than 35 years ago. Steaked King Mackerel or swordfish has texture that can stand up to vinegar, but any firm fish will do. I use Rice or Wondra flour and add bell peppers to the veg mix + onions and only a little carrot. Chayote and raisins?Maybe try in a small batch. Here in Miami they would use Completa or a Goya/Badia seasoning. I keep ghost/habanero peppers whole and cut slits so everyone can remove if too hot
I read with great interest the eloquently-written article on escovitch fish. As a Jamaican, I have a greater appreciation and love for escovitch fish with bammy.
Haven’t made this yet but, in light of the article, wonder why the recipe calls for filets, not a whole fish. How would one adapt this for a whole fish. Would bass be a good choice?

You'd either slice the fish in half or take into account the difference in frying skin down versus skin up. 
The chief difficulty would be with the vinegar, you place the fish skin down because the liquids are more easily absorbed that way. The skin is  a bit of an impenetrable barrier. Even poking a bunch of holes using a fork with sharp tines wouldn't quite be the same.

So the fish doesn’t spoil after two hours at room temperature? Does the vinegar keep it pickled or something? One would imagine with the heat in Jamaica there is some science keeping it fresh for 12 hours!

Kristi, the spice trade was the most lucrative of its time long before refrigeration, we're talking several hundred years ago at least. 
One of the reasons the Europeans were so eager to find a shortcut to India. Spices were one of the only ways to preserve meat and fish. Not talking too much salt either. 
So the closer to the equator, the spicier the food. 
From my 3rd grade social studies teacher- thanks Ms. Levy. 
(the vinegar is something else . . . )

Be really careful with those scotch bonnets or habaneros. Pros often use gloves. If you merely touch them and forget and then rub your eyes, or guys use the bathroom, you'll remember the experience. Until the next time you forget. 
They're so hot that way I wouldn't even bother deseeding them if you're discarding them. The more you play with them the more likely the capsaicin oil gets under your fingernails or whatnot. A regular quick wash does nothing,  requires more like a scrubbing.

Made mostly as written. Halved fish and seasonings. Vegetables made the same, omitting chayote. At 10am I had some great looking, crispy fried fish in some delicious smelling vinegar sauce. After letting it sit for 10 hours…not great. Felt like leftovers.

In regards to the comment from Joanie regarding the dish being brought to Jamaica by Sephardic Jews I would say she is probably  correct.  The dish, with a few changes as to seasonings, is pretty much the same as the Venetian dish  of Jewish origin, fish in saor. . You can also make it with pumpkin or squash, The fish is a handy thing to have in the refrigerator for when you are too lazy to cook, especially in the summer.  Anxious to try this version.
Chayote is known as mirliton in Latin markets
Could this be done with other “fish” (Octopus or Shrimp for instance)? My wife is allergic to fin fish, and certain shellfish.
This was delicious!! Couldn’t find chayote so used zucchini. Let it sit an hour .. really fabulous - don’t hesitate, just try it!

Is there a problem with the amount of vinegar?  I halved the recipe because of the fish I had, but kept the 1/2 cup of vinegar because I'm a fan of it in cooking, and had no sauce at all. Great taste, but no sauce. Maybe one and a half cups?

Pulling Joanie's thread below, I am from Barcelona and we have pretty much the same preparation here called Escabetx in Catalan or Escabeche in Spanish, so the Sephardic connection sounds very plausible!
We make it with sardines, tuna, and even mussels! Always eaten at room temp or cold after a few hours or better, a day of marinating in the fridge. Makes a mean sandwich inside a fresh, crusty baguette. May make some tonight...
If you use a whole fish, how does seasoning go?  Do you open up and butterfly the fish and season w garlic and allspice?  Do you season just the outside?  Thank you in advance.
Suggestions for a substitute for chayote? I've never seen it in DC.
Several web sites I looked at suggested zucchini.
It's also known as mirliton and can be found at Mexican/ Latin markets.
Being 500 miles from the sea, I prefer catfish with this dish and with my sweet & sour Venetial-style fish.  Dark rasins are okay too.

I lived in Jamaica a long time. I went back to the midwest and made this recipe using Blue Gill and Large Mouth Bass. So Good!
Thanks for the recipe for the North Amercans!
If you use whole allspice berries, how would you use them in step 1 to rub onto the fish? Or would you combine them with the berries in step 6?
Oh, I see now that if you use whole allspice berries they show up in step 3.

This looks so exciting to make.  I have a food safety question as I’m immunocompromised, is the fish really safe to eat if left out for several hours?  I know it says to leave out for a minimum of an hour, but reading comments along with the write-up accompanying the recipe, the cook really is encouraged to leave it longer.

Terrific, great recipe for W@H cook. Cut veg (extra) day before, added celery and semi-hot green peppers in addition to habaneros, since we like things spicy, skipped chayote. At noon day of, cooked veg, added parsley and 2-3 TBSP pickled golden raisins (had made to go with roast veal a week earlier).  Rather than fry fish, rubbed with spice rub and olive oil, roasted on a bed of fresh thyme @ 375 for 12 min. Poured hot veg mix on top, covered and let sit till dinner.  Served w steamed limas.
Omitted the raisins but this was very tasty - tangy and full of flavor!

title: Rqaq w Adas (Lentils With Pasta) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: Very confusingly organized. The recipe should state up front exactly how much dry pasta to use, for those of us who don't have the time or energy to make our own dough.
Literally the FIRST thing in the entire recipe is "FOR THE DOUGH (OPTIONAL; SEE TIP):" — exactly how much handholding do you require?

Christopher, the tip at the end of the recipe is very clear about the pasta substitution:

Tip
Traditionally, this dish was a way to use scraps of dough left over from baking bread. You can make a quick dough as outlined above, or simply substitute 4 ounces of dried tagliatelle pasta and add to the lentils at the beginning of Step 5.
The recipe is so confusing I can not hope to try it.
P.S. The photo is gorgeous, and bears no relationship to the directions given in the so-called recipe.
It's topped with the "Garnishes" section of the ingredients list, and plated according to step 9, I believe.
I'm confused about adding fried onions with their oil, tamarind concentrate and the molasses to the lentils in 9 cups of water.  Doesn't sound right...
It seemed weird but it all came together beautifully and did not taste oily.

This was delicious. The recipe reads more complicated than it is. The flavors were picked up in the noodles and the texture of the lentils and additions of pomegranate seeds and crispy onions made for a complex taste with little effort. NOTE: Garnishes definitely make the dish!  I used 9oz dry tagliatelle (at the end of step 5), store bought crispy onions & skipped frying flatbread. My natural foods store had pomegranate molasses and tamarind concentrate in a jar -not tinny. A keeper!
I always have a package of tamarind, a compressed cube of seeds, pods, etc.  - which needs to be soaked and strained before cooking.  How do I substitute that for 'tamarind concentrate'.
the product after soaking and straining is the concentrate; you can also buy it in a jar (sans seeds and pods), which is more convenient but can taste a bit tinny.

I couldn't find tamarind, and also had doubts about whether I wanted to use tamarind and pomegranate molasses together, so I found this recipe for a similar (but not the same) pasta and lentil dish and made it https://zenandzaatar.com/recipe/pasta-lentils-syrian-style-horaa-esbao/. It was great and the recipes were clear enough. It has sumac and coriander instead of tamarind. I'm curious for people who made this recipe as written, what was your opinion on the mix of tamarind and pomegranate?
Thank you Elizabeth. The recipe in your link is much more understandable. I will try this one first and perhaps "graduate" to the other eventually.
This one is indeed easier to follow! I'm going to try it this weekend.
Looks so good, but there's got to be a less confusing way to present this info.

Do you drain the lentils and pasta at the end, or do you serve it in the thickened liquid? The picture looks like it's very dry served on a plate (clearly strained) but the description says stew (unstrained)

I am familiar with the crispy fried onions in these dishes, and there is no way that they'll get brown and crispy with a 3-4 minute dip in the oil. This recipe has so many red flags that I'm bound to doubt it, which is a shame because this mix of flavors would be divine.
For those of us who have had organ transplants and cannot therefore eat pomegranate or cranberries, a good substitute would be raspberry jam reduction.

To make tamarind paste: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021264-tamarind-paste I used 2 tablespoons of it. If you ignore anything having to do with making the dough, the recipe reads much better. Adding tamarind and pomegranate together was delicious, for those who asked. With the lemon, the dish is tangy, so the pomegranate seeds are important. I skipped the fried pita and fried onion. Would recommend a smaller pasta to allow it to be eaten with spoon.
what happens to the 9 cups of water? I see no sign of water in the photo of the finished receipe. Looks lucious.
What can I substitute for pomegranate molasses? Same volume of regular molasses?
Regular molasses will be far too sweet. You can make pomegranate molasses by reducing regular pomegranate juice
It sounds much more complicated to do than it actually is

This recipe could easily be edited to read more easily. It is actually pretty simple and the results quite pleasant. I used a pasta called rishtaya that I had from a local Armenian grocery store. Green lentils worked fine. Tossed the pita chips in a little olive oil and baked until crispy. Sautéed the onions for garnish until dark and starting to crisp. Maybe a little less on point but the next meal is only hours away.

I can't wait to try this recipe, out of respect for all of the many Middle Eastern cuisine traditions. For Western cooks/aficionados of Middle Eastern cooking, I just want to know whether this dish could be made even easier by adding orzo, elbow macaroni, fusilli, gnocchi, and other forms of pasta, simply by adding the dry pasta at just the right moment to the cooking lentils so that the final pilaf is of lentils and mixed pasta? a perfect bed for roasted vegetables,baked in oven simultaneously

Incredibly delicious, deeply flavored, rich stew. I used whole wheat flour for the noodles, and they were truly worth the effort. I also made all of the garnishes, laid the out in stripes on top of the stew, and it was a show stopper. Instant favorite!
Nine cups of water was WAY too much. I had to drain the lentil mixture, which was basically soup. Very tasty after that.

The recipe is a bit scrambled. But if you want to skip the dough part and just use dried pasta, you can go step 1,4,5, then the second part of step 7, then steps 8&9. I used half tablespoon of salt, because I used store bought pita chips - salted, and slightly less oil than recommended.  I used 8oz dried pasta nests.  The end result was delicious, not soupy and very filling - 6 very hearty portions, more like 8 normal.  I will make double the crispy onions for the topping next time.

I was feeling dubious toward the end of making this, and like many others found the recipe "creatively" organized. However, I was surprised to find that it was very good and a hit with my family, even without the fried stuff. It actually wasn't that difficult and the next time would be much more efficient. I had a similar question to to others about if you need to drain it at the end. I didn't need to but it was soupier than the picture.

After reading Naz Deravian's Bottom of the Pot, I made a lot of substitutes. I used tamarind paste, mint instead of cilantro, cranberries instead of pomegranates, dried lemons, and sourdough fried into croutons. It was beautiful with red/green stripes, and just as delicious. I'd coat the onions in the wondra for the garnishes, and double them. I thought it might be too sour, but it all balanced out in the end.
What is this usually served with?

I am confused by step 5. If the onions, etc, are added to the pot with the 9 cups of water, won’t the flavors be diluted? Or will most of the water be absorbed by then? (in which case, how can the dough or tagliatelle cook?). Is the liquid ever drained?
Step 5: The onions in the first sentence are part of the garnishes. The onions in the third sentence are part of the lentils. Two different onion ingredients!
What do you recommend purchasing for the tamarind?

I agree that this recipe is confusing. There are two spots where the onions are fried and it doesn’t make sense when the water from cooking lentils is removed/ the lentils are drained. Maybe it cooks off?

The flavor is subtle and then it hits you. Trust the seasonings. I used almond flour to make the noodles, and they completely disintegrated in the stew. I don’t rule out user error. Added roasted sweet potatoes to the stew, since my homemade pasta didn’t work out.

I made this exactly as written. If this only fed 6-8, you would have very hearty eaters in your home. I’m going to be eating it for a week. Also, mine was pretty soupy. I expected more of the water to be absorbed. Still, it’s a nice, warm flavor and fried pita bits are fantastic.
