FYI pomegranate seeds may not be accessible for many people right now -- the produce manager at my local chain grocery store told me that supply chain issues have made pre-packaged seeds really hard to come by in the past two months due to a shortage in the plastic containers they're packed in.
Where does one find Tamarind concentrate - and what is it?
I buy mine online — Rani brand.

Dry version: Cook lentils, strain, reserve water. Caramelize onions. Combine 1/2 lentils w all onions [cook more onion for leftover lentils]. Cook 6oz egg noodles in lentil water. Fry garlic, cilantro, & pre-fried French's onions for topping. Drizzle noodles topped w lentil+onions w combined substitute tamarind/pom molasses "sauce."/For tamarind: 1 T raspberry jam, 1/2 T balsamic vinegar/For pom molasses: 1 T Worcestershire, 1 T lemon juice, 2 t tomato paste, 2 t brown sugar, up to 2T water
This is tasty but very labor intensive.
This is a very disappointing recipe for the amount of effort.

This makes quite a large batch, too big to fit all of it on my largest platter. Next time I will deviate from tradition and serve the toppings separately so the crunchy ones stay crunchy and the cool ones can stay cool when the leftovers are reheated. My homemade noodles had a tendency to clump into doughy masses. I think this was worth the effort.

Very, very good, even when cold.  I substituted wide egg noodles for the tagliatelle and skipped the pomegranate berries as well as the pita pieces.  The pomegranate molasses and tamarind paste brought a delightful piquancy to this recipe and the sauteed onions in olive oil gave it just enough depth to offset the acidity of the lemon juice.

I followed the instructions closely and it was fantastic. The sweet/sour/salt/earthy/salty and fresh herb combo was a revelation. Pomegranate molasses and tamarind paste work well together and the fresh pasta was so easy it makes me wonder why I’ve never made it before. Took a bit to have all the ingredients to hand but well worth it.

Quick or lazy version: 
- Use pita chips (rather than fry pita)
- Use tagliatelli or fettucini rather than making dough
- Pomegranate: To separate arils (red fruit) from the white part, do the task in big bowl of water: the white parts will float out of the way
I share the opinion that the recipe is poorly organized. I had to keep searching back and forth while cooking. Nevertheless, it was delicious! I will make it again.

Extremely confusion instructions concering the tagliattelle. Where it says "for the dough" it should say that THIS is the tagliatelle.  And it should also say in that section that you can swap out dried taglitelle.  The recipe ingredients do not say "tagaliatelle" at all.
Can this be made ahead of time?

This is essentially thick lentil soup with garnishes, the photo is misleading. We all liked it. Very time consuming. For me, it needed extra pomegranate molasses to really come into its own, which I drizzled onto my portion. The garnishes make it.

I followed the recipe but ended up having to drain the lentils and add back the pomegranate molasses, etc. Everything else was fine although I did reread the recipe repeatedly. I did use the homemade dough for the pasta and will definitely incorporate that part into other recipes.
Are we supposed to drain water from lentils and pasta? It’s almost done cooking but looking like soup

This can be prepared in a sheet pan method in a 425 convection roasting oven.  Cover all ingredients with oil and spices, place parchment paper on the bottom of a flat roasting pan, sliced potatoes spread out on the parchment, sliced onions on top of the potatoes and chicken thighs on top of vegetables. Roast until chicken is 160° F and serve with raita and naan.
Excellent! I added a couple of tablespoons of water after the last batch of chicken to deglaze and add those brown bits to the dish as well.

For those who complain that this is not real Country Captain, just read the entire article. It states right off the bat that this is an earlier Anglo-Indian version of the dish - a precursor to the classic dish so well-known in the American South.

Thank you!  I get tired of people insisting on "authenticity" when the reality of food is hybridity. . . all the time borrowing, changing, making new versions.  I also get tired when people jump in with "expertise" having clearly ignored explanatory text, just wanting to claim superior knowledge.  No food police, please.

As someone who's studied Anglo-Indian cooking in some depth I can say with certainty that this is definitely NOT from that tradition. The people quoted in support of this are just recycling old myths. Both this and the other recipe cited are clearly variants of a well-known Malaysian dish Chicken Kapitan. It probably reached the American South via seafarers as sea cooks were often Malaysians, similar to the first Indian cooks in the UK who came from Sylhet in what is now Bangladesh.

You make a valid point. but at what point can you really keep calling it, "Country Captain" after so many recreations? Even the Times addressed this quite well back in 1991. Like Ms. Brownstone and Mr. Beard, I can only SMH and sigh. https://www.nytimes.com/1991/04/17/garden/long-ago-smitten-she-remains-true-to-the-country-captain.html R.I.P., Molly O'Neill!

I used a 12 inch skillet to brown onions, removed them, only adding a little oil, removed them and added potatoes. Cooked potatoes until brown, removed them. Cooked the chicken in that same pan then added all the ingredients, stirring all until nicely coated. Came out great. All told, used only about 3 tablespoons for each step.

This is an old recipe that my aunt used to make for me going back 45 to 50 years ago. The name of the dish was "Country Captain Chicken". In this version the spices used are in recipe are used in Indian dishes which I love. The original recipe called for salt, pepper, garlic and onions, oregano or thyme and a tomato puree and raisins, yes raisins.  I will try this version, but I probably will add a tablespoon or so of tomato paste. That would add a richness that goes well with the other spices.

I think this is probably similar to your aunt's recipe (although it calls for currants instead of raisins, and fewer spices). https://www.nytimes.com/1991/04/17/garden/long-ago-smitten-she-remains-true-to-the-country-captain.html

Indian biryani dishes often contain raisins. In a typical vindaloo dish (an Indian variant of this recipe) the masala spice mix might well contain cumin, coriander seeds, black peppercorns, green cardamon pods, cinnamon, cloves, dried Kashmiri peppers. You toast these spices lightly, and grind them together when cool. Way more flavor than just using turmeric as the spice.  Fresh mixes have more flavor than mixes of pre-ground spices, but the latter can save effort for this recipe.

This recipe has nothing to do with 'The British Raj'. It is clearly a variant of a well known Malaysian dish: Chicken/Curry Kapitan and part of Nyonya food, Nyonya is a title given to married women from the Peranakan ethnic group  descended from marriages between Chinese immigrants and native Malayans, and famous for their cooking which fuses elements from both groups' culinary traditions. Originally from ports like Penang many male Peranakans were sea cooks which is why the dish has spread.
Try serving this over basmati.
Very tasty! mine came out a bit dry ... Followed the directions, used safflower oil.  Anything i could have done better?
Mine came out a little dry, too, but I added a splash of white wine when finishing, and the dish was saucier.

Ok, I enjoyed this but am not likely to cook it a second time. Flavorful and fun to prep, I'm not about to heat an inch of oil to 340° to fry 'the sides' (onion & potato) again soon. Have just finished cleaning up two pans, utensils and bowls, I'm still am not sure what to do w/ the leftover oil it called for. I like the suggestion of the sheet pan option. Thanks for giving me the spice concoctions. Loved them, but I give this a 3.
You can strain the leftover oil through a coffee filter and then use it a you normally would oil or to fry things again

I wholeheartedly agree - came here to say the same thing. I was skeptical when I saw heat an inch of oil to 340 and serve with white bread. Should have skipped this one - will be cleaning kitchen all week and didn't like the recipe which we found to be oily and bland. On the bright side, I'll be buying a splatter guard.
Did not use chicken (sin of sins I guess) but used chunks of pork instead.  This was good enough to keep in my files to make again.  Think it might just be a good potluck.  Maybe?
Indeed, Nonya cooks make a similar dish with pork adding lemongrass, chillis and shrimp paste. It tastes very good but your pork has to be somewhat fatty.

Very good dish, but next time, I'll add more spices than what are called for here.  I made my own garlic/ginger paste and marinated the chicken pieces with that overnight, so they were strong flavors.  But I'll add more of the powdered spices next time.
Very tasty recipe but I will double the initial sauce/spice mix next time.
Kind of a complex recipe for the end result. One pot this or do the sheet method another person mentioned.
I followed the suggestion to make this on a sheet pan.  It was boring and bland.  Not worth the trouble.
Can the chicken be done in an air fryer?
Try it and let us know :)
Excellent! I added a couple of tablespoons of water after the last batch of chicken to deglaze and add those brown bits to the dish as well.
Can something be substituted for the potatoes? They are a nightshade I avoid.

Sweet Potatoes? Winter squash? Parsnips? Cauliflower? Those all have more flavor than potatoes, but with all the spices, they probably wouldn't overwhelm the dish.  I'm not sure how cooking times would need to be changed.
I wonder what was originally used in India in this sort of dish before potatoes made their way there from the Western hemisphere.

As it was cooking I was afraid it would just taste like burnt stuff.  It was terrific! Followed recipe except upped the ginger and garlic (frozen cubes of each). Served w Jaime rice. I’d add more turmeric and a tad more vinegar next time.

Not joining the "food police," but this is an endlessly re-imaginable dish: recipes from the US south to the old "Joy of Cooking's" boneless skinless breasts (easily dried out), to NYT Cooking's own 2019 version with bone-in-skin-on thighs (nice!) to Bobby Flay's overengineered recipe with skinless-bone-in thighs to these boneless-skinless thighs. While curious what "ginger garlic paste"/"Kashmiri chili powder" add, I'll stick with Joy of Cooking's version plus bone-in-skin-on thighs, BTW.

I’d love it if Sam or someone suggested vegetarian versions or included at least one delicious vegetarian option. I could imagine a country captain with ghee (or peanut oil) and cashews, fried onions, garlic, potatoes, carrots, and so on, tomatoes and other junior league ingredients too.

This recipe was just OK. High heat was just too hot for the chicken. My chicken was overly charred and my house filled with smoke. The end result was decent, but we found it fairly dry. If I make it again, I’d serve with tahini wrapped in lavash or pita as it reminded us of shawarma.
Which part of the recipe demands the most skill, as mentioned in the introduction?

It seems like a vindaloo dish, but without the masala and sauce (and thus flavor). Vindaloo originated in Goa, a Portuguese  colony, from a dish called “carne de vinha d’alhos,” i.e. meat with wine vinegar and garlic. It's made with many kinds of meat and seafood, with some variation in spice mixes (masalas). Thus it can get much more interesting than this recipe (and with less clean-up). If you like this recipe, explore vindaloo options too.
For the American South version of Country Captain (which I love too) - use currants NOT raisins (hate raisins).  Delicious!

title: Uncle Glenn’s Onaga (Steamed Red Snapper With Somen) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: Could kimchee be substituted for the chung choi?

Salting your own mustard greens as recommended is probably a better option.  Chung choi is not spicy in the sense of chili pepper spicy.  It's something put in jook and pork hash.  If anyone knows the Chinese characters, I would love if they would post that.  Baek kimchi (white kimchi made without gochugaru) would probably work. That being said, you know the community, substitutes may not recreate the recipe but they may make something delicious :)

Stripped down, this dish can be made without the scallop powder, chungchoi or the oyster sauce as the soy sauce provides enough salt, and the fish juices will provide the rest of the flavor.  Garlic, ginger, green onions, cilantro are essential.  Fish must be fresh!  Eyes must be full and bulging, gills must be red and not slimy.  Overcooking is the ultimate crime against seafood.  10-12 minutes on high, check with chopstick.  Fish should be flaky, not hard. If cooked to bone, it’s done.

Cannot substiture kimchee for the chung toi...it's very different taste experience. Chung toi is closest to preserved turnips, another Chinese preserved vegetable that is salty and sometimes comes in Hunan flavor (spicy)

We just made this to celebrate the eve of Chinese New Year. We had a small fish, 1.5 pounds, but kept everything else as written. The close work of matchsticking was so worth it. It was like a hot fish salad, so fragrant, crunchy and fish-meaty. It’s a joy.
A quick google search revealed that Chung Choi is also just dried salted radish which can be found at any Chinese grocery store. It is also the same ingredient used by Thais for making pad thai.
What is a good substitute for scallop powder. I’m very allergic to scallops? Thanks
@maggie As a substitute for scallops, the recipe suggests fish sauce.
We need substitutes for those of us who are unable to buy ingredients like Chung Choy. At least, tell us what it is.

Indeed, it would be incredibly helpful if the recipe provided the specific name of the ingredient (to google images), a detailed description of the ingredient's appearance, regionality, where to buy it both in and outside of its locale, subs to look for in Asian markets, and how to make our own versions at home. 

The great news is that the "Tips" section contains every bit of this information. The recipe's intro has a little more about chung choi etc. as well.

You can also use a whole sea bass or 2 smaller fishes and steam them, using a deep pasta pot or fish poacher and a steamer racl. The magic in this dish is the shredded greens atop the fish, which is then sizzled with the hot garlic oil (I  keep the garlic bits in the oil)

I really wanted to like this dish for Chinese New Year, but it's so much easier without the somen and the vegetable toppings. The noodles seem like a waste and just a bed for the fish; the veggies too raw to incorporate when eating the fish.

Part 2 - The souring agent for sinigang varies from region to region in the Philippines. Sour fruits, such as kamias (Averrhoa bilimbi) and batuan (Garcinia morella) are commonly used, as are kalamansi (calamondin/citrofortunella), unripe guava and pineapple. Tomatoes are almost always added. Different cuts of pork and beef, meaty fish, shrimp and other crustaceans are the commonly used proteins singly, never combined. Sometimes water used to wash rice is used to add body to the soup.

Part 1 - Make sure to use sour tamarind pulp and not the more commonly-available sweet and sour variety. Sinigang is meant to be sour-and-savory, not sweet-and-sour like other Southeast Asian soups (e.g., tom yum). There are sinigang powder mixes available but they are loaded with other ingredients such as monosodium glutamate.
Just say no to mushrooms, peppercorns and bayleaves, people! At the risk of sounding intolerant, this is one dish I feel strongly about. Do add those ingredients, but please don't call it sinigang.
Do u have a recipe u prefer ?  Lmk  Thxs
Yes please, Jean, if you could share a recipe you prefer.  Thank you.

Growing up, I learned to make sinigang the way my grandma did.  She'd use rice water (the cloudy water you get after washing rice - usually the second wash.  The first is too dirty/dusty. )  It also helps the  broth have a little more body for the sour tamarind, savory pork and umami rich fish sauce to grab onto.  

I've never heard of using powdered shiitake; I'm gonna need to try that and see what it adds to the flavor.
shiitake mushrooms will surely add flavor but it isn't in traditional sinigang recipes. why not!
What would be a substitute for tamarind pulp? tamarind paste?
If you can't find tamarind, lemon juice can work in a pinch.

yes, tamarind paste.  Use caution - the paste might be more concentrated meaning cook has to dilute with water.

since it's free of vegetable stuff - strings, fibers, seeds, tamarind pod shells, etc.) you could probably skip the bit about dissolving the tamarind pulp in a strainer.

The strainer filters out the inedible, tough, chewy parts of the tamarind pulp.

The paste is already cleaned for you.

How much paste depends upon if it is concentrated or not, lie orange juice concentrate.
Lemon juice is not a good substitute. It won’t be sinigang without the tamarind.
We always use Gabi in our recipe & no eggplant or daikon.

Made exactly as is and loved it! Made again, forgot I didn’t have enough dried mushroom, subbed in ground perilla seed for a cup of lost shroom powder, and made a very good not-this-recipe soup. Glad I kept that stuff in my freezer

THIS WAS A MUDDY MESS.  We've rarely been steered wrong by NYT Cooking.  We are experienced cooks with a broad range of skills, familiarity with Asian cuisines and a well-stocked pantry.  We had tamarind pulp and dried shiitake mushrooms on hand.  We enlisted all the requisite fresh ingredients including okra and came up with a sour, muddy barely palatable stew.  Perhaps if you are from a Filipino family you have a good version of this dish.  if not, this recipe  will not endear you.
With mushroom powder, bay leaves, and peppercorn, this is not "sinigang."

Try to use super ripe Manila/Ataulfo/Champagne Mangoes.  Other mango varieties can be too juicy and fibrous, resulting in an icy and stringy dessert.  It’s also best to know the true taste of the mango you are using.  These can take up to seven days to become super ripe (dark spots ok).  Each fruit should yield 1/2 cup, and you need 2 cups fruit per recipe.  Try using whatever best fruit in season to sub for mango (strawberries, peaches) or use canned, drained fruit cocktail for Crema de Fruta.
@Isa Fabro Question: My local markets (Southern California) don’t have a variety of mangoes. Would Asian markets have the varieties the recipe calls for?
Can I use frozen mango for this since it will be frozen anyway?  Would I need to defrost the mango or can i just puree frozen chunks and keep them that way?  Thank you

I can’t recommend using frozen mango, because common frozen mangoes are very juicy and fibrous, which makes the finished dessert icy and stringy.  Manila mango yields the most fruit without fibers or juice, and has the best authentic taste.  Use fresh, super ripe mangoes, or whatever best fruit is in season.  Manila mangoes are one of the most plentiful fruits in the Philippines, and this classic American ice box pie was adapted to suit Filipino tastes during the turn of the 20th century.
I have tried to substitute frozen mangoes for ripe ones and they just don't have enough flavor.  I wouldn't recommend the substitution.
The recipe intro says "If ripe mangoes aren’t available, frozen ones can be used once defrosted."
Curious if the filling would hold up if I used canned mango pulp instead of fresh mangoes?  Too runny?

Hi!  Depending on the type of Mango used, I use Manila mango not only because it is uniquely filipino, but also because it yields the best amount fruit.  Other mangoes like Kent or Haden are too icy because they have more juice.  If you can find Manila/Champagne/Ataulfo mangoes fresh and ripen then at least seven days, this is best.  Thanks, - I
You may want to drain the canned puree to reduce the liquid.

This recipe looks delicious!

Just want to say I LOVE that Isa Fabro, the chef, is helping answer people's questions and troubleshoot in the comments!

Followed directions exactly first time. Except, it is peach season in Alabama, so peaches. Sumptuous.
Technique changes: I froze each layer as I went & that made spreading each layer evenly much easier. And I used a wet spatula for spreading.
To make this vegan I used whipped coconut cream and sweetened condensed coconut milk. Also used ginger snaps for the crust, which nicely complements the mangoes. Really delicious vegan dessert.

Alphonso (actually Ratnagiri) tinned mangoes (from India) work well. Drain
Berries don't work well in this recipe, nor does pineapple, however, bananas do. This is a great recipe that demonstrates how recipes travel and adapt with globalization. Try shaving some fresh coconut if you have it on hand! (Mom learned a version of this from my Dad's Goa compatriot's wife).
Food unites us!

From Sam Sifton: "Here’s my suggestion if you’re using canned mango, which is usually sweetened when it’s processed: Cut the sweetened condensed milk in the recipe down to a third of a cup, and double the lime juice."

I always feel the need to tweak.  The first time I made this, I followed the instructions to the letter, and it was magnificent.  This week, I used ginger snaps for the crust, and added a good quality vanilla extract to the cream.  Just. Wow. Thank you for this recipe.  It is one of my favorite go to desserts for the summer.  I look forward to trying it with fresh, juicy peaches very soon.

I noticed others have had difficulty getting the bottom crust out in one piece.  I used a spring form pan instead (not quite as "lofty" a presentation, but much easier to deal with).  Plus, I made a circle of parchment paper large enough to fit the bottom of the pan.  When all was said and done, I had no problem slicing the semifreddo right down through the ginger snap crust.
Has anyone ever tried this with other fruits?? Peaches or apricots perhaps???
That's an interesting idea. If you try it  with other fruits please do report on the results and outcome.
You can use whatever best fruit is in season.  You an also use canned, drained fruit cocktail.  This version is more like Crema De Fruta.  Thanks, - I

If I can remember when they're finally in season, I'll certainly try it! Or I could try it now with bananas. I think any fruit could be thickened with an appropriately-flavored instant pudding if too runny, or the right kind of modified food starch if you have access to it. Probably good with different cookies, too.
Any ideas on the total weight of the mangos used?

Hi!  Each Manila mango weighs about 6 - 10 oz, but you have to account for the seed, which can take up to a third of the fruit.  One Mango should yield 1/2 cup fruit.  For this recipe, you need 2 cups of fruit, or approximately 1 lb.  Also, it can take up to seven days to ripen the mangoes properly, preferably in a paper bag like peaches.  Mangoes are also a drupe or stone fruit like peaches.  You could say peaches are the mangoes of western culinary tradition.

Thanks, - I

I will surely be making this soon to pay homage to my late father-in-law, one half Filipino, who LOVED to cook! Sadly, I never met him but have heard many stories of his wonderful meals. I'll attempt to bring him to the table, so to speak, with this. However, I'm curious. The recipe says to use a pie plate but the image of the cake appears much taller than that. Might this work just as well in a spring form pan to achieve the height I see?
And the outside edge looks straight.  What I'm not sure of is if it's made in a 9" springform pan, would there be enough of each ingredient?  I may try this making 1 1/2 of each component!

Hi!  This recipe is a classic Filipino dessert that I first learned about from an older generation “Tita” that made it with canned fruit cocktail.  Also called Crema de Fruta, Mango Float.  Your late father-in-law may have had this dessert growing up.  

I use the easy grab Pyrex glass pie plate to make these, but the one pictured looks like a different 9.5” glass pie plate.  Recipe should still work for this.  You can also modify recipe and create individual parfaits in cocktail glasses.

I ended up having to use frozen mango and it still tastes delicious and was easy to make. I let the frozen mango sit out for a little and then drained it. Let it chill in the freezer between each layer - made it much easier to assemble. Recipe could easily adapt to almost any kind of fruit.
As we are told to freeze this, but then thaw prior to serving, if we plan to serve it the same day, why freeze?

It’s is best to make this a day ahead. Depending how strong your freezer is, it will sometimes take longer than seven hours to set up.  I learned this the hard way, but then served it as a parfait style.  Ice box pies are frozen aka semifreddo style desserts.  Making it a day ahead will also free up your host duties.  Just thaw at room temp for 45 - 1 hour, or keep it frozen for traveling to another party.  It will last for two weeks frozen, but keep in mind the mango flavor will start to fade.

I'm already on my 4th batch making these since i live in the philippines w lots of mangoes. a wonderful dessert to stash in your chest freezer. after its been set overnight in the freezer, i take a hot knife and cut portions out and store in smaller food containers to keep in my fridge freezer. when i feel like eating, i microwave a slice (or two) under the bread defrost setting on my microwave. Ready to eat in a few minutes instead of needing the foresight to defrost several hour
And for a bit of extra indulgence, decoration... and calories... drizzle with some cajeta, a caramel made from goats milk famous in Latin America.
totally delicious! I used frozen mangos to make it easier, and they worked perfectly
Note: this recipe really needs a deep dish pie dish (deeper than 2 in) as the proportions will easily overflow a standard depth pie dish.
I’d also be interested in the possibility of using other fruits
Bananas are good, berries and pineapples not so much. Just IMHO. Haven't tried pears or apples, but I think they would work too. Need to try that!
This was only ok, but was fairly easy and something different.  I thought it was a bit too sweet/rich for a "fruit dessert."  Probably won't make it again.

Not a success for me. Mango variety wasn't marked at store - I bought two different kinds, both very ripe but they got very icy and didn't have they yummy flavor. On the second go round for the cake I left it out of the freezer long enough to soften the mango and then was able to appreciate the idea but by that time the cream had gone very soft.

This was really delicious! Super creamy and fruity, tastes light but rich. I made one mistake and baked crust at 350 for ten minutes, made it hard to get out of pan. In future, will just put crumbs in the pan as instructed. I did  middle and top layers that way and was fine. Also, only had time to freeze three hours, was soft but nobody seemed to care. Table was a mess with droopy whipped cream and mango puree. Yum! Nice recipe!!!
Any idea how maya mangoes would work? http://goodfruitguide.co.uk/product/maya/

I have never tried these, but the description looks great.  The mango should be super ripe and sweet, and you shouldn’t have to add any extra sugar.  Try adding some lime juice and taste test.  I’ve used other mangoes and added lime juice, but for the Manila mango I prefer to keep it natural.

I made this with frozen strawberries which I drained and chopped rather than puréed. The dessert turned out fine, but I question the need to freeze, then thaw. The next time I make it, I’ll assemble and put in the refrigerator ~ no freezing. The 2 Tbs of lime juice was a plus.

Depending how cold your refrigerator is (30 - 45 F) dessert may set firm enough.  Ice box desserts are frozen desserts meant to be served semifreddo, which is why you thaw.  Citrus to enhance the fruit flavor is a normal, but if using Manila mango, taste test the acidity, as it may compete with the delicate taste of this particular mango.  If using Kent for Haden, I would use lime juice, but remember to remove excess strings/fibers, and to  drain excess juice or dessert will be too icy.

Epic fail. Could not find Manila mangos in the grocery so champagne mangos were used. Froze in a pie dish overnight and left out 2-3 hours before serving. Upon slicing I realized it was still frozen. Cream portion was thawed but the puréed mango was icy. The mouthfeel, texture and flavor release was not enjoyable. The best part was the graham cracker when it should have been the mango. Very disappointed in this recipe. Left it overnight in the fridge to thaw and awoke to an inedible soupy mess.

This was delicious, but the freezing part was a disaster. I froze it overnight and brought it out 2 hours before serving. It was impossible to cut and just as impossible to scoop and even though the pan had been generously greased we had to hack at the crust to get it out. Everyone was waiting for the pie plate to break.

Being from India, which is where the mango was first identified, and there are purprtedly a thousand varieties, for this recipe, which I concocted a cheesecake version of, I use canned (and drained) ratnagiri (alphonso) mangoes imported from India. 

Fresh Alphonso is also now available  but rather pricey (for me).

All the varieties mentioned for this recipe - Manila mangoes (a.k.a. Ataulfo or Champagne) are smaller than the green mangos usually found in grocery stores and a deep yellow color.  The season is shorter -  April through July.  They are not available year round.

I had a bumper crop of mangoes this year (Kent) which I chopped and froze.  I thawed them out and drained some of the excess liquid for this recipe -- the freezing seems to have eliminated the stringiness and the resulting puree was quite smooth.

I used half graham crackers and half ginger snaps for the base.  For the topping in addition to the crumbs I minced some candied ginger, lime zest, and chiffonade of fresh mint (best added after removing the Supreme from the freezer) -- delicious!

Made this, but I used 1/4 cup sweetened condensed milk instead of 1/2 cup. The mangoes were sweet enough that it didn't need more sugar.

Used 200 grams of graham cracker crumbs, because that's all I had, and melted coconut oil instead of butter.  

Also, I used an 8" square pan lined with parchment paper. No problem getting it out of the pan, but next time I will use a 9" square pan or reduce the cream by 1/2 cup. 

Fabulous!

"Using stand mixer" is not useful to me, at 81 & still making my happy way through the kitchen.
I would appreciate it if you'd add something for just a plain old hand-held electric beater.

I loved the taste of this when I was making and assembling -- the mangoes were in season and perfect, the cream came out beautifully with sweetened condensed coconut milk.  However, after freezing, the whole thing was rock-hard, and only thawed out to an icy tasteless disappointment.  What could have prevented this?

I do not understand how I will get a two-layer cake, as shown in the photo, with a 9.5 pie plate.  How tall is the finished semi-freddo?  The pie looks like it's more the height of a two-layer cake.  I mean it looks taller than it actually sounds.  

What if I use a standard springform pan instead?  Is the recipe's ingredients enough?

Thank you for your help!

Very good. I used a spring form pan and it filled it by more than 3/4, so if you are going to put this in a pie plate, you need to reduce the filling by half or so (or at least the whipped cream part). If I make again, I will increase the mango by 50% and do three layers to increase the mango flavor. Did add lime to the mango and liked the result. I froze layer by layer as suggested by others and that worked well, no need to freeze for 8 hours that way.

I made this with Trader Joe's speculoos cookies, it was terrific!  Next time, I'll cut back on the cream and use a little less condensed milk because I have about half the mixture left over, and it still was mostly cream and not enough fruit flavor (2 cups).  I might try this with apricots and mango because the mango, though beautifully ripe, was not flavorful enough to assert itself over the sweetness of the cream.  
Still, good enough for a re-do -- yum!

Despite expecting failure, this worked beautifully made as-is! Used 5 Kent mangoes (I live in Ontario, not mango country, also not peach season here). I cut off the flesh (avoiding fibrous inner parts), kept sealed in the fridge overnight and drained off excess liquid in morning. Cream was beautiful. Pulp not icy/stringy. Served it maybe too thawed; could have been firmer. But it still cut okay and crust didn't completely fall apart. CRUST AMAZING but browned butter lost some depth when frozen.

Seems a stick and a half is too much butter 
Crumbs are floating 
I’d also have extra graham crackers for to
DO NOT thaw this “overnight”. It was completely melted, and not even remotely sliceable. Such a waste of ingredients.
This is my son's favorite dessert. Started making it for his birthday years ago. He's turning 21 this week and asked for this mango pie for his birthday celebration! Always so happy to oblige!
Sorry but I can’t figure out why this is getting good reviews. Followed the recipe to the letter and: Pallid taste plus strange texture. Mangos deserve better treatment, in my humble opinion.
No need to be fussy with this recipe. I simply line up the graham crackers on the square or rectangular pan like lasagna noodles.

I've made this a few times and it's always a hit at family parties. I stay pretty faithful to the recipe, other than adding a pinch of salt to the crust and the whipped cream (I like salty sweets)! Since the recipe is fairly simple and lets the mangoes shine, I think it's important to only use atafulo/honey mangoes that are really ripe and in season for the best results.

I really enjoyed this but next time will split the sweetened condensed milk between the cream and the mangos. The only thing about this dessert I didn't love was that the mango layer is too frozen/icy. I think mixing some of the condensed milk in would give it closer to an ice cream texture, so that the two layers would thaw at approx the same rate and so be better matched when I'm eating it.
Lime juice is a must. I might try zest as well next time. With the leftover ingredients, I made little pudding cups and honestly like that presentation more than the pie form
Heavy whipped cream is  like mascarpone? Sorry i am really new to cooking

Heavy whipping cream is 36% fat (or more).  Whipping cream, heavy cream, or heavy whipping cream should work in recipes that incorporate whisked air, but fat content of your cream should be at least 30%.  The carton will specify the fat percentage.  The more fat your cream contains, the more stable it will be when whipped.

Heavy whipping cream comes in liquid form, usually sold by the carton in the dairy section, near the milk.

Hi! The recipe calls for heavy whippING cream – because you're going to whip it yourself adding the sweetened condensed milk. This should be a liquid in a carton, you can find it in the dairy section. For more on how to whip cream, here's a good guide (with a vegan option to boot) http://dish.allrecipes.com/how-to-make-whipped-cream . Happy cooking!
No, "heavy whipping cream" is a liquid.  In most stores I've seen it comes in a carton similar to milk.  Mascarpone is more like a light, dessert-y cheese.

Would Alphonso mangos work for this recipe?
They are going to be in season soon.
YES! See my note above. They are the best! They are actually Ratnagiri from the region of Maharashtra in India.

I too am curious about whether this can be made in 9" springform to look more like the photo. And, does it need to be completely thawed? Wouldn't the chilling keep the layers intact?

Also, if freezing then thawing, wouldn't it be messy getting out of pie plate? I can't think it will  hold up in the pretty layers as in the photo if completely thawed and coming out of a pie plate.
Hi!  My original version of this was in a 9” springform pan.  It was very much like a nude cake.  You can find instructions for that online through Sunset Magazine March 2017.

I always use a 9” small offset spatula when serving any kind of pie.  After slicing use the offset to loosen sides and undercrust, and to lift from pie plate.  The crust is crumbly, but is meant to be.  Also, don’t thaw dessert completely. It is meant to be semifreddo like an ice cream cake.  I have served it like this for many events, and the crust remains intact.  Try making individual parfaits.  Cocktail glass, 1/4 c each crumble and mango, layer condensed milk whip, garnish crumble top.
I would use a spring-form lined with wax paper so one can lift it up cleanly. Just a humble suggestion.

Just made this today, after assembling in our pie-dish my first reaction was a 9” springform pan would be better, which I now see in many comments. This was delicious and a keeper! Question, do you need to freeze leftover or will this keep in refrig for a day or two?

Thank you for the kind words.  It is best to not re-freeze anything after thawing.  Any leftovers will keep in the fridge for three days, but make sure to wrap well so it doesn't take on other flavors in the fridge.  Also, please check my original nude cake mango royale recipe version found in Sunset Magazine March 2017.
Prefer not to re-freeze. But DO refrigerate immediately, and consume really soon. If not, throw into the compost pile! This is not medical advice.

I would love to make this but I would need a vegan version. I'm pretty sure that a reasonable facsimile could be created using coconut milk, which is probably the richest of the diary substitutes, but I'm not clever enough to figure it out myself. Any suggestions?

I am planning to make this in a 9" springform pan as my contribution to the family Easter dinner.  I have a couple questions as Isa Fabro already answered the one regarding the springform pan.

1) Would baking the bottom graham cracker layer work? I note that the butter in the crust is already browned in a pan.  Maybe I could use un-browned butter for the bottom layer?
2) Would using some mascarpone cheese in the filling be a good thing?

Thanks!

You can parbake the bottom crust, 10 mins at 325 F for the springform.  Just remember to completely cool before adding the rest of the ingredients.  I don’t parbake the bottom crust only because I’ve never had problem with the undercrust breaking.  You may use mascarpone cheese as a sub, but I would pair with roasted strawberries (macerate 2 cups halved strawberries with 1 cup sugar, lime juice 1 Tbsp 20 mins, Roast 375 F 20 mins.  Again, be sure everything is cooled before assembly.

For the mascarpone cream, I would do 1 1/2 cups cream, 1/2 cup mascarpone cheese, and 1/2 cup sweetened condensed milk.  First paddle the cheese and sweetened condensed milk.  Scrape down sides, then change to whisk attachment and slowly add cream while whipping on medium to medium high until strong whipped peaks form.

Also, you don’t need to Brown the butter if you want to parbake the undercrust, but the brown butter will give an added depth of flavor, in addition to the parbaking.  In regards to my roasted strawberry suggestion, keep in mind that the roasted strawberries will shrunk in size, so I would supplement the finished roasted strawberries with quartered fresh strawberries to get to the 2 cups needed for the recipe.
How about frozen Kesar mango from India?

use whatever mango product has a fresher flavor.  Fresh fruits work best when in season at peak of ripeness.  

Don't puree unripe mangoes when you can puree frozen mangoes picked when sweet and ripe.

This recipe has to be frozen because no other thickeners are used for cream layers.  Would be difficult to slice with liquid filling and just ooze to the side - like melted ice cream.

One could use unflavored gelatin as a stabilizer and then just chill in the fridge for a few hours.
I'm going to make this as noted above but I also want to experiment with a substitution of coconut cream for my dairy-allergic daughter. Has anyone tried it with canned, full-fat, coconut cream?

I used Ataulfo mangoes and let them sit for several days to get over-ripe. This was delicious! I like the whipped cream with the sweetened condensed milk beaten in. The brown butter in the graham cracker crust was a nice touch. I did find I needed to let the pie sit at room temperature for at least an hour after being in the freezer, so that the mango layer could soften up some.

My family always whole graham crackers, not pulsed. And because all purpose cream is the more ubiquitous cream found in Philippine stores, we use that combined with condensed milk. The result is quite liquidy. But that’s okay because it will seep through to the crackers making them soft. Also we cut the mangoes in thin flat slices and layer them close so there are no spaces in between. We top it with the cream and condensed milk mixture. Overnight in the fridge and ready to serve the next day.

Just made this recipe with the fresh mangos. It came out fantastic, a huge hit with my friends and family. Great recipe and will definitely be one of my favorite "go to" recipes when I want to dazzle. Thank you!

had lots of whip cream left over and the pie was very full
mangos were brown inside so used passion fruit from a can
Looks yummy though

hi Isa: 

Have you ever baked the graham cracker crumbs first to firm it up? Any downside to doing this?

Thanks
How long do you cook the butter?  Do you cook till you clarify it (ghee) or just until you brown the butter (~2 mins)?
It is just saying to brown the butter. The word “brown butter” is used in the directions when it comes time to add it the graham cracker crumbs,

Tried this again today and since had such a difficult time serving last time, only froze it for 3 hours before serving. Still could not get the crust out of the pan. Will not waste my time and money again on this one.
Can you use frozen mangoes if no fresh are available?
Yes, once defrorsted.  See last sentence of introduction to the recipe.

The best substitute ofr Manilla Mangos is Atualfa (sometimes called champagne in the US) and widely available. I'm in Mexico wheremanilla mangos are everywhere andthey arethe very, very best. But atualfas also are relatively fiber free.

This looks and sounds wonderful. Where I live we can only get Maya mangoes, which are very good. Would they work In this recipe? I am not familiar with the varieties mentioned so I can’t compare them to the Maya.
Not a fan of graham crackers, so I think I'll try this using shortbread.
Would this work with passion fruit, and if so what would be the measurement of frozen pulp to use?

I made this as originally written here (doubled for a large party) with the Manila mangoes (I live in FL and am fortunate to have these easily accessible.) Huge success! I really think the ripe / fresh fruit was a big factor.  The cream layer is not too sweet and by adding lime, the mangos were just right. Thank you!!
Used 3/4 portion of graham cracker- butter mixture.

I made it with low fat condensed milk and overripe Ataulfo mangos. Brought it to a party, it was a hit!

Has to be frozen solid to be best.
Would peaches work in this?

Just made this superb pie for the fifth time! It's the 3rd year my son has asked for mango royale for his birthday dessert. I've made it with champagne, common, and frozen, all with great results. It is simply delicious!

I don't have a stand mixer; can I use a hand-held mixer?

Also, how much is a "sleeve"? Is a sleeve the same as one sheet of the graham crackers?

Thank you!

I used three Glenn mangoes from our tree. They worked perfectly and are delicious! I would take the advice of Holle and freeze in between layers for easier spending. I love this and will make it often during mango season here in south Florida!
Wish I could try this lemon bar, looks solo good.
It turned into a gloppy mess for me. But tasted good.
this does not all ft into a standard 9.5 inch Pyrex pie plate. Consider a spring-form pan.

Haven’t even eaten yet - it’s still in the freezer- but want to confirm that using quality mangoes must make a difference.  I got some though my restaurant supply delivery better than anything I’ve ever found at the grocery store.  Sweet and soft - dipped the pits in the leftover whipped cream and slurped away as cleaning up.  Used condensed coconut cream bc that’s all I had but I think might be an improvement - agree with the vegan commenter that the coconut flavor is great with the mango.

This was DELICIOUS and huge. I used a 9"spring form pan. It says serves 10 but I would say more like 16. It's very rich.  I would cut back on the whipped cream and increase the mango puree a bit to cut down on the richness. Also I would love to know (as someone else asked) why freeze and then defrost . I never have that much room in my freezer. And then you probably shouldn't refreeze leftovers so you have to keep it in the fridge??
I used Coco Lopez instead of sweetened condensed milk because my beau adores coconut in his desserts. It turned out great.
The Ataulfo mangoes will get very wrinkly toward the end of ripening. This is usual, and they won't be ripe if they haven't wrinkled.
Would passion fruit puree work as a substitute?
Can I use coconut milk/powdered sugar instead of condensed milk?

I’ve grown up with the Tupperware version (only involves multiple layers of whole graham crackers, mango slices and a heavy cream and condensed milk mixture) and this was such a step up! The brown butter on the crushed graham is a must. I was looking for a recipe to use up a box of mangos and so glad I came across this one. Definitely a great summer desert. 

The recipe says to use 2 cups of the mango purée, I used 1 cup per layer on a 9in springform and 1.5 cups would have made a better layer.

I’ve grown up with the Tupperware version (only involves multiple layers of whole graham crackers, mango slices and a heavy cream and condensed milk mixture) and this was such a step up! The brown butter on the crushed graham is a must. I was looking for a recipe to use up a box of mangos and so glad I came across this one. Definitely a great summer desert. 

The recipe says to make 2 cups of mango purée, I used 1 cup/layer in a 9” springform and 1.5 cups would have made a better layer.

This is excellent but I found I had way too much filling for a 9" pie dish so will use a 10" springform next time. Also made it in the morning and it was perfectly frozen and set in time for dessert that night. We all loved it!
My grandmother used to make this using tidbit pineapple pieces (from the can in natural juices, putting the juice aside) instead of mangoes.
I am not fond of sweetened condensed milk.  what would work as a substitute?

I've just finished making the cream for this and can confirm that you don't get the taste of sweetened condensed milk in the whipped cream; it just tastes like a sweet, luscious whipped cream - to me, at least! There's only 1/2 cup of the condensed in 2 cups of the cream. Maybe try cutting it down to 1/4 cup and see what you think? Using a liquid source of sweet instead of the usual granulated sugar seems to make it unbelievably creamy and smooth compared to the whipped creams I'm used to.
Can I use canned mango puree if fresh isn't available?
use coconut cream & sweetened condensed coconut milk

Good recipe but the measurements for the Graham cracker/butter mixture are off. You will likely need to use an entire box (408g) of Graham crackers to get the consistency right if using 1 and 1/2 sticks of butter as instructed. I used the entire box less 2 (25 crackers) for the butter mixture then loosely crumbled up the remaining two for the topping.
I was hoping to make this ahead of time and freeze for about two months. would this

This was fantastic! The only deviation I made was to make it in a 9 inch springform pan since a pie dish didn't seem like it would be tall enough based on the picture. Froze it and let it thaw since I was bringing to an outdoor party. It was a perfect dessert for a hot day, everyone loved it and asked for the recipe. Will be making again!
Mangoes aren’t readily available in my location and I wonder if ripe seasonal peaches would work. The local peaches are just wonderful
@Montanii Semper Liberian another commenter made this with peaches! I bet it would taste amazing

This is a very forgiving recipe. I had to make two 8 inch pies. I used 2 1/2 cups of heavy cream, 6 oz of sweetened condensed milk, and 2 1/2 cups of mango puree. I added cinnamon to the graham cracker crust and vanilla to the cream filling. Amounts were perfect and the pies just disappeared. Delicious!
When ripe mangos are not available, all South Asian grocery stores carry Kesar, a large can of mango pulp. I think that size can would make 3 pies. Or make ice cream with the leftover pulp!
Dont make this unless you plan to have the whole pie eaten.  It will need to be refrozen otherwise.  Not much flavor for a lot of work.  Wont make again

title: Oxtail Stew in Peanut Sauce (Kare-Kare) Recipe
author: Ligaya Mishan

comments.commentBody: Is there a substitute for oxtails?
Online I found out that the best sub is bone-in beef short ribs or shanks. I have never had an oxtail myself.
Have to use oxtail.  On the other hand, this is not the Kare Kare I know.  Deconstructed Kare Kare?
Maybe try beef shortribs? They respond well to braising.

I first cooked a variation of this with a recipe from a Filipino friend, who said she was influenced by a Vietnamese cook.  Anyway, don't substitute for the oxtails, which exude much more collagen than short ribs.  Makes a big difference in taste.  Also substituted turmeric and smoked paprika
Secondly, I use Adams (or any other) real peanut butter that hasn't had the peanut oil extracted.  Can't imagine why one would introduce added sugar and cheap vegetable oil found in Jiff.
Delicious! Please do not use peanut butters that contain transfats such as those mentioned. Any stirred creamy peanut butter will avoid the danger of transfats.
can one substitute freshly ground peanut butter and add as much sugar to make it as sweet as it should be/you like? I just can't see myself putting Jif or Skippy in my food.

It shouldn’t be a problem. One time my dad used crunchy peanut butter and it actually made the dish better with the added crunch. Filipino food in some dishes like the sweet flavour but if you’re not for it it should be fine.

...and no, you certainly DO NOT HAVE to use oxtails.
It’s all about the sauce
Thanks Sally for the ideas on the substitute for the oil. It worked a treat. I made this tonight. It was delicious. I must say oxtail is. It my favorite but I thought the sauce was a total winner!

Any thing I can replace eggplant with..
Also, achieve oil will be difficult to find in Joburg. Any substitute there?

Achioto/achuete oil is made by steeping annatto seeds in oil until colored (allow to steep until cool before straining out the seeds).  Annatto is a mild flavor, used primarily for color. A mixture of paprika and turmeric is an acceptable substitute.  1/2 teaspoon turmeric and 1/2 teaspoon sweet paprika (not hot) or for color only use a teaspoon crushed safflower florets.

Zucchini would be a reasonable sub for the eggplant.
But it still sounds delicious.

My mother only used one good, sturdy pot and cooked all the vegetables together, adding them in just after the peanut butter so they were never too wilty and there was always a good peanut butter flavor. This has always been my favorite dish growing up in a Filipino family - 100%! I don't recall her adding sugar or the achuete oil. But I will try this recipe sans the sugar - thank you!
I have never cooked (or seen in the store) an oxtail. Although I haven't really looked for it either. But I have a lot of venison shanks that I use to make osso bucco. Think that would work?
Has anyone used a pressure cooker? Here in Brazil they usually do oxtail in one - speeds things up.

You can try veal or beef shanks to substitute for oxtail. 

The addition of wine made this fish less authentic. I love kare-kare but the addition of the wine changed the dish’s character and flavor for me. Will skip it next time and use broth instead.

This certainly veers from the traditional kare-kare, such that I would call it kare-kare inspired, but not the dish itself. The traditional kare-kare is unadorned by alcohol, soy, celery, carrots, and thyme (!). My mother would typically use fresh ground peanut butter without added sugar. Achiote oil is used to give it a saffron orange hue. The dish is intentionally left under salted, to make allowances for a small amount of briny bagoong (salted shrimp paste) that is served on the side.

I made this dish a few times because we really enjoy the wine +soy+ PB combo. Given that it is not a traditional Kare-Kare, I decided to use bone-in beef shank instead of oxtail. Honestly, easier to source and much better to eat. More meat, less fat, less bones...No more oxtail for us!
I would think pork would be a good substitute for the ox tails.  Perhaps a roast of some sort ....
Interesting recipe. I love Kare Kare and  have always made it as a one pot dish (much simpler), but I shall try this version. I’m sure it will be delicious
I have a large batch of lamb stock I'd like to get rid of. What does everyone think about using that instead of beef stock?
Why not!
Been there,  done that.  Start over wiwht beef stock
Does anyone have suggestions for substitutions for the alcohol (red wine and Shaoxing wine) in this recipe? I don't want to cook with alcohol. Thanks!

I grew up eating kare kare in family homes. This the first I've heard of anyone putting alcohol in it and I've always enjoyed it without. The step which calls for wine doesn't exist in my mom's repetoire because she (and all the filipino people i grew up around) didn't cook with wine. If what you are loking for is a sub to help lift off the crispy bits I would use a stock or water. If there is one ingredient not to be missed it is the bagoong. A must-have condiment in small bowls at the table.

I have cooked Kare Kare w/o wine and with wine.  The wine helps breakdown the oxtail during the braising process and adds additional richness to the dish.  

Without the wine, you will be braising the meat a bit longer before it gets tender. 

So to cook without wine, try braising the meat in low sodium or no salt beef broth. Expect to braise for an additional hour.

Maharlika Restaurant in New York City, where this recipe is from, does a modern innovative twist on traditional recipes snd they’re very successful at it. This explains the wine. 

I ordered their arroz caldo and ube waffles and both were excellent.
What is bagoong? don't think I've seen that in local stores. Would Korean or Chinese groceries have it?
It's a fermented shrimp paste, jarred and pretty common in asian grocers. You can't skip it here, trust me.

Bagoong comes in different varieties, depending on what is used as the base. Bagoong monamon is fermented anchovies. Mine is Bagong Balayan, which is Bagoong monamon made in Balayan. bagoong terong is made with bonnet fish. See wikipedia for more. Now that I have some, and read about it, you could use fish sauce as a substitute. Seems like the only real difference is the fish sauce is skimmed off the top, and bagoong is the solids. And for meat? Cow neck- purchased from a Spanish butcher.

I cooked this with the following modifications:  I used broth instead of wine.  I did add a bit more cognac.  I didn’t have the achuete or the ingredient that starts with a B.  Never heard of it.   You can’t miss what you’ve never tasted.  The dish was Excellent, even with those two changes.  I didn’t care for the bok choy, seemed bitter.  Otherwise, this is a beautiful meal, superb taste.  I served it with rice.  To me, it needed something to soak up the incredible gravy,... the sauce.
This dish is a staple to Filipino food and needs Bagoong. Eating this "ulam" without it would be remiss. Google "Bagoong", you will find it at your local Asian store.
Can I cook the oxtails and saucein a crockpot once I brown the meat and cook the veggies separately to keep it simple?

I always study the recipe and comments here prior to doing a first run. I've yet to try this recipe. As I read thru this recipe, I kept stumbling over the suggested times because none of the ingredients and their cook times line up. Prep the carrots, and if you follow the suggested time the carrots will be hard and mostly uncooked. I hate raw carrots, no teeth. Ox Tails take up to 6 hours to make them edible. The green beans? The Bok Choy?

Someone please reconsider the length of times.

I agree this recipe is not the authentic Filipino way of cooking kare-kare, with the Cabernet & Shaoxing wine. That said, it would be interesting to try. Traditionally, too, ground peanuts and rice is used instead of the peanut butter.

Once I browned the oxtails and prepared the sauce, I put it in my slow cooker. it took about 6 hours on LOW setting for the oxtails to become tender. After straining and defatting the sauce, and adding the peanut butter, I cooked the vegetables separately in a skillet with the garlic. 
Don't substitute for the oxtails, and folks, 1/2C of commercial peanut butter will not hurt you and adds the sugar this recipe needs.
sub stock for alcohol
Is the bagoong stirred into the stew at the end or mixed into the rice?  I have Thai fermented fish sauce, is that a good substitute?

As with the other reader, I don't remember alcohol (also soy sauce, carrots, celery nor thyme) being used in kare-kare.  I do remember my grandmother adding a little coconut milk but she opts to skip it because it spoils easily with coconut milk.

Definitely a resounding “no!” to Skippy of Jiff. Most supermarkets have stations for peanuts you can grind and make into peanut butter at the amount you want. Failing that, buy raw peanuts and slow roast them in wok or oven. Then run them in your food processor. The roasting heightens the flavor. Well worth the extra step.

I cooked the oxtail for 2-1/2 hrs. and the meat was fork tender.  Flavor was good, but eating it with a fork and knife was hard because the bones are irregular and the meat didn't readily come off the bone.  The next time, I'll cook it longer and shred it off the bone.
Filipinos (like Thais) eat meals with a spoon and fork vs fork and knife, precisely to eat saucy dishes like this with jasmine rice.
Came out very rich and thick. I would use a bit less peanut in the sauce next time and up the heat a bit.

Loved this recipe! My Filipino friend was impressed that I knew to get Bagoong. It's a shrimp fry, fermented paste that's served alongside as a dip. I purchased the Regular, (as opposed to sweet or spicy), by Tropics, from the refrigerated section at our local Fil-Am; it contains no msg. It's a deeply funky, and contains tiny shell fragments. I find it to be delicious with the sweetness from the peanut butter.  Thanks for the amazing recipe!

Surprisingly wonderful. The sauce is to fantastic; the meat tasty and tender; the veggies complement the dish. Great on the second day too. Oxtails have gotten quite expensive but 3 pounds fed the 2 of us generously for 2 days. I made a smoked paprika substitute for the achuete oil. Found bagoong and used it but would not recommend it. It's really smelly and I preferred an equal amount of fish oil to finish the dish.
I'm allergic to eggplant. Any ideas for a good substitute?

I grew up eating Kare Kare in Philippines and no it doesn’t normally have celery. 
Definitely use oxtail for that collagen and melty meat - but really you can use any beef cut that’s slow cooked to tenderness. 
Definitely use peanut butter - natural or creamy are fine but the creamy ones like Jif are good.

I have been using this recipe in combination with my Mom's classic recipe. It has been a huge success with my family. My Dad prefers the classic "filipino way". but he agreed the new way was also delicious.
Great recipe but the prep and cook time took far longer than outlined.

As a German family living in this country, this is exactly the item we have been adding to our otherwise very traditional American Thanksgiving meal as the token German side dish for many years! As I also prepare it from scratch, I recommend to marinate the sliced red cabbage the night before it is cooked. Marinade should include vinegar, red wine (or apple juice), sugar (or honey), and cloves. And I agree, it needs to cook more than 20 minutes to become as soft as it should be.
The best substitute for butter is goose fat. The cornstarch is unnecessary.

I just made this for tomorrow and it's so delicious that I'm moved to let everyone know. I think it will be a great addition to our normal Thanksgiving fare. I made it a day ahead of time as the recipe suggests. I did make one change - I reduced the amount of sugar by half. I think it's plenty sweet as do my tasters, my husband and daughter. Thank you for a great recipe!

Addictive, if you like sweet/sour veggies. Have made this several times and find it to be very well balanced. This definitely worth a try for something unusual in the cabbage family. Don’t add the cornstarch. The first time I followed the recipe and cornstarch made an unpleasant grainy texture. This time without and it is right on.
I've made this several times, everyone loves it!  One time that I made it I used white cabbage and white wine instead of red.  Just as delicious!
My German grandmother made this for Christmas for years, it is wonderful any other time of the year as well.  The red cabbage is so festive.

Made this for Thanksgiving and it was a smash hit.  It was the perfect counterpoint to turkey and mashed potatoes.  Even the cabbage haters in our family loved it.  Followed suggestion of one reviewer and marinated the cabbage overnight before cooking.  Also followed suggestion to make it ahead of time.  Marinated on Monday, cooked on Tuesday, served it on Thursday.
This needed longer cooking to soften. Benefited from some caraway seeds
Good flavor - next time I’ll cut back on the brown sugar a bit; this was overly sweet for my taste.

My Oma and my mother always made this for Christmas. We do it slightly different, we use a tart jam like black current or tart jelly instead of sugar. We also sauté pancetta first. Great dish for thanksgiving or Christmas with a roast or turkey or ham.
Great recipe! Add 3-4 juniper berries if you have them
This was the big hit of the evening last night! Served with pork schnizel, potatoes and cream of mushroom soup. Everyone had two or three helpings of the red cabbage!

I've made this several times and it's so good - and so simple.  I do agree it tastes even better on the second or third day, and as others have suggested, it is especially wonderful with pork.  One more thing I just have to say:  It makes the house smell wonderful!
Okay, I add bacon. Yum!
Add 4 cloves! Makes taste just like mother made it!

This was good, but a little sweeter than i recalled.  next time I will reduce the sugar.  I also agree with other commenters and simmered the cabbage for about 40 minutes to an hour before adding the vinegar, corn starch, and butter.

This dish is so good! I’m trying to cut back on sugar so I substituted red wine with beef broth. Cut back to 1/4 cup of brown sugar. The overall dish still had a lot of sweetness, probably thanks to the apples. I used arrowroot instead of cornstarch to make it gluten free. I’m not sure that the cornstarch is even needed though. This is going to be a new dish in rotation!

Grew up eating this for every holiday in Germany and at many restaurants. Don’t add the corn starch. Recommend cooking it closed-lid for 2-3 hours on a simmer to make the cabbage more creamy. This will taste best if you make it 24-48 hours in advance and then reheat in a pan.
I make my German grandmother’s already creamy Bavarian sauerkraut recipe even creamier in a slow-cooker. I wonder if I can cook rotkraut the same way?
I always make mine in the instapot. So fast and easy! I do use a slotted spoon as the liquid doesn't reduce. (I also don't use as much liquid to begin with, but it's a different recipe.)
Very intense flavor! I added a star anise for marinating overnight. Cook longer, as mentioned by others.
What can I  use instead of   the wine ?
The best way to replace the red wine is using vinegar (balsamico or red wine vinegar).
What be a substitute for butter?
If you're concerned about not eating dairy, you could substitute clarified butter or ghee.

Thanks you sharing your red cabbage recipe.  As a third generation Californian with German and Swiss heritage, red cabbage is served both Christmas and Thanksgiving as part of our holiday meal no matter what the entree.  My family's recipe is simpler and steams 1.5 hours, but I can't wait to try this!
Love this recipe.  It makes a wonderful, tart counterpoint to the buttery, saltiness of the rest of the Thanksgiving meal.
Can the first step be made in a pressure cooker (stovetop or electric) to reduce the cooking time?

I tossed all ingredients into crock pot, leaving out butter. I added 1/4 teaspoon caraway seeds, 3/4 cup cabernet franc, used dark brown sugar, and 3 bay leaves.  All other ingredients as stated in recipe. It sure smells up the house. Will let it cool overnight and sample next day.

Have made this a couple times, both as written and with some substitutes. In a pinch, in place of the cabbage, an equal weight of bagged coleslaw mix works well. If all you have is sweet apples (ex: gala) use them but cut back on the sugar a little. Agree the cabbage could be cooked longer than the 40 minutes in the recipe.
Followed recipe exactly. It turned out great!
Very tasty and colorful. Took a bit longer than the recipe described.

Added a pinch of pumpkin pie spice in place of the cloves, as well as caraway seed (about 1 tbs) and red currant jelly.  Cooked 50 minutes total and made it the day before it was to be served with Sauerbraten and parsley/butter potatoes.

This is exactly how I (a German living in Germany) season my Rotkohl (or Blaukraut, many names for the same velvety goodness). But it needs at least 40 mins cooking time. And it gets even better after being reheated. So prepare it one day in advance, it makes Thanksgiving or Christmas less stressful and your taste buds will thank you.

Made as written with a bit less sugar (1/3 cup). It was the perfect balance between sweet and sour, and the butter gave it a nice silky feel. I had no trouble with the length of cooking. I think the key is to get the cabbage sliced thin enough. A mandolin is very helpful for that.

Have made a very similar recipe to this for more than 30 years.  The difference between this recipe and the one I use is that mine contains three sliced or chopped onions, no cornstarch & no cayenne but it does contain ½ teaspoon ground cinnamon and ½ teaspoon dried thyme.  The cooking method is also different - all ingredients are divided into two layers in a covered ovenproof dish and cooked in the oven at 180°C  (355°) for 2 ½ hours. Really excellent winter dish.

This is my favorite part of the Thanksgiving meal having grown up/ lived in Munich, Germany for 10 years. Its a great addition to the bland (in my opinion) turkey fare. I have adjusted my recipe over the years to include sweetening it with honey. Using butter and oil and different spices and levels of heat. Either way its deliciously sweet and sour and a holiday favorite. I love surprising people with how delicious Germanic food is when done right.

They used to have this on the Wegmans' hot food buffet, and one time a little blue cheese got onto it from another compartment in my food tray. I discovered it was delicious as an addition, and now always add some to my homemade version.  You can easily gt pre-shredded  red cabbage in the supermarket, which cuts the prep time considerably.  This is always a side dish with our Christmas ham!
Just like my Grandmother’s! I have been trying to recreate this for years and am thrilled that this recipe exists. Now to master the potato dumplings.

I don't understand why people are saying the recipe instructs you to cook for only 20 minutes. It is clearly written that you cook it for 20 minutes, add the remaining ingredients and cook for "20 more minutes, or until most of the liquid has cooked off and cabbage is very tender."
I only had a 6 pound cabbage (they grew big in my garden this year) so I tripled the ingredients. Now at 45 minutes and pretty close to being tender.
Y! U! M! I grew up with Rotkraut back home in Austria but this is for sure the best I’ve ever cooked OR eaten. (Sorry, mum!)

This was amazing! I made this for a German dinner party and it brought some much needed freshness alongside the sausage, potato salad, spaetzle, and weiner schnitzel. I liked that you can make it a day or two in advance to cut down on day-of prep. I made a single recipe for 14 people and we only ate half of it! People loved it, but definitely reached for a smaller portion than I had anticipated. This was great leftover the next day with toast and cheese.
Delicious the first day, even better the second day!
With the addition of carraway seeds, this is a classic Viennese dish.
I Will cut down on the butter next time.   I tasted it as a very strong flavor.   I also added  2 tsp caraway seeds since I remember this in my grandmothers red cabbage.
Can it be frozen?? I’m thinking not but I made waaaaayyy too much so I’m gonna give it a shot
I was wondering the same thing. I did not try it.
This is still an excellent recipe for red cabbage.  A favorite with pork or sausage.
This recipe is genius and this whole section of the NYT makes me proud to be a (naturalized) American.
A true Jamaican Turkey Recipe would see two tablespoons of Jerk Spice added to the brine. Jerking the turkey, so to speak, ensures a juicy bird - the heat is a subtle but delicious flavoring (only).
Don't tear the recipe apart. This is one person's adaptation and nothing more. I am sure this is delicious and easy to make for a person that does not have a wood fired grill or smoker.
I made this turkey for a friendsgiving, first turkey I've ever made. And it got amazing reviews. Multiple people said it was the best turkey they've ever had!

I made this last year (for just me and my bf) minus the juniper berries bc I couldn't find them. It was so juicy, flavorful, and a nice change from the usual routine. I'm making it again this year for my whole family...fingers crossed that they enjoy it as much as we did.
Ad Jerk Spice to the brine - it is a fantastic flavor to add - subtle not hot!

Hi! The flavours in the brine are jerk seasonings, just not pre packaged. The only flavor not included is scotch bonnet pepper which would be too spicy for the average American palate, especially if children are at the table. This isn't really supposed to be a 'jerked' turkey. It would need to be cooked over wood, which, is what also gives jerked food its flavour. The more subtle flavor also doesnt overpower the other dishes being served. Thanks for commenting. Happy Thanksgiving!

With all respect due, the spices in the brine are jerk spices -- allspice, cinnamon, thyme, etc. What jerk spices are not listed in the ingredients? Tell me soon, because I'm preparing the brine right now!

This turkey surpassed all expectations. I used a few ground spices instead of the whole ones, using a ground-to-whole conversion chart. We had a free range, 14.2 pound turkey. It was very lean and rendered very little fat and liquid for basting. And it reached temperature about a half hour early. Next time I might throw some dried habanero flakes into the rub for its fruitiness and heat.

Tonight we're making a risotto from the stock and the leftover turkey. Can't wait!
Are the vegetables that are roasted with the turkey served as a side dish? Alongside the turkey?
Has anyone tried stuffing this? Want to do a cornbread stuffing with it.
This was a big hit with my family. The turkey was moist, flavorful and far from bland. Continuous basting is critical; so don't skip this step.
Question for Francine (or any Jamaican chef!): I’m making this Turkey tomorrow and would like to add a fresh habanero to the recipe. Should I add it to the brine or the rub? Thank you!!
Made as directed. Excellent!

I made this recipe as filling for egg rolls.  Easy and absolutely delicious!  I have an abundance of fresh flat leaf parsley in my garden, so substituted 1/2 of the fresh cilantro with fresh flat leaf parsley.  Add a bit of diced sauted hot pepper (habanero or anaheim) if you want a spicier (hotter) filling.

Delish! My 16 yr old made this for dinner last night as egg rolls and it was a huge hit. We did about two thirds of the recipe and it served 4 of us easily accompanied by hot and sour soup. (Hot and sour soup with ginger, Epicurious) Would be great with chicken, shrimp or tofu. She added ginger to the mix as well.

As a Thanksgiving day stuffing for our turkey, this was stunning! I followed the recipe as written, stuffed the bird, and wowed my family. We ate it with my daughter's homemade kimchi among other favorites.
Anybody tried adding shrimp or shredded chicken to this recipe?  I'm going to try it.
I do not understand the purpose of the eggs.  Are they meant to be a binder for the stuffing when baked in the bird?  Or are they meant to be like eggs in fried rice.  I cannot tell from the picture.

Add pasture-raised eggs to everything. Always. Because they are delicious! So delicious!

I would imagine, in this case, the eggs add flavor and texture, of course! Just like in an egg roll. If you want more of a scoopable dressing, you can always... add more eggs!!!! Yum!

I used this a guide for egg rolls. I started with ground pork and because it didn’t have any cabbage, I substituted shredded carrots and shredded fennel. It was an excellent combination and even my fussy teen son liked it.
